topics.title: Introduction to Biomedical Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Biomedical Telemetry # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Biomedical Telemetry** is the branch of biomedical engineering concerned with the *remote measurement, transmission, and reception of physiological data* from a living subject (human or animal) to a distant monitoring station, using a wired or wireless communication link, without physically tethering the subject to the recording instrument. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):** > *Biomedical telemetry deals with the transmission of biological data from a measuring instrument situated at one location to a receiver situated at a different location. It is the science of automatically measuring and transmitting the readings of an instrument to a remote location.* The etymology is Greek-Latin: **Tele** (τῆλε) = *far/distant* + **Metron** (μέτρον) = *measure*. Hence, telemetry literally means **"to measure from a distance."** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Stethoscope" Imagine a doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat. In a normal setup, the stethoscope is physically connected to the chest piece and the ears. Now, suppose the doctor is in **Kozhikode**, while the patient is climbing a mountain in **Wayanad**. The heartbeat sound must travel kilometers to be heard. In this scenario: - The **stethoscope chest piece** becomes a **sensor/transducer** (e.g., ECG electrode). - The **sound traveling through the stethoscope tube** is replaced by **radio waves (RF), light (optical), or sound (ultrasonic)** traveling through the air. - The **doctor's ears** become an **antenna + receiver + display**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** Telemetry decouples the *location of measurement* from the *location of interpretation*. The patient (source) and the doctor (sink) need not be in the same room, building, city, or even continent. ## 1.3 Core Building Blocks (At a Glance) | # | Block | Function | Real-World Example | |---|-------|----------|-------------------| | 1 | **Transducer / Sensor** | Converts a physiological variable into an electrical signal | ECG electrode, thermistor, pulse oximeter | | 2 | **Signal Conditioner** | Amplifies, filters, A/D converts the weak bioelectric signal | Instrumentation amplifier, active filter | | 3 | **Transmitter (Tx)** | Modulates the conditioned signal onto a carrier wave | RF transmitter at **433 MHz / 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz** | | 4 | **Channel / Medium** | Carries the signal from Tx to Rx | Free space (radio), optical fiber, telephone line | | 5 | **Receiver (Rx)** | Demodulates and reconstructs the original signal | SDR receiver, superheterodyne tuner | | 6 | **Display / Recorder** | Visualizes / stores the recovered bio-signal | LCD monitor, CRO, cloud database | ## 1.4 Classification of Biomedical Telemetry (Foundation Knowledge) Biomedial telemetry is classified based on the **transmission medium** and the **multiplexing scheme**. - **Based on Medium:** - *Wired Telemetry* (e.g., telephone link, fiber optic, LAN/Ethernet). - *Wireless Telemetry* (e.g., Radio Frequency, Microwave, Optical, Ultrasonic). - **Based on Application:** - *In-hospital patient telemetry* (e.g., ambulatory ECG Holter). - *Extra-corporeal / Ambulatory telemetry* (e.g., wearable fitness bands). - *Space & sports telemetry* (e.g., NASA astronaut vitals). - *Animal biotelemetry* (e.g., wildlife tracking collars). - **Based on Multiplexing:** - *Single-channel telemetry* - *Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)* — multiple signals share one channel sequentially. - *Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM)* — multiple signals share one channel at different carrier frequencies. > [!TIP] > **Why telemetry is critical in modern healthcare:** The WHO estimates that **non-communicable diseases account for 74% of global deaths**. Continuous, remote monitoring enabled by telemetry shifts healthcare from *reactive* (treat at hospital) to *proactive* (treat at home), reducing ICU burden by an estimated **30–40%** in chronic care pilots. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The "Why" and the "How" The chain of signal flow in any biomedical telemetry system is **invariably the same**, regardless of the carrier (radio, optical, ultrasonic). Understanding *why* each block exists is the key to writing high-scoring KTU answers. 1. **Why a Transducer?** Human physiology operates in *chemical, mechanical, thermal, and electrical* domains. To measure any of these, we need a *transducer* — a device whose electrical output is a deterministic function of the input physiological quantity. 2. **Why Signal Conditioning?** Bioelectric signals are notoriously weak. For instance, the ECG signal has an amplitude of only **0.5 mV to 5 mV**, and the EEG is even smaller (**10 µV to 100 µV**). The signal must be **amplified** (typically 1000×–10,000×) and **band-pass filtered** to remove DC offset, baseline wander, and 50/60 Hz power-line interference. 3. **Why a Transmitter (Modulation)?** A raw baseband bio-signal occupies a very low frequency (e.g., ECG ≈ 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz). Such low frequencies have antenna lengths that are physically impractically large (Antenna length ≈ λ/4, where λ = c/f). By *modulating* the bio-signal onto a high-frequency carrier (e.g., **433.92 MHz ISM band** or **2.45 GHz**), the antenna shrinks to a few centimeters. 4. **Why a Channel?** The medium introduces **attenuation, noise, fading, and interference**. The design of the telemetry system must account for a minimum **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the receiver. 5. **Why a Receiver?** Performs *demodulation*, *amplification*, *filtering*, and *display* of the recovered bio-signal, often in real time. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **authoritative KTU 2024 quick-reference** for telemetry calculations. Memorize the column headers and the *form* of each equation; values and units are case-sensitive in the answer sheet. | # | Formula / Theorem | Symbolic Form | Where Used | SI / Standard Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing in digitized bio-signals | $f_s, f_m$ in **Hz** | | 2 | **Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum achievable data rate of a telemetry channel | $C$ in **bits/s**, $B$ in **Hz**, $S/N$ dimensionless | | 3 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB)** | $\mathrm{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Link-budget analysis in wireless telemetry | dB | | 4 | **Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)** | $\mathrm{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | RF link budget for outdoor patient telemetry | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz**, result in **dB** | | 5 | **Wavelength of Carrier** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Antenna design for telemetry transmitter | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**, $f$ in Hz | | 6 | **Friis Transmission Equation** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Received power in a radio telemetry link | $P_t, P_r$ in **W**, $G$ dimensionless, $d$ in **m** | | 7 | **Modulation Index (AM)** | $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Depth of amplitude modulation in analog telemetry | Dimensionless (0 ≤ $\mu \leq 1$) | | 8 | **PCM Bit Rate** | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | Bit rate for Pulse Code Modulation in digital telemetry | **bits/s** | | 9 | **Time-Division Multiplexed Frame Rate** | $f_{frame} \geq N \cdot f_m$ | Frame rate required to interleave N signals | **Hz** | | 10 | **Amplifier Gain (dB)** | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Gain of bio-signal preamplifier | dB | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip for KTU Exam Sheets:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` in $\log_2(1+S/N)$ is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` — never use the keyboard `|` as it conflicts with markdown tables and confuses examiners reading digital answer sheets. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cardiology:** Holter monitors use **single-channel TDM telemetry** to record 24–48 hours of ambulatory ECG, detecting intermittent arrhythmias that would be missed in a 10-second in-clinic ECG. - **Neonatal ICU:** Wireless skin-patch sensors transmit SpO₂, heart rate, and respiratory rate via **2.4 GHz Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** to a central nursing station. - **Space Telemetry:** NASA's Bio-Monitor system streams astronaut vitals via **S-band (2.0–2.3 GHz)** and **Ku-band (12–18 GHz)** to ground control. - **Wildlife Telemetry:** GPS-collared animals transmit location + heart rate via **Iridium satellite links (1626.5 MHz)** for ecological research. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!WARNING] > **Exhaustive Mode Enabled.** No algebraic step is omitted. The derivations below are *fully expanded* and are exact replicas of what a KTU Board examiner expects in a 14-mark derivation answer. Practice writing them by hand; each derivation is approximately 1.5 pages of A4. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate from Nyquist Theorem **Statement:** A bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ whose maximum frequency component is $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the inequality $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **Step 1 — Define the sampled signal mathematically.** Let $x(t)$ be a bandlimited analog bio-signal (e.g., ECG). The sampled version $x_s(t)$ is obtained by multiplying $x(t)$ with an ideal impulse train: $$ x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) $$ **Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.** Using the multiplication-in-time / convolution-in-frequency property, and noting that the Fourier transform of an impulse train is another impulse train in the frequency domain: $$ X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Identify the aliasing condition.** Here, $\frac{1}{T_s} = f_s$. So the spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic repetition of the original spectrum, shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$. To prevent spectral overlap (aliasing), the shifted copies must not touch the central copy: $$ 2 f_m \leq f_s $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** $$ \boxed{\,f_s \geq 2 f_m\,} $$ This is the **Nyquist Rate** — the *minimum* sampling frequency. For ECG with $f_m = 150 \text{ Hz}$, $f_s \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$ (clinical standard uses 360 Hz or 500 Hz to provide a guard band). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity **Statement:** The maximum achievable error-free data rate $C$ (bits/s) of a communication channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is given by $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. **Step 1 — Quantize the source into $M$ discrete levels.** For an analog source uniformly quantized into $M$ levels, each sample carries: $$ \text{Information per sample} = \log_2(M) \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Nyquist to determine the sampling rate.** The number of independent samples per second that the bandwidth $B$ can support is, by Nyquist, $2B$ samples per second (for a baseband channel with no DC). **Step 3 — Compute the bit rate.** $$ C = 2B \cdot \log_2(M) \text{ bits/s} $$ **Step 4 — Express $M$ in terms of SNR.** For a channel corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) of variance $\sigma^2$ and a signal of power $S$, the maximum number of distinguishable levels is limited by the noise: $$ M = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} $$ *Reasoning:* Each sample can vary over a voltage range of $\sqrt{S+N} - \sqrt{N}$ when normalized; squaring gives the SNR-scaling factor. The square root arises from the two-sided symmetric quantization grid. **Step 5 — Substitute $M$ back into the bit-rate equation.** $$ C = 2B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Apply the logarithm identity $\log_2(\sqrt{X}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(X)$.** $$ C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 7 — Final result.** $$ \boxed{\,C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** Doubling the bandwidth $B$ doubles the capacity, but doubling the SNR (in linear units) only increases capacity logarithmically. This is why **fiber-optic and ultra-wideband (UWB) telemetry** prioritize *bandwidth* over *power*. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation in dB Form) **Step 1 — Start from Friis' transmission formula in linear form.** $$ \frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 $$ **Step 2 — Take the logarithm base-10 of both sides (multiply by 10 to convert to dB).** $$ 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_r}{P_t}\right) = 10\log_{10}(G_t) + 10\log_{10}(G_r) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) $$ The factor 20 appears (not 10) because the bracketed term is a *distance ratio*, not a power ratio. **Step 3 — Substitute $\lambda = c / f$.** $$ 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{4\pi d f}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{4\pi}\right) - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the constant $\frac{c}{4\pi}$.** With $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$: $$ \frac{c}{4\pi} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{12.566} \approx 2.387 \times 10^7 $$ **Step 5 — Convert to dB.** $$ 20\log_{10}(2.387 \times 10^7) = 20 \times 7.378 \approx 147.56 $$ **Step 6 — Normalize for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.** Multiplying and dividing by the unit conversions ($d_{\text{km}} = 1000 d_{\text{m}}$ and $f_{\text{MHz}} = 10^6 f_{\text{Hz}}$) yields the canonical formula: $$ \boxed{\,\mathrm{FSPL}\ (\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{\text{km}}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f}{\text{MHz}}\right)\,} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Telemetry Parameters | Application | Carrier Frequency | Bandwidth | Typical Range | Antenna | |---|---|---|---|---| | Implantable cardiac telemetry (pacemaker) | 402–405 MHz (MICS band) | < 300 kHz | 2–5 m body-internal | Embedded loop | | Ambulatory Holter (wearable) | 433 MHz ISM | 10 kHz | 10–30 m | Patch / patch dipole | | Hospital Wi-Fi telemetry | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 20–160 MHz | 30–100 m | Chip antenna | | Satellite biotelemetry (wildlife) | 1.6 GHz L-band | 25 kHz | Global | Helical / patch | | Optical telemetry (line-of-sight) | $10^{14}$–$10^{15}$ Hz (IR/visible) | MHz–GHz | 10–100 m | LED / photodiode | | Ultrasonic telemetry (underwater) | 20–200 kHz | 5 kHz | < 50 m | Piezoelectric transducer | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagrams below are **Mermaid-rendered** using strict node-identifier rules. Every node ID is alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `Tx1`), and no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric strings. ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Single-Channel Biomedical Telemetry System ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["S1 SUBJECT
PATIENT OR ANIMAL"] S2["S2 TRANSDUCER
ECG EEG EMG SpO2"] S3["S3 SIGNAL CONDITIONER
AMP FILTER"] S4["S4 A D CONVERTER
OPTIONAL FOR DIGITAL"] S5["S5 MODULATOR
AM FM PCM"] T1["T1 TRANSMITTER
RF CARRIER"] CH["CH WIRELESS CHANNEL
FREE SPACE FIBER"] R1["R1 RECEIVER
ANTENNA TUNER"] R2["R2 DEMODULATOR
REVERSE OF MOD"] R3["R3 DISPLAY
LCD CRO PC"] R4["R4 RECORDER
STORAGE CLOUD"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> T1 T1 --> CH CH --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R4 ``` **Interpretation of the Flow:** - `S1 SUBJECT PATIENT OR ANIMAL` is the *source* of the physiological variable. - `S2 TRANSDUCER ECG EEG EMG SpO2` converts the variable into a measurable electrical signal. - `S3 SIGNAL CONDITIONER AMP FILTER` boosts the weak bioelectric signal to a usable level. - `S4 A D CONVERTER OPTIONAL FOR DIGITAL` samples the conditioned analog waveform. - `S5 MODULATOR AM FM PCM` encodes the baseband signal onto a carrier. - `T1 TRANSMITTER RF CARRIER` radiates the modulated wave via antenna. - `CH WIRELESS CHANNEL FREE SPACE FIBER` carries the signal across the distance. - `R1 RECEIVER ANTENNA TUNER` captures the incoming EM wave. - `R2 DEMODULATOR REVERSE OF MOD` recovers the original baseband signal. - `R3 DISPLAY LCD CRO PC` presents the data in real time to the clinician. - `R4 RECORDER STORAGE CLOUD` archives the data for retrospective analysis. ## 4.2 Architectural Block — Multi-Channel TDM Telemetry ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["A1 CH1 ECG"] A2["A2 CH2 EEG"] A3["A3 CH3 EMG"] A4["A4 CH4 BLOOD PRESSURE"] MUX["MUX TIME DIVISION
MULTIPLEXER"] TX["TX SINGLE RF
TRANSMITTER"] CH1["CH1 SHARED
WIRELESS CHANNEL"] DEMUX["DEMUX TIME DIVISION
DEMULTIPLEXER"] B1["B1 ECG MONITOR"] B2["B2 EEG MONITOR"] B3["B3 EMG MONITOR"] B4["B4 BP MONITOR"] A1 --> MUX A2 --> MUX A3 --> MUX A4 --> MUX MUX --> TX TX --> CH1 CH1 --> DEMUX DEMUX --> B1 DEMUX --> B2 DEMUX --> B3 DEMUX --> B4 ``` **Interpretation:** Four biosignals (ECG, EEG, EMG, BP) are *time-sliced* into a single data stream by the multiplexer `MUX TIME DIVISION MULTIPLEXER`, transmitted over one shared channel `CH1 SHARED WIRELESS CHANNEL`, then separated at the receiver end. This architecture saves bandwidth and hardware cost in ICU multi-parameter monitors. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Radio vs Optical vs Ultrasonic Telemetry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RF["RF TELEMETRY"] RFS["RFS ELECTRODE"] RFC["RFC RF TX 433 MHZ"] RFR["RFR RF RX ANTENNA"] RFS --> RFC --> RFR end subgraph OPT["OPTICAL TELEMETRY"] OPS["OPS LED DRIVER"] OPB["OPB PHOTODIODE"] OPS --> OPB end subgraph US["ULTRASONIC TELEMETRY"] USS["USS PIEZO TX 40 KHZ"] USR["USR PIEZO RX HYDROPHONE"] USS --> USR end ``` > [!TIP] > Use this **comparative diagram** in answer-book sketches when the question asks *"Compare radio, optical, and ultrasonic telemetry."* A side-by-side Mermaid block is far more examiner-friendly than three separate drawings. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** Part A questions are *direct recall / one-line definition / short explanatory* type. They test **Remember (L1) and Understand (L2)** of Bloom's Taxonomy. Word limit: 60–80 words. Provide the definition, one example, and one application. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B] *(3 Marks)* **Define biomedical telemetry. List its three major applications.** **Model Answer:** > Biomedical telemetry is the technique of **measuring and transmitting physiological data from a remote subject to a distant receiving station** without a physical connection. The word is derived from the Greek *tele* (far) and *metron* (measure). > > **Three major applications:** > 1. **Ambulatory patient monitoring** — e.g., Holter ECG recording. > 2. **Space and military medicine** — e.g., astronaut vital-sign transmission to ground control. > 3. **Wildlife and veterinary biotelemetry** — e.g., tracking heart rate in free-ranging animals. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] *(3 Marks)* **Distinguish between single-channel and multi-channel telemetry with one example of each.** **Model Answer:** > | Feature | Single-Channel | Multi-Channel | > |---|---|---| > | **Number of signals** | One physiological signal at a time | Two or more signals simultaneously | > | **Hardware** | Simpler, cheaper | Requires multiplexer | > | **Bandwidth usage** | Low | Higher (FDM/TDM needed) | > | **Example** | Wireless ECG patch transmitting HR | ICU monitor with ECG + SpO₂ + BP | > > *Example of single-channel:* A wireless pulse oximeter finger probe. > *Example of multi-channel:* A 3-lead ambulatory ECG + respiration monitor. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one 14-mark question. You are given an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B** (both from the same module). Each question has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. Attempt **any ONE**. ### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a **single-channel radio telemetry system** used for ECG monitoring. *(7 Marks)* **A. (b)** Derive the **Shannon–Hartley channel capacity theorem** and compute the maximum data rate of a telemetry channel of bandwidth $4 \text{ kHz}$ and $\mathrm{SNR} = 30 \text{ dB}$. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — A(a) The **single-channel radio telemetry system** for ECG monitoring consists of the following sequential blocks (refer to **Section 4.1** for the Mermaid diagram). 1. **Subject (S1):** The patient whose ECG is to be monitored. 2. **Transducer (S2):** Three Ag/AgCl surface electrodes placed on the chest in a standard lead configuration (Lead II). The electrode acts as a transducer, converting ionic bioelectric potentials (~1 mV) into an equivalent electronic voltage. 3. **Signal Conditioner (S3):** An **instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620)** with differential gain $A_v \approx 1000$ amplifies the millivolt signal. A **band-pass filter (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz)** rejects DC offset and high-frequency noise. A **50 Hz notch filter** removes mains interference. 4. **A/D Converter (S4) — optional:** For digital telemetry, the analog ECG is sampled at $f_s \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$ (per Nyquist theorem) and quantized to 12 bits. 5. **Modulator (S5):** The baseband ECG modulates an RF carrier. **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is preferred over AM for noise immunity — typical deviation $\Delta f = \pm 4 \text{ kHz}$ for narrowband FM. 6. **Transmitter (T1):** A **Colpitts or crystal oscillator** at 433.92 MHz (ISM band) drives a $\lambda/4$ monopole antenna. Effective radiated power is typically < 10 mW (compliant with FCC Part 15 / ERC 70-03). 7. **Channel (CH):** Free space between the patient and the receiver antenna. 8. **Receiver (R1):** A **superheterodyne receiver** with a local oscillator offset by 10.7 MHz IF performs the down-conversion. 9. **Demodulator (R2):** A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** or **discriminator circuit** (e.g., LM565) recovers the original ECG waveform. 10. **Display / Recorder (R3, R4):** The recovered ECG is shown on a bedside monitor, archived in a hospital PACS, or pushed to a clinician's mobile device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key advantages of this architecture:** Patient is *untethered* (mobility), real-time, low-power, FCC/ETSI compliant. #### Model Solution — A(b) **Step 1 — State the Shannon–Hartley theorem.** $$ C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Derive (as in Section 3.2).** The derivation proceeds by quantizing the source into $M$ levels, applying Nyquist's sampling rate, and substituting $M = \sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (justified by Gaussian noise statistics and symmetric quantization): $$ C = 2B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right) = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Convert SNR from dB to linear.** $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** Given $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$ and $S/N = 1000$: $$ C = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$.** $$ \log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/sample} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the final answer.** $$ C = 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39{,}868 \text{ bits/s} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps} $$ **Step 7 — Conclusion.** $$ \boxed{\,C \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}\,} $$ > This is **more than sufficient** to transmit a 12-bit, 300-Hz-sampled ECG (≈ 3.6 kbps) and leaves substantial margin for FEC (forward error correction) headers and encryption. --- ### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **B. (a)** Classify the various **types of biomedical telemetry systems** with a clear flowchart. *(7 Marks)* **B. (b)** A telemetry transmitter operates at $f = 900 \text{ MHz}$ with a transmit power of $P_t = 100 \text{ mW}$ and isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$). If the receiver is at $d = 5 \text{ km}$, compute the **received power** and the **free-space path loss**. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution — B(a) Biomedical telemetry systems are classified along **three orthogonal axes** (refer to **Section 1.4** for the conceptual taxonomy): **Axis 1 — By Transmission Medium:** 1. **Wired Telemetry** - Telephone-line telemetry (e.g., transtelephonic ECG). - Optical-fiber telemetry (used in MRI rooms where RF is forbidden). - Power-line carrier telemetry (PLCT). 2. **Wireless Telemetry** - **Radio-frequency (RF) telemetry** — most common; 27 MHz, 433 MHz, 2.4 GHz. - **Microwave telemetry** — 1–30 GHz; used in satellite links. - **Optical telemetry** — IR or visible light; line-of-sight. - **Ultrasonic telemetry** — 20–200 kHz; used underwater or in shielded rooms. **Axis 2 — By Multiplexing Scheme:** 1. *Single-channel telemetry* — one signal. 2. *Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM)* — multiple signals at different carrier frequencies. 3. *Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)* — multiple signals sequentially interleaved. **Axis 3 — By Application Domain:** 1. *In-hospital patient telemetry* (bedside → central nursing station). 2. *Ambulatory telemetry* (Holter, wearable patches). 3. *Implantable telemetry* (pacemaker, ICD). 4. *Space and extreme-environment telemetry* (astronaut, deep-sea diver). 5. *Animal biotelemetry* (livestock, wildlife, lab rodents). **Flowchart summary (in text form):** ``` BIOMEDICAL TELEMETRY ├── By Medium │ ├── Wired (Telephone, Fiber, PLC) │ └── Wireless (RF, Microwave, Optical, Ultrasonic) ├── By Multiplexing │ ├── Single-channel │ ├── FDM │ └── TDM └── By Application ├── Hospital ├── Ambulatory ├── Implantable ├── Space / Extreme └── Animal ``` #### Model Solution — B(b) **Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI.** $$ f = 900 \text{ MHz} = 900 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ P_t = 100 \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ W} $$ $$ d = 5 \text{ km} = 5000 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute wavelength.** $$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ m} \approx 0.3333 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Friis transmission equation.** $$ P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the numbers.** $$ P_r = 0.1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \left(\frac{0.3333}{4 \pi \cdot 5000}\right)^2 $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the bracketed term.** $$ \frac{0.3333}{4 \pi \cdot 5000} = \frac{0.3333}{62{,}831.85} = 5.305 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 6 — Square it.** $$ (5.305 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.814 \times 10^{-11} $$ **Step 7 — Compute $P_r$.** $$ P_r = 0.1 \times 2.814 \times 10^{-11} = 2.814 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 2.814 \text{ pW} $$ **Step 8 — Convert to dBm for clarity.** $$ P_r \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.814 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.814 \times 10^{-9}) \approx -85.5 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 9 — Compute FSPL using the dB formula.** $$ \mathrm{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{\text{km}}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f}{\text{MHz}}\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(900) $$ $$ = 32.44 + 13.98 + 59.08 = 105.50 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 10 — Final answers.** $$ \boxed{\,P_r \approx 2.81 \text{ pW} \approx -85.5 \text{ dBm}\,} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\mathrm{FSPL} \approx 105.5 \text{ dB}\,} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit conversion slip:** Many students write $d = 5$ m instead of $5 \text{ km}$ when plugging into the formula — this changes the answer by **60 dB** (a factor of 1,000,000). Always convert to SI or the formula's native units *before* computing. > 2. **Missing $G_t, G_r$:** Defaulting to unity isotropic gain is fine if explicitly stated; forgetting them entirely yields the wrong sign convention. > 3. **Sign of dB:** Remember that $\mathrm{FSPL}$ is a *loss* (positive dB). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write "negative path loss" thinking it is a gain. > 4. **Telemetry-specific pitfall:** When asked "derive", students often *state* the Shannon formula without showing the $\log_2$ derivation (Step 1 → Step 7). KTU examiners specifically reward the intermediate algebraic steps. Always write out: $M$ → Nyquist rate → $2B \log_2 M$ → $M = \sqrt{1+S/N}$ → final form. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Print this checklist on a sticky note before every KTU exam day.** - [ ] **Etymology first:** *Tele* = far, *Metron* = measure. Always start with the Greek root — examiners love it. - [ ] **Six blocks, in order:** *Subject → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Modulator → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Demodulator → Display.* - [ ] **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. ECG ($f_m = 150 \text{ Hz}$) → sample at 360 Hz clinically. - [ ] **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. Doubling $B$ doubles $C$; doubling $S/N$ adds only $\sim 1$ bit/s/Hz. - [ ] **FSPL formula:** Memorize $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$. - [ ] **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — only valid in *far-field* ($d \geq 2D^2/\lambda$). - [ ] **Wavelength = c/f.** Use this to design $\lambda/4$ antennas. For 433 MHz, $\lambda/4 \approx 17.3$ cm. - [ ] **SNR in dB:** Use $10 \log_{10}(P_1/P_2)$ for power, $20 \log_{10}(V_1/V_2)$ for voltage. - [ ] **Multiplexing trade-off:** FDM is *continuous* (analog-friendly), TDM is *sampled* (digital-friendly). Modern telemetry prefers TDM. - [ ] **Modulation choice:** FM > AM for bio-signals (better noise rejection, constant envelope for power efficiency). - [ ] **ISM bands for medical telemetry:** 27 MHz, 174–216 MHz, 433 MHz, 608–614 MHz (WMTS), 2.4 GHz. Implantable MICS band: 402–405 MHz. - [ ] **Safety limits:** FCC EIRP ≤ 10 mW for body-worn devices; SAR limits per IEEE C95.1 (1.6 W/kg over 1 g tissue). - [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** Never use the keyboard `|` inside markdown table cells; use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. - [ ] **Examiner's pet answer:** Always draw a *labeled block diagram* even if the question does not explicitly ask — a single diagram earns 2 free marks. - [ ] **Real-world anchor:** Remember the analogy — *invisible stethoscope.* It explains telemetry in 30 seconds to any non-technical reader. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Biomedical Telemetry** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme | Bloom's Levels: Remember (L1) → Analyze (L4)*

topics.title: Typical Biomedical Telemetry System: Challenges in Biomedical Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Biomedical Telemetry System: Challenges in Biomedical Telemetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Biomedical Telemetry System** is an integrated electronic instrumentation framework designed to acquire, encode, modulate, and wirelessly (or via wired link) transmit physiological signals — such as ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO₂, body temperature, and blood pressure — from a **remote or ambulatory subject** to a **central monitoring station** for real-time clinical observation, diagnosis, and data archival, without restricting the natural mobility of the patient.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT862 – Telemedicine)** terminology, the system is classified as a *closed-loop patient monitoring infrastructure* whose fundamental purpose is to **bridge the spatial gap** between the biological source (human body) and the diagnostic/therapeutic decision-maker (clinician).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A *typical* biomedical telemetry chain consists of seven canonical blocks — **Sensor → Amplifier → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Encoder/Modulator → RF Transmitter → Receiver & Display**. Every KTU board question on this topic is expected to be answered using this canonical block architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **news correspondent reporting live from a remote war zone**. The reporter (the *sensor on the patient's body*) speaks softly into a sensitive microphone (the *bio-amplifier*). The audio is filtered to remove background rumble (the *signal conditioner*), converted into a stream suitable for satellite transmission (the *ADC + encoder*), and beamed to a TV station (the *RF link*). At the studio, the signal is decoded, displayed, and analyzed by an expert (the *clinician at the receiver*).

In the same way, the human body produces **tiny electrical whispers (millivolts to microvolts)** that must be captured, cleaned, digitized, transmitted, and reconstructed faithfully for a doctor miles away to make life-saving decisions. Any disturbance in this chain — a dropped signal, electrical hum, or patient shock — can be catastrophic.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Numbers)

The KTU examiner expects the following engineering constants to be memorized:

- **ECG signal amplitude:** **0.5 mV – 5 mV**
- **EEG signal amplitude:** **5 µV – 300 µV**
- **EMG signal amplitude:** **50 µV – 5 mV**
- **Frequency band of ECG:** **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**
- **Frequency band of EEG:** **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz**
- **Standard body-implantable transmission frequency (MICS band):** **402 MHz – 405 MHz**
- **Allowed leakage current (patient auxiliary):** ≤ **10 µA** (CF-type applied parts, IEC 60601-1)
- **Typical biotelemetry carrier frequency (ISM):** **433 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in exams:** Questions on bandwidth selection, antenna design, and amplifier gain are routinely set around the ECG/EEG amplitude and frequency figures. Memorize them.

## 1.4 The Seven Canonical Blocks of a Biomedical Telemetry System

| Block # | Functional Block | Engineering Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Bio-Sensor / Transducer** | Converts physiological variable (chemical, mechanical, electrical) into electrical signal |
| 2 | **Bio-Amplifier (High CMRR ≥ 100 dB)** | Amplifies µV/mV-level signals while rejecting 50/60 Hz interference |
| 3 | **Signal Conditioner (Filter + Isolation)** | Band-limits signal (0.05–150 Hz for ECG) and protects patient from electrical shock |
| 4 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | Converts analog bio-signal to digital bits (typically 8–16 bit, ≥ 250 Hz sampling for ECG) |
| 5 | **Encoder / Modulator (FSK, PSK, OOK)** | Maps digital bits onto a carrier wave for efficient transmission |
| 6 | **RF Transmitter + Antenna** | Radiates modulated signal through free space / tissue |
| 7 | **Receiver + Demodulator + Display** | Recovers original bio-signal and presents to clinician |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isolation** (galvanic/optical) is mandatory between the patient side and the mains-powered circuitry. The KTU board routinely asks for the **isolation amplifier** concept (e.g., AD210) in 7-mark sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through — How a Biomedical Telemetry System Works

The system operation follows a strict signal-flow logic. We dissect each stage below:

### Stage 1 — Sensing (Transduction)
- The sensor interfaces the *biological domain* with the *electrical domain*.
- **Example:** Ag/AgCl electrodes convert ionic currents in the body into electronic currents at the skin-electrode interface.
- **Why?** Biological signals are inherently ionic; electronics operate on electrons. A transducer is the universal translator.

### Stage 2 — Bio-Amplification
- Bio-signals are extremely weak. A high-gain, low-noise differential amplifier is used.
- The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is critical:
$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$
- For ECG, CMRR must be ≥ **100 dB** to reject 50 Hz mains hum picked up equally by both electrodes.
- **Why a differential topology?** It suppresses signals common to both electrodes (e.g., environmental noise) while amplifying the differential bio-potential.

### Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning (Filtering & Isolation)
- A **bandpass filter** restricts bandwidth to preserve signal of interest and reject noise.
- For ECG: Band-Pass = **0.05 Hz (high-pass) – 150 Hz (low-pass)**.
- An **isolation barrier** (transformer / optocoupler) breaks the galvanic path to protect the patient.
- **Why filter?** The signal occupies a known spectral band; everything outside is noise.

### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion
- Sampling theorem (Nyquist) dictates:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$
- For ECG ($f_{\max} \approx 150$ Hz), the minimum sampling rate is **300 Hz**; clinical systems use **250–500 Hz** at **12-bit** resolution.
- **Why digital?** Digital transmission is noise-immune and enables encryption.

### Stage 5 — Encoding / Modulation
- Digital bits are modulated onto a carrier. Common schemes: **OOK (On-Off Keying), FSK (Frequency Shift Keying, more robust), PSK, QPSK**.
- For medical telemetry, **FSK** is preferred for its low BER in noisy environments.
- **Why modulate?** Baseband bio-signals cannot propagate efficiently through antennas (length << λ).

### Stage 6 — RF Transmission
- The modulated carrier is radiated via an antenna whose physical length is approximately $\lambda/4$.
- For a 433 MHz carrier: $\lambda = c/f = (3\times 10^8)/(433\times 10^6) \approx 0.69$ m, so antenna length $\approx$ **17 cm** — practical for wearable devices.

### Stage 7 — Reception, Demodulation & Display
- The receiver's RF front-end amplifies the weak incoming signal, down-converts it, demodulates it, and reconstructs the bio-signal waveform for clinical display (LCD/PC monitor/Hospital Information System).

## 2.2 Challenges in Biomedical Telemetry (KTU High-Yield Topic)

A *challenge* is any engineering constraint that degrades the **fidelity**, **safety**, or **usability** of the telemetry chain. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these into **eight canonical challenges**, each described below.

### Challenge 1 — Low Amplitude of Bio-Signals
- ECG, EEG, EMG exist in the **µV–mV** range. Thermal and shot noise of the amplifier can easily drown the signal.
- **Engineering response:** Use of **low-noise operational amplifiers** (e.g., AD620, INA128) with input-referred noise ≤ **10 nV/√Hz**.

### Challenge 2 — Power-Line (50/60 Hz) Interference
- Mains hum is capacitively/inductively coupled into the body and cables.
- **Engineering response:** Differential amplification with high CMRR, driven-right-leg (DRL) circuit, notch filter at 50/60 Hz, and twisted-pair shielded cables.

### Challenge 3 — Patient Safety & Electrical Isolation
- Direct connection to mains can deliver lethal currents.
- **Engineering response:** Battery-powered transmitter side, **galvanic isolation amplifiers** (e.g., AD210, ISO124), and adherence to **IEC 60601-1** standard (leakage ≤ **10 µA** for CF-type).

### Challenge 4 — Motion Artifacts
- Electrode-skin impedance changes with movement, producing baseline wander.
- **Engineering response:** Adhesive Ag/AgCl electrodes with electrolyte gel, motion-correction algorithms (e.g., adaptive filtering, wavelet denoising), and accelerometer-based artifact rejection.

### Challenge 5 — Limited Bandwidth & Multipath Fading
- The human body is a lossy dielectric medium. Wireless signals suffer attenuation, reflection, and fading, especially for implantable devices.
- **Engineering response:** Use of the **Medical Implant Communication Service (MICS) band (402–405 MHz)** because tissue absorption is minimum here, and diversity antennas.

### Challenge 6 — Data Security & Patient Privacy
- Wireless transmission of medical data is vulnerable to eavesdropping and tampering.
- **Engineering response:** **AES-128/256 encryption**, authentication protocols (HIPAA / GDPR compliance), and spread-spectrum techniques.

### Challenge 7 — Power Consumption & Battery Life
- Wearable/implantable devices must operate for days to years on a single battery.
- **Engineering response:** Low-power ASIC design, duty cycling, energy harvesting (body heat, motion), and sleep-mode protocols (Bluetooth Low Energy).

### Challenge 8 — Biocompatibility & Miniaturization
- Implanted sensors must not trigger immune response; wearable sensors must be light and unobtrusive.
- **Engineering response:** Use of **titanium / medical-grade silicone encapsulation**, hermetic packaging, and System-on-Chip (SoC) integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Any 14-mark question on this module *will* test at least 4 of the above challenges. A tabular comparison followed by a circuit-level solution is the highest-scoring answer template.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units / Boundary |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz; for ECG: $f_s \geq 300$ Hz |
| 2 | Antenna length (quarter-wave) | $L = \dfrac{c}{4f_c}$ | meters; $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s |
| 3 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB; target $\geq 100$ dB |
| 4 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB; clinical ECG: $\geq 40$ dB |
| 5 | Thermal (Johnson) Noise Voltage | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V; $k_B = 1.38\times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 6 | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_{\text{skin}} \approx 10^3$ – $10^5\;\Omega$ at 1 Hz | Ohms (frequency-dependent) |
| 7 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | dB; $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 8 | Tissue Attenuation (approx. for muscle) | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ dB/cm @ 433 MHz | dB/cm |
| 9 | Maximum Patient Leakage Current | $I_{\text{leak}} \leq 10\;\mu\text{A}$ (CF type) | Amperes (per IEC 60601-1) |
| 10 | Link Budget Equation | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{tissue}}$ | dBm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, $\log$ and absolute-value-like expressions are deliberately written using `\log_{10}` and division forms so the **markdown pipe characters** never break. This is a strict KTU-Engine formatting rule.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Biomedical telemetry is the **engineering backbone** of:
- **ICU multi-parameter patient monitors** (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE).
- **Holter monitors** (24-h ambulatory ECG).
- **Cardiac event recorders** and **pacemakers** (bi-directional telemetry).
- **Wireless fetal monitoring** in pregnancy.
- **Ambulatory EEG / epilepsy monitoring caps**.
- **Sports physiology and wearable fitness bands** (consumer-grade derivatives).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Required Bio-Amplifier Gain

**Given:** An ECG signal has peak amplitude $V_{\text{in}} = 1$ mV. The ADC accepts a full-scale input of $V_{\text{FS}} = 5$ V.

**Find:** The minimum amplifier gain $A_v$ required to drive the ADC to near full scale.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the relationship between input, gain, and output.**

The amplifier output voltage is given by:
$$V_{\text{out}} = A_v \cdot V_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for the gain.**

$$A_v = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the boundary values.**

To use the ADC efficiently, we set $V_{\text{out}} \approx V_{\text{FS}} = 5$ V and $V_{\text{in}} = 1$ mV $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ V:
$$A_v = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**
$$A_v = 5000$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels for intuition.**

$$A_v \vert_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(5000) \approx 20 \times 3.699 \approx 73.98 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** The bio-amplifier must provide a voltage gain of approximately **5000 (≈ 74 dB)**. In practice, this is split into a pre-amplifier (×10) and a post-amplifier (×500) for noise optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** A single-stage gain of 5000 in one op-amp is unstable. Always cascade two stages with proper bandwidth design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Johnson–Nyquist Thermal Noise at the Amplifier Input

**Given:** The bio-amplifier input resistance is $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, bandwidth $\Delta f = 150$ Hz (ECG band), temperature $T = 310$ K (body temp).

**Find:** The RMS thermal noise voltage appearing at the input.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Johnson noise formula.**
$$V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the constants.**

- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
- $T = 310$ K
- $R = 10 \times 10^{3}\;\Omega$
- $\Delta f = 150$ Hz

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator $4 k_B T R \Delta f$.**

$$4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (10 \times 10^{3}) \times 150$$

Group the powers of 10 and the magnitudes separately:
$$\text{Numerator} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times 150 \times 10^{-23}$$

$$= 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 10 \times 150 \times 10^{3-23}$$

$$= 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 1500 \times 10^{-20}$$

Compute the multiplier:
$$4 \times 1.38 = 5.52$$
$$5.52 \times 310 = 1711.2$$
$$1711.2 \times 1500 = 2{,}566{,}800$$

So the numerator $\approx 2.5668 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-20} = 2.5668 \times 10^{-14}$.

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**
$$V_n = \sqrt{2.5668 \times 10^{-14}}$$

$$V_n = \sqrt{2.5668} \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.602 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}$$

$$V_n \approx 0.16 \;\mu\text{V (RMS)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

This thermal noise ($\approx 0.16\;\mu$V RMS) is **comparable in magnitude to the lowest EEG signals** ($\sim 5\;\mu$V). This is why low-noise design and bandwidth limiting are non-negotiable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Sampling Rate & Bit Depth for ECG Telemetry

**Given:** ECG with $f_{\max} = 150$ Hz; signal range $V_{\text{pp}} = 5$ mV; required resolution $\Delta V = 10\;\mu$V.

**Find:** Minimum sampling rate $f_s$ and number of ADC bits $n$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Nyquist theorem.**
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

In practice, oversample at $f_s = 500$ Hz to ease anti-aliasing filter design.

**Step 2 — Relate resolution and bit depth.**

The quantisation step of an $n$-bit ADC is:
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $n$.**
$$2^{n} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{\Delta V}$$

We must have $V_{\text{FS}} \geq V_{\text{pp}} = 5$ mV; take $V_{\text{FS}} = 5$ mV exactly:
$$2^{n} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-5}} = 500$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ using logarithm.**
$$n = \log_2(500)$$

Using $\log_2(500) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(500)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{2.699}{0.3010} \approx 8.97$:

$$n \geq 9 \text{ bits}$$

In practice, $n = 12$ bits is standard in clinical ECG digitisation to allow headroom for amplification.

**Step 5 — Summary of design parameters.**

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Practical Value Used |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $f_s$ | $\geq 300$ Hz | $500$ Hz |
| $n$ | $\geq 9$ bits | $12$ bits |
| Data rate | $500 \times 12 = 6000$ bps/channel | $\sim 6$ kbps per ECG lead |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quarter-Wave Antenna Length for a Biotelemetry Transmitter

**Given:** Carrier frequency $f_c = 433$ MHz (ISM band, widely used in medical telemetry).

**Find:** Quarter-wave antenna length $L$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength.**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{433 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4.33 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{3}{4.33} \approx 0.6928 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Quarter-wave length.**
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{0.6928}{4} \approx 0.1732 \text{ m} \approx 17.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Result:** The antenna must be **≈ 17 cm** long — feasible for a wearable patch but bulky for an implant, justifying the use of higher frequencies (e.g., 2.4 GHz → $\lambda/4 \approx$ 3.1 cm) or miniature helical antennas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Biomedical Telemetry System

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the complete signal path from the **biological source** to the **clinician's display**, with explicit annotation of *where each engineering challenge arises* (shown in dashed feedback paths labelled "C1–C8").

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[BIO_SOURCE: Patient body] --> A2[BLOCK_1: Sensor_Electrode]
    A2 --> A3[BLOCK_2: Bio_Amplifier_CMRR100dB]
    A3 --> A4[BLOCK_3: Bandpass_Filter_0_05_to_150Hz]
    A4 --> A5[BLOCK_4: Isolation_Amplifier_IEC60601]
    A5 --> A6[BLOCK_5: ADC_12bit_500sps]
    A6 --> A7[BLOCK_6: FSK_Modulator_Encoder]
    A7 --> A8[BLOCK_7: RF_Front_End_433MHz]
    A8 --> A9[CHANNEL: Wireless_Tissue_Loss]
    A9 --> A10[BLOCK_8: Receiver_Demodulator]
    A10 --> A11[BLOCK_9: DSP_Decoding_Denoise]
    A11 --> A12[BLOCK_10: Display_PC_HIS]
    A12 --> A13[CLINICIAN: Doctor_Analyst]

    A2 -. "C1: High skin impedance" .-> A3
    A3 -. "C2: 50Hz interference" .-> A4
    A4 -. "C3: Patient safety" .-> A5
    A5 -. "C4: Motion artifact" .-> A6
    A6 -. "C5: Bandwidth limit" .-> A7
    A7 -. "C6: Data security" .-> A8
    A8 -. "C7: Battery drain" .-> A9
    A9 -. "C8: Multipath fading" .-> A10
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** (→) depict the forward signal path.
- The **dashed arrows** (-. "-. C#: Challenge" .->) identify the *engineering challenge* introduced at each interface.
- The numbering **C1–C8** corresponds exactly to the eight canonical challenges enumerated in Section 2.2.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two Sub-System Architectures: Wearable vs. Implantable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph W1[WEARABLE_TELEMETRY: e.g. Holter Monitor]
        W1A[Chest_Electrodes] --> W1B[Portable_Box_on_Belt]
        W1B --> W1C[Antenna_433MHz_Whip]
        W1C --> W1D[Smartphone_or_Hospital_Receiver]
    end

    subgraph I1[IMPLANTABLE_TELEMETRY: e.g. Pacemaker]
        I1A[Subcutaneous_Lead] --> I1B[Titanium_Hermetic_Can]
        I1B --> I1C[MICS_402_to_405MHz_Antenna]
        I1C --> I1D[External_Programmer_Reader]
    end

    W1D --> NET1[Hospital_WiFi_or_Cloud]
    I1D --> NET1
    NET1 --> CLI[Central_Clinician_Console]
```

**Interpretation:** Both architectures share the *patient → encoder → wireless link → receiver → clinician* paradigm, but they differ in antenna size, power budget, biocompatibility constraints, and regulatory band allocation.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Patient Monitoring (Telemetry + Actuation)

> **Note:** In advanced telemedicine, the telemetry system is not merely one-way (monitoring); it can be **bi-directional**, allowing the clinician to send therapeutic commands (e.g., reprogramming an implanted defibrillator).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient_Body] --> S1[Sensor]
    S1 --> T1[Telemetry_Transmitter]
    T1 --> RF1[Wireless_Link]
    RF1 --> R1[Receiver_Station]
    R1 --> D1[Display_and_AI_Decision]
    D1 --> CMD[Therapeutic_Command_Channel]
    CMD --> RF1
    RF1 --> AC1[Actuator_or_Drug_Pump]
    AC1 --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define a biomedical telemetry system. List any four of its essential functional blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *biomedical telemetry system* is an electronic instrumentation system that **measures, encodes, and remotely transmits physiological signals** from a patient to a distant monitoring/recording station without physical tethering, enabling ambulatory and remote care.

The four essential functional blocks are:
1. **Sensor / Electrode** — to transduce the physiological variable.
2. **Bio-Amplifier** — to amplify the weak (µV–mV) bio-signal.
3. **Signal Conditioner (Filter + Isolation)** — to band-limit and isolate the patient.
4. **RF Transmitter** — to radiate the modulated signal over a wireless channel.

*(The student may list **Modulator/Encoder** and **Receiver/Display** in place of items 3 or 4 — full marks will be awarded if four are correctly stated.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Mention any three major challenges encountered in the design of a wireless biomedical telemetry system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Low signal amplitude** — Bio-signals such as EEG (5–300 µV) and ECG (0.5–5 mV) are extremely weak and easily corrupted by amplifier noise.
2. **50/60 Hz power-line interference** — Mains hum is capacitively coupled into the body and cables, requiring high-CMRR differential amplifiers.
3. **Patient safety and electrical isolation** — Direct connection to mains-powered circuits can cause microshock; isolation amplifiers and battery-powered patient sides are mandatory (IEC 60601-1).

*(Acceptable alternatives: motion artifacts, multipath fading, data security, biocompatibility, limited battery life.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern: 1 × 14 Marks)

> **Pattern Note (KTU 2024):** Every Module carries a 14-mark ESE question with **internal choice** — i.e., the student must answer *either* **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **typical single-channel ECG telemetry system**. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **major challenges in biomedical telemetry**, with specific reference to *patient safety, signal-to-noise ratio, and data security*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

A typical single-channel ECG telemetry system consists of **seven functional blocks** (refer Section 1.4 above):

1. **Bio-electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Two electrodes placed on the patient's chest pick up the differential cardiac potential (typically 1 mV) plus common-mode mains interference.
2. **Differential Bio-Amplifier:** Provides high gain (≈ 1000) and high CMRR (≥ 100 dB); a third electrode (right-leg driver) actively cancels common-mode noise.
3. **Bandpass Filter (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz):** Removes DC baseline drift (high-pass) and high-frequency muscle noise (low-pass).
4. **Isolation Amplifier (e.g., AD210):** Provides galvanic isolation (typically 5 kV RMS) between the patient side and the line-powered circuitry, ensuring patient safety.
5. **ADC (12-bit, ≥ 500 sps):** Converts the analog ECG to digital samples satisfying the Nyquist criterion.
6. **FSK Modulator + RF Transmitter (433 MHz):** Encodes the digital bits as frequency-shift keying and transmits via a quarter-wave antenna (≈ 17 cm).
7. **Receiver + Demodulator + Display:** Recovers the ECG waveform for clinician review on a monitor or chart recorder.

Each block is **necessary and sequential** — removing any one breaks the chain.

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Challenge 1 — Patient Safety:**
- Bio-telemetry systems use skin-contact electrodes, making the patient electrically vulnerable. Currents as low as **10 µA** passing through the heart can trigger ventricular fibrillation.
- **Solution:** Use **isolation amplifiers** that break the galvanic path; use **battery-powered** patient-side transmitters; comply with **IEC 60601-1** standard leakage limits (CF-type applied parts: ≤ 10 µA). Additionally, current-limiting resistors and defibrillation-protection circuits are placed at the electrode interface.

**Challenge 2 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):**
- ECG signals are 0.5–5 mV; noise sources (thermal, 50 Hz, motion) can exceed this.
- **Solution:** Use low-noise instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, noise ≤ 9 nV/√Hz), apply high-CMRR differential topology, use driven-right-leg circuits, and digital filtering (notch at 50 Hz, adaptive filters for motion).

**Challenge 3 — Data Security:**
- Wireless transmission over the public RF spectrum is susceptible to eavesdropping and tampering — a serious HIPAA/GDPR concern.
- **Solution:** Use **AES-128/256 encryption** at the link layer, mutual authentication between transmitter and receiver, spread-spectrum modulation (e.g., LoRa in medical IoT), and secure cloud storage with end-to-end encryption.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a multi-parameter patient telemetry system** used in an ICU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a focus on **wireless channel impairments**, discuss the engineering challenges in **implantable biotelemetry**. Compare the suitability of the MICS band (402–405 MHz) and the 2.4 GHz ISM band for this application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(a)

A **multi-parameter ICU telemetry system** simultaneously acquires and transmits several biosignals: **ECG (lead II), SpO₂ (photoplethysmogram), respiration (impedance pneumograph), invasive/non-invasive blood pressure, body temperature, and capnography (EtCO₂)**.

**Architecture:**

1. **Multiple Sensors** — Each parameter has a dedicated transducer (e.g., PPG probe for SpO₂, thermistor for temperature).
2. **Multiplexer + Individual Signal Conditioners** — Each channel has its own filter and isolation stage.
3. **Time-Division Multiplexer (TDM)** — Channels are sequentially sampled and combined.
4. **Single ADC + Frame Encoder** — A microcontroller forms a data frame (header + channel ID + samples) and encrypts it.
5. **RF Transmitter** — One transmitter (e.g., 433 MHz or 2.4 GHz) handles the combined stream.
6. **Central Nursing Station Receiver** — Receives frames, de-multiplexes, displays waveforms in real time, and triggers **alarms** when parameters cross thresholds.

**Alarms:** Visual (LED) + Audible (buzzer) + remote (pager/SMS to the doctor).

#### Model Solution — Q(b)

**Implantable Telemetry Challenges — Channel Impairments:**

- **Tissue absorption:** Body tissues (skin, fat, muscle) attenuate RF signals. Attenuation increases with frequency — roughly 0.5 dB/cm at 433 MHz and 1.5 dB/cm at 2.4 GHz.
- **Multipath fading:** Internal reflections off organ boundaries.
- **Antenna miniaturization:** Implanted antennas cannot be large.

**MICS Band (402–405 MHz) vs. 2.4 GHz ISM:**

| Parameter | MICS (402–405 MHz) | ISM 2.4 GHz |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Tissue attenuation | Low (~0.5 dB/cm) | Higher (~1.5 dB/cm) |
| Antenna size | Larger ($\lambda/4$ ≈ 18 cm) | Smaller ($\lambda/4$ ≈ 3.1 cm) |
| Bandwidth | Narrow (3 MHz) | Wide (80 MHz) |
| Regulatory status | Reserved for medical implants | Crowded (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth) |
| Battery life | Better (lower freq → lower current) | Worse |
| Best suited for | Deep implants (pacemakers, ICDs) | Superficial sensors / wearable |

**Conclusion:** For *deep implants*, the **MICS band is the gold standard** because tissue loss is minimized and the spectrum is regulated exclusively for medical use. The **2.4 GHz band** is better for *wearable and surface-mount* devices where antenna size matters more than tissue loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "telemetry is only wireless." It can be *wired* (e.g., bedside monitor with cable) or *wireless*; the key feature is *remote patient-side acquisition*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this oversight.
> 2. **Do not** confuse *isolation* (galvanic, for patient safety) with *insulation* (general). The KTU board specifically tests for the *isolation amplifier* concept.
> 3. **Do not** omit the **CMRR** numerical value (≥ 100 dB). A qualitative mention of "high CMRR" without the figure loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Do not** state the Nyquist rate as $f_s = 2f$ only. State the *inequality* $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$ and justify it with a practical oversampling factor.
> 5. **Do not** list challenges without their *engineering solutions*; the board pattern always demands a *problem–solution* pair for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ A **biomedical telemetry system** acquires and remotely transmits physiological signals — its purpose is to *bridge the spatial gap* between patient and clinician.
- ⭐ The **seven canonical blocks**: Sensor → Bio-Amplifier → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Encoder/Modulator → RF Transmitter → Receiver/Display.
- ⭐ Standard bio-signal ranges: **ECG = 0.5–5 mV**, **EEG = 5–300 µV**, **EMG = 50 µV–5 mV**.
- ⭐ **CMRR must be ≥ 100 dB**; CMRR$_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$.
- ⭐ **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$; for ECG, $f_s \geq 300$ Hz (use 500 Hz).
- ⭐ **ADC bit depth**: 12 bits is standard for clinical ECG.
- ⭐ **Antenna length** $L = c/(4f_c)$; for 433 MHz, $L \approx 17$ cm.
- ⭐ **MICS band (402–405 MHz)** is reserved for **implantable** telemetry due to low tissue loss.
- ⭐ **IEC 60601-1** standard mandates leakage current ≤ **10 µA** for CF-type applied parts.
- ⭐ **Eight canonical challenges**: low amplitude, mains interference, patient safety, motion artifacts, bandwidth/fading, data security, power consumption, biocompatibility.
- ⭐ **Isolation amplifier** is mandatory between patient and mains.
- ⭐ **Driven-right-leg (DRL) circuit** improves CMRR in practice.
- ⭐ **FSK** is the preferred modulation in noisy medical telemetry.
- ⭐ **Encryption (AES-128/256)** is essential for HIPAA/GDPR compliance.
- ⭐ Thermal (Johnson) noise at amplifier input: $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$; for ECG front-end $V_n \approx 0.16\;\mu$V RMS.
- ⭐ Free-space path loss (Friis): $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d / \lambda)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations of Biomedical Telemetry Device: Energy Transfer Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations of Biomedical Telemetry Device: Energy Transfer Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biomedical Telemetry — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Telemetry** is the branch of biomedical engineering concerned with the **remote measurement, transmission, and reception of physiological data** from a subject (human or animal) to a distant monitoring station, without physical constraint on the subject's mobility.

A **Biomedical Telemetry Device** is a system that acquires one or more bioelectric/biochemical signals (ECG, EMG, EEG, SpO₂, temperature, blood pressure, etc.), conditions them, encodes them, and transmits the information over a chosen physical medium to a receiver where the data is reconstructed and displayed.

The **Design Considerations** are the engineering, clinical, and regulatory constraints that shape the architecture of such a device, and they can be grouped into five major pillars:

| Pillar | Core Concern |
|---|---|
| **Patient Safety** | Isolation from mains, leakage current limits, biocompatibility |
| **Signal Integrity** | SNR, bandwidth, dynamic range, motion artefacts |
| **Power Budget** | Battery life, energy harvesting, low-power design |
| **Form Factor** | Size, weight, wearability, ergonomics |
| **Reliability & Security** | Data encryption, fail-safe operation, regulatory compliance (IEC 60601) |

## 1.2 Energy Transfer — The Unifying Design Question

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Transfer** in biomedical telemetry refers to the **physical mechanism by which electrical power (and/or information-carrying signal energy) is moved between the on-body/in-body subsystem and the external monitoring subsystem.**

There are **two distinct kinds of energy transfer** to be considered in any telemetry design:

1. **Signal Energy Transfer** — the carrier conveying the physiological data.
2. **Power Energy Transfer** — the energy needed to energise the implanted/wearable sensor node (especially critical for implants, where battery replacement is invasive).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The News Helicopter**
> Imagine a news helicopter (the *transmitter*) circling over a city. It needs two things to keep doing its job: (1) a way to **send the video signal** down to the studio (signal energy transfer) and (2) **fuel** to keep flying (power energy transfer). Sometimes the helicopter is "refuelled" mid-air from a tanker plane — this is exactly like **wireless power transfer** in an implanted pacemaker recharging through the skin. The "design considerations" are the engineer's checklist: how high to fly (frequency), how heavy the camera can be (power budget), how clear the picture must be (signal integrity), and the rules of the air traffic authority (regulatory compliance — FCC, ICMR, IEC).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics recur throughout the design equations:

- **Speed of electromagnetic waves in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ \text{F/m}$
- **Characteristic impedance of free space:** $Z_{0} \approx 377 \ \Omega$
- **Standardised medical telemetry bands:** **WMTS (Wireless Medical Telemetry Service) 608–614 MHz, 1395–1400 MHz, 1427–1432 MHz (USA); ISM bands 433 MHz, 2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz (global).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Transfer Types

The energy transfer types used in biomedical telemetry are classified as follows:

### A. Based on Transmission Medium

#### A1. Hard-Wire (Conductive) Transfer
- Direct galvanic connection via insulated cables.
- **Used in:** bedside monitors, ICU, stress-test ECG, Holter download.
- **Pros:** highest SNR, no FCC licensing, secure, can carry power.
- **Cons:** restricts patient mobility, connector fatigue, infection risk.

#### A2. Wireless (Radiative) Transfer
Information/signal carried by electromagnetic (EM) waves propagating through air/tissue.

- **Radio Frequency (RF):** 30 kHz – 300 MHz (e.g., **433 MHz** ISM).
- **Microwave:** 300 MHz – 300 GHz (e.g., **2.4 GHz** Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, **5.8 GHz**).
- **Infrared (IR):** 300 GHz – 400 THz; line-of-sight only; cannot penetrate tissue (used for in-room patient monitoring).

#### A3. Hybrid (Inductive / Capacitive) Coupling — Near-Field
- Operating in the **reactive near-field region** where $r \ll \lambda/2\pi$.
- Predominantly **magnetic (inductive)** or **electric (capacitive)** field coupling.

### B. Based on Direction of Energy Flow

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **One-way (Simplex)** | Sensor → Receiver only (e.g., Holter ECG). |
| **Two-way (Duplex)** | Sensor ↔ Receiver, with reverse link for command/power (e.g., smart implants). |
| **Power-and-data simultaneous** | Energy and information share the same channel (e.g., RFID, inductively-powered pacemakers). |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — Wireless Telemetry Chain

1. **Signal acquisition:** electrodes/transducers pick up bio-signals.
2. **Signal conditioning:** amplification, filtering (bandwidth limited to ~0.5 Hz – 40 Hz for ECG).
3. **A/D conversion:** sampling at $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist).
4. **Encoding & modulation:** AM / FM / OOK / FSK / QPSK / OFDM.
5. **Up-conversion & amplification:** carrier $f_{c}$ is set; PA drives the antenna.
6. **Channel propagation:** free-space, body-area network, or through-tissue loss.
7. **Reception & demodulation:** LNA, mixer, baseband processing.
8. **Display / storage / network upload.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below must be memorised for the KTU University Exam. No vertical pipe `|` is used inside table cells — `$\vert$` is substituted for absolute-value separators.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m ; $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 2 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)$ | dB ; $d$ = link distance |
| 3 | Received power (Friis) | $P_{r} = P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}\, \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2}$ | W ; $G$ = antenna gain (linear) |
| 4 | Link budget in dB | $P_{r}\text{[dBm]} = P_{t}\text{[dBm]} + G_{t} + G_{r} - FSPL - L_{misc}$ | dBm |
| 5 | Skin-depth in tissue | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | m ; $\sigma$ = tissue conductivity |
| 6 | Specific Absorption Rate | $SAR = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^{2}}{\rho}$ | W/kg ; **FCC/ICNIRP limit = 1.6 W/kg (averaged over 1 g)** |
| 7 | Inductive coupling coefficient | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_{1}\, L_{2}}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Mutual inductance (coaxial coils) | $M \approx \mu_{0}\, N_{1}\, N_{2}\, \dfrac{\pi r^{2}}{d}$ | H (for $d \gg r$) |
| 9 | Power transfer efficiency (inductive) | $\eta = \dfrac{k^{2}\, Q_{1}\, Q_{2}}{1 + k^{2}\, Q_{1}\, Q_{2}}$ | dimensionless ; $Q$ = coil quality factor |
| 10 | Resonant inductive link efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{(k\sqrt{Q_{1}Q_{2}})^{2}}{(1 + k\sqrt{Q_{1}Q_{2}})^{2}}$ | dimensionless (max at resonance) |
| 11 | Capacitive coupling reactance | $X_{C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | Near-field / far-field boundary | $r_{FF} = \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda}$ | m ; $D$ = antenna largest dimension |
| 13 | Thermal noise power | $N = k_{B}\, T\, B$ | W ; $k_{B} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 14 | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left( 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} \right)$ | bits/s |
| 15 | Battery life estimate | $t_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{load}\, \eta_{sys}}$ | hours |

## 2.4 Why & How — Engineering Rationale

- **Why a high carrier frequency?** Because antenna size shrinks as $\lambda$ decreases — a 2.4 GHz patch antenna is ~3 cm, wearable.
- **Why inductively coupled implants?** EM waves are heavily attenuated in tissue (~**1–5 dB/cm at 1 MHz** dropping to >10 dB/cm above 1 GHz). Magnetic fields, in contrast, pass through tissue with low loss, making them ideal for **transcutaneous energy transfer (TET)** in cochlear implants, LVADs, and retinal prostheses.
- **Why IR for in-room monitoring?** IR cannot pass through walls → inherently secure and licence-free.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Implanted pacemakers & ICDs** — inductively coupled TET for recharging and programming.
- **Wireless capsule endoscopy (PillCam)** — transmits ~2 fps video at 433 MHz from inside the GI tract.
- **Remote fetal monitoring (tele-CTG)** — enables rural obstetrics.
- **ICU patient monitoring** — eliminates cable clutter, reduces nurse workload.
- **Wearable fitness / mHealth** — Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) at 2.4 GHz.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Friis Free-Space Transmission Equation

> This is the most important derivation for any wireless-telemetry numerical in the KTU exam. It gives the received power as a function of transmitted power, antenna gains, wavelength, and distance.

### Given
- Isotropic transmit power $P_{t}$ (W) feeding an antenna of gain $G_{t}$.
- Distance $d$ (m) to a receive antenna of gain $G_{r}$.
- Wavelength $\lambda$ (m).
- Free-space propagation.

### Step 1 — Power Flux Density at the Receiver

The transmit antenna concentrates $P_{t}$ over an effective aperture of area $4\pi d^{2}$ (for an isotropic radiator).

$$
S = \dfrac{P_{t}\, G_{t}}{4\pi\, d^{2}} \quad [\text{W/m}^{2}]
$$

*Text:* Power density equals transmitted power multiplied by antenna gain divided by the spherical surface area at distance $d$.

### Step 2 — Effective Aperture of the Receive Antenna

The receive antenna collects power through its effective aperture $A_{e}$.

$$
A_{e} = \dfrac{G_{r}\, \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

*Text:* Effective aperture is proportional to the square of the wavelength and the receive antenna's gain.

### Step 3 — Received Power

$$
P_{r} = S \cdot A_{e} = \dfrac{P_{t}\, G_{t}}{4\pi\, d^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{G_{r}\, \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

Multiplying numerators and denominators:

$$
P_{r} = P_{t}\, G_{t}\, G_{r}\, \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi\, d} \right)^{2} \quad [\text{W}]
$$

*Text:* This is the **Friis Transmission Equation** — the cornerstone of link-budget analysis in biomedical telemetry.

### Step 4 — Expressing in Decibels (Practical Form)

Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
P_{r}\text{[dBm]} = P_{t}\text{[dBm]} + G_{t}\text{[dBi]} + G_{r}\text{[dBi]} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi\, d}{\lambda} \right)
$$

*Text:* The bracketed term is the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** in dB.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Resonant Inductive Power-Transfer Efficiency

> Essential for numerical on implanted devices (cochlear implants, artificial hearts, neurostimulators).

### Given
- Primary coil self-inductance $L_{1}$, secondary $L_{2}$.
- Coupling coefficient $k$.
- Quality factors $Q_{1} = \omega L_{1}/R_{1}$ and $Q_{2} = \omega L_{2}/R_{2}$.

### Step 1 — Reflected Impedance

The secondary circuit, when referred to the primary, presents a reflected impedance:

$$
Z_{ref} = \dfrac{(\omega M)^{2}}{Z_{2}}
$$

where $M = k \sqrt{L_{1} L_{2}}$ and $Z_{2} = R_{2} + j\omega L_{2} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{2}}$.

### Step 2 — Resonance Condition

At resonance $\omega L_{2} = 1/(\omega C_{2})$, the secondary impedance reduces to $Z_{2} = R_{2}$ (purely resistive). The reflected impedance becomes:

$$
Z_{ref} = \dfrac{(\omega M)^{2}}{R_{2}} = \dfrac{\omega^{2}\, k^{2}\, L_{1}\, L_{2}}{R_{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Power Delivered vs. Power Consumed

Power delivered to the secondary is $P_{out} = \vert I_{2} \vert^{2} R_{L}$; power lost in $R_{2}$ is $P_{loss,2} = \vert I_{2} \vert^{2} R_{2}$. Combining all losses and using $Q = \omega L / R$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{k^{2}\, Q_{1}\, Q_{2}}{(1 + k^{2}\, Q_{1}\, Q_{2})}
$$

*Text:* Efficiency approaches **100 % as $k\sqrt{Q_{1}Q_{2}} \to \infty$** — hence the design push toward high-Q coils (Litz wire) and tight coupling.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU-Style Problem

> **Question:** A wearable ECG patch transmits $P_{t} = 10$ mW at $f = 433$ MHz with $G_{t} = G_{r} = 2$ dBi to a bedside receiver at $d = 3$ m. Compute the received power and the path loss. Comment on link margin for a receiver sensitivity of $-90$ dBm.

### Step 1 — Wavelength

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{433 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.693 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 2 — FSPL

$$
FSPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi \cdot 3}{0.693} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(54.41) \approx 34.7 \ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 3 — Received Power

Convert $P_{t}$ to dBm: $10 \text{ mW} = 10 \text{ dBm}$.

$$
P_{r} = 10 + 2 + 2 - 34.7 = -20.7 \ \text{dBm} \ (\approx 8.5 \ \mu\text{W})
$$

### Step 4 — Link Margin

$$
\text{Margin} = P_{r} - S_{min} = -20.7 - (-90) = +69.3 \ \text{dB}
$$

*Text:* An enormous **69.3 dB link margin** — the design is comfortably viable; the engineer can reduce $P_{t}$ by ~60 dB (to ~10 µW) to save battery, or extend range to ~300 m.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy-Transfer Choice vs. Clinical Use

| Clinical Application | Preferred Energy-Transfer Type | Frequency | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bedside ICU monitor | **Hard-wire** | DC – 100 kHz | Highest SNR, isolation transformer for safety |
| Wireless Holter (24 h ECG) | **RF wireless** | 433 MHz – 2.4 GHz | Mobility, sufficient data rate (~10 kb/s) |
| Implanted pacemaker telemetry | **Inductive (TET)** | 100 kHz – 10 MHz | Low tissue loss, powers the implant, magnetically-coupled coils |
| Capsule endoscopy | **Microwave RF** | 433 MHz / 2.4 GHz | Penetrates abdomen wall, supports ~2 fps video |
| Smart insulin pump closed loop | **BLE** | 2.4 GHz | Low power, smartphone integration |
| Operating-room multi-parameter | **Infrared** | ~30–60 THz | Non-interference with RF surgical devices, line-of-sight secure |
| Wearable sweat sensor | **Capacitive / Body-channel** | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | Human body itself becomes the channel, ultra-low power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Biomedical Telemetry System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Bio Sensor 1 ECG] --> B1[Signal Conditioner]
    A2[Bio Sensor 2 EMG] --> B1
    A3[Bio Sensor 3 SpO2] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[ADC Sampler]
    C1 --> D1[Encoder Modulator]
    D1 --> E1[Power Amplifier]
    E1 --> F1[Antenna TX]
    F1 -->|RF Link 433 MHz| G1[Antenna RX]
    G1 --> H1[Demodulator Decoder]
    H1 --> I1[Display Storage Network]
    J1[Battery or Energy Harvester] --> E1
    J1 --> C1
    J1 --> D1
    K1[Inductive TET Coupler] --> J1
```

*Description:* The transmit chain (left half) acquires, conditions, digitises, encodes, modulates, amplifies and radiates. The receive chain (right half) reverses the process. The power subsystem (bottom) is shared by all transmit-side blocks and may itself be energised transcutaneously by inductive TET.

## 4.2 Energy-Transfer Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Design Selection] --> Q1{Is patient stationary?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| HW[Hard Wire Conductive Link]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is sensor implanted or on body?}
    Q2 -->|Implanted| Q3{Power source type?}
    Q3 -->|Rechargeable| IND[Inductive TET 100 kHz 10 MHz]
    Q3 -->|Primary battery| RF1[Low Frequency RF 30 to 50 MHz]
    Q2 -->|On body| Q4{Range required?}
    Q4 -->|Less than 5 m| BLE[BLE or ZigBee 2.4 GHz]
    Q4 -->|5 to 50 m| WIFI[Wi Fi 2.4 or 5 GHz]
    Q4 -->|More than 50 m| CELL[Cellular 4G 5G]
    Q1 -->|Critical care secure| IR[Infrared Optical]
```

*Description:* A practical selection flowchart used by biomedical engineers when matching the clinical scenario to the appropriate energy-transfer mechanism.

## 4.3 Impedance / Coupling Schematic — Inductive TET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[AC Source V1 100 kHz] --> C1P[Tuning Cap C1]
    C1P --> L1P[Primary Coil L1]
    L1P -->|Coupling k| L2S[Secondary Coil L2]
    L2S --> C2S[Tuning Cap C2]
    C2S --> REC[Rectifier and Regulator]
    REC --> BAT[Implant Battery or Load]
    R1P[Coil R1] --- L1P
    R2S[Coil R2] --- L2S
```

*Description:* Two magnetically-coupled resonant tanks ($L_{1}C_{1}$ and $L_{2}C_{2}$) tuned to the same frequency. The secondary's AC output is rectified and regulated to DC for the implant's load.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**Q1. List the major design considerations of a biomedical telemetry device and explain why *patient safety* is given the highest priority.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A biomedical telemetry device is designed with the following major considerations: (1) **patient safety** — limiting leakage current to <10 µA (Type CF, IEC 60601) and ensuring galvanic isolation; (2) **signal fidelity** — adequate SNR, bandwidth, and motion artefact rejection; (3) **power budget** — long battery life, low-power ICs, energy harvesting; (4) **form factor and biocompatibility** — miniaturised, hypoallergenic, ergonomic; (5) **reliability and data security** — encryption, fail-safe alarms, regulatory compliance.

Patient safety is the highest priority because the device is in continuous contact with — or implanted in — a vulnerable human subject. Even a small leakage current can cause **microshock-induced ventricular fibrillation** when applied directly to the myocardium (e.g., via a catheter).
