**Reading the diagram**: The patient first encounters the **consent** layer (Layer 6), then proceeds through identity verification (Layer 1), secure transmission (Layer 2), secure storage (Layer 3) interacting with the EHR, clinical decision support (Layer 4) reaching the doctor, prescription safety (Layer 5), and finally legal/jurisdictional validation (Layer 7) before the consultation outcome is conveyed back to the patient.

## 4.2 Risk Assessment & Mitigation Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["START: IDENTIFY TELEMEDICINE HAZARD"]
    p1["ASSIGN PROBABILITY P (1-5)"]
    s1["ASSIGN SEVERITY S (1-5)"]
    d1["ASSIGN DETECTABILITY D (1-5)"]
    calc["COMPUTE QRS = P TIMES S TIMES D"]
    classify{"CLASSIFY QRS"}
    lowOut["LOW RISK (QRS 1-20): DOCUMENT"]
    medOut["MEDIUM RISK (QRS 21-60): MITIGATE IN 6 MONTHS"]
    highOut["HIGH RISK (QRS 61-100): IMMEDIATE ACTION"]
    critOut["CRITICAL RISK (QRS GREATER 100): HALT SERVICE"]
    monitor["MONITOR & REASSESS QUARTERLY"]
    
    startNode --> p1 --> s1 --> d1 --> calc --> classify
    classify -->|1-20| lowOut --> monitor
    classify -->|21-60| medOut --> monitor
    classify -->|61-100| highOut --> monitor
    classify -->|"greater 100"| critOut --> monitor
```

## 4.3 Incident Response Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram models the **post-incident** safety workflow, based on the **NIST 800-61** Computer Security Incident Handling Guide, adapted for telemedicine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    detect["1. DETECT SAFETY INCIDENT (e.g. data breach)"]
    contain["2. CONTAIN: ISOLATE AFFECTED SYSTEMS"]
    eradicate["3. ERADICATE: REMOVE THREAT / ROOT CAUSE"]
    recover["4. RECOVER: RESTORE SERVICES"]
    notify["5. NOTIFY: INFORM PATIENTS, REGULATORS, ETHICS BOARD"]
    review["6. POST-INCIDENT REVIEW & LESSONS LEARNED"]
    update["7. UPDATE SAFETY POLICIES"]
    
    detect --> contain --> eradicate --> recover --> notify --> review --> update
    update -.->|FEEDBACK LOOP| detect
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) of Safety-Critical Information in a Teleconsultation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patientDFD["PATIENT (DATA ORIGIN)"]
    deviceDFD["WEARABLE SENSOR (e.g. ECG)"]
    gatewayDFD["GATEWAY / MOBILE APP"]
    cloudDFD["SECURE CLOUD SERVER (TLS 1.3, AES-256)"]
    doctorDFD["REMOTE PHYSICIAN (DECISION)"]
    auditDFD["AUDIT LOG & COMPLIANCE VAULT"]
    
    patientDFD --> deviceDFD
    deviceDFD --> gatewayDFD
    gatewayDFD -->|ENCRYPTED CHANNEL| cloudDFD
    cloudDFD --> doctorDFD
    doctorDFD -->|E-PRESCRIPTION| gatewayDFD
    gatewayDFD -->|NOTIFICATION| patientDFD
    
    cloudDFD -.->|IMMUTABLE LOG| auditDFD
    doctorDFD -.->|METADATA ONLY| auditDFD
    gatewayDFD -.->|ACCESS LOGS| auditDFD
```

**Interpretation**: All clinical data is encrypted in transit and at rest. The audit log is a **read-only, tamper-evident** record used for medico-legal safety and post-incident investigation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]

**Define telemedicine safety. List any four major safety issues in telemedicine.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Telemedicine safety** is the set of clinical, technical, legal, and ethical measures that prevent harm to patients, providers, and data when healthcare is delivered remotely using ICT.

**Four major safety issues:**
1. **Clinical safety** — risk of misdiagnosis due to lack of physical examination.
2. **Data security** — risk of unauthorized access to electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI).
3. **Informed consent** — patient must understand the limitations of tele-mode.
4. **Technical reliability** — network/device failure can interrupt critical care.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four issues, 0.5 each.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]

**What is the role of ISO/TS 13131:2021 in telemedicine safety?**

**Model Answer:**

The **ISO/TS 13131:2021** standard titled *"Health informatics — Telehealth services — Quality planning guidelines"* provides a globally recognized framework for planning, implementing, and auditing the quality and safety of telehealth services. It defines seven key quality criteria: **patient safety, clinical effectiveness, care coordination, patient experience, workforce, technology, and data management**. In essence, it serves as the **safety blueprint** for any telehealth system, helping organizations identify and mitigate clinical, technical, and organizational risks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks on 14-mark Part B questions on safety issues by:
> 1. **Conflating privacy with security** — privacy is about *who* sees the data; security is about *how* it is protected. Examiners expect you to distinguish these.
> 2. **Giving generic answers** — e.g., "there is risk of hacking." Instead, mention the specific attack (e.g., **man-in-the-middle attack on unencrypted video streams**) and a specific countermeasure (e.g., **DTLS-SRTP encryption**).
> 3. **Forgetting the Indian context** — always reference the **Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 (India)** and **NMC** wherever applicable.
> 4. **Skipping real-world examples** — quoting the **WannaCry NHS incident (2018)** or the **SingHealth breach (2017)** instantly elevates your answer.
> 5. **Not concluding** — always end with a mitigation matrix or recommendation table; it leaves a strong final impression on the examiner.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the major clinical and technical safety issues in telemedicine with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction** (1 mark):
Telemedicine, despite its enormous reach, introduces a unique set of safety challenges because the traditional safeguards of a physical consultation — direct observation, palpation, and the immediate physical presence of a clinician — are partially or wholly replaced by digital intermediaries.

**Clinical Safety Issues** (3 marks):

1. **Misdiagnosis due to limited physical examination** — In specialties like dermatology, orthopedics, and cardiology, a physical exam is often essential. A 2019 study in the *Journal of Telemedicine and Telecare* found that **12–15% of tele-diagnoses** in dermatology required in-person follow-up for confirmation.
2. **Delayed emergency response** — A patient experiencing chest pain on a teleconsultation may not receive the same urgent triage as in an ER. The 2014 "Tele-ICU Death" case in the U.S. (reported by the *New England Journal of Medicine*) highlighted how network failure during tele-ICU monitoring can delay life-saving interventions.
3. **Drug-related errors** — E-prescriptions are vulnerable to dosage errors, especially in pediatric and geriatric populations, where a 10-fold overdose can be fatal.
4. **Patient identity confusion** — Two patients with similar names on a video call can result in the wrong medical record being accessed, leading to wrong treatment.

**Technical Safety Issues** (2 marks):

1. **Network unreliability** — Bandwidth fluctuations, latency spikes, and packet loss can corrupt video and audio quality, leading to miscommunication of clinical findings.
2. **Device malfunction** — Connected medical devices (e.g., remote ECG monitors, smart inhalers) may malfunction, transmit erroneous data, or fail to alert in emergencies.
3. **Software bugs and version mismatch** — Incompatible software versions between patient and provider can lead to lost clinical data.
4. **Cybersecurity threats** — Ransomware, phishing, and man-in-the-middle attacks can disrupt the entire teleconsultation pipeline.

**Conclusion** (1 mark):
Clinical and technical safety in telemedicine is best ensured through a **layered defense** strategy — combining real-time clinical decision support, redundant network paths, validated medical device software, and a robust incident response mechanism.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the legal and ethical safety issues in telemedicine with reference to the Indian Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction** (1 mark):
The legal and ethical framework for telemedicine in India was formalized by the **Board of Governors of the Medical Council of India, in partnership with NITI Aayog**, which released the **Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (TPG) 2020** as part of the **Indian Medical Council (Professional Conduct, Etiquette and Ethics) Regulations, 2002**. These guidelines address seven categories of safety issues.

**Legal Safety Issues** (3 marks):

1. **Cross-state/ cross-border practice** — Prior to TPG 2020, a doctor registered in Kerala could not legally consult a patient in Tamil Nadu. TPG 2020 permits **all India practice** for registered medical practitioners, but cross-border (international) consultations are still restricted.
2. **Registration & licensing** — Only practitioners registered with the **National Medical Commission (NMC)** or a State Medical Council may practice telemedicine. A violation can lead to license suspension.
3. **Liability & malpractice** — A physician is fully responsible for the diagnosis and treatment advice given via telemedicine, and the **Consumer Protection Act 2019** applies in case of negligence.
4. **Documentation & record retention** — TPG 2020 mandates that teleconsultation records be maintained for **at least 3 years** (or as per state law, whichever is longer), and that e-prescriptions be digitally signed.

**Ethical Safety Issues** (3 marks):

1. **Informed consent** — TPG 2020 requires explicit consent (implied, explicit, or written) from the patient before any teleconsultation, including consent for recording.
2. **Confidentiality & privacy** — Physicians must ensure ePHI is protected; disclosure without consent is a breach under both **Indian Medical Council Regulations** and the proposed **Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA)**.
3. **Non-abandonment of care** — The doctor must ensure continuity of care; if a teleconsultation is inadequate, the patient must be referred for in-person evaluation.
4. **Prescription ethics** — Schedule X and narcotic drugs cannot be prescribed via telemedicine. Schedule H drugs require an authenticated e-prescription.

**Conclusion** (1 mark):
The TPG 2020 has significantly strengthened legal and ethical safety in Indian telemedicine. However, gaps remain — particularly in **cross-border practice, pediatric tele-emergency, and AI-assisted triage**, all of which require further regulatory refinement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)] — Internal Choice Alternative

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the data security safety issues in telemedicine. Mention two real-world breach incidents.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction** (1 mark):
Data security is the backbone of telemedicine safety. When patients share sensitive medical information over a digital channel, they expect the same level of confidentiality as in a physical clinic — but the digital channel introduces new attack surfaces.

**Data Security Safety Issues** (4 marks):

1. **Unauthorized access (Breach of confidentiality)** — Attackers may exploit weak passwords, stolen credentials, or session hijacking to access ePHI.
2. **Data interception (Man-in-the-middle attacks)** — Unencrypted video streams can be intercepted, exposing confidential patient-physician conversations.
3. **Ransomware attacks** — Hospitals have been crippled by ransomware that encrypts EHRs and demands payment; this directly disrupts telemedicine services.
4. **Insider threats** — Disgruntled employees or careless staff may leak patient data, intentionally or unintentionally.
5. **Insecure APIs and mobile apps** — Many telemedicine apps expose application programming interfaces (APIs) that, if not properly secured, leak patient data to third parties.
6. **Lack of end-to-end encryption** — Some platforms encrypt data only in transit but not in storage, leaving it vulnerable on cloud servers.

**Real-World Breach Incidents** (2 marks):

1. **NHS WannaCry Ransomware Attack (May 2017)** — The **WannaCry** ransomware infected **80 NHS trusts** in the UK, forcing the cancellation of **19,000 appointments** and disrupting multiple telemedicine and remote-diagnostic services. The attack exploited an unpatched vulnerability in Windows (EternalBlue), costing the NHS an estimated **£92 million**.

2. **SingHealth Data Breach (June 2018)** — In Singapore, attackers compromised the **SingHealth** EHR system, exfiltrating the personal and medical data of **1.5 million patients**, including Prime Minister Lee Hsien Loong. The attackers gained access through a privileged workstation. This incident led to a complete overhaul of Singapore's healthcare cybersecurity policy.

**Block Diagram** — Refer to the **Mermaid DFD in Section 4.4** of these notes, which illustrates the secure data flow with TLS 1.3 and AES-256 encryption, plus an immutable audit log.

**Conclusion** (1 mark):
Data security safety in telemedicine requires a **defense-in-depth** strategy — combining encryption (in transit and at rest), multi-factor authentication, regular patching, employee training, immutable audit logs, and an incident response plan aligned with standards such as **NIST 800-61** and **ISO 27001**.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss medication safety in telemedicine. What is the role of Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) in preventing prescription errors?

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction** (1 mark):
Medication safety is a critical subset of telemedicine safety, as remote prescribing inherently removes the traditional safeguard of a pharmacist physically reviewing the prescription and counseling the patient.

**Medication Safety Issues in Telemedicine** (3 marks):

1. **Dosage errors** — Particularly in pediatric teleconsultations, weight-based dosing calculations are error-prone. A 2020 WHO report estimated that **medication errors cost $42 billion globally** annually.
2. **Drug-drug interactions** — Patients on multiple chronic medications may develop dangerous interactions that the remote physician is not fully aware of if the EHR is incomplete.
3. **Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing** — Telemedicine has been associated with **higher rates of inappropriate antibiotic prescribing** for upper respiratory tract infections (URTIs), contributing to antimicrobial resistance (AMR). A 2019 *JAMA Internal Medicine* study found telemedicine visits had a **50% higher antibiotic prescription rate** for URTIs compared to in-person visits.
4. **Schedule H/X drug misuse** — Even with the TPG 2020 guidelines, there is a risk of prohibited drugs being prescribed over informal channels (e.g., WhatsApp).
5. **Lack of follow-up** — Remote prescriptions may not be followed up, leading to patients continuing harmful drug regimens.

**Role of Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS)** (3 marks):

1. **Automated drug-interaction alerts** — When a physician enters a new prescription, the CDSS checks against the patient's active medication list and warns about potential interactions.
2. **Dose verification** — For pediatric and geriatric patients, the CDSS can verify that the prescribed dose is within the safe range for the patient's weight, age, and renal/hepatic function.
3. **Allergy checking** — The CDSS cross-references the prescription against the patient's documented allergies, preventing adverse drug reactions.
4. **Guideline-based recommendations** — The CDSS can suggest first-line antibiotic choices based on local antibiogram data, helping combat AMR.
5. **Audit trail** — Every alert, override, and acceptance is logged, providing a medico-legal record of the physician's decision-making process.

**Conclusion** (1 mark):
Medication safety in telemedicine hinges on the **integration of CDSS into the e-prescription workflow**, combined with **pharmacist verification** for high-risk drugs, **patient education** on dosage, and **systematic follow-up** to monitor for adverse drug reactions. Together, these measures transform remote prescribing from a high-risk activity into a clinically safe one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Exam Ready)**

- **Definition**: Telemedicine safety is multi-dimensional — clinical, technical, legal, ethical, and organizational.
- **Four Pillars**: Clinical, Technical, Informational, Organizational.
- **Seven-Layer Model**: Identity → Network → Storage → Clinical Decision → Medication → Consent → Legal.
- **QRS Formula**: $\text{QRS} = P \times S \times D$ (FMEA-based).
- **Risk Classes**: 1–20 (Low), 21–60 (Medium), 61–100 (High), >100 (Critical).
- **Encryption Compliance**: HIPAA requires $L_{\text{eff}} \ge 112$ bits.
- **Indian TPG 2020**: Allows all-India tele-practice for registered doctors; prohibits Schedule X & narcotics; mandates 3-year record retention; requires informed consent.
- **Real-World Incidents to Remember**: 
   - 2017 NHS WannaCry Ransomware (UK)
   - 2018 SingHealth Data Breach (Singapore)
   - 2019 Higher antibiotic Rx rate in U.S. telemedicine (JAMA)
- **Key Standards**: HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 13131:2021, IEC 80001-1, DISHA, NIST 800-61, TPG 2020.
- **Avoid These Common Pitfalls**:
   - Confusing privacy with security
   - Generic answers without specific examples
   - Missing the Indian regulatory context
   - Forgetting to draw a block diagram in 14-mark questions
- **Top 3 High-Yield Exam Topics** (predicted for 2024–2025):
   1. Real-world breach case studies + lessons learned (14-mark favorite)
   2. QRS / FMEA risk assessment with worked numerical example
   3. Indian TPG 2020 — patient rights, physician duties, and prohibited prescriptions
- **One-Liner to Remember**: *"In telemedicine, safety is not a feature — it is the foundation."*
