topics.title: Analogue Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analogue Devices ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Analogue Devices (in Telemedicine context)** are biomedical instruments and signal-conditioning circuits that operate on **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude electrical signals** to sense, amplify, filter, and transmit physiological information from a patient to a remote diagnostic or monitoring node. In strict KTU 2024 terminology, an *analogue device* in a telemedicine chain is any front-end hardware block whose input and output are represented by continuously varying voltages or currents (as opposed to discrete binary levels). These devices form the **analogue front-end (AFE)** of virtually every biomedical telemetry system — ECG, EEG, EMG, pulse oximetry, electronic stethoscopy, and electronic blood-pressure measurement. The generic signal chain is: $$\text{Biological Source} \rightarrow \text{Transducer} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{Modulator/Transmitter}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key property of analogue signals:** Infinite resolution in time and amplitude, but **susceptible to additive noise** and **signal-degradation over transmission distance** — which is why pure analogue telemedicine has now been largely superseded by digital telemetry, although the *acquisition stage remains analogue*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a stethoscope worn by a doctor standing metres away from a patient. The doctor's ear receives a faint sound, the bell of the stethoscope mechanically *amplifies* the heart/lung sounds and routes them through a tube (a *channel*) to the ear. If the doctor moves further away, the sound becomes noisy and weak. An **analogue device does the same job, but electrically**: - The *stethoscope bell* → **transducer / electrode** (converts heart sound or bioelectric potential into a tiny voltage). - The *rubber tube* → **wired or wireless analogue channel**. - The *doctor's eardrum* → **amplifier + display** (boosts the weak mV-level signal to a useful level). So: an **Analogue Device = "an electrical stethoscope"** that picks up, amplifies, filters, and relays a continuous biological waveform to a remote observer. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics to Remember The following performance metrics govern the design and evaluation of every analogue device used in telemedicine: - **Gain (A):** ratio of output signal amplitude to input signal amplitude (unitless, often expressed in dB). - **Bandwidth (BW):** range of frequencies over which the device amplifies signals faithfully (Hz). - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** ratio of desired signal power to noise power (dB). - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** ability to reject interference common to both inputs (dB). - **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** ideally **infinite** so the device does not load the source. - **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** ideally **zero** so the device drives any load. - **Power Consumption:** critical for wearable/portable telemedicine nodes. > [!IMPORTANT] > Physiological signals occupy characteristic bandwidths. ECG ≈ **0.05 – 150 Hz**, EEG ≈ **0.5 – 100 Hz**, EMG ≈ **20 – 2000 Hz**, body temperature is essentially **DC**. The analogue device must be designed for the relevant bandwidth, otherwise the signal is distorted or lost. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-Level Anatomy of an Analogue Biomedical Device A telemedicine-grade analogue device is built from four functional stages: 1. **Transducer / Electrode Stage** - Converts a physiological variable (pressure, temperature, biopotential, sound) into a proportional voltage. - Examples: Ag/AgCl skin electrodes (biopotentials), thermistor (temperature), piezoelectric crystal (sound/pressure), photodiode (SpO₂). 2. **Preamplifier Stage (Instrumentation Amplifier — INA)** - Provides high input impedance, low noise, adjustable gain (typically **1 – 1000**), and very high CMRR (≥ **80 dB**). - Built from **three operational amplifiers (op-amps)** plus an external gain-setting resistor. 3. **Filter Stage** - **Low-pass filter (LPF):** removes high-frequency noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz mains interference after mains notch, RF pickup). - **High-pass filter (HPF):** removes DC offset and baseline wander. - **Band-pass filter (BPF):** combination used in ECG/EEG/EMG to select the diagnostic band. - Implemented as **active filters** using op-amps + RC networks (better than passive filters because they provide gain and avoid loading). 4. **Output / Telemetry Stage** - Drives a display (cathode-ray oscilloscope, paper chart recorder, LCD) or a transmitter (AM/FM modulator, radio link, telephone line). ### 2.2 The Operational Amplifier — Foundation of Every Analogue Stage The **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is the universal building block. Its ideal characteristics are: - Open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL} \rightarrow \infty$ - Input impedance $Z_{in} \rightarrow \infty$ - Output impedance $Z_{out} \rightarrow 0$ - CMRR $\rightarrow \infty$ - Bandwidth $\rightarrow \infty$ Two canonical configurations used in telemedicine front-ends: **(a) Inverting amplifier** The input signal is applied to the inverting terminal through $R_{in}$; the feedback element is $R_f$. $$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$ **(b) Non-inverting amplifier** The input signal is applied to the non-inverting terminal. $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$$ ### 2.3 The Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — the Workhorse of Biopotential Telemetry A 3-op-amp INA gives **differential gain with extremely high CMRR**. Its overall closed-loop gain is: $$A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$$ where $R_{gain}$ is the external gain-setting resistor. The two input op-amps buffer the source (giving very high $Z_{in}$), and the difference amplifier provides the differential gain and rejects common-mode voltages (such as 50 Hz mains hum picked up equally by both electrodes). ### 2.4 Active Filters (used in ECG / EEG / EMG) A **first-order active low-pass filter** has transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ Its **−3 dB cutoff frequency** is: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$ A **first-order active high-pass filter** has transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$ with the same cutoff formula. By cascading HPF + LPF, a **band-pass filter** is realised. ### 2.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and Noise Figure For any analogue telemetry link, the quality of reception is governed by: $$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal,rms}}{V_{noise,rms}}\right)$$ The **noise figure (NF)** of the cascaded stages obeys the **Friis formula**: $$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$ This shows why the **first stage (preamplifier) must have low noise and high gain** — its noise contribution dominates the entire chain. ### 2.6 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) $$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$ where $A_d$ is the differential gain and $A_c$ is the common-mode gain. A good INA used in ECG telemetry has $CMRR \geq 90$ dB, meaning common-mode interference (e.g., 50 Hz mains) is rejected by a factor of $\geq 10^{4.5} \approx 31623$. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|--------------------|---------|--------------| | 1 | Inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Unitless; **−** sign = 180° phase inversion | | 2 | Non-inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Always $\geq 1$ | | 3 | Instrumentation amplifier gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Single external $R_{gain}$ sets gain | | 4 | CMRR (in dB) | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | dB; higher = better rejection | | 5 | SNR (in dB, voltage form) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | dB; reference: ECG SNR $\approx 40$ dB | | 6 | Friis noise formula | $F = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \cdots$ | First-stage noise dominates | | 7 | First-order LPF/HPF cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz; RC in seconds | | 8 | First-order LPF transfer fn. | $H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | s = $j\omega$ for sinusoidal | | 9 | First-order HPF transfer fn. | $H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | Blocks DC | | 10 | ECG bandwidth | $0.05 \leq f \leq 150$ Hz | Diagnostic bandwidth | | 11 | EEG bandwidth | $0.5 \leq f \leq 100$ Hz | Diagnostic bandwidth | | 12 | EMG bandwidth | $20 \leq f \leq 2000$ Hz | Diagnostic bandwidth | | 13 | Thermal (Johnson) noise | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | | 14 | Power gain in dB | $G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Use 20 log for voltage gain | | 15 | Ideal op-amp open-loop gain | $A_{OL} \rightarrow \infty$ | Practical $\geq 10^{5}$ | ### 2.8 Real-World Utility Analogue devices are still the *only* option for the very first stage of any biomedical acquisition: transducers generate continuous voltages, and you cannot digitise a signal that has not been pre-amplified and filtered. In modern wearable telemedicine patches (e.g., wireless ECG Holters), the analogue device is a **single mixed-signal ASIC** that integrates the INA, filters, and an ADC. Understanding the analogue stage is therefore non-negotiable, because it sets the *noise floor*, *dynamic range*, and *CMRR* of the entire system. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverting Amplifier Gain **Statement to prove:** $A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ for the inverting op-amp configuration. **Circuit description:** The non-inverting (+) input is grounded. The input signal $V_{in}$ is fed through $R_{in}$ to the inverting (−) input. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ back to the inverting input. **Step 1 — Apply the two ideal op-amp rules.** (Rule 1) The inputs draw no current: $i^{+} = i^{-} = 0$. (Rule 2) The two inputs are at the same potential (virtual short): $V^{+} = V^{-}$. **Step 2 — Compute the voltage at the inverting node.** Since the non-inverting input is grounded, $V^{+} = 0$. By the virtual short, $V^{-} = V^{+} = 0$ V. This is the famous *virtual ground*. **Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.** The current entering the node through $R_{in}$ must equal the current leaving through $R_f$ (since no current flows into the op-amp input): $$i_{in} = i_f$$ $$\frac{V_{in} - V^{-}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $V^{-} = 0$.** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the gain.** $$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}} V_{in}$$ $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$ The **negative sign** is a direct consequence of the virtual-ground rule and KCL; it represents a 180° phase inversion of the input. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain **Statement to prove:** $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$. **Step 1 — Circuit setup.** $V_{in}$ is applied to the (+) terminal. A divider $R_{in}$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to $V_{out}$) feeds the (−) terminal. **Step 2 — Virtual short.** $V^{-} = V^{+} = V_{in}$. **Step 3 — KCL at the inverting node.** No current into the op-amp, so the current through $R_{in}$ equals the current through $R_f$: $$\frac{0 - V^{-}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $V^{-} = V_{in}$.** $$-\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply out and solve.** $$-V_{in} R_f = V_{in} R_{in} - V_{out} R_{in}$$ $$V_{out} R_{in} = V_{in} R_{in} + V_{in} R_f = V_{in}\,(R_{in} + R_f)$$ $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}}$$ Note that $A_v \geq 1$ always, and is **positive** (no phase inversion) — the input and output are in phase. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Instrumentation Amplifier Gain The classic 3-op-amp INA has two input buffers $A_1, A_2$ (each with feedback resistor $R_1$ and gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$) feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier $A_3$ (with resistors $R_2, R_3$). **Step 1 — Gain of the input buffer pair.** Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the two INA input voltages. The output of the first stage is: $$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ This result is obtained by superposition: the common-mode component is rejected by symmetry, and the differential component is amplified by $\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)$. **Step 2 — Gain of the difference amplifier stage.** The difference amplifier gives: $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2})$$ **Step 3 — Combine both stages.** $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ $$\boxed{A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}}$$ The CMRR of this configuration is limited by the **resistor matching** in the difference-amp stage; a mismatch of $\delta$ between $R_2$ and $R_3$ limits the CMRR to roughly $20\log_{10}(1/\delta)$ dB. For $\delta = 0.0001$ (0.01 %), $CMRR \approx 80$ dB. ### 3.4 Derivation of the First-Order Low-Pass Filter Cutoff Consider a non-inverting op-amp buffer with an RC network at its input. The transfer function from input to op-amp (+) input is a simple voltage divider: $$H(s) = \frac{V_x(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$ The op-amp buffer has unity gain, so the overall transfer function equals the divider ratio. The **−3 dB cutoff** is the frequency at which the magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its DC value: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ $$\Rightarrow 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_c RC = 1$$ $$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation of SNR in dB from Voltage Amplitudes For biomedical signals, $P = V_{rms}^{\,2}/R$, so the ratio of powers equals the square of the ratio of voltages: $$SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s^{\,2}/R}{V_n^{\,2}/R}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)^{2}$$ Using the property $10 \log_{10}(x^2) = 20 \log_{10}(x)$: $$\boxed{SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{s,rms}}{V_{n,rms}}\right)}$$ ### 3.6 Numerical Example — ECG Front-End Design (typical KTU-style problem) A wireless ECG node uses an INA with $R_1 = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_{gain} = 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. The ECG peak amplitude at the skin is $V_{diff} = 1$ mV. A 50 Hz notch is implemented as a 2nd-order active BP-reject. Compute the INA output and SNR if $V_{noise,rms} = 5\ \mu V$. **Step 1 — Compute INA gain.** $$A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25}{2.5}\right) \times \frac{10}{10} = (1 + 20) \times 1 = 21$$ **Step 2 — Compute INA output voltage.** $$V_{out} = 21 \times 1\ \text{mV} = 21\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 3 — Compute SNR at the INA output (signal amplified, noise amplified similarly).** $$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1\ \text{mV}}{5\ \mu V}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(200) \approx 46.0\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — After the 50 Hz notch (suppresses mains by 40 dB), the SNR improves by ≈ 40 dB** if mains was the dominant noise, giving an effective SNR of ≈ **86 dB** — well above the **40 dB** clinical-quality threshold. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Analogue Biomedical Device (Telemedicine AFE) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PATIENT
BIOLOGICAL SOURCE] --> B[TRANSDUCER OR ELECTRODE] B --> C[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER INA] C --> D[ANALOG FILTER STAGE
HPF PLUS LPF PLUS 50HZ NOTCH] D --> E[OUTPUT DRIVER
OR ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER] E --> F[TELEMETRY TRANSMITTER
AM FM OR WIRELESS] F --> G[REMOTE DISPLAY
OR RECEIVING NODE] style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style D fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 style E fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style F fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style G fill:#d0e0e3,stroke:#134f5c,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Trace the path of a biopotential (e.g., ECG) from the patient to the remote node. Each block has a specific analogue function described in Section 2. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[INPUT V1] --> A1[OPAMP A1
BUFFER WITH GAIN] V2[INPUT V2] --> A2[OPAMP A2
BUFFER WITH GAIN] A1 --> A3[OPAMP A3
DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER] A2 --> A3 A3 --> VOUT[OUTPUT VOUT] RG[RGAIN
GAIN SETTING RESISTOR] -.-> A1 RG -.-> A2 R2A[R2] --> A3 R3A[R3] --> A3 style V1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 style V2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 style A1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style A2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style A3 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 style VOUT fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style RG fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — ECG Analogue Front-End (AFE) Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[ECG LEAD 1] --> P1[PREAMP 1] S2[ECG LEAD 2] --> P2[PREAMP 2] S3[ECG LEAD 3] --> P3[PREAMP 3] RL[RIGHT LEG DRIVE] --> P1 RL --> P2 RL --> P3 P1 --> IA[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER] P2 --> IA P3 --> IA IA --> HPF[HPF 0.05 HZ
REMOVES DC OFFSET] HPF --> LPF[LPF 150 HZ
REMOVES HIGH FREQ NOISE] LPF --> NOTCH[50 HZ NOTCH
REJECTS MAINS HUM] NOTCH --> ADC[ADC DRIVER
UNITY GAIN BUFFER] ADC --> DIG[DIGITAL TELEMETRY
ENCODER AND TX] style S1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000 style S2 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000 style S3 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000 style RL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 style P1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style P2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style P3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style IA fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 style HPF fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style LPF fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style NOTCH fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style ADC fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 style DIG fill:#d0e0e3,stroke:#134f5c,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Analogue Modulation for Wireless Telemedicine ```mermaid flowchart LR BIO[BIO SIGNAL
ECG EEG EMG] --> AAMP[PREAMP AND FILTER] AAMP --> MOD[MODULATOR
AM FM OR PWM] MOD --> TX[RF TRANSMITTER
ANTENNA] TX -. RF LINK .-> RX[RF RECEIVER
ANTENNA] RX --> DMOD[DEMODULATOR] DMOD --> DISP[DISPLAY
OR DATA LOGGER] style BIO fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,color:#000 style AAMP fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style MOD fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 style TX fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style RX fill:#e6d7f1,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000 style DMOD fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000 style DISP fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > In modern telemedicine, the modulator block is replaced by an **ADC + digital encoder (codec) + digital transmitter (Bluetooth / Wi-Fi / ZigBee / LoRa)**. The analogue device still exists as the **front-end AFE** that conditions the biopotential before digitisation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q1.** Define an *analogue device* in the context of telemedicine. Give two examples. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An analogue device in telemedicine is a biomedical instrument that operates on **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude electrical signals** to acquire, amplify, filter, and transmit physiological information from a patient to a remote node. **(2 marks)** > Two examples: (i) electronic stethoscope, (ii) analogue ECG amplifier front-end, (iii) pulse-oximeter photodiode + amplifier, (iv) electronic blood-pressure (NIBP) monitor. **(1 mark for any two)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q2.** State the significance of **CMRR** in an instrumentation amplifier used for ECG telemetry. What is the typical minimum acceptable value? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) measures the ability of the INA to **reject interference that appears equally on both inputs** (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum picked up by body and lead wires). **(1 mark)** > $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$. A high CMRR means a small common-mode signal produces a negligible output. **(1 mark)** > For clinical-grade ECG telemetry, a CMRR of **at least 80 dB (preferably ≥ 90 dB)** is required. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]** > > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > > **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier and derive its overall voltage gain. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An ECG telemetry system uses an INA with $R_1 = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_{gain} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$. The differential ECG signal is 0.8 mV (peak). Compute the INA output. If the input-referred noise is 6 μV (rms), find the output SNR in dB. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for visual). **(1 mark for diagram)** > > **Derivation (6 marks):** > > Step 1: The two input op-amps A1 and A2 form a buffered differential pair. Apply the virtual-short and KCL at each inverting input. The current through $R_{gain}$ is: > > $$i_{RG} = \frac{V_{o1} - V_{o2}}{R_{gain}}$$ > > Since the same current flows through both $R_1$ feedback resistors: > > $$V_{o1} = V_1 + i_{RG} R_1, \quad V_{o2} = V_2 - i_{RG} R_1$$ > > Subtracting: > > $$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 i_{RG} R_1 = (V_1 - V_2) + 2 R_1 \cdot \frac{V_{o1} - V_{o2}}{R_{gain}}$$ > > Rearranging: > > $$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2) \quad\textbf{(3 marks)}$$ > > Step 2: The difference amplifier A3 gives: > > $$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2}) \quad\textbf{(1 mark)}$$ > > Step 3: Combined overall gain: > > $$\boxed{A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}} \quad\textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > > **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** > > Step 1: Compute gain. > > $$A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 50}{5}\right) \times \frac{20}{10} = (1 + 20) \times 2 = 21 \times 2 = 42 \quad\textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > > Step 2: Output voltage. > > $$V_{out} = 42 \times 0.8\ \text{mV} = 33.6\ \text{mV (peak)} \quad\textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > > Step 3: Output noise voltage (gain multiplies noise as well). > > $$V_{n,out} = 42 \times 6\ \mu V = 252\ \mu V\ (rms) \quad\textbf{(1 mark)}$$ > > Step 4: SNR in dB. > > $$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{out,rms}}{V_{n,out,rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.8\ \text{mV}}{6\ \mu V}\right)$$ > > $$= 20 \log_{10}(133.33) \approx 20 \times 2.125 = 42.5\ \text{dB} \quad\textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > > **Result:** $V_{out} = 33.6$ mV, $SNR \approx 42.5$ dB — above the 40 dB clinical ECG threshold. --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an ECG analogue front-end (AFE) used in telemedicine. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An active first-order low-pass filter is used in the ECG AFE with $R = 33\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 47\ \text{nF}$. Compute the −3 dB cutoff frequency. State the transfer function and find the filter gain at 50 Hz and 1 kHz. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram). **(2 marks)** > > **Functions of each block (5 marks):** > > - **ECG Leads (1, 2, 3):** pick up differential biopotentials from the body surface using Ag/AgCl electrodes. **(1 mark)** > - **Right-Leg Drive (RLD):** actively drives the right-leg electrode to reduce common-mode voltage, improving effective CMRR by 10–20 dB. **(1 mark)** > - **Preamplifiers (P1, P2, P3):** buffer the high-impedance electrode signals; provide input impedance $\geq 10\ \text{M}\Omega$. **(1 mark)** > - **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** provides differential gain (typically 10–100) and rejects common-mode noise (CMRR $\geq$ 80 dB). **(1 mark)** > - **HPF (0.05 Hz):** removes DC electrode offset and baseline wander. **(0.5 mark)** > - **LPF (150 Hz):** limits bandwidth to the diagnostic ECG range and removes RF/muscle noise. **(0.5 mark)** > - **50 Hz Notch:** rejects mains-line interference. **(0.5 mark)** > - **ADC Driver:** unity-gain buffer that drives the ADC. **(0.5 mark)** > > **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** > > Step 1: Transfer function. > > $$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} \quad\textbf{(1 mark)}$$ > > Step 2: Cutoff frequency. > > $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 33\,000 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}}$$ > > $$= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.551 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{9.745 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 102.6\ \text{Hz} \quad\textbf{(2 marks)}$$ > > Step 3: Magnitude response. > > $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} \quad\textbf{(1 mark)}$$ > > Step 4: Gain at 50 Hz. > > $$\vert H(50) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (50/102.6)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.2375}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.2375}} = 0.899$$ > > In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.899) \approx -0.93$ dB. **(1.5 marks)** > > Step 5: Gain at 1 kHz. > > $$\vert H(1000) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (1000/102.6)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 95.0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{96}} \approx 0.102$$ > > In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.102) \approx -19.8$ dB. **(1.5 marks)** > > **Interpretation:** At 50 Hz the filter passes the signal with negligible attenuation (well below cutoff). At 1 kHz the signal is attenuated by ≈ 20 dB — appropriate for an ECG LPF, since ECG energy above 150 Hz is unwanted. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Analogue Device Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the inverting amplifier gain** — many students write $A_v = R_f/R_{in}$ instead of $-R_f/R_{in}$. Lose 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing voltage-gain dB and power-gain dB.** Use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage/current ratios and $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios. > 3. **Writing CMRR as a ratio instead of in dB** without stating both. The question usually expects the dB form. > 4. **Forgetting the buffer-pair gain in the INA derivation.** The complete answer must include the $\left(1 + 2R_1/R_{gain}\right)$ factor — partial credit of only 2–3 marks is given if the difference-amp stage is derived but the input stage is skipped. > 5. **Not mentioning the units** in numerical answers (Hz, dB, V, mV, μV). Always annotate. > 6. **Treating ideal op-amp assumptions as universal** — practical op-amps have finite gain, input bias current, and offset voltage. In numerical design problems, the bandwidth-gain trade-off (Gain-Bandwidth Product, GBP) should be checked. > 7. **Confusing CMRR with PSRR** (Power-Supply Rejection Ratio). These are *different* quantities and examiners will deduct marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Analogue device = continuous-signal hardware** in a telemedicine chain; the AFE is always analogue even when the rest of the system is digital. - **Order of stages in a biopotential AFE:** Transducer → INA → HPF → LPF → 50 Hz Notch → ADC Driver → Telemetry. - **Op-amp ideal rules:** $i^+ = i^- = 0$; $V^+ = V^-$; $A_{OL} \to \infty$. Memorise these — they drive every derivation. - **Inverting amplifier gain:** $A_v = -R_f/R_{in}$ (negative = 180° phase inversion). - **Non-inverting amplifier gain:** $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$ (always positive and $\geq 1$). - **INA gain:** $A_v = (1 + 2R_1/R_{gain})(R_3/R_2)$ — single resistor $R_{gain}$ sets gain; symmetry gives high CMRR. - **First-order filter cutoff:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — use this to design ECG/EEG/EMG bandwidth. - **CMRR:** $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ in dB; clinical ECG requires $\geq$ 80 dB. - **SNR:** $20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ in dB; clinical ECG threshold ≈ 40 dB. - **Friis formula:** first-stage noise dominates; always use a low-noise high-gain preamplifier as the first INA stage. - **Diagnostic bandwidths:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz, EEG 0.5–100 Hz, EMG 20–2000 Hz, body temperature DC. - **Johnson noise:** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — thermal noise floor that limits the smallest detectable signal. - **Active vs Passive filters:** active filters (op-amp + RC) provide gain, isolation, and avoid loading; passive filters (RLC) are used only at very high frequencies. - **Modern telemedicine reality:** AFE is analogue; everything after the ADC is digital. The AFE determines the **noise floor, dynamic range, CMRR, and patient-safety isolation** of the entire system. - **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) is telemedicine-specific** — it actively reduces 50 Hz mains interference at the patient, improving the effective CMRR by 10–20 dB. - **One-thing-per-line in numerical answers** — show the formula, the substitution, the arithmetic, and the boxed answer; KTU examiners reward this style heavily.

topics.title: Laser Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laser Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Laser Display** is an advanced optoelectronic visualization system that uses **coherent, monochromatic laser radiation** from one or more primary-color laser sources (Red, Green, Blue) to directly synthesize images on a screen, the retina, or a volumetric medium. In the context of **Telemedicine**, laser displays constitute a critical visualization technology for transmitting high-fidelity medical imagery (radiographs, CT/MRI slices, dermatological photos, surgical fields) between geographically separated sites, enabling remote diagnosis, tele-mentoring, and immersive 3D anatomical visualization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Laser Displays are studied under *Medical Imaging and Visualization Technologies* because they offer **3× wider color gamut** and **> 2× higher brightness** than conventional LED/LCD backlit displays, which is critical for accurate medical image interpretation during teleconsultation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a regular TV as a **"flashlight with a colored filter"** — it shines white light through red, green, and blue filters to make colors. The filters *waste* a lot of light and the colors are "muddy" because white light contains many wavelengths mixed together.

A **Laser Display is like a "musician with three perfect voices"** — it has three pure-tone singers (a red laser, a green laser, a blue laser) that each produce a single, razor-sharp wavelength. When they sing together in the right proportions, they can reproduce almost *any* color the human eye can perceive, much more vividly than filtered light. In telemedicine, this matters because a dermatologist viewing a skin lesion remotely needs to see **subtle color variations** that indicate malignancy — a muddy color display could mean a missed diagnosis.

## 1.3 Why Lasers? (Key Physical Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Display Metrics for Medical Visualization:**
> - **Color Gamut (NTSC):** Conventional LCD ≈ 72% ; Laser Display ≈ **> 200%**
> - **Luminance (Brightness):** Laser Displays routinely exceed **5000 lumens**
> - **Spectral Purity (Δλ):** Laser ≈ **< 2 nm** ; LED ≈ 20–30 nm
> - **Wavelengths used in medical laser displays:** **Red ≈ 635 nm**, **Green ≈ 532 nm**, **Blue ≈ 445 nm**

## 1.4 Classification of Laser Displays (KTU Taxonomy)

| Type | Mechanism | Telemedicine Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Laser TV / Laser Phosphor Display** | Blue laser excites phosphor wheel | Conference-room teleconsultation screens |
| **Laser Beam Scanning (LBS) / Retinal Projection** | MEMS mirrors scan RGB beams onto retina | Wearable surgical heads-up displays |
| **Holographic Laser Display** | Laser interference records 3D wavefronts | 3D anatomical model transmission for surgical planning |
| **Volumetric Laser Display** | Laser excites voxels in a transparent medium | True-3D radiological image viewing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

A laser display system is a *synthesizer* of spatially and temporally modulated optical energy. The functional chain is:

1. **Electrical Video Signal Ingestion:** The input is a digital/analog video stream (HDMI, DICOM, SDI) carrying pixel-by-pixel intensity values $(R, G, B)$ and timing/sync data.
2. **Laser Source Excitation:** Three semiconductor or DPSS (Diode-Pumped Solid-State) lasers emit at the primary wavelengths. These represent the *additive primaries*.
3. **Beam Collimation & Combination:** Collimating lenses convert diverging laser beams into parallel rays. **Dichroic mirrors** (wavelength-selective beam splitters) combine the three beams into a single coaxial white-light-equivalent beam.
4. **Spatial Modulation (Image Formation):** An SLM (Spatial Light Modulator) — either a **MEMS mirror array** or a **Galvanometer/polygon scanner** — deflects each pixel's beam along X-Y coordinates.
5. **Intensity Modulation (Grayscale/Color Depth):** A driver circuit varies the **PWM duty cycle** of each laser diode, encoding 8-bit (256) to 12-bit (4096) intensity levels.
6. **Projection / Retinal Delivery:** The modulated beam lands on a screen (front/rear projection), is scanned onto the retina, or interferes inside a holographic plate.
7. **Human Visual System (HVS) Integration:** The viewer's eye/brain integrates the rapidly-scanned spots into a perceived image via **persistence of vision (τ ≈ 50 ms)**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why three lasers?** Because the human eye has three types of cone cells (L, M, S). Matching these with three monochromatic primaries (R, G, B) gives a *metameric match* covering the entire perceivable gamut.
> - **Why modulation by PWM, not analog current?** Lasers are threshold devices; analog current variation is non-linear and slow. PWM provides linear, fast (ns-scale) intensity control.
> - **Why coherent light for holography?** Only coherent waves can produce a stable interference pattern — the foundation of holography. Without laser coherence, no 3D medical holograms for tele-surgical planning.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | Beam divergence half-angle; $D$ = beam waist diameter |
| 2 | $P_{pixel} = P_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{on}}{T_{frame}}$ | Per-pixel optical power (PWM modulation) |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{1}{\theta_{scan}}$ | Angular resolution in scan direction |
| 4 | $G_{gamut} = \dfrac{A_{display}}{A_{NTSC}} \times 100\%$ | Color gamut area ratio (CIE 1931 chromaticity) |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{A}$ | Luminance (cd/m²); $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux |
| 6 | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot L}$ | Beer-Lambert law for safety / tissue interaction |
| 7 | $f_{frame} = \dfrac{v_{mirror} \cdot N_{lines}}{N_{pixels/line}}$ | Required MEMS mirror frequency |
| 8 | $MPE = 10^{\frac{\log_{10}(t) - a}{b}}$ | Maximum Permissible Exposure (laser safety) |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Image signal-to-noise ratio |
| 10 | $D_{spot} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi d}$ | Focused spot diameter (Airy disk) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safeguard Applied:** All absolute-value bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` rather than `|` to prevent breaking the table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tele-radiology:** Laser displays reproduce > 200% NTSC gamut, critical for spotting subtle contrast in mammograms and X-rays.
- **Tele-surgery (e.g., Da Vinci system):** Retinal laser scanning displays (HMDs) project 3D surgical field with sub-millisecond latency.
- **Holographic Tele-presence:** Future 6G telemedicine uses volumetric laser displays to project a remote patient as a 3D hologram for the consulting physician.
- **Pathology & Dermatology:** High color-fidelity displays reduce *metameric failure* (two distinct colors appearing identical), reducing misdiagnosis by an estimated **15–20%** in published studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spot Size and Resolution of a Scanned Laser Display

The smallest spot a laser display can form on a screen is governed by diffraction. We derive the Airy-disk diameter $D_{spot}$ at the projection screen.

**Step 1: Setup of the optical system.**
A collimated laser beam of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a modulator aperture of diameter $d$. A projection lens of focal length $f$ focuses the beam onto a screen.

**Step 2: Apply Fraunhofer diffraction theory.**
For a circular aperture uniformly illuminated by a plane wave, the far-field intensity pattern is the Airy disk. The first zero of the Airy pattern occurs at the angular radius:
$$
\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 3: Convert angular radius to linear spot diameter.**
The linear diameter on the focal plane (screen) is:
$$
D_{spot} = 2 \cdot f \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 4: Substitute θ.**
$$
D_{spot} = 2 \cdot f \cdot \left(1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Simplify to the KTU form.**
$$
D_{spot} = \frac{2.44 \, \lambda f}{d} \approx \frac{4 \lambda f}{\pi d}
$$

This is the *minimum pixel size* the display can render. Pixels smaller than this cannot be resolved — the spot will overlap into adjacent pixels and blur the image.

**Engineering Interpretation:** For a green laser ($\lambda = 532 \text{ nm}$), a 5 mm aperture, and a 50 mm projection lens:
$$
D_{spot} = \frac{4 \times 532 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.050}{0.005} = 2.13 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 21.3 \, \mu\text{m}
$$
This is well below human visual acuity at 3 m viewing distance, ensuring *pixel-less* images — a hallmark of laser displays.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Color Gamut Area in CIE 1931 Space

**Step 1: Define the color gamut triangle.**
In the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram, the three primary laser wavelengths $(x_R, y_R)$, $(x_G, y_G)$, $(x_B, y_B)$ form a triangle. The display's gamut area is:
$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \left| (x_R(y_G - y_B) + x_G(y_B - y_R) + x_B(y_R - y_G)) \right|
$$

**Step 2: Compute the NTSC reference area (0.21, 0.71), (0.14, 0.08), (0.67, 0.33).**
$$
A_{NTSC} = 0.1582 \text{ (normalized units)}
$$

**Step 3: Gamut ratio.**
$$
G_{\%} = \frac{A_{display}}{A_{NTSC}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation for typical medical laser display primaries (R=635 nm, G=532 nm, B=445 nm).**
Using CIE 1931 tables:
- Red (635 nm): $(0.714, 0.286)$
- Green (532 nm): $(0.171, 0.746)$
- Blue (445 nm): $(0.156, 0.018)$

Substituting:
$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0.714(0.746 - 0.018) + 0.171(0.018 - 0.286) + 0.156(0.286 - 0.746) \right|
$$

$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0.714(0.728) + 0.171(-0.268) + 0.156(-0.460) \right|
$$

$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0.5198 - 0.0458 - 0.0718 \right| = \frac{1}{2}(0.4022) = 0.2011
$$

$$
G_{\%} = \frac{0.2011}{0.1582} \times 100\% \approx 127.1\%
$$

**Note:** In practice, gamut ratios of 150–210% NTSC are achieved with even narrower laser line-widths.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PWM Modulation Depth and Per-Pixel Power

**Step 1: Frame budget.** A 60 Hz display has a frame period $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{60} \approx 16.67$ ms.

**Step 2: Time per pixel.**
$$
\tau_{pixel} = \frac{T_{frame}}{N_{pixels}}
$$
For a 1920 × 1080 display:
$$
\tau_{pixel} = \frac{16.67 \text{ ms}}{2{,}073{,}600} \approx 8.04 \, \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: PWM on-time for an 8-bit grayscale level $k$ (0–255).**
$$
\tau_{on} = \frac{k}{255} \cdot \tau_{pixel}
$$

**Step 4: Per-pixel optical energy.**
$$
E_{pixel} = P_{laser} \cdot \tau_{on}
$$

**Step 5: Average irradiance on screen.**
For a projected area $A_{spot}$ and refresh rate $f_r$:
$$
E_{screen} = P_{laser} \cdot \frac{\tau_{on}}{T_{frame}} \cdot \frac{1}{A_{spot}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Display Technologies for Telemedicine

| Parameter | LCD (LED backlit) | OLED | **Laser Display** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Color Gamut (% NTSC) | 70–90% | 100–115% | **150–210%** |
| Peak Luminance (cd/m²) | 500–1500 | 1000–2000 | **3000–5000+** |
| Black Level | Low (backlight bleed) | True black | True black (beam-off = 0) |
| Lifetime (hours) | 60,000 | 30,000 | **> 25,000 (laser) / 60,000 (phosphor)** |
| Response Time | 5–10 ms | < 1 ms | **< 1 μs** |
| Power Efficiency (lm/W) | 5–10 | 10–20 | **15–30** |
| 3D / Holographic Capability | No | No | **Yes** |
| Suitability for Color-Critical Telemedicine | Moderate | High | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** For color-critical tele-diagnosis (dermatology, pathology, wound imaging), laser displays are superior due to the *metameric-failure resistance* of narrow-band primaries.

## 3.5 Laser Safety Classification (IEC 60825-1) — Mandatory for Telemedicine Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Class 1:** Safe under all conditions. Required for consumer/clinical devices.
> - **Class 2:** Visible (400–700 nm), blink reflex protects. Limit: $1 \text{ mW}$.
> - **Class 3R:** Up to 5 mW; direct beam can be hazardous.
> - **Class 3B / 4:** Hazardous; requires engineering controls, key switch, and warning labels. Used in surgical lasers, NOT in display output.

For a laser display, the *accessible emission limit* (AEL) for Class 1 is computed as:
$$
AEL = 7 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t^{0.75} \text{ J (for 400–700 nm, t in s)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Telemedicine Laser Display End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Medical Imaging Source: CT, MRI, X-ray] --> enc1[DICOM Encoder]
    enc1 --> net1[Telemedicine Network: 5G, 6G, Fiber]
    net1 --> dec1[DICOM Decoder]
    dec1 --> proc1[Video Signal Processor]
    proc1 --> drv1[RGB Laser Driver: PWM Modulation]
    drv1 --> ldR[Red Laser 635 nm]
    drv1 --> ldG[Green Laser 532 nm]
    drv1 --> ldB[Blue Laser 445 nm]
    ldR --> col1[Collimating Lens Assembly]
    ldG --> col1
    ldB --> col1
    col1 --> dm1[Dichroic Beam Combiner]
    dm1 --> mod1[Scanning Modulator: MEMS Mirror]
    mod1 --> scr1[Display Screen: Projection / Retinal / Holographic]
    scr1 --> obs1[Remote Physician Visual Perception]
    obs1 --> dec1
    saf1[Safety Interlock: IEC 60825-1 Class 1] -.-> drv1
    saf1 -.-> mod1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Subsystems of a Laser Display Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr[Power Supply: Constant Current Driver] --> ldA[Red Laser Module]
    pwr --> ldB[Green Laser Module]
    pwr --> ldC[Blue Laser Module]
    ldA --> optA[Beam Shaping Optics]
    ldB --> optB[Beam Shaping Optics]
    ldC --> optC[Beam Shaping Optics]
    optA --> bcs[Beam Combiner System: X-Cube or Dichroic Mirrors]
    optB --> bcs
    optC --> bcs
    bcs --> exp[Beam Expander]
    exp --> mems[MEMS Micromirror Scanner: 2 Axis]
    mems --> fpga[FPGA Controller: Sync and PWM]
    fpga --> encIn[Video Input: HDMI 2.1 / DisplayPort / SDI]
    temp[Thermistor Temperature Sensor] --> pwr
    phot[Photodiode Feedback: Optical Power Monitor] --> fpga
    phot -.-> ldA
    phot -.-> ldB
    phot -.-> ldC
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Application Mapping in Telemedicine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app1[Tele-Radiology] --> dispA[High-Brightness Laser Projector]
    app2[Tele-Dermatology] --> dispB[Wide-Gamut Laser Monitor]
    app3[Tele-Surgery] --> dispC[Retinal Scanning HMD]
    app4[Medical Education] --> dispD[Volumetric / Holographic Display]
    app5[Telepathology] --> dispB
    app6[Tele-Cardiology: Echocardiography] --> dispA
    dispA --> spec1[Requirement: 4K, 5000 lm, 10 bit color]
    dispB --> spec2[Requirement: 99 percent Adobe RGB, calibrated]
    dispC --> spec3[Requirement: 120 Hz stereoscopic, sub 5 ms latency]
    dispD --> spec4[Requirement: 3D voxel rate greater than 10 Hz]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Color Synthesis via Additive Mixing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Input: 8 bit Red Value] --> pwm1[PWM Channel R]
    inp2[Input: 8 bit Green Value] --> pwm2[PWM Channel G]
    inp3[Input: 8 bit Blue Value] --> pwm3[PWM Channel B]
    pwm1 --> mix1[Time Division Multiplexed Beam]
    pwm2 --> mix1
    pwm3 --> mix1
    mix1 --> eye[Human Eye: Trichromatic Integration]
    eye --> perc[Perceived Color: CIE XYZ Stimulus]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Define a laser display. List any two advantages of laser displays over conventional LCD displays in telemedicine applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A laser display is a visualization system that uses three coherent monochromatic laser sources (Red λ ≈ 635 nm, Green λ ≈ 532 nm, Blue λ ≈ 445 nm) whose beams are spatially scanned and intensity-modulated to synthesize images directly on a screen or the retina.
**Two advantages:**
1. **Wider color gamut** (> 150% NTSC vs. ~72% for LCD) → more accurate reproduction of medical images such as dermatological lesions and histopathology slides.
2. **Higher luminance and contrast** (up to 5000+ lumens, true black via beam-off) → enables visualization of subtle grayscale variations in radiographs and CT slices during remote consultation.
*(Any two of: longer life, faster response, 3D/holographic capability, lower power-per-lumen — full credit.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** 
**What is the role of dichroic mirrors in a laser display system?**

**Model Answer:**
Dichroic mirrors are wavelength-selective beam splitters used to **combine the three primary-color laser beams (R, G, B) into a single co-axial beam** without significant power loss. They reflect one wavelength band while transmitting another, enabling efficient beam co-alignment. They are also used at the output to *separate* unwanted wavelengths for filtering or safety shutters. They have high reflectance (R > 99%) at the target wavelength and high transmittance (T > 95%) at the pass wavelength.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a laser television system used in telemedicine conference rooms. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare the color gamut of a laser display with an LCD display. Show that the medical laser display with primaries at 635 nm, 532 nm and 445 nm achieves a gamut area ratio of approximately 127% NTSC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Working — 7 marks**

```
[Video Input] → [DICOM Decoder] → [Video Processor]
                                          ↓
   ┌──────────────┬──────────────┐
   ↓              ↓              ↓
[Red LD 635]  [Green LD 532]  [Blue LD 445]
   ↓              ↓              ↓
[Collimator]   [Collimator]   [Collimator]
   └──────→ [Dichroic Combiner X-Cube] ←──────┘
                       ↓
              [MEMS 2-axis Scanner]
                       ↓
               [Projection Screen]
                       ↓
            [Viewer's Eye / Camera]
```

**Working Principle:**
- Medical video (e.g., live surgery feed, radiological image) is decoded and split into three primary-color channels.
- Three laser diodes (or DPSS lasers) emit at R, G, B wavelengths simultaneously.
- Each beam is collimated and then combined using an X-cube dichroic prism into a single white-coaxial beam.
- A 2-axis MEMS micromirror (operating at ~30 kHz resonant on fast axis, ~60 Hz on slow axis) raster-scans the combined beam across the screen pixel-by-pixel.
- The intensity of each laser is PWM-modulated 8–12 bits per color to encode grayscale.
- The screen reflects the scanned beam; the viewer's eye/brain integrates the scanning spots via persistence of vision into a perceived full-color image.
- A photodiode feedback loop continuously monitors the optical output to compensate for temperature drift and aging of the laser diodes, ensuring *DICOM GSDF calibration* — critical for diagnostic-grade telemedicine.

**(b) Gamut Comparison & Derivation — 7 marks**

For LCD with CCFL/LED backlight + color filters, typical gamut is **70–90% NTSC** because the primaries are *broadband* and de-saturated.

For laser display with the given primaries, the gamut area in CIE 1931 is computed as follows:

Using chromaticity coordinates:
- Red (635 nm): $(x_R, y_R) = (0.714, 0.286)$
- Green (532 nm): $(x_G, y_G) = (0.171, 0.746)$
- Blue (445 nm): $(x_B, y_B) = (0.156, 0.018)$

$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \vert x_R(y_G - y_B) + x_G(y_B - y_R) + x_B(y_R - y_G) \vert
$$

$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0.714(0.728) + 0.171(-0.268) + 0.156(-0.460) \vert
$$

$$
A_{display} = \frac{1}{2} \vert 0.5198 - 0.0458 - 0.0718 \vert = 0.2011
$$

NTSC reference area $A_{NTSC} = 0.1582$.

$$
G_{\%NTSC} = \frac{0.2011}{0.1582} \times 100\% \approx 127.1\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The laser display exceeds the LCD gamut by approximately **40 percentage points**, allowing a clinician to perceive color variations in dermatology, pathology, and wound images that would otherwise be *metamerically lost*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Explain MEMS-based laser beam scanning used in retinal projection displays for tele-surgery. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss laser safety classifications per IEC 60825-1 as applicable to a medical laser display. Why is Class 1 mandatory for a telemedicine endpoint display? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MEMS Laser Beam Scanning — 7 marks**

A **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical System) micromirror** is a silicon-based device with a tiny mirror (~1–3 mm diameter) suspended on torsional hinges, electrostatically actuated.

**Construction:**
- Single-crystal silicon mirror suspended by two pairs of flexure beams.
- Comb-drive or parallel-plate electrodes beneath the mirror apply electrostatic torque.
- Mirror can tilt on two axes: **fast scan (horizontal, ~30 kHz resonant)** and **slow scan (vertical, ~60 Hz quasi-static)**.

**Working:**
1. The combined RGB laser beam strikes the MEMS mirror.
2. The mirror's tilt angle $\theta(t)$ deflects the beam by $2\theta$ in both X and Y.
3. The fast axis traces a horizontal line; the slow axis steps down to the next line.
4. Synchronized with the PWM video signal, the system paints one frame per scan cycle.
5. In retinal projection, a **pupil-relay lens** focuses the scanned beam through the pupil onto the retina, forming an image directly on the fovea — bypassing the screen entirely. This gives a "virtual screen" of arbitrary size (effectively 60"–200") perceived by the surgeon.

**Advantages in tele-surgery:**
- **Sub-millisecond latency** — critical for robotic surgical feedback loops.
- **High contrast (> 10,000:1)** — true black because the laser is off, not blocked.
- **Compact, light-weight** — wearable heads-up display (HMD).
- **No screen-induced artifacts** — image is independent of ambient lighting.

**(b) Laser Safety Classification — 7 marks**

Per **IEC 60825-1**, lasers are classified based on accessible emission limits (AEL) and wavelength:

| Class | Accessible Emission | Hazard | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class 1** | < 0.39 mW (visible) | Safe under all reasonable conditions | Consumer/clinical displays |
| **Class 1M** | Up to Class 1 limit but diverging/expanding beam | Safe unless optics used | Projection displays |
| **Class 2** | < 1 mW (400–700 nm) | Blink reflex (0.25 s) protects | Visible pointers |
| **Class 2M** | Up to Class 2 but expanded | Safe unless optics | — |
| **Class 3R** | 1–5 mW | Low risk; direct intra-beam viewing hazardous | Lab devices |
| **Class 3B** | 5–500 mW | Direct beam hazardous; diffuse OK | Research lasers |
| **Class 4** | > 500 mW | Hazardous; skin & fire risk | Surgical/industrial cutting |

**Why Class 1 is mandatory for telemedicine endpoint displays:**
1. **Patient & operator safety:** Telemedicine endpoints are used by non-trained personnel (patients, nurses, general physicians) who may not be aware of laser hazards.
2. **Uncontrolled environments:** Devices may be used in homes, mobile clinics, ambulances — Class 3B/4 controls (key switch, warning labels) are not enforceable.
3. **Regulatory compliance:** Medical electrical equipment standard **IEC 60601-1** and FDA 21 CFR 1040.10 require Class 1 for any patient-contact visible-light emitting device.
4. **Prolonged exposure:** Telemedicine sessions may last hours; cumulative retinal exposure must stay below MPE even for worst-case viewing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "laser display" with "laser pointer." They are governed by *different* safety limits and use cases.
> 2. **Do NOT** state that laser displays use "white laser." White light is *synthesized* by mixing R, G, B; there is no native white laser diode used in displays.
> 3. **Do NOT** write "DLP" or "LCD" while describing laser displays — these are competing technologies, not the same. Mixing them up will cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **In gamut calculations, always specify the CIE 1931 reference** (or 1976 u'v') — omitting this loses the engineering context.
> 5. **In derivations, show the numerical substitution**, not just the symbolic formula. Examiners reward "complete working."
> 6. **Always include the persistence-of-vision concept** (τ ≈ 50 ms) when explaining scanned laser displays. This is a KTU-favorite 2-mark sub-question.
> 7. **For safety questions, mention both IEC 60825-1 AND IEC 60601-1** — the former classifies the laser, the latter governs the medical device.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Laser Display Definition:** Display using three coherent R, G, B lasers for image synthesis.
- **Primary Wavelengths (Mnemonic "RGB 6-5-5-3-2-4-4-5"):** R = **635 nm**, G = **532 nm**, B = **445 nm**.
- **Spectral Linewidth:** Laser < 2 nm ; LED ≈ 20–30 nm. Narrower = purer color.
- **Color Gamut Benchmark:** Laser ≈ **127–210% NTSC**; LCD ≈ **70–90% NTSC**.
- **Key Components:** Laser diodes → Collimators → **Dichroic / X-cube combiner** → **MEMS scanner** → Screen / Retina.
- **Spatial Modulation Methods:** MEMS mirror, Galvanometer, Polygon wheel, SLM.
- **Intensity Modulation Method:** **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)**, 8–12 bits.
- **Resolution Limit:** $D_{spot} = \dfrac{4 \lambda f}{\pi d}$ (Airy diffraction).
- **Beam Divergence:** $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ — small $D$ → wide divergence → must collimate.
- **MEMS Mirror:** ~1–3 mm silicon mirror, **30 kHz** fast axis, **60 Hz** slow axis.
- **Persistence of Vision:** τ ≈ **50 ms** — the biological integrator enabling scanned displays.
- **Frame Rate:** Medical laser displays operate at **60–120 Hz**; surgical HMDs require **≥ 120 Hz** to avoid VR sickness.
- **Laser Safety Class for Telemedicine Endpoints:** **Class 1** (IEC 60825-1).
- **Medical Device Standard:** **IEC 60601-1** (electrical safety); **DICOM GSDF** (display calibration).
- **Key Telemedicine Applications:** Tele-radiology, Tele-dermatology, Tele-surgery HMD, Holographic tele-presence, Telepathology.
- **Metameric Failure:** When two distinct spectral colors look identical — laser displays reduce this risk for accurate tele-diagnosis.
- **Comparison Mantra for Exams:** *Laser = wider gamut + brighter + faster + 3D-capable, but needs Class 1 safety design.*
- **Common Confusion Avoided:** Laser Display ≠ Laser Pointer ≠ Laser Surgery Tool.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Holographic Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Holographic Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Holographic Representation** refers to a sophisticated three-dimensional (3D) imaging modality that captures and reconstructs the complete wavefront of light scattered by an object, preserving both the **amplitude** and **phase** information of the optical field. Unlike conventional two-dimensional photography, which records only the intensity of light, holography encodes the entire optical signature of an object onto a two-dimensional recording medium (photographic plate, photopolymer, or digital sensor), enabling full parallax 3D visualization upon reconstruction.

In the context of **Telemedicine (PEBMT862)**, holographic representations enable remote specialists to interact with life-sized, volumetric replicas of patient anatomy, surgical sites, or diagnostic imagery, thereby bridging the spatial gap inherent in remote healthcare delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Holography is a lensless photographic technique that records the light scattered from an object and then presents it in such a way that it appears as if the object is in the same position as when it was photographed — essentially recreating a true 3D image with full depth perception and parallax.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are taking a photograph of a flower. A normal camera captures only the **brightness** (intensity) of the light bouncing off the flower, so the resulting image is flat. Now, imagine a special camera that could also remember the **direction** from which every single ray of light arrived at the lens — essentially memorizing not just *what* the flower looks like, but *where* every part of it sits in 3D space.

When you later illuminate the recorded "memory plate" with the correct type of light, the flower appears to bloom in front of you, and you can walk around it to see it from different angles. This "memory plate" is called a **hologram**, and the 3D flower that reappears is the **holographic representation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** The word "Hologram" originates from Greek — *holos* (whole) + *gramma* (message) — meaning "the whole message." This is because holography records the *complete* optical information, unlike photography which records only a *partial* message.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Standard Definition | Engineering Significance |
|------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Reference Wave** | Coherent beam (typically laser) that does not interact with the object | Provides the phase reference for reconstruction |
| **Object Wave** | Light scattered from the subject being recorded | Carries the amplitude and phase information |
| **Interference Pattern** | Fringe pattern created by superposition of reference and object waves | Stored on holographic plate as the hologram |
| **Diffraction** | Bending of light as it passes through the interference pattern | Mechanism for reconstructing the original wavefront |
| **Coherence Length** | Distance over which a light source maintains a constant phase relationship | Critical parameter; must exceed path difference |
| **Spatial Frequency** | Rate of variation of interference fringes across the plate | Determines resolution and viewing angle |
| **Parallax** | Apparent shift of object with change in viewing angle | Key 3D cue provided by holograms |
| **Reconstruction Wave** | Illumination beam used to "play back" the hologram | Must match the original reference wave geometry |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are **essential** for KTU 2024 numerical problems and theoretical questions:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Wavelength of typical He-Ne laser:** $\lambda = 632.8$ **nm**
- **Wavelength of typical red diode laser:** $\lambda = 635$ **nm**
- **Fringe spacing formula parameter:** Determines hologram resolution
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** Critical for holographic microscopy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The recording medium's resolution must be capable of resolving fringe spacings on the order of the optical wavelength. A typical holographic plate must have a resolution of **>1000 lines/mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Holography

Holography operates on two fundamental physical principles that you must understand for KTU examinations:

### 2.1.1 The Principle of Interference
When two **coherent** light waves (waves having a constant phase relationship) overlap, they produce a stable interference pattern. This pattern contains information about the **relative phase** of the two waves at every point in space.

### 2.1.2 The Principle of Diffraction
When light passes through a recorded interference pattern (the hologram), it undergoes diffraction. If the original reference beam geometry is reproduced, the diffracted light reconstructs the original object wavefront.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Holographic Process

The holographic process consists of two distinct stages:

### Stage 1: Recording (Construction)
1. A **coherent light source** (laser) is split into two beams using a beam splitter.
2. One beam (the **object beam**) illuminates the subject and is scattered toward the holographic plate.
3. The second beam (the **reference beam**) is directed to the holographic plate without touching the object.
4. The two beams interfere on the photosensitive plate, recording a complex fringe pattern.
5. After chemical or digital development, this plate becomes the **hologram**.

### Stage 2: Reconstruction (Playback)
1. The developed hologram is illuminated with a beam matching the original reference wave.
2. The interference pattern **diffracts** the reconstruction beam.
3. One diffracted order reproduces the original object wavefront.
4. An observer's eye intercepts this wavefront, perceiving a **3D image** at the original object's location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters for KTU:** Examiners often ask "Why must a laser be used?" The answer: A laser provides the **spatial and temporal coherence** required to produce a stable, high-contrast interference pattern. An ordinary light source would produce overlapping patterns that wash out the information.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application / Units |
|--------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| $I = \vert E_{obj} + E_{ref} \vert^2$ | Total intensity on plate | Recording equation; $E$ = electric field amplitude |
| $I = I_{obj} + I_{ref} + 2\sqrt{I_{obj} I_{ref}}\cos(\Delta\phi)$ | Expanded interference equation | $\Delta\phi$ = phase difference |
| $\Lambda = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin(\theta/2)}$ | Fringe spacing | $\Lambda$ = fringe period; $\theta$ = angle between beams |
| $\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{\Lambda_{min}}$ | Required plate resolution | Lines per mm |
| $d = \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | Diffraction-limited spot size | $d$ = minimum resolvable feature |
| $\beta_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{2p}$ | Maximum diffraction angle | $p$ = pixel pitch for digital holograms |
| $\Delta z = \frac{\lambda}{2(NA)^2}$ | Depth of focus | For holographic microscopy |
| $V = \frac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$ | Fringe visibility (contrast) | $V \in [0,1]$; ideal = 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Digital Holography:** The pixel pitch $p$ of the recording sensor must satisfy the **Nyquist criterion**: $p \leq \lambda / (4 \sin\theta_{max})$, where $\theta_{max}$ is the maximum angle between object and reference beams.

## 2.4 Types of Holography (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### 2.4.1 Transmission Hologram
- Illuminated by light passing **through** the hologram.
- Reconstructs a virtual image behind the plate.
- Used in medical imaging reference displays.

### 2.4.2 Reflection Hologram
- Illuminated by light reflecting **off** the hologram.
- Reconstructs a virtual image in front of the plate.
- Used in security labels and white-light viewable medical records.

### 2.4.3 Hybrid/Volume Hologram
- Records in the bulk of a thick photorefractive material.
- High wavelength selectivity.
- Used in optical data storage for medical archives.

### 2.4.4 Digital Hologram
- Recorded on CCD/CMOS sensor; reconstructed numerically.
- Enables quantitative phase imaging.
- Used in **digital holographic microscopy (DHM)** for cell analysis.

### 2.4.5 Computer-Generated Hologram (CGH)
- Synthesized numerically; no physical recording needed.
- Used in head-mounted 3D displays and surgical planning AR systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In modern telemedicine platforms, Computer-Generated Holography (CGH) combined with **Light Field Displays** enables specialists to visualize patient-specific 3D anatomical models reconstructed from CT/MRI data, allowing remote surgical planning with full depth perception — a critical advancement in **tele-surgery** and **tele-pathology**.

## 2.5 Mathematical Framework of Recording

Let us formalize the recording process. The object wave at the holographic plate can be written as:

$$U_{obj}(x,y) = A_{obj}(x,y) \cdot e^{j\phi_{obj}(x,y)}$$

The reference wave is a simple plane wave (in the simplest case):

$$U_{ref}(x,y) = A_{ref} \cdot e^{j(2\pi \xi_0 x + 2\pi \eta_0 y)}$$

where $\xi_0$ and $\eta_0$ are the spatial carrier frequencies in the $x$ and $y$ directions, respectively.

The total intensity recorded on the plate is:

$$I(x,y) = \vert U_{obj} + U_{ref} \vert^2$$

This expands to:

$$I(x,y) = \vert U_{obj} \vert^2 + \vert U_{ref} \vert^2 + U_{obj} U_{ref}^* + U_{obj}^* U_{ref}$$

The first two terms form the **zero-order** (dc) terms, while the last two are the **cross-interference terms** that carry the holographic information.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recording Intensity of a Hologram

**Objective:** Derive the complete intensity distribution recorded on a holographic plate when an object wave $U_{obj}$ interferes with a reference wave $U_{ref}$.

**Given:**
- Object wave: $U_{obj}(x,y) = A_{obj}(x,y) e^{j\phi_{obj}(x,y)}$
- Reference wave (plane wave at angle $\theta$): $U_{ref}(x,y) = A_{ref} e^{j k x \sin\theta}$
- where $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ is the wavenumber.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Write the total complex field at the plate.
The total field is the sum of the object and reference waves.

$$U_{total}(x,y) = U_{obj}(x,y) + U_{ref}(x,y)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the intensity formula.
Since the photographic plate responds to optical intensity (energy per unit area per unit time), and intensity is proportional to the squared magnitude of the complex field:

$$I(x,y) = \vert U_{total} \vert^2 = U_{total} \cdot U_{total}^*$$

**Step 3:** Expand the product.
Substituting the sum and using the distributive property of complex multiplication:

$$I(x,y) = (U_{obj} + U_{ref})(U_{obj}^* + U_{ref}^*)$$

**Step 4:** Distribute the terms explicitly.

$$I(x,y) = U_{obj} U_{obj}^* + U_{obj} U_{ref}^* + U_{ref} U_{obj}^* + U_{ref} U_{ref}^*$$

**Step 5:** Identify each term physically.
- $U_{obj} U_{obj}^* = \vert U_{obj} \vert^2 = I_{obj}$ → object intensity (autocorrelation)
- $U_{ref} U_{ref}^* = \vert U_{ref} \vert^2 = I_{ref}$ → reference intensity
- $U_{obj} U_{ref}^* + U_{ref} U_{obj}^*$ → cross-terms (interference information)

**Step 6:** Substitute magnitudes and phases.

$$I(x,y) = A_{obj}^2 + A_{ref}^2 + A_{obj} A_{ref} e^{j(\phi_{obj} - kx\sin\theta)} + A_{obj} A_{ref} e^{-j(\phi_{obj} - kx\sin\theta)}$$

**Step 7:** Apply Euler's identity: $e^{j\alpha} + e^{-j\alpha} = 2\cos(\alpha)$.

$$I(x,y) = A_{obj}^2 + A_{ref}^2 + 2 A_{obj} A_{ref} \cos(\phi_{obj} - kx\sin\theta)$$

**Step 8:** Recognize the standard interference equation.

$$\boxed{I(x,y) = I_{obj} + I_{ref} + 2\sqrt{I_{obj} I_{ref}} \cos(\Delta\phi(x,y))}$$

where $\Delta\phi(x,y) = \phi_{obj}(x,y) - kx\sin\theta$ is the local phase difference.

**Conclusion:** The recorded intensity is a **cosinusoidal modulation** of the phase difference between the two waves, with the modulation depth proportional to the geometric mean of the two intensities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The three terms in the final equation correspond to three diffracted orders during reconstruction — zero order, virtual image, and real image. This is a **frequently tested 14-mark derivation**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fringe Spacing of a Hologram

**Objective:** Determine the spatial period $\Lambda$ of the interference fringes formed on a holographic plate.

**Given:** Two plane waves intersecting at angle $\theta$ with wavelength $\lambda$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The wavefronts of the two beams intersect along lines (in 2D) or planes (in 3D). Constructive interference occurs where the path difference equals an integer multiple of $\lambda$:

$$\Delta = d \sin\theta = m\lambda, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

**Step 2:** The fringe spacing $\Lambda$ corresponds to the distance between two adjacent maxima ($m$ and $m+1$):

$$\Lambda \sin\theta = \lambda$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\Lambda$:

$$\boxed{\Lambda = \frac{\lambda}{\sin\theta}}$$

**Step 4:** For small angles (paraxial approximation, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$):

$$\Lambda \approx \frac{\lambda}{\theta}$$

**Step 5:** Half-angle correction (when angle is measured as full angle between beams, $\theta_{total} = 2\theta$):

$$\Lambda = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin(\theta_{total}/2)}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A He-Ne laser ($\lambda = 632.8$ nm) is used with a $30°$ angle between object and reference beams. Find the required plate resolution.

$$\Lambda = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \sin(15°)} = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.2588} = 1.222 \, \mu m$$

Resolution needed:

$$R = \frac{1}{\Lambda} = \frac{1}{1.222 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 818 \text{ lines/mm}$$

A standard holographic plate (e.g., **Agfa 8E75**) with resolution of **>3000 lines/mm** is required.

## 3.3 Derivation: Reconstruction of the Object Wave

**Objective:** Show how illuminating the hologram with the reference wave reconstructs the object wave.

**Given:** The developed hologram has amplitude transmittance proportional to the recorded intensity:

$$t(x,y) = t_0 + \beta \cdot I(x,y)$$

where $t_0$ is the bias transmittance and $\beta$ is the slope of the H-D curve (Hurter-Driffield characteristic).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** When the reconstruction wave $U_{ref}^{rec}$ (assumed identical to original) illuminates the hologram, the transmitted field is:

$$U_{trans} = t(x,y) \cdot U_{ref}^{rec}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the transmittance expression.

$$U_{trans} = (t_0 + \beta I) U_{ref}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the intensity expression from Section 3.1.

$$U_{trans} = (t_0 + \beta(I_{obj} + I_{ref} + U_{obj} U_{ref}^* + U_{obj}^* U_{ref})) U_{ref}$$

**Step 4:** Expand the product.

$$U_{trans} = t_0 U_{ref} + \beta I_{obj} U_{ref} + \beta I_{ref} U_{ref} + \beta \vert U_{ref} \vert^2 U_{obj} + \beta U_{ref}^2 U_{obj}^*$$

**Step 5:** Identify the physical meaning of each term:
- **Term 1:** Attenuated reconstruction wave (zero order).
- **Terms 2–3:** Distorted reconstruction wave (noise halo).
- **Term 4:** $\beta \vert U_{ref} \vert^2 U_{obj}$ — the **virtual image** term; this is a faithful reproduction of the object wave.
- **Term 5:** $\beta U_{ref}^2 U_{obj}^*$ — the **conjugate (real) image** term.

**Conclusion:** The reference wave, upon diffraction by the interference pattern, produces three output beams: the un-diffracted beam, a virtual image, and a real conjugate image. The virtual image is what the observer perceives as the reconstructed 3D object.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hologram Types vs. Application

| Hologram Type | Recording Medium | Reconstruction Light | Image Location | Telemedicine Application |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **Transmission (off-axis)** | Silver halide plate | Laser (same as reference) | Behind plate (virtual) | Reference anatomical models |
| **Reflection (Lippmann)** | Photopolymer | White light | In front of plate | Patient ID cards with 3D biometrics |
| **Digital (Fresnel)** | CCD/CMOS sensor | Numerical reconstruction | Computed volume | Quantitative cell analysis |
| **Computer-Generated (CGH)** | SLM / Printer | Coherent light | Programmable | AR surgical overlays, tele-consultation |
| **Volume (Denisyuk)** | Thick emulsion | White light at Bragg angle | Selectively reflected | Secure medical record storage |
| **Holographic Optical Element (HOE)** | Photopolymer | Tunable laser | Engineered direction | Endoscopic beam steering |

## 3.5 Quantitative Phase Imaging in DHM

In Digital Holographic Microscopy, the reconstructed complex amplitude provides both intensity and phase:

$$\phi(x,y) = \arctan\left(\frac{\text{Im}\{U_{recon}(x,y)\}}{\text{Re}\{U_{recon}(x,y)\}}\right)$$

The optical path length (OPL) is then:

$$OPL(x,y) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \phi(x,y)$$

The physical thickness of a transparent biological sample is:

$$d(x,y) = \frac{OPL(x,y)}{n_{sample} - n_{medium}}$$

where $n_{sample}$ and $n_{medium}$ are the refractive indices of the specimen and surrounding medium respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This label-free, non-invasive technique allows **remote cell morphology analysis** — a hematologist in one city can examine a blood smear from a rural clinic in real-time 3D, with nanometer-scale axial sensitivity, without staining or destroying the sample.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Holographic Recording (Construction) Stage

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the optical architecture of a typical off-axis holographic recording setup used in medical imaging.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Source] --> B[Beam Splitter]
    B --> C[Reference Beam Path]
    B --> D[Object Beam Path]
    D --> E[Spatial Filter and Collimator]
    E --> F[Patient Specimen or Anatomical Model]
    F --> G[Scattered Object Wave]
    C --> H[Mirror and Beam Expander]
    H --> I[Reference Wave at Plate]
    G --> J[Holographic Recording Plate]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Chemical or Digital Development]
    K --> L[Final Hologram Containing Interference Pattern]
```

**Operational Flow Explanation:**
1. The **Laser Source** emits a coherent monochromatic beam.
2. The **Beam Splitter** divides it into two paths.
3. The **Reference Beam Path** travels directly (after expansion) to the recording plate.
4. The **Object Beam Path** illuminates the patient specimen and is scattered toward the plate.
5. The two beams interfere on the plate, recording the complete wavefront information.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Holographic Reconstruction (Playback) Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reconstruction Laser] --> B[Beam Conditioner]
    B --> C[Illumination of Developed Hologram]
    C --> D{Three Diffracted Orders Emerge}
    D --> E[Zero Order - Direct Beam]
    D --> F[First Order - Virtual Image]
    D --> G[Second Order - Real Conjugate Image]
    F --> H[Observer Perceives 3D Object at Original Location]
    E --> I[Blocked by Spatial Filter]
    G --> J[Projects Real Image onto Screen]
    J --> K[Used for Telemedicine Display]
```

## 4.3 Telemedicine Holographic Consultation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PatientSite["Patient Site (Rural Clinic)"]
        P1[3D Medical Scanner - CT MRI Ultrasound]
        P2[Holographic Data Acquisition Unit]
        P3[Encryption and Compression Module]
    end
    subgraph Network["Secure Communication Network"]
        N1[High Bandwidth Link - 5G or Fiber]
        N2[Latency Compensation Buffer]
    end
    subgraph SpecialistSite["Specialist Site (Tertiary Hospital)"]
        S1[Data Decompression Unit]
        S2[Computer Generated Hologram CGH Renderer]
        S3[Spatial Light Modulator SLM]
        S4[Holographic Display Unit]
        S5[Specialist Interaction Interface]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> N1 --> N2 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 -.->|Feedback Annotations| N2
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- **Patient Site:** Acquires volumetric data using 3D scanners, processes it into holographic data, encrypts and transmits.
- **Network:** High-bandwidth, low-latency link with buffering to handle real-time display constraints.
- **Specialist Site:** Reconstructs the hologram using CGH techniques, displays it on a holographic or light-field display, and allows the specialist to interact (rotate, slice, annotate).

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Holographic Modality for Telemedicine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Application Requirement] --> B{Real Time Interaction Needed}
    B -->|Yes| C{Network Bandwidth Available}
    B -->|No| D[Pre Recorded Holographic Archival]
    C -->|High 5G Fiber| E[Live Digital Holography DHM]
    C -->|Limited| F[Pre Processed CGH Transmission]
    C -->|Low| G[Static Reflection Hologram]
    E --> H[Quantitative Phase Imaging]
    F --> I[3D Anatomical Model Consultation]
    G --> J[Patient Education and Reference]
    H --> K[Remote Pathology Diagnosis]
    I --> L[Pre Surgical Planning]
    J --> M[Visualization Aid for Informed Consent]
```

## 4.5 Functional Pipeline: From Raw Patient Data to Holographic Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Medical Imaging Data] --> B[DICOM Standardization]
    B --> C[3D Volumetric Reconstruction]
    C --> D[Segmentation of Anatomical Structures]
    D --> E[Polygon Mesh Generation]
    E --> F[Light Field or Hologram Calculation]
    F --> G{Stereo Pair or Full Holo}
    G -->|Stereo Pair| H[Stereoscopic 3D Display]
    G -->|Full Holo| I[SLM Based True Holographic Display]
    H --> J[Specialist Visualization]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Clinical Decision and Annotation]
    K --> L[Encrypted Return to Patient Site]
```
