[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Telemedicine Module 2] **Define a Virtual Screen Device. List three optical techniques used in its construction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Virtual Screen Device (VSD) is an optoelectronic system that produces a perceivable image at a focal distance different from the device's physical surface, enabling the user to view digital information without a conventional physical screen. *(1 mark)* Three optical techniques used in its construction: 1. **Refractive optics** — using convex lenses (e.g., eyepiece-based HMDs). *(1 mark)* 2. **Reflective optics** — using curved partially-silvered mirrors or beam combiners (e.g., HUDs). *(½ mark)* 3. **Diffractive optics / Holographic Optical Elements (HOEs)** — using gratings recorded in photopolymer (e.g., HoloLens 2 waveguides). *(½ mark)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Telemedicine Module 2] **What is the Vergence-Accommodation Conflict (VAC), and why is it a concern in telemedicine VR sessions?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Vergence-Accommodation Conflict is the optical mismatch between the **vergence demand** (the angle at which the two eyes converge to fuse an image at its perceived depth) and the **accommodation demand** (the focus the eye's lens must apply to keep that image sharp). *(1½ marks)* In a VR headset, the eyes verge as if the image is at, say, 1.5 m away, but must accommodate at the physical screen distance (~35 mm), creating a conflict of up to 27 D. *(1 mark)* In telemedicine, prolonged VR consultations (e.g., telerehabilitation, mental-health VR therapy) can cause **eye strain, headache, and nausea**, reducing patient compliance and clinician comfort. Hence, modern devices use **varifocal** or **light-field** optics to mitigate VAC. *(½ mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Telemedicine Module 2] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a Head-Mounted Display (HMD) used in telemedicine. Differentiate between **see-through (AR)** and **immersive (VR)** HMDs. **(b) [7 marks]** A micro-OLED display used in a surgeon's AR HMD has a diagonal of 0.5 inches and is paired with an eyepiece of focal length 18 mm. Calculate (i) the diagonal Field of View, and (ii) the horizontal FOV if the aspect ratio is 16:9. Comment on whether this FOV is suitable for intra-operative AR overlay. ### Model Solution #### (a) Working Principle and AR vs. VR HMDs — 7 marks **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Source Video] → [Decoder] → [Microdisplay] ↘ [IMU Sensors] → [Pose Processor] → [Display Driver] ↓ [Collimating Optics] ↓ [Beam Combiner] ↙ ↘ [Real World] [User's Eye] ``` **Working Principle (2 marks):** - A microdisplay (OLED/MicroLED) generates the image. - A collimating lens renders the image at optical infinity. - A semi-transparent combiner reflects the virtual image into the eye **while** transmitting the real-world view. - IMU sensors (gyroscope, accelerometer) track head pose at ≥ 90 Hz; the pose processor updates the rendered image to maintain spatial stability. **AR vs. VR HMD Differences (2 marks):** | Parameter | See-Through AR HMD | Immersive VR HMD | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Optical combiner | Translucent mirror / waveguide | Opaque display panel | | Real-world visibility | Yes | No | | Typical FOV | 30° – 52° | 90° – 210° | | Telemedicine use | Surgical overlay, vein-finder | Therapy, simulation | | Latency tolerance | < 20 ms (surgical) | < 20 ms (comfort) | #### (b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks **Given:** - Diagonal: $d = 0.5 \text{ in} = 0.5 \times 0.0254 = 0.0127 \text{ m}$ - Focal length: $f = 18 \text{ mm} = 0.018 \text{ m}$ - Aspect ratio: 16:9 **(i) Diagonal FOV (3 marks):** $$ \tan\!\left(\frac{\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}}}{2}\right) = \frac{d/2}{f} = \frac{0.00635}{0.018} = 0.3528 $$ $$ \frac{\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}}}{2} = \arctan(0.3528) = 19.43^{\circ} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{FOV}_{\text{diag}} = 38.86^{\circ} \approx 38.9^{\circ}} $$ **(ii) Horizontal FOV (3 marks):** For a 16:9 aspect ratio, the horizontal extent $w$ and vertical extent $h$ relate to the diagonal $d$ as: $$ w = d \times \frac{16}{\sqrt{16^2 + 9^2}} = d \times \frac{16}{18.36} $$ $$ w = 0.0127 \times 0.8714 = 0.01107 \text{ m} $$ Now: $$ \tan\!\left(\frac{\text{FOV}_{H}}{2}\right) = \frac{w/2}{f} = \frac{0.005535}{0.018} = 0.3075 $$ $$ \frac{\text{FOV}_{H}}{2} = \arctan(0.3075) = 17.08^{\circ} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{FOV}_{H} = 34.16^{\circ} \approx 34.2^{\circ}} $$ **Comment on suitability (1 mark):** A horizontal FOV of **34.2°** is **acceptable for procedural AR overlay** (e.g., HoloLens 2 reports ~43° horizontal). It is **not sufficient for fully immersive VR surgery rehearsal** (which requires 90°+ horizontal FOV), but it is **well-suited to the typical 30°–50° FOV range used in intra-operative AR HMDs** such as the Microsoft HoloLens 2 or Magic Leap 2. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) ### Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Telemedicine Module 2] **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of a **Virtual Retinal Display (VRD)**. What are its advantages over conventional microdisplay-based HMDs in telemedicine applications? **(b) [7 marks]** A telemedicine VRD uses a red laser (λ = 650 nm) scanned by a MEMS mirror. The eye's effective focal length is 17 mm and the entrance pupil diameter during bright ambient is 2 mm. Calculate the diffraction-limited retinal spot diameter. If the field of view is 30°, how many resolvable "pixels" can the VRD produce, assuming the spot size equals one pixel? Comment on clinical relevance. ### Model Solution #### (a) Virtual Retinal Display (VRD) — 7 marks **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Video Source / Patient Data] → [Laser Modulator] ↓ [Red / Green / Blue Lasers] ↓ [Beam Combiner] ↓ [MEMS Mirror X-Y Scanner] ↓ [Pupil Relay Optics] ↓ [Human Retina — Direct Scanning] ``` **Working Principle (2 marks):** - Three low-power lasers (R, G, B) are intensity-modulated by the video signal. - A 2-axis MEMS mirror raster-scans the combined beam across the retina. - The eye's lens focuses the beam; the perceived image is formed by **scanning photons directly onto photoreceptors**, not by viewing a display surface. **Advantages over microdisplay HMDs in telemedicine (2 marks):** 1. **Always-in-focus image:** Light enters the pupil as a collimated/raster-scanned beam; depth of focus is not limited by a physical screen. Critical for reading X-rays at varying head tilts. 2. **Very high brightness & sunlight readability:** Required for outdoor/mass-casualty triage. 3. **Lower power consumption:** No backlight; efficient for battery-driven rural devices. 4. **No screen-door effect:** Pixels are formed by scanning, not by a fixed panel, eliminating visible pixel grid during long sessions. #### (b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks **Given:** - $\lambda = 650 \text{ nm} = 650 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ - $f = 17 \text{ mm} = 0.017 \text{ m}$ - $D = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$ - $\text{FOV} = 30^{\circ}$ **(i) Diffraction-limited spot diameter (3 marks):** $$ A_r = \frac{2.44 \cdot \lambda \cdot f}{D} $$ $$ A_r = \frac{2.44 \times 650 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.017}{0.002} $$ Numerator: $$ 2.44 \times 650 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.017 = 2.44 \times 650 \times 17 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ = 2.44 \times 11050 \times 10^{-12} = 26962 \times 10^{-12} = 2.696 \times 10^{-8} $$ Divide: $$ A_r = \frac{2.696 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.348 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 13.48 \text{ μm} $$ $$ \boxed{A_r \approx 13.5 \text{ μm}} $$ **(ii) Number of resolvable pixels (2 marks):** Convert FOV to mm on the retina. The focal length effectively maps angle to retinal arc length: $$ \text{Retinal length across FOV} = 2 \times f \times \tan\!\left(\frac{30°}{2}\right) $$ $$ = 2 \times 17 \text{ mm} \times \tan(15°) $$ $$ = 2 \times 17 \times 0.2679 = 9.11 \text{ mm} $$ Number of pixel spots across this length: $$ N = \frac{9.11 \text{ mm}}{13.48 \text{ μm}} = \frac{9110 \text{ μm}}{13.48 \text{ μm}} \approx 675 $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 675 \text{ pixels across the FOV}} $$ **(iii) Clinical relevance comment (2 marks):** - ~675 resolvable spots is **insufficient for diagnostic radiology** (which needs > 1500 across the FOV). - It is **adequate for procedural AR guidance** (e.g., surgical checklists, ECG display, anatomy labels). - The system must increase the **entrance pupil D** (e.g., to 3 mm or use a larger beam) to **reduce** the diffraction spot and increase pixel count. - Alternatively, **multi-pass scanning** or **higher-frequency MEMS** can increase the effective resolution. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Virtual Screen Devices** > - **Do not confuse FOV with magnification.** FOV is an angle (in degrees), magnification is a ratio. Examiners give **0 marks** if units are swapped. > - **Always convert inches to meters** for microdisplay diagonals. A common error is computing FOV with mixed units, giving wildly wrong answers. > - **VAC formula expects diopters (m⁻¹)**, not meters. Converting both vergence and accommodation distances to diopters is a **mandatory 1-mark step**. > - For AR HMDs, the optical combiner is a **key 1-mark keyword**. Forgetting it costs 1 mark. > - The Diffraction-limited spot formula constant is **2.44** (Airy). Some textbooks use **1.22** for radius; **state clearly** that 2.44 gives the **diameter** to avoid losing half a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Virtual Screen Devices (KTU Module 2)** - **Definition:** VSD = display that creates a perceived image at a focal distance different from the device's physical surface, using optical, retinal, or holographic principles. - **Core types:** HMD, Smart Glasses, HUD, VRD, HOE-based waveguide. - **Key optical techniques:** Refractive (lens), Reflective (mirror/combiner), Diffractive (HOE/waveguide), Retinal (laser scanning). - **Critical metrics:** FOV, PPD, Eye Relief, Exit Pupil, Refresh Rate (≥ 60 Hz), Latency (< 20 ms), Luminance (≥ 500 nits for OR). - **FOV formula:** $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ — must convert units carefully. - **PPD formula:** $\text{PPD} = \frac{\text{horizontal pixels}}{\text{FOV}}$; diagnostic radiology needs ≥ 30 PPD. - **Vergence-Accommodation Conflict:** $\Delta = \left\vert P_{\text{ver}} - P_{\text{acc}} \right\vert$ in diopters; mitigations include varifocal and light-field displays. - **Retinal spot size (Airy):** $A_r = \frac{2.44 \lambda f}{D}$ — 2.44 gives diameter. - **Telemedicine advantages:** hands-free, mobile, low cognitive load, supports rural & field medicine. - **AR vs. VR:** AR = see-through combiner + small FOV + surgical overlay; VR = opaque display + wide FOV + therapy/simulation. - **Latency budget:** total motion-to-photon must be < 20 ms for clinical safety. - **KTU-favourite exam topics:** FOV numericals, PPD analysis, VAC numerical, retinal spot calculation, AR/VR comparison, HMD block diagram. - **Pitfalls:** Mixing units, swapping FOV and magnification, forgetting the 2.44 constant, using `|` in LaTeX. - **Emerging tech to mention:** Holographic retinal projection, contact-lens displays (Mojo Lens), light-field HMDs, AI-driven gaze-tracked AR.

topics.title: Computation of Storage Systems: Computer Speed-Up Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computation of Storage Systems: Computer Speed-Up Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computation of Storage Systems

### Formal Definition
**Storage Systems** in the context of telemedicine refer to the hierarchical organization of computer memory and data retention devices used to store, retrieve, and manage digital medical data — including patient Electronic Health Records (EHR), DICOM medical images, video consultations, and real-time biosignal streams. The *computation* of storage systems involves the quantitative estimation of **storage capacity**, **access time**, **transfer bandwidth**, and **redundancy overhead** required to support a given telemedicine workload.

### Storage Hierarchy (Top → Bottom)
1. **CPU Registers** — fastest, smallest (bytes)
2. **Cache Memory (L1/L2/L3)** — nanosecond access
3. **Main Memory (RAM)** — volatile, microsecond access
4. **Solid-State Drives (SSD)** — non-volatile, fast random access
5. **Hard Disk Drives (HDD)** — bulk secondary storage
6. **Network-Attached Storage (NAS) / Storage Area Network (SAN)** — enterprise PACS storage
7. **Cloud / Cold Archives** — long-term retention (years)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In telemedicine, **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)** typically occupies tiers 4–7, where a single hospital may generate **terabytes of DICOM data per year** requiring rigorous storage capacity planning.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a hospital as a **massive library**:
- The **reading desk** (RAM/Cache) is where the doctor actively examines a patient's X-ray — instant access, but limited space.
- The **stacks nearby** (SSD/HDD) hold the current month's patient files — quick retrieval.
- The **central archive building in another city** (Cloud/NAS) stores decades of records — slow to fetch, but virtually unlimited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Storage Metrics in Telemedicine:**
> - **Capacity** measured in bytes (KB, MB, GB, TB, PB)
> - **Access Time** in milliseconds (ms)
> - **Bandwidth / Throughput** in MB/s or Gbps
> - **DICOM Standard** is the global benchmark for medical imaging storage.

---

## 1.2 Computer Speed-Up Systems

### Formal Definition
A **Computer Speed-Up System** is any architectural, algorithmic, or hardware-based mechanism that reduces the execution time of a computational task by employing **parallelism, pipelining, caching, or hardware acceleration**. In telemedicine, speed-up systems are essential for real-time image reconstruction, AI-based diagnostics, and high-throughput teleconsultation platforms.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine **100 patients** waiting for a single doctor (serial processing) vs. **10 doctors each handling 10 patients in parallel** (parallel processing). The total time collapses dramatically — this is **speed-up**.

Speed-up $S$ is defined as:

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$$

where $T_{\text{serial}}$ is execution time on a single processor and $T_{\text{parallel}}$ is execution time on $N$ parallel processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Speed-Up Metrics:**
> - **Speed-up Factor (S)** — ratio of serial to parallel time
> - **Efficiency (E)** — speed-up per processor, $E = S/N$
> - **Throughput** — tasks completed per unit time
> - **Latency** — time to complete one task end-to-end
> - **Amdahl's Law** — theoretical maximum speed-up bound
> - **Gustafson's Law** — scaled speed-up for larger problems

### Classification of Speed-Up Techniques
| Technique | Example in Telemedicine |
|---|---|
| **Pipelining** | Streaming ECG signal processing |
| **Multiprocessing (SMP/MMP)** | Distributed PACS servers |
| **GPU Acceleration** | AI tumor detection in MRI |
| **Caching** | Frequently accessed patient records |
| **Load Balancing** | Teleconsultation server farms |
| **Data Compression** | DICOM image transfer optimization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Storage Computation

### 2.1.1 Storage Capacity Estimation for Medical Data

For a telemedicine system, total storage $C_{\text{total}}$ is computed as:

$$C_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{patients}} \times F_{\text{studies}} \times S_{\text{study}} \times (1 + R_{\text{redundancy}})$$

where:
- $N_{\text{patients}}$ = number of patients served
- $F_{\text{studies}}$ = average imaging studies per patient per year
- $S_{\text{study}}$ = average size per study (MB)
- $R_{\text{redundancy}}$ = RAID / backup overhead ratio (typically 0.25 for RAID-5, 1.0 for full mirror)

### 2.1.2 Medical Image Size Computation
A single DICOM image size depends on **matrix dimensions** and **bit depth**:

$$S_{\text{image}} = M \times N \times \frac{B}{8} \text{ bytes}$$

where $M \times N$ = pixel matrix (e.g., 512×512) and $B$ = bit depth (e.g., 16 bits for CT).

For a **multi-slice CT study** with $K$ slices:

$$S_{\text{study}} = K \times M \times N \times \frac{B}{8}$$

### 2.1.3 Transfer Time Estimation

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{S_{\text{data}}}{B_{\text{bandwidth}}}$$

where $B_{\text{bandwidth}}$ is the available network throughput in MB/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAID Redundancy Overhead:**
> - **RAID 0** → $R = 0$ (no redundancy)
> - **RAID 1** → $R = 1.0$ (100% mirror)
> - **RAID 5** → $R = 0.25$ (1 parity disk per 4 data)
> - **RAID 6** → $R = 0.5$ (2 parity disks per 4 data)

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Computer Speed-Up

### 2.2.1 Amdahl's Law (Fixed Workload Speed-Up)

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$

where:
- $p$ = fraction of program that is parallelizable (0 ≤ p ≤ 1)
- $N$ = number of processors
- $(1 - p)$ = sequential fraction (irreducible)

As $N \to \infty$:

$$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

### 2.2.2 Gustafson's Law (Scaled Workload Speed-Up)

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}} = N - (1 - p)(N - 1)$$

This reflects **weak scaling** — the problem size grows with the number of processors (e.g., processing more patient records on a larger cluster).

### 2.2.3 Efficiency Metric

$$E = \frac{S}{N}$$

For an ideal linear speed-up, $E = 1$. Realistic systems have $E < 1$ due to synchronization, communication, and load imbalance.

### 2.2.4 Pipeline Speed-Up

For a pipeline of $k$ stages processing $n$ tasks:

$$S_{\text{pipeline}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)} \approx k \text{ for large } n$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{total}} = N \cdot F \cdot S \cdot (1 + R)$ | Total storage capacity | Bytes / TB |
| 2 | $S_{\text{image}} = M \cdot N \cdot B/8$ | Single DICOM image size | Bytes |
| 3 | $S_{\text{study}} = K \cdot M \cdot N \cdot B/8$ | Multi-slice study size | Bytes |
| 4 | $T_{\text{transfer}} = S_{\text{data}} / B$ | Transfer time | Seconds |
| 5 | $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = 1 / \left[(1-p) + p/N\right]$ | Amdahl speed-up | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{\text{max}} = 1 / (1-p)$ | Max Amdahl limit | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = N - (1-p)(N-1)$ | Gustafson speed-up | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $E = S / N$ | Parallel efficiency | $0 < E \leq 1$ |
| 9 | $S_{\text{pipeline}} = nk / [k + (n-1)]$ | Pipeline speed-up | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput} = 1 / T_{\text{stage}}$ | Pipeline throughput | Tasks/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Amdahl's Law is used in telemedicine to **justify or reject** investments in GPU clusters for AI radiology. If only 40% of a diagnostic pipeline is parallelizable ($p = 0.4$), even infinite GPUs give a maximum speed-up of $1/0.6 \approx 1.67\times$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law from First Principles

**Step 1 — Decompose execution time:**
Let the total serial execution time be $T = 1$ (normalized). The execution splits into a **parallel portion** of fraction $p$ and a **sequential portion** of fraction $(1 - p)$.

$$T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - p) \cdot T + p \cdot T = T$$

**Step 2 — Apply parallelization:**
On $N$ processors, the parallel fraction is divided by $N$, while the sequential fraction remains unchanged.

$$T_{\text{parallel}} = (1 - p) \cdot T + \frac{p \cdot T}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute speed-up ratio:**

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{(1 - p) \cdot T + p \cdot T}{(1 - p) \cdot T + \frac{p \cdot T}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $T$ (normalized to 1):**

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$

This is the **canonical Amdahl's Law** expression.

**Step 5 — Limit as $N \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

Hence the **theoretical ceiling** is governed entirely by the sequential fraction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — PACS Storage Capacity

**Problem:** A district telemedicine hub serves **50,000 patients/year**. Each patient undergoes on average **3 imaging studies** (X-ray, CT, ultrasound). Average study size = **150 MB**. Compute total annual raw storage and the effective capacity needed with **RAID 5** redundancy.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $N_{\text{patients}} = 50{,}000$
- $F_{\text{studies}} = 3$
- $S_{\text{study}} = 150$ MB
- $R_{\text{RAID5}} = 0.25$

**Step 1 — Raw capacity:**

$$C_{\text{raw}} = 50{,}000 \times 3 \times 150 \text{ MB}$$

$$C_{\text{raw}} = 22{,}500{,}000 \text{ MB} = 22.5 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply RAID-5 overhead:**

$$C_{\text{effective}} = 22.5 \text{ TB} \times (1 + 0.25)$$

$$C_{\text{effective}} = 22.5 \times 1.25 = 28.125 \text{ TB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world adjustment:** Add ~30% for metadata, DICOM headers, and database indexes → recommended capacity $\approx$ **37 TB/year**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Amdahl's Law in AI Radiology

**Problem:** An AI-based lung nodule detection pipeline has **75% parallelizable** work (CNN inference on GPU). The remaining **25%** is sequential (data loading, post-processing, I/O). Compute speed-up using **8 GPUs** and the theoretical maximum.

**Solution:**

Given: $p = 0.75$, $N = 8$, $(1 - p) = 0.25$.

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law with $N = 8$:**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.25 + \frac{0.75}{8}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.25 + 0.09375 = 0.34375$$

**Step 3 — Final speed-up:**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.91$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical maximum as $N \to \infty$:**

$$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**

$$E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{2.91}{8} \approx 0.364 \text{ or } 36.4\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Adding more than ~8 GPUs yields diminishing returns. The sequential I/O bottleneck caps the system at 4×. Optimizing data loading is the real engineering priority.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Pipeline Speed-Up for ECG Processing

**Problem:** An ECG telemedicine system processes incoming samples through 4 pipeline stages: **Acquire → Filter → Detect R-peak → Classify**. Each stage takes 10 ms. Compute speed-up over serial processing for **100 beats**.

**Solution:**

Given: $k = 4$ stages, $T_{\text{stage}} = 10$ ms, $n = 100$ beats.

**Step 1 — Serial time:**

$$T_{\text{serial}} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{stage}} = 100 \times 4 \times 10 = 4000 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined time:**

$$T_{\text{pipeline}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{\text{stage}} = (4 + 99) \times 10 = 1030 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Speed-up factor:**

$$S = \frac{4000}{1030} \approx 3.88$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state throughput:**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{stage}}} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100 \text{ beats/sec}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law | Pipeline Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Workload** | Fixed | Scaled with $N$ | Stream of identical tasks |
| **Best for** | Hardware-bound | Big-data growth | Streaming data |
| **Telemedicine use** | AI inference scaling | Cloud PACS expansion | ECG / vitals processing |
| **Limit** | $1/(1-p)$ | Linear $N$ | $k$ (number of stages) |
| **Efficiency** | Decreases with $N$ | Stays constant | Approaches 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Telemedicine Storage Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CPU Registers and Cache L1 L2 L3] --> B[Main Memory RAM]
    B --> C[Local SSD Storage]
    C --> D[Hospital PACS Server NAS SAN]
    D --> E[Regional Telemedicine Cloud]
    E --> F[National EHR Archive Cold Storage]
    F --> G[Disaster Recovery Offsite Backup]
    D --> H[Backup and RAID Redundancy Layer]
    H --> I[Failover and Mirroring System]
    B --> J[Real Time ECG Vitals Stream Processor]
    J --> K[GPU Cluster for AI Inference]
    K --> L[Diagnostic Output to Clinician]
```

## 4.2 Computer Speed-Up Architecture for AI Telemedicine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient Data Input] --> Q1[Task Scheduler and Load Balancer]
    Q1 --> R1[Sequential Stage Data Loading I/O]
    R1 --> S1[Parallel Stage CNN Inference on GPU 1]
    Q1 --> S2[GPU 2 Inference Engine]
    Q1 --> S3[GPU 3 Inference Engine]
    Q1 --> S4[GPU 4 Inference Engine]
    S1 --> T1[Result Aggregator]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1
    R1 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[Post Processing Sequential Stage]
    U1 --> V1[Diagnostic Report Output]
    T1 -.feedback.-> Q1
```

## 4.3 Speed-Up vs Number of Processors (Amdahl Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Amdahl Curve Generator] --> B[Sequential Fraction equals 1 minus p]
    A --> C[Processor Count N]
    B --> D[Compute S equals 1 over 1 minus p plus p over N]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Plot Speed Up vs Processors]
    E --> F[Identify Saturation Point]
    F --> G[Optimal N for Telemedicine Budget]
```

## 4.4 Storage Data Flow in a PACS System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Modality CT MRI X Ray] --> N[DICOM Acquisition Gateway]
    N --> O[Compression Engine JPEG2000 Lossless]
    O --> P[Storage Controller with RAID]
    P --> Q[Short Term SSD Tier]
    P --> R[Long Term HDD Tier]
    P --> S[Cloud Archive Tier]
    Q --> T[Radiologist Workstation Fast Retrieval]
    R --> T
    S --> U[Disaster Recovery Site]
    T --> V[Diagnostic Reading and Reporting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four Mermaid diagrams above capture the **functional interactions** of storage hierarchy, AI speed-up, Amdahl analysis, and PACS data flow — the four pillars of computation in a telemedicine system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define storage hierarchy in the context of a telemedicine system. List any four levels with their typical access times.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Storage hierarchy is the layered organization of computer memory based on speed, cost, and capacity. The four levels are:
1. **CPU Registers** — access time < 1 ns
2. **Cache Memory** — 1–10 ns
3. **Main Memory (RAM)** — 50–100 ns
4. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD/PACS)** — 0.1–10 ms

In telemedicine, this hierarchy ensures that active patient data and DICOM images are served from the fastest tiers, while historical records reside in archival storage.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for levels, 1 mark for access times / telemedicine context.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum speed-up achievable if 90% of a telemedicine diagnostic algorithm is parallelizable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$

Given $p = 0.9$, as $N \to \infty$:

$$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$

The theoretical maximum speed-up is **10×**, regardless of how many processors are added.

*(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive Amdahl's Law for parallel speed-up. A telemedicine AI diagnostic system has a sequential fraction of 0.2. Compute the speed-up when using (i) 4 processors and (ii) 16 processors. Comment on the practical limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Gustafson's Law and compare it with Amdahl's Law. Why is Gustafson's Law more appropriate for scaling cloud-based PACS storage in telemedicine? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $T$ be the total serial time. Split into sequential $(1 - p)T$ and parallel $pT$. On $N$ processors:

$$T_{\text{parallel}} = (1 - p)T + \frac{pT}{N}$$

$$S = \frac{T}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting $p = 0.8$, $N = 4$ (2 marks):**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $N = 16$ (1 mark):**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Practical limit (1 mark):**
As $N \to \infty$, $S_{\text{max}} = 1 / 0.2 = 5$. The sequential 20% bottleneck caps speed-up at 5×.

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Gustafson's Law (3 marks):**
$$S_{\text{Gustafson}} = N - (1 - p)(N - 1)$$

This models **weak scaling** — the workload grows proportionally with $N$. For $p = 0.8$, $N = 16$:

$$S = 16 - 0.2 \times 15 = 16 - 3 = 13$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Amdahl | Gustafson |
|---|---|---|
| Workload | Fixed | Scaled |
| Speed-up bound | $1/(1-p)$ | Linear in $N$ |
| Suitable for | Algorithm-bound | Data-bound |

**Why Gustafson suits PACS (2 marks):**
As a hospital network expands, the **patient data volume grows** with the number of servers. Gustafson's model captures this reality — more servers handle more patients, not the same patients faster.

---

### Question B (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A telemedicine center generates DICOM images of size **512 × 512 × 16 bits**. A CT study contains **250 slices**. Compute (i) image size, (ii) study size, and (iii) annual storage for **10,000 patients** each with **2 studies**. Assume RAID-5 overhead. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the storage hierarchy of a PACS system used in telemedicine. Discuss the role of caching in improving access speed. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $M = N = 512$, $B = 16$ bits, $K = 250$ slices, $N_{\text{patients}} = 10{,}000$, $F = 2$, RAID-5 ($R = 0.25$).

**(i) Image size (2 marks):**

$$S_{\text{image}} = 512 \times 512 \times \frac{16}{8} = 512 \times 512 \times 2 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 512 \text{ KB}$$

**(ii) Study size (2 marks):**

$$S_{\text{study}} = 250 \times 512 \text{ KB} = 128{,}000 \text{ KB} = 125 \text{ MB}$$

**(iii) Annual capacity (3 marks):**

$$C_{\text{raw}} = 10{,}000 \times 2 \times 125 \text{ MB} = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ MB} \approx 2.5 \text{ TB}$$

$$C_{\text{effective}} = 2.5 \times 1.25 = 3.125 \text{ TB}$$

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
Refer to the **PACS Data Flow diagram in Section 4.4**. The hierarchy flows from modality → DICOM gateway → compression → RAID storage → SSD/HDD/Cloud tiers → radiologist workstation.

**Role of caching (2 marks):**
- Frequently accessed current studies are kept in **SSD cache** for sub-millisecond retrieval.
- **Read-ahead caching** prefetches adjacent slices during scrolling.
- **Write-back caching** accelerates study archiving from modalities.

**Quantitative benefit (2 marks):**
If cache hit rate = 80% and cache access time = 0.1 ms vs disk = 10 ms, average access time:

$$T_{\text{avg}} = 0.8 \times 0.1 + 0.2 \times 10 = 0.08 + 2.0 = 2.08 \text{ ms}$$

Speed-up over disk-only = $10 / 2.08 \approx 4.8\times$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors in storage problems** — confusing MB with MiB, or forgetting to convert bits to bytes (divide by 8). KTU examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 2. **Amdahl vs Gustafson mix-up** — using Amdahl's formula when the problem implies scaled workload. Read carefully whether the **problem size is fixed or growing**.
> 3. **Forgetting the sequential fraction** in Amdahl's Law — writing $S = N/p$ instead of the correct denominator.
> 4. **RAID overhead** — many students compute raw capacity and forget to apply the redundancy factor, losing 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Pipelining speed-up** — using the asymptotic formula $S = k$ without checking that $n$ is sufficiently large.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Storage hierarchy** in telemedicine progresses from **registers → cache → RAM → SSD → HDD/PACS → cloud archive**, with access times spanning **nanoseconds to seconds**.
- **DICOM image size** = $M \times N \times B/8$ bytes; a typical CT study is **100–150 MB**.
- **Annual PACS storage** $= N_{\text{patients}} \times F_{\text{studies}} \times S_{\text{study}} \times (1 + R_{\text{RAID}})$.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1/[(1-p) + p/N]$ — caps speed-up at $1/(1-p)$.
- **Gustafson's Law**: $S = N - (1-p)(N-1)$ — allows linear scaling with growing data.
- **Parallel efficiency** $E = S/N$; ideal value is 1.
- **Pipeline speed-up** approaches the **number of stages** for long streams.
- **RAID overheads**: RAID 0 → 0, RAID 1 → 100%, RAID 5 → 25%, RAID 6 → 50%.
- **Caching** reduces average access time dramatically (Amdahl-like effect on I/O).
- **Telemedicine-specific applications**: AI radiology (GPU clusters), PACS archival (RAID + cloud), real-time ECG (pipelining), large-scale EHR analytics (Gustafson scaling).
- **Always convert bits to bytes** by dividing by 8 in storage calculations.
- **Always check whether a problem is fixed-workload (Amdahl) or scaled-workload (Gustafson)** before applying formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEBMT862 – Telemedicine (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the term **"Magnetic"** refers to the study of **bioelectromagnetic phenomena**, the magnetic properties of biological tissues, and the role of magnetic principles in biomedical signal acquisition, transmission, and remote diagnostics. This sub-topic establishes the physical foundation for understanding how magnetic fields interact with the human body, how biomagnetic signals are generated, and how magnetic resonance and magnetic sensing technologies enable long-distance clinical interpretation — the very heart of telemedicine practice.

Formally, **biomagnetism** is the discipline concerned with the magnetic fields produced by living organisms (and by the bioelectric currents flowing through neural, cardiac, and muscular tissues), while **bioelectromagnetism** is the broader science describing the interaction of electromagnetic fields with biological systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 – Magnetic):**
> *"The study of magnetic fields in biological systems, including biomagnetic signal generation, magnetic properties of tissues, electromagnetic interaction with the body, and the application of magnetic principles in diagnostic and therapeutic devices used in telemedicine."*

The governing physical quantities that recur throughout this module are:

- **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** — measured in **Tesla (T)**, with $1 \text{ T} = 10^4 \text{ Gauss}$.
- **Magnetic Field Strength (H)** — measured in **Ampere per metre (A/m)**.
- **Magnetic Permeability ($\mu$)** — measured in **Henry per metre (H/m)**, with free-space value $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$.
- **Magnetic Susceptibility ($\chi_m$)** — dimensionless, indicating how a material responds to an applied field.
- **Magnetic Flux ($\Phi$)** — measured in **Weber (Wb)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Earth's magnetic field is approximately **$50 \, \mu T$**, while a clinical MRI scanner typically operates at **$1.5 \text{ T}$ to $7 \text{ T}$** — a difference of roughly **$10^5$** in magnitude. This scale jump is critical when discussing safety in telementored MRI-guided procedures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body as an extremely complex **electrical circuit board**. Every heartbeat, every thought, every muscle twitch is a tiny **electric current** flowing through biological tissue. By the right-hand rule of physics (Ampère's Law), **wherever current flows, a magnetic field is created around it** — just as a wire carrying current deflects a compass needle nearby.

So your heart is essentially a **weak electromagnet**; your brain is an even **weaker one**; and your skeletal muscles generate **local magnetic dipoles** when they contract. The body's intrinsic magnetic signals are extraordinarily faint (femtotesla to picotesla range), which is why **Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices (SQUIDs)** housed in magnetically shielded rooms are required to record them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the body as a city, electric currents as traffic, and the magnetic field as the "noise" — the rumble of vehicles in surrounding streets. A telemedicine link is like a microphone-and-speaker system that picks up this distant "rumble" and transmits it to an expert sitting in another city, who listens with the right instruments to "hear" what is happening inside the body without being physically present.

The magnetic field lines around a current-carrying biological fibre can be visualised as concentric circles wrapping the fibre (right-hand grip rule), and the strength drops off with **$1/r^2$** for a short dipole and **$1/r^3$** for a localised current source inside a volume conductor.

## 1.3 Magnetic Properties of Biological Tissues

Biological tissues are predominantly **diamagnetic** (very weakly repelled by a magnetic field), because water — the principal constituent of the body — has a magnetic susceptibility of $\chi_m \approx -9.05 \times 10^{-6}$. However, a few tissues and substances are **paramagnetic** (weakly attracted), notably:

- Deoxyhaemoglobin
- Certain transition-metal complexes (iron in ferritin, hemosiderin)
- Molecular oxygen ($O_2$)

This tissue-dependent magnetic response is the **fundamental basis of MRI contrast** and, by extension, **teleradiology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Diamagnetic** → $\chi_m < 0$ → repelled by field → water, lipids, most soft tissues.
> - **Paramagnetic** → $\chi_m > 0$ (small) → weakly attracted → deoxyhaemoglobin, $O_2$, gadolinium contrast agents.
> - **Ferromagnetic** → $\chi_m \gg 0$ → strongly attracted → iron, nickel, cobalt (NOT normally present in body; safety hazard near MRI).

## 1.4 Why "Magnetic" Matters in Telemedicine

Telemedicine is essentially the **remote acquisition, transmission, and interpretation** of physiological data. The magnetic domain contributes to telemedicine in five principal ways:

1. **Teleradiology using MRI** — high-resolution anatomical imaging transferred across PACS networks.
2. **Remote biomagnetic monitoring** — MCG (heart) and MEG (brain) signals captured locally and interpreted by remote experts.
3. **Magnetic wireless power and telemetry** — inductive coupling for implantable devices (e.g., pacemakers, capsule endoscopes).
4. **Bioelectromagnetic therapy** — pulsed electromagnetic field (PEMF) therapy remotely prescribed and monitored.
5. **EMI/EMC compliance** — ensuring remote monitoring devices are not corrupted by stray magnetic fields.

> [!NOTE]
> A key syllabus-mandated learning outcome (CO) for this module is the student's ability to **explain the generation, detection, and clinical relevance of biomagnetic fields** and **describe at least three telemedicine applications based on magnetic principles.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Magnetic Theory — The "Why" and "How"

The foundation rests on four classical laws. Each law is unpacked below in a structured "why → how" manner so the logic is transparent.

### 2.1.1 Biot–Savart Law (How a current element creates a magnetic field)

- **Why:** A moving charge (or equivalently a current in a conductor) produces a magnetic field in the surrounding space. The Biot–Savart Law quantifies this contribution from an infinitesimal current element.
- **How:** The contribution $d\vec{B}$ at a field point depends on the current $I$, the length element $d\vec{l}$, the angle $\theta$ between $d\vec{l}$ and the position vector $\hat{r}$, and the inverse-square of distance $r$.

The differential magnetic flux density produced at a point in vacuum by a steady current $I$ flowing along an element $d\vec{l}$ is:

$$d\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \, \frac{I \, d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$$

- $\mu_0$ — permeability of free space $= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$.
- $\hat{r}$ — unit vector from the source element to the field point.
- The cross product implies the field lines form **right-handed concentric circles** around the current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** The Biot–Savart Law is the theoretical starting point for calculating the magnetic field generated by an action potential travelling along a nerve fibre or a depolarisation wavefront sweeping across the myocardium — both of which are the basis of MCG and MEG.

### 2.1.2 Ampère's Circuital Law (How total enclosed current is summed)

- **Why:** Integrating the Biot–Savart Law over a closed current loop is cumbersome for symmetric geometries. Ampère's Law gives a simpler integral form.
- **How:** The line integral of $\vec{H}$ around any closed path equals the total conduction current $I_{enc}$ threading the path.

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$$

For a long straight conductor, this yields $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$, and for a solenoid with $N$ turns per unit length carrying current $I$, the internal field is $H = NI$, leading to $B = \mu N I$ inside the solenoid.

### 2.1.3 Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction (How time-varying fields induce voltages)

- **Why:** This is the cornerstone of wireless telemetry and inductive powering of implantable medical devices.
- **How:** A time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi$ through a coil of $N$ turns induces an electromotive force (EMF).

$$\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \frac{d(BA\cos\theta)}{dt}$$

- The negative sign reflects Lenz's Law — the induced EMF opposes the change in flux.

> [!NOTE]
> **Telemedicine linkage:** Inductive coupling uses Faraday's Law to transmit power and data across the skin to an implanted sensor (e.g., capsule endoscope, intracranial pressure sensor) — no battery replacement surgery is needed. Telemetry of the sensor's data is then carried out by modulating the same inductive link.

### 2.1.4 The Lorentz Force (How magnetic fields act on moving charges)

- **Why:** Charged particles (e.g., ions in blood, electrons in tissue) experience a force when moving through a magnetic field. This is exploited in magnetically-guided drug delivery and in the deflection coils of imaging systems.
- **How:** The force on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ is:

$$\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

- In cyclotron motion, a charged particle in a uniform $\vec{B}$ follows a helical trajectory with radius $r = \dfrac{mv_\perp}{qB}$ and cyclotron frequency $f_c = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$.

## 2.2 Maxwell's Equations (Compact Form)

These four equations form the bedrock of classical electromagnetism. The differential (point) form is given below for completeness — **KTU frequently tests conceptual understanding of these.**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0} \quad \text{(Gauss's Law)}$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0 \quad \text{(No magnetic monopoles)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \quad \text{(Faraday's Law)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{(Ampère–Maxwell Law)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term $\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ (Maxwell's displacement current) is what allows electromagnetic waves — the very carrier of telemedicine signals — to propagate through empty space. Without it, no wireless telemedicine would be possible.

## 2.3 Biomagnetism — Source of Body's Own Magnetic Field

Every ionic current $\vec{J}$ inside the body produces a magnetic field. For a primary current density $\vec{J}^p$ in an inhomogeneous volume conductor, the **magnetic field outside the body** is given by (Sarvas, 1987 formula, often cited in MEG analysis):

$$\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \vec{J}^p(\vec{r}') \times \frac{\vec{r} - \vec{r}'}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert^3} \, dV'$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **magnetoencephalogram (MEG)** measures the magnetic field generated by postsynaptic neuronal currents in cortical pyramidal cells, typically in the range of **$10 \text{ fT to } 1 \text{ pT}$** ($10^{-14}$ to $10^{-12}$ Tesla). For reference, the Earth's field is $50 \, \mu T$ — about $10^{10}$ times stronger. Hence the need for magnetically shielded rooms (MSR) and SQUID sensors.

The **magnetocardiogram (MCG)** captures the heart's magnetic field, typically in the **$10 \text{ pT to } 100 \text{ pT}$** range, generated by the same ionic currents that produce the ECG.

## 2.4 Magnetic Susceptibility of Tissues and MRI Contrast

The magnetic susceptibility $\chi_m$ of a tissue determines its **T1 and T2 relaxation behaviour** in MRI — and therefore its image contrast. The bulk magnetisation $\vec{M}$ induced in a tissue by an applied field $\vec{H}$ is:

$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$$

The total magnetic flux density in the tissue is:

$$\vec{B} = \mu_0(\vec{H} + \vec{M}) = \mu_0(1 + \chi_m)\vec{H} = \mu_0 \mu_r \vec{H} = \mu \vec{H}$$

where $\mu_r = 1 + \chi_m$ is the **relative permeability**.

Approximate susceptibility values (SI units) relevant to clinical MRI:

- Water: $\chi_m \approx -9.05 \times 10^{-6}$
- Air: $\chi_m \approx +0.36 \times 10^{-6}$
- Deoxyhaemoglobin: $\chi_m \approx -7.9 \times 10^{-6}$
- Oxyhaemoglobin: $\chi_m \approx -6.5 \times 10^{-6}$
- Fat: $\chi_m \approx -7.6 \times 10^{-6}$
- Gadolinium contrast: very large $\chi_m > 0$ (strong paramagnetic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOLD (Blood-Oxygen-Level-Dependent) imaging** exploits the susceptibility difference between oxy- and deoxyhaemoglobin. Functional MRI (fMRI) is thus a direct magnetic application, and fMRI studies can be transmitted across the globe for teleneuroradiology.

## 2.5 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) — The Physics of MRI

When a tissue is placed in a strong static field $\vec{B}_0$, the net nuclear magnetisation precesses about $\vec{B}_0$ at the **Larmor frequency**:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

- $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** (for hydrogen, $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad·s}^{-1}\text{T}^{-1}$).
- For a 1.5 T scanner, $f_0 \approx 63.87 \text{ MHz}$ (in the radiofrequency range).

A 90° RF pulse tips the magnetisation into the transverse plane; the subsequent **free induction decay (FID)** at the Larmor frequency is detected by receiver coils. Tissues differ in their **spin-lattice (T1)** and **spin-spin (T2)** relaxation times, producing the contrast seen in MR images.

> [!NOTE]
> **Telemedicine linkage:** MR images, often several hundred megabytes per study, are compressed (DICOM JPEG 2000 lossless / lossy) and transmitted over PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) to a teleradiologist anywhere in the world.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it is the high-yield cheat sheet for the Magnetic sub-topic.**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | Biot–Savart Law | $d\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{I \, d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$ | $B$: T, $I$: A, $r$: m | Field of a current element |
| 2 | Ampère's Law | $\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$ | $H$: A/m | Useful for symmetric geometries |
| 3 | Magnetic flux density in a material | $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H} = \mu_0(1+\chi_m)\vec{H}$ | $\mu$: H/m, $\chi_m$: dimensionless | Defines $\chi_m$ |
| 4 | Solenoid field | $B = \mu_0 \, n \, I$ (inside long solenoid) | $n$: turns/m | Used in MRI gradient/RF coils |
| 5 | Faraday's Law | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $\varepsilon$: V, $\Phi$: Wb | Foundation of wireless telemetry |
| 6 | Lorentz Force | $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | $F$: N, $v$: m/s | Used in magnetic drug guidance |
| 7 | Larmor Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | $f_0$: Hz, $B_0$: T | For ${}^1H$, $\gamma/2\pi = 42.577$ MHz/T |
| 8 | Cyclotron radius | $r = \dfrac{m v_\perp}{q B}$ | $r$: m | Charged particle in uniform $B$ |
| 9 | Biomagnetic field (Sarvas) | $\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \vec{J}^p \times \dfrac{\vec{r} - \vec{r}'}{\vert \vec{r}-\vec{r}' \vert^3} dV'$ | $J^p$: A/m² | MCG/MEG forward model |
| 10 | Signal attenuation (shielded) | $B_{attenuated} = B_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | $t$: s, $\tau$: s | Magnetic shielding time constant |
| 11 | Energy density of B-field | $u = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ | $u$: J/m³ | Relevant to SAR safety |
| 12 | Inductance of solenoid | $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / \ell$ | $L$: H | Used in inductive link design |
| 13 | Mutual inductance coupling | $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | $k$: dimensionless, $0 \le k \le 1$ | Telemetry link efficiency |
| 14 | Wave impedance in free space | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377 \, \Omega$ | $\Omega$ | Governs antenna–tissue interface |
| 15 | SAR | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^2}{\rho}$ | W/kg | Limits for MRI RF exposure |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Real Telemedicine Systems

- **Implant telemetry:** Inductive links based on Faraday's Law power and read out implanted sensors (e.g., intraocular pressure transducer for glaucoma patients). The link budget, bandwidth, and patient safety are designed using Maxwell's equations.
- **MCG/MEG-linked telemedicine:** Local hospitals install SQUID systems in magnetically shielded rooms; the digital biomagnetic data is encrypted and sent to a tertiary neuroscience centre for expert analysis.
- **Telerobotic MRI-guided surgery:** Surgeon operates from a remote console, controlling instruments inside the bore of an MRI scanner. The strong $B_0$ field requires all instruments to be non-ferromagnetic — a design constraint flowing directly from Lorentz-force considerations.
- **PEMF therapy:** Pulsed electromagnetic fields prescribed and monitored remotely for bone-healing, depression (rTMS), and chronic pain.
- **Wearable magnetometers:** Modern body-worn magneto-impedance sensors and fluxgate magnetometers enable ambulatory cardiac monitoring transmitted to the cloud — essentially **point-of-care magnetocardiography entering the telemedicine era**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop (Biot–Savart)

This is a classic KTU derivation. The result is used as a building block for **modelling the MCG signal** and for designing the **transmit/receive RF coils of an MRI scanner**.

### Statement
A circular loop of radius $a$ carries a steady current $I$. Determine the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ at a point on the axis of the loop, at a perpendicular distance $x$ from its centre.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**
Let the loop lie in the $xy$-plane, centred at the origin, and let the field point be at $(0, 0, x)$ on the $z$-axis. The position vector from a source point on the loop $\vec{r}' = (a\cos\phi, a\sin\phi, 0)$ to the field point $\vec{r} = (0, 0, x)$ is:

$$\vec{r} - \vec{r}' = (-a\cos\phi, -a\sin\phi, x)$$

The distance magnitude is:

$$R = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert = \sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$$

By symmetry this distance is the **same for every point on the loop** because every source point is at the same perpendicular distance $a$ from the axis.

**Step 2 — Express the current element**
The current element is $d\vec{l} = a \, d\phi \, \hat{\phi}$, where $\hat{\phi} = (-\sin\phi, \cos\phi, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the cross product**
Using $\hat{\phi} \times \hat{r}$ where $\hat{r} = (-a\cos\phi, -a\sin\phi, x)/R$:

$$\hat{\phi} \times \hat{r} = \left(\frac{x \cos\phi}{R}, \frac{x \sin\phi}{R}, \frac{a}{R}\right)$$

The radial components ($x\cos\phi$ and $x\sin\phi$) will cancel upon integration around the loop by symmetry. Only the axial component $a/R$ survives.

**Step 4 — Apply Biot–Savart Law**

$$d\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi} \frac{d\vec{l} \times \hat{R}}{R^2} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi R^2} \left(\frac{x \cos\phi}{R}, \frac{x \sin\phi}{R}, \frac{a}{R}\right) a \, d\phi$$

The $z$-component of $d\vec{B}$ is:

$$dB_z = \frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{4\pi R^3} d\phi = \frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{4\pi (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} d\phi$$

**Step 5 — Integrate around the loop**
Integrate over $\phi$ from $0$ to $2\pi$:

$$B_z = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{4\pi (a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} d\phi$$

Since the integrand is independent of $\phi$:

$$\boxed{B_z = \frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Limiting cases**
- At the centre ($x = 0$): $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2a}$.
- Far from the loop ($x \gg a$): $B \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2 x^3} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \dfrac{m}{x^3}$, where $m = I \pi a^2$ is the magnetic dipole moment — **the same $1/r^3$ fall-off that biomagnetic dipoles obey.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** A small loop with many turns ($N$) and radius $a$ produces an axial field $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I a^2}{2(a^2+x^2)^{3/2}}$ — this is exactly the principle behind the **surface receive coils of MRI scanners** and the **transmit coils of inductive telemetry implants**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Induced EMF in an Implanted Telemetry Coil (Faraday's Law)

### Statement
A patient has a subcutaneous implant containing a flat receiver coil of $N = 50$ turns, area $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, oriented parallel to an external primary coil that produces a sinusoidally varying magnetic field of amplitude $B_0 = 5 \text{ mT}$ at 100 kHz. Compute the peak induced EMF in the receiver coil.

### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Time-dependent external field**
Let the external field be $B(t) = B_0 \sin(\omega t)$, where $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $f = 100 \text{ kHz}$.

**Step 2 — Magnetic flux through one turn**
Since the coil area is small and the field is approximately uniform over it:

$$\Phi(t) = B(t) A = B_0 A \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law**

$$\varepsilon(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N B_0 A \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak (amplitude) value of the EMF is:

$$\varepsilon_{peak} = N B_0 A \omega$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

$$N = 50, \quad B_0 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}, \quad A = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s} \approx 6.2832 \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\varepsilon_{peak} = 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 6.2832 \times 10^{5}$$

Carrying out the multiplication step by step:

$$\varepsilon_{peak} = 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.25$$

$$0.25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.2832 \times 10^{5} = 2.5 \times 6.2832 = 15.708$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{peak} \approx 15.7 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Telemedicine utility:** This ~16 V peak is more than sufficient to power a low-duty-cycle implanted sensor (e.g., capsule endoscope transmitting at 402–405 MHz) — the inductive link both powers the implant and back-telemeters the data. The patient's clinical readings are then relayed to a remote physician.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Larmor Frequency for Hydrogen at 1.5 T and 3 T (NMR/MRI)

### Statement
Compute the Larmor precession frequency of ${}^1H$ protons in (a) a 1.5 T clinical MRI scanner, and (b) a 3 T scanner.

### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Use the Larmor equation**

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

For ${}^1H$, the gyromagnetic ratio in convenient units is:

$$\frac{\gamma}{2\pi} = 42.577 \text{ MHz/T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for 1.5 T**

$$f_0 = 42.577 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.866 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for 3 T**

$$f_0 = 42.577 \text{ MHz/T} \times 3 \text{ T} = 127.731 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Wavelength in tissue**
Inside the body, RF propagation speed is roughly $c/n$, with tissue refractive index $n \approx 1.4$ to 1.6 for muscle at 64 MHz. Hence:

$$v \approx \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{63.866 \times 10^6} \approx 3.13 \text{ m at 1.5 T}$$

This wavelength is large compared to body dimensions, justifying the **quasi-static approximation** often used in MRI RF safety analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for telemedicine:** Teleradiology over 4G/5G networks requires a stable, predictable bandwidth budget. Knowing the RF frequency of the scanner helps in planning **EMI/EMC compatibility** of the in-room LAN infrastructure (e.g., fibre-optic isolation to avoid RF leakage into the scan room).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Magnetic Field Strength at the Centre of an MCG Dipole Source

### Statement
A depolarisation wavefront of area $S = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ carries a current $I = 10 \, \mu A$ at a depth $d = 5 \text{ cm}$ from the chest surface. Estimate the magnetic field just outside the chest.

### Step-by-step solution

**Step 1 — Model as a current loop**
Treat the depolarisation wavefront as a current loop of area $S$. Its magnetic dipole moment is:

$$m = I \times S = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 10^{-9} \text{ A·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic field on the dipole axis at distance $d$**
Using the result of Derivation 1 for $x \gg a$:

$$B \approx \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \frac{m}{d^3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}, \quad d = 0.05 \text{ m}, \quad m = 10^{-9} \text{ A·m}^2$$

$$B \approx \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2\pi} \times \frac{10^{-9}}{(0.05)^3} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{10^{-9}}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{B \approx 1.6 \text{ pT}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is consistent with the typical MCG field magnitude (10–100 pT for stronger cardiac signals). Detection requires **SQUID magnetometers** with sensitivity $\sim 1 \text{ fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ and a **magnetically shielded room** with shielding factor $\geq 10^4$ at low frequencies.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Mapping of Magnetic Modalities in Telemedicine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix should be memorised. It is a favourite KTU Part B mapping question.**

| Modality | Source Signal | Typical Field | Sensor | Telemedicine Use |
|----------|--------------|---------------|--------|------------------|
| **MRI** | ${}^1H$ precession in $B_0$ | $1.5\text{–}7$ T (static) | RF receiver coil | Teleradiology via PACS |
| **fMRI** | BOLD susceptibility change | $1.5\text{–}3$ T | EPI sequence | Functional mapping consultation |
| **MCG** | Cardiac ionic currents | $10\text{–}100$ pT | SQUID | Remote arrhythmia analysis |
| **MEG** | Cortical postsynaptic currents | $10\text{ fT}\text{–}1$ pT | SQUID array (头盔式) | Epilepsy surgery planning via teleneurology |
| **PEMF Therapy** | Externally applied pulses | $1\text{–}100$ mT | Treatment coil | Prescribed & monitored remotely |
| **Inductive Telemetry** | Modulated H-field | $0.1\text{–}10$ mT | Implant coil | Data/power to/from implants |
| **Magnetometer Wearable** | Cardiac/spatial | nT range | Fluxgate / GMR | Cloud-uploaded cardiac rhythm |

## 3.6 Master Problem — End-to-End Telemedical MRI Workflow with Magnetic Link

A rural hospital acquires an MRI study. The DICOM file (~250 MB) is compressed and uploaded to a cloud server where a teleradiologist interprets it.

**Step 1 — Acquisition:** At 1.5 T, ${}^1H$ Larmor frequency $\approx 63.87$ MHz.

**Step 2 — Image reconstruction:** K-space filled with phase-encoded echoes; Fourier transform yields an image.

**Step 3 — Compression:** JPEG 2000 lossy compression, ratio 5:1 → 50 MB.

**Step 4 — Encryption and transmission:** AES-256 encryption over TLS via 100 Mbps broadband.

**Step 5 — Transit time:**

$$t = \frac{50 \text{ MB}}{100 \text{ Mbps}} = \frac{50 \times 8 \text{ Mb}}{100 \text{ Mbps}} = 4 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6 — Remote interpretation and report:** Teleradiologist annotates the study, signs digitally, and the report is pushed back to the rural centre in under a minute.

> [!NOTE]
> This end-to-end workflow — **MRI acquisition → magnetic physics → digital transmission → remote interpretation** — is the canonical example used in KTU Module 2 to illustrate the convergence of magnetic principles with telemedicine.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Biomagnetic Signal Chain (MCG/MEG → Telemedicine)

This Mermaid block renders the **complete functional architecture** of a biomagnetic telemedicine pipeline — from ionic currents inside the body all the way to the remote physician's display.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bioelectric Current in Tissue] --> B[Primary Current Density Jp]
    B --> C[Bioelectric Field Potential Phi]
    B --> D[Biomagnetic Field B]
    C --> E[ECG EEG Electrodes]
    D --> F[SQUID Magnetometer Array]
    E --> G[Analog Front End Amplifier]
    F --> H[Flux Locked Loop FLL]
    G --> I[16 bit ADC at 1 kHz]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Digital Signal Processing]
    J --> K[Noise Cancellation via Reference Channels]
    K --> L[Feature Extraction]
    L --> M[AES 256 Encryption]
    M --> N[Cloud Server TLS Channel]
    N --> O[Remote Specialist Workstation]
    O --> P[Visualisation and Report Generation]
    P --> Q[Report Pushed Back to Local Hospital]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above directly maps the path of a magnetic signal from its biophysical origin (the primary current density) through SQUID detection, digitisation, encryption, and finally remote display. This is the kind of "block diagram" a KTU Part B question may ask a student to draw and explain.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — MRI Scanner Functional Architecture (Magnetic Sub-systems)

This block diagram outlines the **magnetic subsystem** of an MRI scanner. Although the device itself is stationary, the resulting images are routinely transmitted to remote radiologists — making this a magnetic sub-system with direct telemedicine relevance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Magnet
        M1[Superconducting Main Coil B0 1.5T] --> M2[Gradient Coils X Y Z]
        M2 --> M3[RF Body Coil Transmit]
        M3 --> M4[RF Receive Coils Array]
    end
    M1 --> M5[Patient in Bore]
    M2 --> M5
    M3 --> M5
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M6[Raw k space Data]
    M6 --> M7[Reconstruction FFT]
    M7 --> M8[DICOM Image Series]
    M8 --> M9[Local PACS Server]
    M9 --> M10[Compression JPEG2000]
    M10 --> M11[VPN Tunnel to Remote Site]
    M11 --> M12[Remote Teleradiologist Console]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Inductive Telemetry Link for Implanted Sensor

This block diagram shows how Faraday's Law of induction is used to **wirelessly power and read out an implanted medical sensor** — a classic telemedicine scenario.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Primary Coil] --> B[Time Varying Magnetic Field B t]
    B --> C[Secondary Coil Inside Body]
    C --> D[Rectifier and Voltage Regulator]
    D --> E[Implanted Microcontroller]
    E --> F[Sensor e.g. Pressure Temperature]
    F --> G[Modulated Load Switching]
    G --> H[Reflected Impedance seen by Primary]
    H --> I[DEMOD Demodulation at External Reader]
    I --> J[Bluetooth or 4G Uplink]
    J --> K[Telemedicine Cloud Dashboard]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "reflected impedance" path is the **back-telemetry channel** — the implant transmits data by varying its load, which modulates the primary coil's current. This elegant two-way inductive link avoids any battery in the implant, ideal for long-term implanted sensors.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Magnetic Susceptibility and MRI Contrast

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Static Field B0] --> B[Tissue Magnetisation M]
    B --> C{Type of Tissue}
    C -->|Diamagnetic Water Fat| D[Negative Susceptibility]
    C -->|Paramagnetic DeoxyHb Gd| E[Positive Susceptibility]
    C -->|Air Lung Bowel| F[Strong Local Field Distortion]
    D --> G[Short T1 Bright on T1w]
    E --> H[Short T1 Dark on T2w]
    F --> I[Susceptibility Artefact]
    G --> J[Anatomical MRI Contrast]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Motion Correction Needed]
    J --> L[DICOM Stack for Teleradiology]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Define biomagnetism. Mention the order of magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the human heart and the human brain, and name the instrument used to detect these signals.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Biomagnetism** is the branch of science concerned with the weak magnetic fields produced by living organisms, arising from ionic currents flowing in nerves, muscles, and other excitable tissues.
>
> The **human heart** produces a magnetocardiographic (MCG) field in the range of approximately **$10 \text{ pT to } 100 \text{ pT}$** (picotesla) at the chest surface.
>
> The **human brain** produces a magnetoencephalographic (MEG) field in the range of approximately **$10 \text{ fT to } 1 \text{ pT}$** (femtotesla to picotesla) at the scalp.
>
> These extraordinarily weak fields are detected using a **Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID)**, which must be operated inside a **magnetically shielded room (MSR)** with shielding factor $\geq 10^4$ to suppress environmental noise (e.g., urban power-line interference, vehicles, Earth's field variations).

**[Marks distribution: Definition 1, Heart 1, Brain 1 — partial credit allowed.]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**List any three telemedicine applications that depend primarily on magnetic principles. Briefly state the role of magnetism in each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Teleradiology using MRI** — Magnetic resonance imaging exploits the Larmor precession of hydrogen nuclei in a strong static magnetic field. The resulting image data is transmitted over PACS networks to a remote radiologist for interpretation.

2. **Remote biomagnetic monitoring (MCG/MEG)** — SQUID-based systems at local hospitals capture the heart's and brain's magnetic fields. The digital biomagnetic data is encrypted and transmitted to a specialist at a distant centre for expert analysis (e.g., pre-surgical epilepsy mapping, arrhythmia localisation).

3. **Inductive powering and telemetry of implanted sensors** — Faraday's law of induction is used to wirelessly transfer both power and data across the skin to/from an implanted sensor (e.g., capsule endoscope, intracranial pressure monitor, retinal prosthesis). This enables continuous remote patient monitoring without repeated surgical battery replacement.

**[Marks distribution: Each correct application 1 mark + brief role 1 mark = 3 marks.]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** confuse $\mu_0$ (permeability of free space, H/m) with $\mu$ (absolute permeability of a material, also H/m). Examiners frequently test this.
> - **Do not** confuse the *static* magnetic field (e.g., $B_0$ of MRI, 1.5 T) with the *time-varying* magnetic field (e.g., gradient fields, kHz to MHz range). They have very different safety implications.
> - **Always** state units explicitly. A numerical answer without a unit is treated as incomplete (typically loses 0.5 to 1 mark).
> - In derivation questions, **show every integration step**. Students often skip the symmetry argument — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> - The Biot–Savart Law, Ampère's Law, and Faraday's Law are **all vector laws**. Writing them as scalar equations without mentioning the cross product is a common mistake.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **principle of operation of a SQUID magnetometer** and explain why it is the preferred sensor for biomagnetic field measurement. List **two major applications** in telemedicine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **magnetic flux density on the axis of a circular current loop** of $N$ turns, radius $a$, carrying current $I$, at a point $x$ from the centre. Hence calculate the field at the centre of a loop with $N = 100$, $a = 2 \text{ cm}$, and $I = 1.5 \text{ A}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A **Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID)** is the most sensitive detector of magnetic flux commercially available. It consists of one or two **Josephson junctions** inserted into a superconducting ring.

- **Principle of operation:** When the SQUID ring is cooled below its critical temperature (typically $\sim 4 \text{ K}$ using liquid helium), the ring becomes superconducting. The Josephson junctions allow Cooper pairs to tunnel through a thin insulating barrier, producing a DC Josephson current. The voltage across the SQUID is a **periodic function of the magnetic flux** threading the ring, with period equal to the **flux quantum**:

$$\Phi_0 = \frac{h}{2e} \approx 2.068 \times 10^{-15} \text{ Wb}$$

- A tiny change in the external magnetic flux (e.g., from a biomagnetic source) produces a measurable change in the SQUID voltage. The sensor is interfaced with a **flux-locked loop (FLL)** electronics to linearise the response.
- The **white noise floor** of modern SQUIDs is approximately **$1$ to $5 \text{ fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$** at 1 Hz to 1 kHz — low enough to detect MEG signals (10 fT) with high signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Why preferred for biomagnetism:** No other technology matches the fT-level sensitivity. Optical pumping magnetometers (OPMs) are an emerging alternative but have higher low-frequency drift.

**Two telemedicine applications:**

1. **Magnetoencephalography (MEG)** for **remote pre-surgical epilepsy mapping** — local hospital acquires the MEG, transmits data to a tertiary epilepsy centre for source localisation.
2. **Fetal magnetocardiography (fMCG)** — non-invasive recording of the fetal heart's magnetic signals transmitted to a remote cardiologist for prenatal arrhythmia assessment.

**[7 marks: Diagram 2, Principle 2, Sensitivity 1, Applications 2.]**

#### Model Solution for (b)

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** of these notes. The boxed result for an $N$-turn coil is:

$$B_z(x) = \frac{\mu_0 N I a^2}{2(a^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 1 — Set $x = 0$ for the field at the centre:**

$$B_z(0) = \frac{\mu_0 N I a^2}{2 a^3} = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}, \quad N = 100, \quad I = 1.5 \text{ A}, \quad a = 0.02 \text{ m}$$

$$B_z(0) = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.02}$$

Numerator: $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 1.5 = 4\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 6\pi \times 10^{-5}$

Denominator: $2 \times 0.02 = 0.04$

$$B_z(0) = \frac{6\pi \times 10^{-5}}{0.04} = \frac{6\pi \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.5\pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$$

$$B_z(0) \approx 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T} \approx 4.71 \text{ mT}$$

$$\boxed{B_z(0) \approx 4.71 \text{ mT}}$$

**[7 marks: Derivation 5, Numerical substitution 1, Final answer with unit 1.]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define **magnetic susceptibility**. Classify biological tissues based on their magnetic susceptibility and explain how this property is exploited in **BOLD functional MRI (fMRI)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An inductive telemetry link consists of a primary coil of $L_1 = 200 \, \mu H$ outside the body and a secondary coil of $L_2 = 50 \, \mu H$ inside the body. The mutual inductance is $M = 20 \, \mu H$ and the operating frequency is 200 kHz. Calculate **(i)** the **coupling coefficient** $k$, and **(ii)** the **mutual impedance** magnitude $\vert Z_M \vert$ of the link. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

- **Magnetic susceptibility** is the dimensionless constant $\chi_m$ that quantifies the degree of magnetisation $\vec{M}$ acquired by a material when placed in an external magnetic field $\vec{H}$:

$$\vec{M} = \chi_m \vec{H}$$

The total magnetic flux density inside the material is then $\vec{B} = \mu_0 (1 + \chi_m) \vec{H}$.

- **Classification of biological tissues:**

| Class | $\chi_m$ sign | Example | Behaviour |
|-------|---------------|---------|-----------|
| Diamagnetic | $\chi_m < 0$ (small) | Water, lipids, most soft tissue | Weakly repelled by field |
| Paramagnetic | $\chi_m > 0$ (small) | Deoxyhaemoglobin, oxyhaemoglobin (weakly), $O_2$, gadolinium chelates | Weakly attracted |
| Ferromagnetic | $\chi_m \gg 0$ | Iron, nickel, cobalt (not normally in body) | Strongly attracted — safety hazard near MRI |

- **Exploitation in BOLD fMRI:** The **blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD)** signal arises because **deoxyhaemoglobin is paramagnetic** while **oxyhaemoglobin is much less so**. When a region of the brain becomes more active, the local increase in blood flow overcompensates for oxygen extraction, leading to a **decrease in deoxyhaemoglobin concentration** and hence a **decrease in local magnetic susceptibility**. This alters the local field homogeneity, **lengthens T2\***, and produces a small but measurable **increase in MR signal**. The resulting time series is used to infer neural activity.

- **Telemedicine link:** fMRI studies acquired at a rural centre can be uploaded for **remote functional mapping** by a neuroradiologist or for AI-assisted analysis in a tertiary centre.

**[7 marks: Definition 1, Classification 3, BOLD mechanism 3.]**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Coupling coefficient $k$**

By definition, the coupling coefficient is:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

Substituting:

$$k = \frac{20 \, \mu H}{\sqrt{200 \, \mu H \times 50 \, \mu H}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{10000}} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.2$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.2}$$

This is a typical loosely-coupled inductive link used across the skin (≈ 1–2 cm separation).

**(ii) Mutual impedance magnitude $\vert Z_M \vert$**

The mutual impedance of an inductive link at angular frequency $\omega$ is:

$$Z_M = j \omega M$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert Z_M \vert = \omega M = 2 \pi f M$$

Substitute $f = 200 \text{ kHz} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}$ and $M = 20 \, \mu H = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ H}$:

$$\vert Z_M \vert = 2 \pi \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 2 \pi \times 4 = 8 \pi \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\vert Z_M \vert \approx 25.13 \, \Omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical note:** The link power transfer efficiency depends strongly on $k^2$. At $k = 0.2$, efficiency is poor unless resonance tuning is added at both ends (series or parallel LC resonance at 200 kHz). This is a standard design exercise in biomedical telemetry courses.

**[7 marks: Part i 3, Part ii 4.]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Magnetic Principles in Telemedicine (KTU Module 2)**

- **Definition block:** Biomagnetism is the study of magnetic fields produced by the body (10 fT to 100 pT). Bioelectromagnetism is the broader study of EM interaction with biological tissue.

- **Key laws (must remember):**
  - Biot–Savart Law: $d\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{I \, d\vec{l} \times \hat{r}}{r^2}$
  - Ampère's Law: $\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$
  - Faraday's Law: $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$
  - Lorentz Force: $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
  - Larmor Frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ — for ${}^1H$, $\gamma/2\pi = 42.577$ MHz/T.

- **Constants:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $\Phi_0 = h/2e \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-15}$ Wb; Earth's field $\approx 50 \, \mu T$.

- **Tissue susceptibility:** Most soft tissue is diamagnetic ($\chi_m < 0$); deoxyhaemoglobin is paramagnetic; iron is ferromagnetic (MRI safety hazard).

- **MRI quick facts:** 1.5 T → 63.87 MHz, 3 T → 127.73 MHz. Images transmitted via PACS / DICOM / JPEG 2000 to remote radiologist.

- **Biomagnetic sensing:** MEG ≈ 10 fT–1 pT; MCG ≈ 10–100 pT; detected by **SQUID** in a **magnetically shielded room**; sensitivity $\sim 1 \text{ fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$.

- **Inductive telemetry:** Based on Faraday's Law; uses coupled coils (one external, one implanted) for both **power transfer and back-telemetry**; design governed by coupling coefficient $k = M/\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ and resonant tuning.

- **Five telemedicine applications to memorise:**
  1. Teleradiology (MRI/fMRI)
  2. Remote MCG/MEG interpretation
  3. Inductive powering of implants
  4. PEMF therapy (remote prescription & monitoring)
  5. Wearable magnetometer cardiac monitoring

- **Safety considerations:** Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) limits for RF; projectile hazard from ferromagnetic objects in MRI; gradient-induced peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS); helium quench venting protocols.

- **Common exam traps:**
  - Confusing $\mu_0$ with $\mu$ or $\mu_r$.
  - Forgetting the $1/(4\pi)$ in Biot–Savart.
  - Treating MRI's static field as a time-varying field (it is static; the *gradients* are time-varying).
  - Writing Biot–Savart or Faraday's Law as scalar (must use vector form or $\times$ / $-$ sign).

- **High-yield numerical skill:** Calculating the centre field of a current loop $B = \mu_0 N I / (2a)$, the Larmor frequency, and the coupling coefficient $k$.

- **Mnemonic for Maxwell's equations:** **"Diverse BEAST"** — Divergence of B = 0 Always (no monopoles), and the other three (Gauss E, Faraday, Ampère-Maxwell).

- **Cross-module link:** Always connect magnetic principles to **telemedicine outcomes** — e.g., the very same physics that allows SQUID detection of MEG is what makes MRI possible, and both produce data that is then transmitted for remote interpretation. Examiners reward this "why we care" framing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
