# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

Classify the antennas used in telemedicine based on the **operating scenario** (in-body, on-body, off-body). Give one example antenna for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Telemedicine antennas are classified by the body-centric propagation zone they operate in:

1. **In-body (Implantable):** Antennas operating entirely inside the human body, e.g., a **PIFA** or **loop antenna** used in cardiac pacemakers. Frequency: MICS (402–405 MHz). Critical constraints: biocompatibility, low SAR, miniaturization.

2. **On-body (Wearable):** Antennas mounted on the skin or clothing, e.g., a **microstrip patch** integrated into an ECG chest patch. Frequency: ISM 2.4 GHz. Key feature: conformal, flexible, omnidirectional.

3. **Off-body (External):** Antennas on a nearby device or base station, e.g., a **patch array** on a hospital WLAN access point. Higher gain, often directional, may use 5.8 GHz ISM or UWB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark per scenario + example = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

Why is the **MICS band (402–405 MHz)** preferred for implantable medical antennas over the **2.4 GHz ISM band**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lower tissue attenuation at MICS:** The attenuation constant $\alpha$ in muscle tissue at 402 MHz is approximately **2–3 Np/m**, whereas at 2.4 GHz it is **~10 Np/m** — a 4–5× reduction in path loss. **(1 mark)**

2. **Better penetration depth:** Lower frequencies penetrate deeper, enabling reliable communication from deep implants (e.g., pacemakers in the chest cavity) to a surface reader. **(1 mark)**

3. **Regulatory safety:** MICS is reserved worldwide for medical implant communication, avoiding interference from Wi-Fi/Bluetooth; the low frequency also limits SAR for the same radiated power. **(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark each — (i) lower loss, (ii) deeper penetration, (iii) regulatory + safety.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B Pitfalls):**
> 1. Forgetting the **length extension** $\Delta L$ term — most students compute $L$ without it and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. Using the **free-space** wavelength in the resonant frequency formula — must use $\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}$.
> 3. Reporting VSWR without the **magnitude** (e.g., 1.5, not 1.5:1).
> 4. Confusing **directivity** and **gain** — gain = efficiency × directivity.
> 5. Not stating **units** for the SAR and resonant frequency — examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** [7 marks] Design a rectangular microstrip patch antenna for a **wearable telemedicine ECG monitor** operating at $f_r = 2.45 \text{ GHz}$. The substrate is Rogers RT/duroid 5880 with $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ and $h = 0.7875 \text{ mm}$. Compute the patch dimensions $W$ and $L$, the effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{eff}$, and the length extension $\Delta L$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **selection criteria** for choosing an antenna for an **implantable capsule endoscope**. Compare MICS and UWB bands. Justify your choice with quantitative reasoning (path loss, antenna size, data rate).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $f_r = 2.45 \text{ GHz}$, $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $h = 0.7875 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Width $W$ [2 marks]:**

$$
W = \frac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r+1}} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2 \times 2.45 \times 10^9}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3.2}}
$$

$$
W = 0.06122 \times 0.7906 = 0.04841 \text{ m} \approx 48.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{eff}$ [2 marks]:**

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{2.2+1}{2} + \frac{2.2-1}{2}\left[1+12\cdot\frac{0.0007875}{0.04841}\right]^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.6 \times (1.1952)^{-0.5} = 1.6 + 0.6 \times 0.9147 \approx 2.149
$$

**Step 3 — Length extension $\Delta L$ [1 mark]:**
$W/h = 48.41/0.7875 = 61.47$.

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = 0.412 \cdot \frac{(2.149+0.3)(61.47+0.264)}{(2.149-0.258)(61.47+0.813)} \approx 0.412 \times \frac{150.74}{132.27} \approx 0.470
$$

$$
\Delta L \approx 0.470 \times 0.7875 \approx 0.370 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Patch length $L$ [2 marks]:**

$$
L = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L
$$

$$
L = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2 \times 2.45 \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{2.149}} - 2(0.370 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
L = \frac{3\times 10^8}{7.182 \times 10^9} - 0.00074 = 0.04178 - 0.00074 \approx 41.04 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer [Q.A(a)]:**
- $W \approx 48.4 \text{ mm}$
- $L \approx 41.0 \text{ mm}$
- $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx 2.15$
- $\Delta L \approx 0.37 \text{ mm}$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Selection criteria for implantable capsule endoscope [3 marks]:**
1. **Size** — antenna must fit in a swallowable capsule (~10 mm × 25 mm).
2. **Biocompatibility** — encapsulation with medical-grade polymer (e.g., parylene-C).
3. **Tissue safety** — SAR < 1.6 W/kg (FCC, 1 g averaging).
4. **Operating frequency choice** — depends on data rate and tissue loss trade-off.
5. **Radiation pattern** — should direct energy outward (away from body) toward the on-body reader.

**Comparison MICS vs UWB [4 marks]:**

| Parameter | MICS (402–405 MHz) | UWB (3.1–10.6 GHz) |
|---|---|---|
| Tissue attenuation (muscle) | ~2.5 Np/m | ~10–15 Np/m |
| Path loss at 50 mm depth | ~10 dB | ~45 dB |
| Antenna electrical size | $\lambda/4 \approx 18.6 \text{ cm}$ (large) | $\lambda/4 \approx 7.5 \text{ mm}$ (compact) |
| Data rate | ~100 kbps | >100 Mbps (video) |
| SAR for same radiated power | Low | High (must be limited) |

**Justification [2 marks]:**
For **capsule endoscopy** requiring real-time 2–5 Mbps video streaming, the **UWB band is preferred** because the **miniaturization** benefit (mm-sized antenna) outweighs the higher tissue loss, which is mitigated by short-range on-body receiver placement (within 10–15 cm). For **low-data-rate implants** (pacemakers, neural stimulators) where link margin is critical, **MICS is preferred**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the **resonant frequency expression** for a rectangular microstrip patch antenna in the $\text{TM}_{10}$ mode. Clearly state the role of the effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{eff}$ and the length extension $\Delta L$. A patch has $L = 40 \text{ mm}$, $W = 50 \text{ mm}$, $h = 1.6 \text{ mm}$, and is on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$. Compute the resonant frequency.

**(b)** [7 marks] With the aid of a block diagram, describe a **complete wireless telemedicine monitoring system** and explain how the **choice of antenna** at each stage (implant, on-body, base station) is determined by system requirements. Discuss **SAR** implications.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of $f_r$ [3 marks]:**

The dominant mode of a rectangular microstrip patch antenna is $\text{TM}_{10}$, where the field varies as half a sinusoidal cycle along the resonant length $L$. The resonant condition is:

$$
L_{eff} = \frac{\lambda_0}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}
$$

Rearranging for frequency:

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{2 L_{eff}\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} = \frac{c}{2(L+2\Delta L)\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}
$$

**Role of $\varepsilon_{eff}$ and $\Delta L$ [1 mark]:**

- **$\varepsilon_{eff}$** accounts for the fact that part of the field travels in the substrate (permittivity $\varepsilon_r$) and part in air — it is a weighted average.
- **$\Delta L$** corrects for the **electrical lengthening** caused by fringing fields at the patch edges. Without $\Delta L$, the computed $f_r$ would be **higher** than measured.

**Numerical computation [3 marks]:**

Given $L = 40 \text{ mm}$, $W = 50 \text{ mm}$, $h = 1.6 \text{ mm}$, $\varepsilon_r = 2.5$.

First, $\varepsilon_{eff}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{2.5+1}{2} + \frac{2.5-1}{2}\left[1+12\cdot\frac{0.0016}{0.050}\right]^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.75 + 0.75 \times (1.384)^{-0.5} = 1.75 + 0.75 \times 0.8502 \approx 2.388
$$

Next, $\Delta L$ ($W/h = 50/1.6 = 31.25$):

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = 0.412 \cdot \frac{(2.388+0.3)(31.25+0.264)}{(2.388-0.258)(31.25+0.813)} \approx 0.412 \times \frac{80.51}{67.13} \approx 0.494
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.494 \times 1.6 \text{ mm} \approx 0.790 \text{ mm}
$$

Finally, $f_r$:

$$
f_r = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2(40\times 10^{-3} + 2\times 0.79\times 10^{-3})\sqrt{2.388}}
$$

$$
f_r = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2(0.04158)(1.5454)} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{0.12855} \approx 2.334 \text{ GHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_r \approx 2.33 \text{ GHz}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Block diagram of wireless telemedicine monitoring system [3 marks]:**

(See Section 4.3 Mermaid block diagram — Sensor → AFE → ADC → Baseband → RF Transceiver → Antenna → Receiver → Decoder → Display.)

**Antenna selection at each stage [3 marks]:**

| Stage | Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Implant** (e.g., pacemaker) | Loop / PIFA in MICS band | Low tissue loss, deep penetration, biocompatible packaging |
| **On-body relay** (e.g., chest patch) | Microstrip patch in 2.4 GHz ISM | Compact, conformal, sufficient bandwidth for vitals data |
| **Base station** (hospital / clinic) | Patch array / high-gain directional | Aggregates multiple patients, requires beam-steering and high gain |

**SAR discussion [1 mark]:**

- SAR must remain below **1.6 W/kg** (FCC, 1 g) or **2.0 W/kg** (ICNIRP, 10 g).
- Implantable antennas typically operate below **25 µW EIRP** in the MICS band to comply.
- Wearable antennas should maintain separation > 5 mm from skin or use low-conductivity textile substrates to reduce SAR.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Antenna Types for Requirements)**

- ✅ Telemedicine antennas are classified by **operating scenario** (in-body, on-body, off-body) **before** any other criterion.
- ✅ The **three primary antenna families** for telemedicine are: **microstrip patch**, **PIFA**, and **UWB slot/patch**.
- ✅ **MICS band (402–405 MHz)** = best for deep implants; **ISM 2.4 GHz** = best for wearables; **UWB (3.1–10.6 GHz)** = best for capsule video.
- ✅ The **resonant frequency formula** $f_r = c/[2(L+2\Delta L)\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}]$ is the most-tested equation in KTU exams — memorize it **with the $\Delta L$ correction**.
- ✅ The **patch width formula** $W = (c/2f)\sqrt{2/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$ is independent of substrate height.
- ✅ **VSWR < 2** is the typical design target — corresponds to $RL > 9.54$ dB and $\vert\Gamma\vert < 0.333$.
- ✅ **SAR limits**: FCC 1.6 W/kg, ICNIRP 2.0 W/kg — must be **stated with units** in exam answers.
- ✅ **Friss equation** must be modified by an **exponential tissue attenuation term** for body-centric links.
- ✅ **Gain = $\eta_r \times$ Directivity** — do not interchange them.
- ✅ **Loop antennas** are used for **magnetic near-field** inductive telemetry (capsule, RFID-tagged implants).
- ✅ **Array antennas** are used at the **base station** for beam-steering and multi-user MIMO in hospital WLANs.
- ✅ **Reflector / helix antennas** are used for **satellite telemedicine** in remote/rural settings.
- ✅ Always provide **units** (mm, GHz, dBi, W/kg) in numerical answers — examiners deduct for missing units.
- ✅ The **effective permittivity** $\varepsilon_{eff}$ is **always between 1 and $\varepsilon_r$** — sanity-check your computed value.
- ✅ **Friss equation** + **tissue loss** = **complete WBAN link budget** — a frequent KTU Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration and Operational Issues: System Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integration and Operational Issues: System Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Integration in Telemedicine** is the disciplined process of combining disparate clinical, administrative, and communication subsystems (Hospital Information Systems, Electronic Medical Records, Picture Archiving and Communication Systems, Laboratory Information Systems, video-conferencing units, IoT biomedical sensors, and remote monitoring devices) into a unified, interoperable, and functionally cohesive healthcare delivery platform. The objective is to enable seamless data exchange, coordinated clinical workflows, and end-to-end information continuity between geographically distributed healthcare stakeholders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):** System Integration refers to the engineering and managerial practice of linking heterogeneous computing and clinical systems using standard interfaces, middleware, and service-oriented architectures so that the resulting Telemedicine ecosystem behaves as a single logical entity supporting the **Continuity of Care Record (CCR)** and the **Electronic Health Record (EHR)**.

**Interoperability** (the property that makes integration possible) is formally classified by **HIMSS (Healthcare Information and Management Systems Society)** into **four ascending levels**:

| Level | Name | Description |
|:-----:|:-----|:------------|
| **L1** | **Foundational** | Basic one-way data exchange between systems |
| **L2** | **Structural** | Standardized message format (HL7 v2, DICOM) preserved |
| **L3** | **Semantic** | Shared meaning of data using ontologies (SNOMED-CT, LOINC) |
| **L4** | **Organizational** | Policy, workflow, and legal interoperability across institutions |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for explicitly identifying the **interoperability level** in a given scenario. Always state the level in your answer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body. The **heart**, **lungs**, **kidneys**, and **brain** are all separate organs built by different tissues, with different functions and even different "languages" (electrical, chemical, mechanical). Yet they work in perfect synchrony because the **nervous system and bloodstream** act as universal **integration buses**, carrying signals and chemicals between organs.

**Telemedicine System Integration** is the digital equivalent of building that nervous system for healthcare:

- The **organs** = the hospital systems (HIS, PACS, LIS, EMR, billing, pharmacy).
- The **nervous system** = the **middleware / Integration Engine (e.g., Mirth Connect, Rhapsody, InterSystems HealthShare)**.
- The **common language** = the **HL7 / FHIR / DICOM standards**.
- The **brain's coordination** = the **clinical workflow engine** that decides what happens when a message arrives.

Without integration, each system is an **island**; with integration, the entire hospital becomes a **smart, connected organism** capable of remote diagnosis, tele-consultation, and continuous patient monitoring.

> [!TIP]
> When answering KTU questions, use this **"Organs and Nerves"** analogy if you are asked to "explain in simple terms" — examiners reward lucid, imaginative explanations.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards and Metrics

- **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** — message standard. **Versions 2.x (pipe-delimited) and 3 (XML/RIM based)**, with **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** being the modern RESTful standard.
- **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** — the **de-facto standard for medical images**; a single CT slice is typically **0.5 MB** uncompressed.
- **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** — profiles that bind HL7 + DICOM into clinical workflows (e.g., XDS, XDS-I, ATNA, PIX).
- **Typical clinical bandwidth requirement** for a tele-consultation: **384 kbps (CIF)** to **2 Mbps (HD)**.
- **Mean acceptable end-to-end latency** for real-time tele-echography / tele-surgery: **≤ 150 ms (one-way)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of System Integration Approaches

System integration in telemedicine can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Architectural Style (How the systems are wired)**
   - **Point-to-Point (P2P)** — direct connections; simple but unscalable.
   - **Hub-and-Spoke (Star)** — central integration engine; **most common in hospitals**.
   - **Bus / Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM)** — asynchronous pub/sub.
   - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** — web services (SOAP / REST).
   - **Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)** — Kafka / MQTT streams for IoMT.

2. **Functional Depth**
   - **Data Integration** — move bits between databases.
   - **Application Integration** — share business logic.
   - **Process Integration** — orchestrate multi-system workflows (BPMN engines).
   - **Presentation Integration** — unified UI (e.g., clinician portal).

3. **Coupling Level**
   - **Tight Coupling** — systems call each other directly; brittle.
   - **Loose Coupling** — interaction via messages / APIs; **the KTU-recommended approach**.

4. **Integration Boundary**
   - **Intra-enterprise** (inside one hospital).
   - **Inter-enterprise** (between hospitals, labs, insurance).
   - **Patient-facing** (mHealth, wearables).

---

## 2.2 The Canonical Telemedicine Integration Stack

A typical KTU-modeled telemedicine platform is layered as follows (top-down):

- **Layer 5 — Presentation Layer**: Web portal, mobile app, clinician dashboard.
- **Layer 4 — Workflow / Process Layer**: BPMN engine, scheduling, tele-consultation session manager.
- **Layer 3 — Application Services Layer**: API Gateway (REST/FHIR), authentication (OAuth 2.0 / SMART-on-FHIR).
- **Layer 2 — Integration / Middleware Layer**: Mirth/Rhapsody, ESB, message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka).
- **Layer 1 — Data Standards Layer**: HL7 v2.x, HL7 FHIR, DICOM, X12 (billing), NCPDP (pharmacy).
- **Layer 0 — Physical / Transport Layer**: LAN, WAN, MPLS, 4G/5G, satellite, VPN, TLS 1.3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each layer has its own **failure modes**. The **Integration Engine** (Layer 2) is the single point that, if it fails, brings down the entire hospital's communication. KTU loves to ask about **"single point of failure"** mitigation via **clustering, load-balancing, and active-active redundancy**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **derived from first principles** in Section 3. They are mathematically verifiable.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Application |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----|:------------|
| 1 | Data Transfer Time | $T = \dfrac{S}{B \cdot \eta}$ | seconds (s) | Time to push an image/EMR |
| 2 | Effective Throughput | $B_{eff} = B \cdot \eta$ | bits per second (bps) | After overhead deduction |
| 3 | End-to-End Latency | $T_{lat} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | seconds (s) | Tele-consultation feel |
| 4 | One-Way Light-Travel Delay | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c \cdot n}$ | seconds (s) | Satellite link budget |
| 5 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | unitless | DICOM JPEG2000 lossless |
| 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = B \cdot RTT$ | bits | TCP window sizing |
| 7 | Image Size (uncompressed) | $S = R \cdot C \cdot D \cdot k$ | bytes | $R,C$ rows/cols, $D$ depth, $k=2$ |
| 8 | System Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | fraction (0–1) | "Five 9s" = 99.999% |
| 9 | Annual Downtime | $D = (1-A) \cdot 525{,}600$ | minutes/year | SLA compliance |
| 10 | ROI of Integration | $ROI = \dfrac{(Benefit - Cost)}{Cost} \times 100\%$ | percent | TCO justification |

**Legend of symbols** (KTU accepts any consistent notation):
- $S$ = File/data size (bits)
- $B$ = Nominal link bandwidth (bps)
- $\eta$ = Protocol efficiency factor (typically **0.75–0.85** for TCP/IP, **0.95** for UDP)
- $T_{tx}$ = Transmission delay, $T_{prop}$ = Propagation delay, $T_{proc}$ = Processing delay, $T_{queue}$ = Queuing delay
- $d$ = Link distance (m), $c$ = $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $n$ = refractive index of medium (≈ 1.5 for fiber)
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $525{,}600 = 365 \times 24 \times 60$ minutes per year

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tele-radiology PACS integration** uses **DICOM C-STORE / C-MOVE** to push a 250 MB CT study across a 100 Mbps link — engineers must compute transfer time to dimension storage and pre-fetch caches.
- **Tele-ICU continuous monitoring** generates **~1 GB/day/bed** of waveform data; integration with the **EHR via HL7 FHIR Observation resources** demands queuing theory (M/M/1 model) to size the broker.
- **mHealth wearables** (e.g., continuous glucose monitors) send **~10 messages/min** at low bandwidth but high message rate — integration must use **MQTT** (low overhead) over **HL7 v2** (heavyweight).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> The following derivations are **mandatory exam-prep territory**. Skipping these steps costs 3–4 marks per question in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Data Transfer Time for a DICOM Image

**Problem context (KTU-style):** A radiologist at Hospital A wishes to push a CT series of 512 slices to Hospital B. Each slice is a $1024 \times 1024$ matrix, 16-bit pixel depth. The link is 100 Mbps Ethernet with a protocol overhead factor $\eta = 0.80$. Find the transfer time.

**Step 1 — Compute the uncompressed size of one slice.**

Each pixel is 16 bits = 2 bytes. Number of pixels per slice = $1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576$.

$$
S_{slice} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pixels} \times 2 \text{ bytes/pixel}
$$

$$
S_{slice} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} \approx 2 \text{ MB per slice}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total series size for 512 slices.**

$$
S_{total} = 512 \times 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert bytes to bits** (network bandwidth is in bits per second).

$$
S_{bits} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

$$
S_{bits} = 8{,}589{,}934{,}592 \text{ bits} \approx 8.59 \text{ Gbits}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Data Transfer Time formula with overhead.**

$$
T = \frac{S_{bits}}{B \cdot \eta}
$$

$$
T = \frac{8.59 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \times 0.80}
$$

$$
T = \frac{8.59 \times 10^{9}}{8.0 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
T = 107.37 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.79 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

A full CT series transfers in **~1.8 minutes** on a 100 Mbps link. If we used **lossless JPEG2000** compression with $CR = 3$, the time drops to **~36 seconds**, justifying the integration of a **DICOM compression gateway** in the PACS-to-PACS bridge.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Latency Budget for a Tele-Surgery Link

**Problem context:** A tele-surgery setup uses a geostationary satellite at 36,000 km altitude. Compute the one-way propagation delay, then add the encoding, decoding, and queuing delays to obtain total latency. State whether the link is acceptable for tele-surgery (threshold ≤ 150 ms).

**Step 1 — Compute the slant range.**

For geostationary orbit, the one-way distance from Earth's surface to the satellite is approximately:

$$
d \approx 36{,}000 \text{ km} = 3.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}
$$

The signal travels **uplink** (Earth → satellite) and **downlink** (satellite → Earth), but for one-way latency we consider a single leg only.

**Step 2 — Compute propagation delay** (refractive index $n \approx 1$ for free space).

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{c \cdot n} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
T_{prop} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — For a complete round-trip (surgeon ↔ patient) via satellite**, the signal traverses the link **twice** (uplink + downlink of surgeon, and the same for patient response):

$$
T_{prop, RTT} = 4 \times 120 \text{ ms} = 480 \text{ ms}
$$

Wait — let us be careful. **For a two-way interactive dialogue** where surgeon and patient are both on Earth using **one satellite as a bent-pipe relay**, the signal goes:

Surgeon → Satellite → Patient (1 leg each, 120 ms each, total 240 ms one-way) and then Patient → Satellite → Surgeon (another 240 ms). For an interactive loop the **full round-trip** is **~480 ms**.

**Step 4 — Add encoding + decoding + queuing delays.**

Assume $T_{proc} = 30$ ms (video codec), $T_{queue} = 10$ ms (router buffering).

$$
T_{lat, one-way} = T_{prop, one-way} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}
$$

$$
T_{lat, one-way} = 120 + 30 + 10 = 160 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the tele-surgery threshold.**

Since $160 \text{ ms} > 150 \text{ ms}$, the link is **borderline unacceptable** for force-feedback tele-surgery. The only way to make it usable is to use **LEO constellations (Starlink at ~550 km altitude)**, which gives:

$$
T_{prop, LEO} = \frac{550{,}000}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 1.83 \text{ ms per leg}
$$

Total RTT ≈ **~15–30 ms**, well within the safe envelope.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — System Availability and Annual Downtime

**Problem context:** A telemedicine hub has $MTBF = 5000$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour. Compute (a) availability, (b) annual downtime in minutes.

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} = \frac{5000}{5001}
$$

$$
A = 0.99980 = 99.980\%
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to annual downtime.**

$$
D = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D = (1 - 0.99980) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D = 0.00020 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D = 105.12 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 45 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on SLA compliance.**

For **"Five-Nines"** ($A = 99.999\%$), the allowed downtime is:

$$
D_{5n} = (1 - 0.99999) \times 525{,}600 = 5.256 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

To achieve "Five-Nines", we need clustering + automatic failover, typically with $MTTR \leq 36$ seconds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Integration Style

| Scenario | Recommended Architecture | Standard | Justification |
|:---------|:-------------------------|:---------|:--------------|
| Two legacy HIS systems (one hospital merger) | **Point-to-Point** with **HL7 v2 bridge** | HL7 v2.x | Lowest cost, low message volume |
| Hospital-wide EHR rollout | **Hub-and-Spoke** (Integration Engine) | HL7 v2 + FHIR | Centralized control, audit logging |
| Multi-hospital HIE (Health Information Exchange) | **Bus / SOA** with **IHE XDS** | IHE XDS, ATNA | Document registry + repository model |
| Real-time ICU streaming | **Event-Driven (Kafka/MQTT)** | HL7 FHIR Subscriptions | High throughput, low latency |
| Mobile mHealth app for patients | **REST API Gateway** | HL7 FHIR R4, OAuth 2.0 | Lightweight, developer-friendly |
| Cross-border tele-consultation | **Federated SOA + Blockchain ledger** | HL7 FHIR + XCA | Audit, consent, cross-jurisdiction |

> [!TIP]
> KTU question stems often describe a scenario and ask "Which integration architecture is most suitable?" — this matrix is your ready reckoner. Justify your choice with **at least two technical reasons** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Telemedicine System Integration — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical **Hub-and-Spoke** integration of a telemedicine platform, showing data flow between clinical systems, the integration engine, and external endpoints.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hospital Information System HIS]:::src
    B[Electronic Medical Record EMR]:::src
    C[PACS Imaging Server]:::src
    D[Laboratory Information System LIS]:::src
    E[Pharmacy Management System]:::src
    F[IoMT Wearable Devices]:::src

    G[Integration Engine Mirth Rhapsody]:::core
    H[Message Queue RabbitMQ Kafka]:::core
    I[API Gateway FHIR REST]:::core
    J[Identity Provider OAuth 2.0]:::core

    K[Tele Consultation Portal]:::client
    L[Mobile mHealth App]:::client
    M[Specialist Clinician Dashboard]:::client
    N[Public Health Registry]:::ext
    O[Insurance Payer System]:::ext

    P[Audit and Logging Service]:::sec
    Q[TLS 1.3 VPN Firewall]:::sec

    A -->|HL7 v2 ADT| G
    B -->|HL7 FHIR R4| I
    C -->|DICOM C-STORE| G
    D -->|HL7 v2 ORU| G
    E -->|NCPDP SCRIPT| G
    F -->|MQTT HL7 FHIR| H
    G --> H
    I --> H
    J -.->|OAuth tokens| I
    H --> K
    H --> L
    H --> M
    H --> N
    H --> O
    P -.->|logs| G
    P -.->|logs| I
    Q --> G
    Q --> I

    classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef client fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef sec fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- **Blue nodes (A–F)** are **source clinical systems** that emit events (admissions, lab results, images, vitals).
- **Yellow nodes (G–J)** form the **integration core**: the engine translates protocols, the queue buffers bursts, the gateway exposes APIs, and the IdP issues security tokens.
- **Green nodes (K–M)** are **consumers** — humans or apps receiving data.
- **Red nodes (N, O)** are **external inter-enterprise partners**.
- **Purple nodes (P, Q)** are **cross-cutting security services** (audit + transport security).

---

## 4.2 Integration Failure and Recovery Flow

The following Mermaid state diagram shows how a robust integration engine handles message delivery failures — a frequent KTU question on "operational issues".

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Received
    Received --> Validating
    Validating --> Persisted: ACK to source
    Validating --> DLQ: schema error
    Persisted --> Routing
    Routing --> Delivered: success
    Routing --> Retrying: transient error
    Retrying --> Routing: attempt less than 3
    Retrying --> DLQ: attempts exhausted
    Delivered --> [*]
    DLQ --> ManualReview
    ManualReview --> Routing: operator fixes
    ManualReview --> Quarantine: poison message
    Quarantine --> [*]
```

**Key operational concepts embedded in this diagram:**

- **ACK (Acknowledgement)** — sender is informed the message was safely stored before processing.
- **DLQ (Dead-Letter Queue)** — quarantine for un-processable messages; prevents infinite retry loops.
- **At-Least-Once Delivery** — the default guarantee; duplicates are handled by downstream idempotency keys (FHIR `versionId`).
- **Poison Message** — a message that consistently fails; escalated to a human operator.

---

## 4.3 Interoperability Layering — A Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L5[Layer 5 Presentation Portal]:::top
    L4[Layer 4 Workflow BPMN]:::mid
    L3[Layer 3 Application Services API]:::mid
    L2[Layer 2 Integration Engine ESB]:::mid
    L1[Layer 1 Standards HL7 DICOM FHIR]:::mid
    L0[Layer 0 Transport Network]:::bot

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5 -.->|user feedback| L4

    classDef top fill:#198754,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#fd7e14,color:#fff
    classDef bot fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The downward arrows show the **call/dependency direction**; the upward arrow shows **telemetry and feedback**. This is a **classic KTU 2-mark sub-question** to "draw and label the telemedicine integration stack".

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** In System Integration questions, students frequently **omit the standard name/version** (e.g., writing "HL7" instead of "HL7 v2.5 or FHIR R4"). This alone costs **1 mark**. Always specify the **standard, version, and message type** (e.g., "HL7 v2.5 ADT^A01 message"). Similarly, never draw a diagram without **labeling arrows and naming protocols** — a bare block diagram earns only 2 of 5 marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define System Integration in the context of Telemedicine. List any three major integration challenges faced in a multi-hospital telemedicine network.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

**Definition (1 mark):** System Integration in Telemedicine is the process of interconnecting heterogeneous clinical, administrative, and communication systems (HIS, EMR, PACS, LIS) using standard interfaces and middleware to enable seamless data exchange and coordinated workflows across geographically distributed healthcare providers.

**Three major challenges (2 marks — 1/2 mark each, brief explanation):**

1. **Heterogeneity of data formats** — different vendors use proprietary schemas; requires HL7/FHIR translation.
2. **Interoperability barriers** — semantic mismatches between coding systems (ICD-10 vs SNOMED-CT).
3. **Security and privacy** — exchanging PHI across institutions risks breaches; demands end-to-end encryption, ABHA/Aadhaar-based consent (in Indian context).
4. *(any other valid point: bandwidth, latency, change management, regulatory compliance)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between HL7 v2.x and HL7 FHIR. Mention any two advantages of FHIR for telemedicine integration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HL7 v2.x | HL7 FHIR |
|:-------|:---------|:---------|
| **Format** | Pipe-delimited text (e.g., `PID\x1c...\x1c`) | JSON / XML over RESTful HTTP |
| **Parsing** | Custom parsers needed | Standard web tools (Postman, browsers) |
| **Learning curve** | Steep | Gentle for web developers |
| **Resource model** | Segments/messages | RESTful resources (Patient, Observation) |

**Two advantages of FHIR for telemedicine (2 marks):**

1. **Native web compatibility** — works with mobile apps, wearables, and cloud platforms using standard HTTPS, eliminating custom VPN tunnels.
2. **Built-in OAuth 2.0 / SMART-on-FHIR** — supports fine-grained, patient-mediated consent, crucial for cross-institutional tele-consultation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Hub-and-Spoke architecture** for integrating a hospital's HIS, PACS, and LIS into a unified telemedicine platform. Discuss the role of an **Integration Engine** and the **HL7 v2.x and DICOM standards** in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tele-cardiology consultation streams a 4-lead ECG at 500 samples/s, 12-bit resolution, from a remote clinic to a tertiary hospital 200 km away over a 4G link (nominal 50 Mbps, protocol efficiency 0.85). Compute: **(7 marks)**

   (i) The data rate generated by the ECG source in kbps.
   (ii) The end-to-end one-way latency, given propagation speed $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber, processing delay 5 ms, queuing delay 2 ms.
   (iii) The number of such patients that can be simultaneously monitored on a single 50 Mbps link (assuming negligible overhead per stream).

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the Hub-and-Spoke with three sources (HIS, PACS, LIS) at the perimeter, a central Integration Engine, and downstream consumers (clinician dashboard, EHR, tele-consultation portal). Label all arrows with the relevant protocol (HL7 v2.x ADT/ORU for HIS and LIS, DICOM C-STORE for PACS). *(See Section 4.1 Mermaid for reference.)*

**Integration Engine role (3 marks):**

- Acts as the **central message broker**; receives messages from all source systems in their native format.
- **Translates** proprietary schemas to standard HL7 v2.x or FHIR format using transform scripts/maps.
- **Routes** messages to the correct downstream consumer based on message type and content filters.
- Provides **reliable delivery**: ACK/NACK, retries, DLQ, message persistence.
- **Logs** every transaction for HIPAA/ABDM audit compliance.

**HL7 v2.x role (1 mark):** Used for **administrative (ADT — Admission/Discharge/Transfer)**, **clinical (ORU — Observation Result)**, and **financial** messaging. For our scenario: HIS emits ADT^A01 on patient admission; LIS emits ORU^R01 on lab result availability.

**DICOM role (1 mark):** Used for **medical image transfer** between modalities (CT, MRI) and PACS, and from PACS to the tele-consultation portal via **DICOM C-STORE / C-FIND / C-MOVE** service classes.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Data rate of one ECG stream (2 marks):**

Sample rate = 500 samples/s. Resolution = 12 bits/sample. Number of leads = 4.

$$
R_{ECG} = 4 \text{ leads} \times 500 \text{ samples/s} \times 12 \text{ bits/sample}
$$

$$
R_{ECG} = 24{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 24 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(ii) End-to-end one-way latency (3 marks):**

Propagation delay:

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
T_{prop} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

Transmission delay (negligible at this data rate, but we include for completeness — using effective bandwidth):

$$
B_{eff} = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 0.85 = 42.5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{R_{ECG}}{B_{eff}} = \frac{24{,}000}{42.5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.00056 \text{ ms}
$$

Total latency:

$$
T_{lat} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}
$$

$$
T_{lat} \approx 0 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

This is **well within the 150 ms threshold** for clinical acceptability.

**(iii) Number of simultaneous patients (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{B_{eff}}{R_{ECG}} = \frac{42.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}}{24 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
N \approx 1770 \text{ patients}
$$

**Engineering note:** In practice, the limit is set by **server-side processing** and **cellular uplink contention**, not raw bandwidth. Realistic planning assumes **~50–100 patients per cell sector**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **interoperability**? Explain the **four levels of interoperability** defined by HIMSS, with one telemedicine example for each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Integration Engine of a regional telemedicine hub has $MTBF = 8000$ hours and $MTTR = 2$ hours. The hospital plans to upgrade to a clustered engine with $MTBF = 25{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 0.5$ hours. Compute: **(7 marks)**

   (i) The availability and annual downtime (in minutes) of the **current** engine.
   (ii) The availability and annual downtime of the **upgraded** engine.
   (iii) Comment on whether the upgrade meets a "Five-Nines" ($99.999\%$) SLA.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Interoperability is the ability of two or more systems to exchange, interpret, and use information seamlessly.

**Four levels (6 marks — 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Foundational (L1):** Basic electronic transport of data from one system to another. No interpretation.  
   *Example:* A wearable fitness band pushes raw heart-rate bytes via Bluetooth to a phone — the phone just stores the number.

2. **Structural (L2):** Defines the **format** of messages so the receiver can parse them.  
   *Example:* A PACS sends a DICOM file with standard tag structure that any DICOM viewer can open.

3. **Semantic (L3):** Ensures **shared meaning** of data across systems using common vocabularies.  
   *Example:* Two hospitals using **SNOMED-CT** to code "Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus" — both understand it as the same concept, regardless of local language.

4. **Organizational (L4):** Aligns **policy, workflow, and legal frameworks** so information can flow across institutions despite different governance.  
   *Example:* Two hospitals in different states sharing tele-radiology reports after signing a **Business Associate Agreement (BAA)** and integrating with the national **ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission)** health ID.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Current engine (2.5 marks):**

$$
A_{cur} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 2} = \frac{8000}{8002}
$$

$$
A_{cur} = 0.99975 = 99.975\%
$$

$$
D_{cur} = (1 - 0.99975) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00025 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{cur} = 131.4 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 2 \text{ hours } 11 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**(ii) Upgraded engine (2.5 marks):**

$$
A_{up} = \frac{25000}{25000 + 0.5} = \frac{25000}{25000.5}
$$

$$
A_{up} = 0.99998 = 99.998\%
$$

$$
D_{up} = (1 - 0.99998) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00002 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{up} = 10.512 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**(iii) SLA comment (2 marks):**

Five-Nines requires $A \geq 0.99999$. The upgraded engine gives $A = 0.99998$, which is **just short of Five-Nines** by **0.001%**. To reach Five-Nines, the hospital must:

- Add **geographic redundancy** (active-active across two data centers).
- Use **automated failover** with health-check probes, reducing MTTR further to ≤ 0.1 hours (6 minutes).
- Implement **chaos engineering** testing (Netflix Chaos Monkey style) to validate resilience.

With these additional measures, $MTTR \approx 0.1$ hr would yield:

$$
A = \frac{25000}{25000.1} = 0.999996 \approx 99.9996\%
$$

This **comfortably exceeds Five-Nines**.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these bullets before entering the exam hall.** Each is a potential 1- or 2-mark sub-question.

- **System Integration** = connecting heterogeneous healthcare systems via standards + middleware to behave as one logical system.
- **Four levels of interoperability** (HIMSS): **Foundational → Structural → Semantic → Organizational**.
- **HL7 v2.x** = pipe-delimited, mature, hospital backbone (ADT, ORU, ORM, MDM messages).
- **HL7 FHIR** = modern, RESTful, JSON/XML, web- and mobile-friendly; uses **resources** like `Patient`, `Observation`, `Encounter`.
- **DICOM** = standard for medical images; **service classes** include C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET.
- **IHE profiles** (XDS, XDS-I, ATNA, PIX, PDQ) bind HL7+DICOM into end-to-end clinical workflows.
- **Integration Engine** examples: **Mirth Connect (open-source), Rhapsody, InterSystems HealthShare, Cloverleaf**.
- **Architecture patterns**: Point-to-Point, Hub-and-Spoke (most common), Bus/MOM (Kafka), SOA, EDA.
- **Coupling**: prefer **loose coupling** (message-based) over tight coupling (direct API calls).
- **Data Transfer Time formula**: $T = S / (B \cdot \eta)$.
- **End-to-End Latency formula**: $T_{lat} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$.
- **Tele-surgery latency threshold** ≤ **150 ms one-way**; tele-consultation ≤ **400 ms**.
- **Five-Nines availability** = **99.999%** = **5.256 min downtime/year**.
- **Operational issues** to mention in essays: **change management, user training, vendor lock-in, data migration, downtime procedures, regulatory compliance (HIPAA / ABDM / DISHA in India)**.
- **Security triad in integration**: **Confidentiality (TLS 1.3 + AES-256), Integrity (HMAC, digital signatures), Availability (clustering, DDoS protection)**.
- **Consent management**: **SMART-on-FHIR** for patient-mediated granular consent.
- **Always specify standard + version + message type** in exam answers (e.g., "HL7 v2.5 ADT^A04") to earn full marks.
- **Diagrams must have labeled arrows** showing protocols — a block diagram with bare lines earns only partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Store-and-Forward Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Store-and-Forward Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Store-and-Forward Operation** is a fundamental asynchronous mode of telemedicine service delivery in which clinically relevant medical data — including still images, video clips, audio recordings, scanned documents, biosignals, and structured patient history — is **captured at the patient/primary-care end, stored locally, and later transmitted electronically** to a remote specialist or diagnostic center for interpretation, analysis, and clinical decision-making at a deferred time.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT862 - Telemedicine)** syllabus, Store-and-Forward (S&F) is classified as one of the three principal operational models of telemedicine, the other two being **Real-Time (Synchronous) Consultation** and **Remote Monitoring (Telesurveillance)**. It is also referred to in literature as **"Asynchronous Telemedicine"** or **"Deferred Consultation Telemedicine."**

### Standard Operational Pipeline

$$
\underbrace{\text{Data Acquisition}}_{\text{Stage 1}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Local Storage}}_{\text{Stage 2}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Secure Transmission}}_{\text{Stage 3}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Specialist Review}}_{\text{Stage 4}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Diagnostic Feedback}}_{\text{Stage 5}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The S&F model is *preferred* in specialties such as **Dermatology, Radiology, Pathology, Ophthalmology, and Wound Care** because these disciplines rely heavily on **high-resolution static images** and **structured patient metadata** rather than live verbal/physical examination.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you take a photograph of a strange skin rash on your arm using your phone. Instead of rushing to a dermatologist's clinic, you **save the photo** on your phone, then at night you **upload (forward) the image** to a skin specialist in another city via an app. The specialist opens it the next morning, examines it, types a diagnosis, and sends you a prescription through the same app. This entire exchange did **not** happen live — it was stored, sent, reviewed, and answered — all in **deferred time**.

**The Plain-English "S&F Analogy" — The "Courier Post" Model:**

Think of Store-and-Forward as a **registered postal parcel system** for medical information:
- **STORE** = The sender (patient/clinic) packs the medical record into a "parcel."
- **FORWARD** = The parcel is handed to a secure courier (internet/network).
- **RECEIVE & OPEN** = The specialist physician opens the parcel at their convenience.
- **REPLY** = The doctor writes back a report and posts it back to the sender.

| Everyday Object | S&F Telemedicine Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Postal parcel | Encrypted medical data packet |
| Postage stamp | Encryption/authentication header |
| Post office queue | Store buffer (local server/PACS) |
| Mailbox | Specialist's inbox/EMR portal |
| Return receipt | Diagnostic report / feedback message |

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Asynchronous"?** Because the **sender (patient) and receiver (specialist) are NOT online at the same time.** Time decoupling is the defining feature of S&F — unlike real-time telemedicine where both parties are simultaneously present (synchronous).

---

## 1.3 Quantitative Metrics & Standards Used in S&F

The following standardized performance metrics are routinely used to evaluate S&F telemedicine systems:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **DICOM** (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) — **the universal image standard** for radiology S&F systems.
> - **HL7** (Health Level Seven) — the messaging standard for **structured patient metadata** exchange.
> - **PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System) — **the storage backbone** of S&F in hospitals.
> - **IHE** (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise) — profiles defining **workflow interoperability**.
> - **Bandwidth Requirement:** typically **256 kbps – 10 Mbps** depending on image modality.
> - **File Sizes:** Dermatology photo ≈ **500 KB – 2 MB**, Mammogram ≈ **50 MB**, CT slice ≈ **512 KB**, Whole CT volume ≈ **150 – 500 MB**.
> - **Standard Image Formats:** JPEG, JPEG2000, TIFF, DICOM (.dcm), PNG.
> - **Encryption Standard:** **AES-256**, **TLS 1.3**, and **HIPAA-compliant** transmission.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Store-and-Forward — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The S&F model is structured into **five sequential operational layers**. Each layer has a distinct engineering responsibility.

### Layer 1 — Data Acquisition (Capture Stage)

- Patient data is captured using **digital medical peripherals** such as:
  - **Dermatoscope** (for dermatology — captures magnified skin images at **10x – 70x** magnification)
  - **Digital X-ray sensor / Flat-panel detector** (radiology)
  - **Whole-slide scanner** (pathology — typically **40x** objective, producing **gigapixel** slides)
  - **Fundus camera / OCT device** (ophthalmology)
  - **ECG / SpO₂ recorder** (cardiology)
  - **Smartphone camera** (low-resource settings, mHealth-S&F)
- Data is encoded in standard formats (DICOM, JPEG, TIFF, PDF).
- **Acquisition latency** depends on the imaging device and is the **first source of delay** in the S&F pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why" of Capture:** Every later stage is only as accurate as the captured image. In radiology, a poorly positioned chest X-ray is useless to a remote radiologist — **garbage in, garbage out** applies directly to S&F.

### Layer 2 — Local Storage (Buffering Stage)

- The captured data is stored on a **local server, PACS node, or cloud buffer** with metadata (patient ID, timestamp, modality, referring physician).
- **DICOM Header** carries fields such as:
  - `(0010,0010)` — Patient Name
  - `(0008,0020)` — Study Date
  - `(0008,0060)` — Modality (CT, MR, US, etc.)
  - `(0028,0010)` — Image Rows
  - `(0028,0011)` — Image Columns
- **Storage sizing formula** (see Section 3 for derivation):

$$
S_{\text{storage}} = N_{\text{studies}} \times \bar{F}_{\text{size}} \times R_{\text{retention}}
$$

where $N_{\text{studies}}$ is annual study count, $\bar{F}_{\text{size}}$ is average file size, and $R_{\text{retention}}$ is retention period in years.

### Layer 3 — Secure Transmission (Forwarding Stage)

- Data is transmitted over **secure channels** (typically HTTPS/TLS, VPN, or dedicated healthcare networks like **SWAN — State Wide Area Network** in India).
- **Compression** may be applied using **lossless** (for diagnostic accuracy — DICOM supports JPEG-LS, JPEG2000) or **lossy** (for non-diagnostic previews).
- Bandwidth-time relationship:

$$
T_{\text{transmit}} = \frac{F_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{eff}}} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

where $B_{\text{eff}}$ is **effective bandwidth** (after protocol overheads).

### Layer 4 — Specialist Review (Asynchronous Interpretation)

- The remote specialist accesses the case via a **DICOM viewer, PACS web client, or telemedicine portal**.
- A **structured report** is created (often using **HL7 CDA — Clinical Document Architecture** or **DICOM-SR — Structured Report**).
- Turn-around time (TAT) is a key performance metric:

$$
\text{TAT} = T_{\text{received}} - T_{\text{submitted}}
$$

### Layer 5 — Diagnostic Feedback (Closure Stage)

- The report is delivered back to the referring physician through the same secure channel.
- Integrated with **Electronic Medical Record (EMR)** systems via **HL7 messaging**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Metric | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission Time | $T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{size}}}{B_{\text{eff}}}$ | seconds (s) | S&F upload/download delay |
| 2 | Storage Sizing | $S = N \cdot \bar{F} \cdot R$ | bytes (B) | PACS capacity planning |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{F_{\text{orig}}}{F_{\text{comp}}}$ | unitless | Lossless vs lossy comparison |
| 4 | Bit Rate | $R_b = f_s \times n_b$ | bits/second (bps) | Raw image bitrate |
| 5 | Image Size (uncompressed) | $F = W \times H \times D \times n_b$ | bytes | Single image size |
| 6 | PSNR (image quality) | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | dB | Lossy compression quality |
| 7 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{mn}\sum (I - K)^2$ | unitless | Compression fidelity |
| 8 | Turn-Around Time | $\text{TAT} = t_{\text{review}} - t_{\text{submit}}$ | hours | Service-level KPI |
| 9 | Throughput | $\text{Th} = \dfrac{N_{\text{cases}}}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | cases/day | S&F system capacity |
| 10 | Bandwidth Utilization | $\eta_B = \dfrac{B_{\text{used}}}{B_{\text{allocated}}}$ | % | Network efficiency |

> [!TIP]
> **Where:**
> - $F_{\text{size}}$ = file size in bits, $B_{\text{eff}}$ = effective bandwidth (bps)
> - $W, H$ = image width/height in pixels, $D$ = bit depth (e.g., 8, 12, 16)
> - $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $n_b$ = bits per sample
> - $L$ = peak signal value (e.g., 255 for 8-bit), MSE = Mean Squared Error
> - $N_{\text{cases}}$ = number of cases processed, $T_{\text{period}}$ = time window

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute values in tables** — KTU board answers use LaTeX, and pipe symbols break markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | S&F Use Case | Clinical Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Teleradiology** | Off-hours CT/MRI reading from distant centers | **24/7** radiological coverage for rural hospitals |
| **Teledermatology** | Mole/lesion image submission via smartphone app | **80%** triage accuracy in benign vs malignant cases |
| **Telepathology** | Whole-slide image (WSI) transmission for cancer diagnosis | **Sub-second navigation** of gigapixel slides remotely |
| **Teleophthalmology** | Diabetic retinopathy screening using fundus photos | Population-scale **DR screening** in primary care |
| **Wound Care** | Periodic wound photos from home health nurses | Chronic wound monitoring without travel |
| **Tele-cardiology** | ECG strips uploaded to cardiologist | Faster MI detection in remote EDs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Image File Size Formula (Uncompressed Bitmap)

A medical image, when stored uncompressed as a raw bitmap, has a file size determined by its dimensions and bit depth.

### Given:
- Width = $W$ pixels
- Height = $H$ pixels
- Bit depth = $D$ bits per pixel
- Number of channels = $C$ (e.g., $C = 1$ for grayscale, $C = 3$ for RGB color)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Total number of pixels in a single image:**

$$
N_{\text{pixels}} = W \times H
$$

This counts every individual pixel in the rectangular image matrix.

**Step 2 — Total number of bits per image (single channel):**

$$
B_{\text{single}} = W \times H \times D
$$

This multiplies the pixel count by the number of bits used to represent intensity per pixel.

**Step 3 — Include color channels (for color images):**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = W \times H \times D \times C
$$

Each color channel contributes independently; for an RGB image, $C = 3$.

**Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes (1 byte = 8 bits):**

$$
F_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \times H \times D \times C}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to megabytes (divide by $1024 \times 1024$):**

$$
F_{\text{MB}} = \frac{W \times H \times D \times C}{8 \times 1024^2}
$$

### Worked Example:
A chest X-ray has $W = 2048$, $H = 2048$, $D = 12$ bits, $C = 1$.

$$
F_{\text{MB}} = \frac{2048 \times 2048 \times 12 \times 1}{8 \times 1024^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576}
$$

$$
= \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{8{,}388{,}608}
$$

$$
F_{\text{MB}} = 6.0 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A single **12-bit, 2048×2048** chest X-ray consumes approximately **6 MB** of raw storage. A CT study with 500 such slices would be ≈ **3 GB** uncompressed — justifying lossy compression in S&F.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transmission Time for a Store-and-Forward Case

### Given:
- Total S&F case size = $F$ (in bits)
- Available bandwidth = $B$ (in bits per second)
- Protocol overhead factor = $\alpha$ (typically 10–15%, so effective throughput ≈ $0.85B$)

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Effective usable bandwidth after TCP/IP, encryption, and protocol overheads:**

$$
B_{\text{eff}} = B \times (1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission time using the basic rate-time-size relationship:**

$$
T_{\text{tx}} = \frac{F}{B_{\text{eff}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$
T_{\text{tx}} = \frac{F}{B(1-\alpha)}
$$

### Worked Example:
A teledermatology case = $F = 2$ MB = $2 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 1.6 \times 10^7$ bits.
Available bandwidth $B = 2$ Mbps $= 2 \times 10^6$ bps.
Overhead $\alpha = 0.15$.

$$
B_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.15) = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
T_{\text{tx}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{1.7 \times 10^6} = 9.41 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A small dermatoscopic image (2 MB) uploads in **under 10 seconds** on a 2 Mbps link — easily feasible for S&F over broadband, 4G, or even stable 3G.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) for Lossy Compression Fidelity

PSNR quantifies how much a **lossily compressed** image deviates from the original — critical for S&F diagnostic accuracy.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Mean Squared Error between original ($I$) and compressed ($K$) image of size $m \times n$:**

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m \, n} \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(i,j) - K(i,j) \right]^2
$$

The sum is over all pixel locations, and squaring penalizes large deviations more heavily.

**Step 2 — Peak signal value $L$ (e.g., $L = 255$ for 8-bit, $L = 4095$ for 12-bit):**

The maximum possible pixel value defines the dynamic range.

**Step 3 — PSNR in decibels:**

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}} \right) \text{ dB}
$$

A higher PSNR indicates **less distortion** introduced by compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptable PSNR for diagnostic S&F:** PSNR $\geq$ **40 dB** is generally considered **diagnostically lossless** for radiology. Values **30–40 dB** are acceptable for non-diagnostic previews. Below **30 dB** risks diagnostic error.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use S&F vs Real-Time

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Real-Time (Synchronous) |
|---|---|---|
| Specialist availability needed | No (deferred) | Yes (simultaneous) |
| Bandwidth requirement | Low (256 kbps – 2 Mbps) | High (1 – 10 Mbps) |
| Cost per consultation | **Low** (~$5 – $30) | **High** (~$50 – $200) |
| Diagnostic accuracy (derm.) | 70 – 85% | 85 – 95% |
| Turnaround time | 4 – 48 hours | < 30 minutes |
| Best for | Image-based specialties | Emergency, interactive cases |
| Patient interaction | None | Live video/audio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Store-and-Forward Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient at Remote Clinic] --> B[Medical Data Acquisition Device]
    B --> B1[Dermatoscope]
    B --> B2[Digital X-Ray Sensor]
    B --> B3[Whole Slide Scanner]
    B --> B4[Fundus Camera]
    B --> B5[ECG Recorder]
    B --> C[Local DICOM Gateway Server]
    C --> D[Local PACS Storage Buffer]
    D --> E[HL7 Metadata Encoder]
    E --> F[Encryption Module AES-256]
    F --> G[Secure Transmission Channel TLS 1.3]
    G --> H[Central Cloud PACS Repository]
    H --> I[Specialist Workstation Viewer]
    I --> J[Specialist Diagnosis Report]
    J --> K[HL7 CDA Structured Report Encoder]
    K --> L[Secure Return Channel]
    L --> M[Referring Physician EMR]
    M --> N[Patient Follow-up and Treatment]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#fff4e1
    style N fill:#e1ffe1
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#ffe1e1
```

---

## 4.2 S&F Data Pipeline — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Acquisition Layer]
        A11[Imaging Device] --> A12[Analog to Digital Converter]
        A12 --> A13[Format Encoder JPEG DICOM TIFF]
    end
    subgraph A2[Storage Layer]
        A13 --> A21[Local PACS Node]
        A21 --> A22[Database Patient Metadata]
        A21 --> A23[RAID Disk Array]
    end
    subgraph A3[Transport Layer]
        A21 --> A31[Compression Module]
        A31 --> A32[Encryption TLS VPN]
        A32 --> A33[Network Router Internet SWAN]
    end
    subgraph A4[Review Layer]
        A33 --> A41[Remote Specialist Viewer]
        A41 --> A42[Reporting Tool DICOM-SR]
    end
    subgraph A5[Feedback Layer]
        A42 --> A51[Report Encoder HL7 CDA]
        A51 --> A52[Return Channel]
        A52 --> A53[Referring Physician EMR]
    end
    style A1 fill:#f0f8ff
    style A2 fill:#fffaf0
    style A3 fill:#fff0f0
    style A4 fill:#f0fff0
    style A5 fill:#f8f0ff
```

---

## 4.3 S&F Message Sequence (Asynchronous Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Patient
    participant C as Capturing Clinic
    participant S as Local PACS Server
    participant N as Network Cloud
    participant SP as Remote Specialist
    participant EMR as Referring Physician EMR
    P->>C: Clinical visit, image captured
    C->>S: Upload images + DICOM metadata
    S->>S: Encrypt and queue case
    S->>N: Forward case over TLS
    N->>SP: Deliver to specialist inbox
    Note over SP: Asynchronous delay hours
    SP->>SP: Review images, write report
    SP->>N: Send HL7 CDA report
    N->>EMR: Deliver to referring physician
    EMR->>P: Notification and treatment plan
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Reference: S&F vs Real-Time Telemedicine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Telemedicine Operation Modes] --> B[Asynchronous S&F]
    A --> C[Synchronous Real-Time]
    A --> D[Remote Monitoring]
    B --> B1[Store Capture then Transmit]
    B --> B2[Specialist Reviews Later]
    B --> B3[Report Delivered Deferred]
    C --> C1[Live Video Conference]
    C --> C2[Simultaneous Interaction]
    C --> C3[Immediate Feedback]
    D --> D1[Continuous Vitals Stream]
    D --> D2[Sensor to Cloud]
    D --> D3[Alert on Threshold Breach]
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The S&F block diagram emphasizes **decoupling in time** — the capture block has no real-time dependency on the review block. The transport block is **buffered**, allowing store-and-forward to tolerate intermittent connectivity, unlike real-time telemedicine which requires an **always-on** connection.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Store-and-Forward operation in telemedicine. List any two specialties where it is most commonly applied.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Store-and-Forward (S&F) is an **asynchronous mode of telemedicine** in which patient data such as clinical images, biosignals, scanned reports, and demographic information is **captured, stored locally, and later transmitted** to a remote specialist for evaluation at a **deferred time**, without requiring simultaneous presence of patient and physician.

> **Two specialties where S&F is most applied:**
> 1. **Teleradiology** — transmission of X-ray, CT, MRI images for off-site radiological interpretation.
> 2. **Teledermatology** — submission of dermatoscopic photographs for remote dermatological diagnosis.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Telepathology, Teleophthalmology, Wound-care telemed)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Store-and-Forward and Real-Time telemedicine on the basis of (i) synchronicity, (ii) bandwidth requirement, and (iii) diagnostic turnaround time.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Real-Time Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Synchronicity** | **Asynchronous** — patient and specialist need not be online simultaneously | **Synchronous** — both must be online at the same instant |
| (ii) **Bandwidth** | **Low** — typically 256 kbps – 2 Mbps | **High** — typically 1 – 10 Mbps (live video) |
| (iii) **Turnaround Time** | **Hours to days** (4 – 48 hours typical) | **Minutes** (immediate, < 30 min) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five operational stages** of a Store-and-Forward telemedicine system with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of DICOM and HL7 standards** in ensuring interoperability of Store-and-Forward systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The five operational stages of a Store-and-Forward telemedicine system are:

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:**
Medical data is captured using modality-specific devices such as dermatoscopes (dermatology), flat-panel X-ray detectors (radiology), whole-slide scanners (pathology), and fundus cameras (ophthalmology). Captured signals are digitized by an ADC and encoded into standard formats (DICOM, JPEG, TIFF).

**Stage 2 — Local Storage:**
The captured data, along with patient metadata (name, ID, age, study date), is stored in a local PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) or hospital information server. DICOM headers carry essential metadata fields.

**Stage 3 — Secure Transmission:**
The stored data is encrypted (typically **AES-256** or **TLS 1.3**) and forwarded over secure networks (internet, VPN, SWAN, or 4G/5G). Compression (lossless JPEG-LS or lossy JPEG2000) is applied to reduce bandwidth.

**Stage 4 — Specialist Review:**
The remote specialist accesses the case through a DICOM viewer or PACS web client. The specialist examines the image and clinical history asynchronously, on their own schedule.

**Stage 5 — Diagnostic Feedback:**
The specialist generates a structured report (DICOM-SR or HL7 CDA) and sends it back to the referring physician, who integrates it into the patient's EMR and initiates treatment.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**
`Patient → Imaging Device → Local PACS → Encryption/Compression → Secure Network → Specialist Viewer → Diagnostic Report → Referring Physician EMR → Patient`

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine):**
- DICOM is the **universal standard** for medical image format and transmission, ensuring that an image captured in one vendor's scanner can be **interpreted** by another vendor's viewer.
- It standardizes **header metadata** (patient name, modality, study date, image dimensions) and **network protocols** (DICOM Upper Layer over TCP/IP).
- In S&F, DICOM files are pushed from a modality to PACS using **DICOM C-STORE** and queried using **DICOM C-FIND/C-MOVE**.

**HL7 (Health Level Seven):**
- HL7 is the **messaging standard** for **structured clinical text** data (demographics, lab results, clinical notes, billing).
- Versions include **HL7 v2.x** (pipe-delimited text messages), **HL7 v3** (XML-based), and **FHIR** (RESTful JSON).
- In S&F, HL7 ensures that the **patient demographics, order information, and final reports** are exchanged correctly between the HIS (Hospital Information System) and PACS.

**Interoperability Role:**
- DICOM handles **image binary data and metadata** while HL7 handles **clinical text and workflow messages**.
- Together they enable an **end-to-end interoperable S&F pipeline**: a CT scan (DICOM) and a corresponding radiology report (HL7 ORU message) can be exchanged seamlessly across multi-vendor hospital systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Choice]`

**(a)** With suitable equations, derive the **file size of an uncompressed medical image** and compute the size of a 12-bit, 2048 × 2048 grayscale chest X-ray. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **security and privacy considerations** in Store-and-Forward telemedicine, including encryption and HIPAA/Indian IT Act provisions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Derivation:**

Let $W$ = image width (pixels), $H$ = image height (pixels), $D$ = bit depth (bits/pixel), $C$ = number of color channels.

**Step 1 — Total pixels:**
$$
N_{\text{pixels}} = W \times H
$$

**Step 2 — Total bits per image (single channel):**
$$
B = W \times H \times D
$$

**Step 3 — Multi-channel images:**
$$
B_{\text{total}} = W \times H \times D \times C
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes:**
$$
F_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \times H \times D \times C}{8}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $W = 2048$, $H = 2048$, $D = 12$ bits, $C = 1$ (grayscale).

$$
F_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{2048 \times 2048 \times 12 \times 1}{8}
$$

$$
= \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{8} = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to MB:
$$
F_{\text{MB}} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1024^2} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 6.0 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Result:** The chest X-ray consumes approximately **6 MB** of uncompressed storage.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Security and Privacy Considerations in S&F Telemedicine:**

**1. Encryption in Transit (Network Layer):**
- All S&F data is transmitted over **TLS 1.3** or **IPsec VPN** tunnels.
- Certificate-based mutual authentication prevents man-in-the-middle attacks.

**2. Encryption at Rest (Storage Layer):**
- Local PACS and cloud storage use **AES-256** encryption to protect data at rest.
- Database fields (e.g., patient name, Aadhaar ID) are tokenized or hashed.

**3. Access Control and Authentication:**
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** ensures only authorized physicians and technicians view cases.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** is mandated for portal login.

**4. Audit Trails:**
- Every access (view, download, modify) is logged with **timestamp, user ID, IP, and action**.
- Audit logs are retained for a minimum of **6 years** (HIPAA) or as per hospital policy.

**5. Compliance Frameworks:**

| Framework | Applicability | Key Mandate |
|---|---|---|
| **HIPAA** (USA) | US health data | Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification |
| **DPDP Act 2023** (India) | Indian digital personal data | Consent-based processing, data fiduciary duties |
| **IT Act 2000 + Amendments** (India) | All electronic records | Section 43A — compensation for data misuse; Section 72A — punishment for disclosure |
| **GDPR** (EU) | EU patient data | Right to erasure, data portability, lawful basis |

**6. De-identification and Anonymization:**
- **DICOM anonymization** strips 18+ identifying tags (name, address, institution) before forwarding.
- Critical for research and teaching use of S&F archives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mistakes Students Make):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention BOTH in-transit AND at-rest encryption** — students often mention only HTTPS and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing HL7 with DICOM** — remember: **DICOM = images**, **HL7 = text/messages**.
> 3. **Writing HIPAA without Indian equivalents** — for KTU Kerala context, always mention **IT Act 2000 and DPDP Act 2023**.
> 4. **Skipping the role of DICOM headers** in metadata handling — this is a frequently tested 2-mark concept.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert bits to bytes** in image-size derivations (a very common arithmetic error).
> 6. **Not distinguishing "lossless" vs "lossy" compression** — diagnostic S&F demands lossless or diagnostically-lossless (PSNR ≥ 40 dB).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **S&F is ASYNCHRONOUS** — time-decoupled transmission is its defining feature.
- [x] **Five Stages:** Acquisition → Storage → Transmission → Review → Feedback.
- [x] **DICOM = Images + Binary Metadata** | **HL7 = Text + Workflow Messages** | **PACS = Storage Backbone**.
- [x] **Image File Size:** $F = (W \times H \times D \times C) / 8$ bytes.
- [x] **Transmission Time:** $T_{\text{tx}} = F_{\text{bits}} / B_{\text{eff}}$.
- [x] **Compression:** Lossless (JPEG-LS) for diagnostic, Lossy (JPEG2000) for previews.
- [x] **PSNR ≥ 40 dB** = diagnostically lossless threshold.
- [x] **Encryption:** TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest.
- [x] **Best Specialties:** Teleradiology, Teledermatology, Telepathology, Teleophthalmology.
- [x] **Advantages:** Low bandwidth, low cost, time-flexible, scalable.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** No real-time interaction, delayed diagnosis, depends on capture quality.
- [x] **Indian Compliance:** IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission) standards.
- [x] **PACS Integration:** Uses DICOM C-STORE (push), C-FIND (query), C-MOVE (retrieve).
- [x] **Turn-Around Time (TAT):** typically **4 – 48 hours** for S&F services.
- [x] **Storage Retention:** **6 years minimum** (HIPAA) or per institutional policy.
- [x] **Key Distinction:** S&F ≠ Real-Time. S&F = deferred, buffered, asynchronous; Real-Time = live, synchronous, immediate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Telemedicine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Real-Time Telemedicine # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Real-Time Telemedicine ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Real-Time Telemedicine** is defined as the synchronous delivery of healthcare services, clinical information, and medical expertise between geographically separated participants using interactive telecommunications technologies that operate with **minimal end-to-end latency** (typically < **300 ms** for clinical usability, ideally < **150 ms** for surgical applications). Unlike store-and-forward (asynchronous) telemedicine, real-time telemedicine requires bidirectional, live interaction where the temporal gap between data acquisition and remote interpretation is imperceptible to the human senses. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, real-time telemedicine encompasses **three primary modalities**: 1. **Videoconferencing-based teleconsultation** (clinician–patient or clinician–clinician) 2. **Real-time telemonitoring** (continuous physiological data streaming) 3. **Telementoring and Telesurgery** (live guidance or robotic intervention) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Real-Time Telemedicine is the cornerstone of "live" clinical encounters. The defining characteristic is **synchrony** — both parties interact at the same time, just as in a face-to-face consultation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **video call with your family** living abroad. You see them, hear them, and they respond to you instantly. Now replace the family with a **cardiologist in Thiruvananthapuram and a patient in a village clinic in Wayanad** — connected via a live video link where the doctor can listen to the heart, observe symptoms, and guide the local nurse in real time. That is Real-Time Telemedicine in action. Think of it as a **"virtual OPD"** — the doctor's Out-Patient Department is no longer confined by four walls; it is extended to anywhere a stable network connection exists. A second analogy: Real-Time Telemedicine is to medicine what **live sports broadcasting** is to entertainment — there is no buffer, no "we'll get back to you later" — the action happens *now*, and any lag disrupts the entire experience. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Store-and-Forward:** > - *Store-and-Forward* (Asynchronous): Send a photo of a skin lesion → Doctor reviews later → Replies. Like sending an **email**. > - *Real-Time* (Synchronous): Live video call with doctor. Like a **WhatsApp video call**. > > Both are telemedicine, but only the second is "real-time." ## 1.3 Core Operational Components A real-time telemedicine system is built on the integration of the following components: | Component | Role in Real-Time System | |-----------|--------------------------| | **Audio Channel** | Voice communication; minimum **3.4 kHz** bandwidth for speech intelligibility | | **Video Channel** | Visual examination, wound inspection, gesture-based instruction | | **Data Channel** | Streaming of vitals (ECG, SpO₂, BP) from medical devices | | **Control/Metadata Channel** | Session initiation, device handshake, QoS signaling | | **Synchronization Layer** | Aligns audio/video/data temporally using timestamps (RTP/RTCP) | ## 1.4 Physical & Performance Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Metrics to Memorize:** > - **Maximum acceptable one-way latency:** < **300 ms** (clinical teleconsultation) > - **Ideal latency for telesurgery:** < **150 ms** > - **Minimum video frame rate for clinical assessment:** **15–30 fps** > - **Standard video resolution:** **CIF (352×288)**, **4CIF (704×576)**, or **HD 720p/1080p** > - **Audio codec standard:** G.711 (64 kbps), G.722 (wideband), or Opus | > - **Video codec standard:** **H.264/AVC** (most widely deployed) or **H.265/HEVC** > - **Network protocol stack:** SIP + RTP/RTCP + UDP/IP (preferred for low latency) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Real-Time Telemedicine System A real-time telemedicine system can be decomposed into **five functional layers**, each addressing a specific concern from the physical transmission medium to the clinical application: ### Layer 1 — **Acquisition Layer** This is where biomedical signals and audiovisual data originate. - **Audio:** Microphone picks up clinician/patient speech → sampled at **8 kHz** (narrowband) or **16 kHz** (wideband). - **Video:** Camera captures patient visual signs at **15–30 fps**. - **Medical data:** ECG machine, pulse oximeter, electronic stethoscope stream digitized data (e.g., ECG sampled at **250–500 Hz**). ### Layer 2 — **Encoding / Compression Layer** Raw audio/video is huge (uncompressed HD video ≈ **1.5 Gbps**). Compression is mandatory. - **Audio codecs:** G.711, G.722, G.729, Opus - **Video codecs:** H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, VP8/VP9 - **Medical signal compression:** Wavelet-based, or lossless (for ECG waveform integrity) ### Layer 3 — **Transport Layer** Uses **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** because TCP's retransmission introduces unacceptable delay. **RTP (Real-Time Transport Protocol)** rides on UDP to provide timestamping and sequencing. **RTCP (RTP Control Protocol)** provides QoS feedback (jitter, packet loss). ### Layer 4 — **Network Layer** Operates over **IP networks** — LAN, MAN, WAN, 4G/5G, satellite, or even DSL. - **QoS mechanisms** like DiffServ, MPLS, and traffic shaping prioritize telemedicine packets. ### Layer 5 — **Clinical Application Layer** - Teleconsultation UI - Telemetry dashboards - Telesurgery control consoles - Electronic Medical Record (EMR) integration ## 2.2 Communication Standards & Protocols > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Favourite Topics — Memorize these:** | Standard | Purpose | Key Feature | |----------|---------|-------------| | **H.323** | ITU-T standard for multimedia over packet networks | Used in legacy telemedicine carts;
