supports H.264 video | | **SIP (Session Initiation Protocol)** | IETF standard (RFC 3261) for session setup | Lightweight, modular; foundation of VoIP/telemedicine | | **RTP/RTCP** | Real-time media transport + QoS feedback | Timestamps, sequence numbers, jitter reporting | | **H.264/AVC** | Video compression | 50% better compression than MPEG-2 | | **HL7 / FHIR** | Medical data interoperability | Connects EMR to teleconsultation platform | | **DICOM** | Medical imaging standard | Used when real-time imaging (e.g., ultrasound) is streamed | | **IEEE 11073** | Personal Health Device communication | Standardizes real-time wearable data exchange | ## 2.3 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous — A Comparative View | Parameter | Synchronous (Real-Time) | Asynchronous (Store-and-Forward) | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Interaction mode** | Live, bidirectional | Time-shifted, often unidirectional | | **Latency tolerance** | < 300 ms | Hours to days acceptable | | **Bandwidth need** | High (≥ 384 kbps for video) | Lower (can transmit in chunks) | | **Bandwidth need** | High (≥ 384 kbps for video) | Lower (can transmit in chunks) | | **Clinical examples** | Tele-ICU, telesurgery, emergency triage | Teleradiology, teledermatology, telepathology | | **Technology emphasis** | QoS, jitter control, codec speed | Compression ratio, image fidelity, storage | | **Cost** | Higher (dedicated link, premium devices) | Lower (commodity internet suffices) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Equation / Standard | Units / Notes | |---------|---------------------|---------------| | **One-way latency budget** | $L_{total} = L_{cap} + L_{enc} + L_{net} + L_{dec}$ | ms; must be < 300 ms | | **Network jitter** | $J = \max(t_{arrival} - t_{expected})$ | ms; clinically tolerable < 50 ms | | **Packet loss tolerance** | $P_{loss} < 1\%$ | Above this, video becomes unusable | | **Audio bandwidth (G.711)** | $B = 64$ kbps | PCM, μ-law/A-law | | **Audio bandwidth (G.722)** | $B = 48/56/64$ kbps | Wideband 7 kHz audio | | **Required video bitrate (H.264 SD)** | $B \geq 384$ kbps | For 25–30 fps at CIF/4CIF | | **Required video bitrate (H.264 HD)** | $B \geq 1$ Mbps | For 720p clinical use | | **ECG sampling (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | With $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz for clinical ECG, $f_s \geq 300$ Hz | | **ECG data rate (lossless)** | $R = f_s \times N \times b$ | e.g., $500 \times 12 \times 16$ = 96 kbps per channel | | **H.264 compression ratio** | $CR \approx 50{:}1$ to $100{:}1$ | For typical teleconsultation video | | **Mean Opinion Score (MOS)** | $1 \leq MOS \leq 5$ | $MOS \geq 3.5$ acceptable; $MOS \geq 4.0$ good | > [!NOTE] > **Note on codec bitrate:** G.722's three bitrates (48, 56, 64 kbps) reflect how much of the 16 kHz sampling is used. Wider bandwidth preserves stethoscope fidelity — a key requirement in telecardiology. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Real-time telemedicine systems are deployed in: - **Telesurgery** (e.g., Da Vinci robotic systems, transcontinental Lindbergh operation 2001) - **Tele-ICU** (remote intensivist monitoring of multiple ICUs) - **Telestroke** (golden-hour thrombolysis decision-making via live video) - **Mobile telemedicine ambulances** (5G-enabled, transmitting pre-hospital ECG to hospital) - **Remote robotic ultrasound** (sonographer guides a robotic probe remotely) - **Telepsychiatry** (avoids stigma and travel) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Trade-off Principle:** Every real-time telemedicine system balances the **"triple constraint"** — **latency**, **fidelity**, and **bandwidth cost**. Reducing latency often sacrifices fidelity (lower video quality) or requires expensive bandwidth (leased lines, 5G slicing). KTU exam questions frequently test this trade-off understanding. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Budget for a Real-Time Telemedicine Session A common KTU-style problem: *"Compute whether the latency budget satisfies clinical requirements for the following system configuration."* ### Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2024 Style) > A teleconsultation system has the following measured components: > - Camera capture + A/D conversion: **40 ms** > - H.264 encoding delay: **60 ms** > - Network transit (jitter buffer excluded): **85 ms** > - Jitter buffer wait: **40 ms** > - H.264 decoding: **30 ms** > - Display/present: **25 ms** > - Round-trip audio: measured **RTT = 200 ms** > > **Determine:** > (a) One-way end-to-end latency > (b) Whether it satisfies the < 300 ms threshold > (c) The impact of adding error concealment vs. retransmission > (d) Required network bandwidth for H.264 SD video at 25 fps ### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step) #### Part (a): One-Way End-to-End Latency The total end-to-end latency is the sum of all delay components along the pipeline: $$ L_{total} = L_{cap} + L_{enc} + L_{net} + L_{jb} + L_{dec} + L_{disp} $$ Substituting the given values: $$ L_{total} = 40 + 60 + 85 + 40 + 30 + 25 $$ $$ L_{total} = 280 \text{ ms} $$ **Logic:** Each subsystem contributes its own delay; we accumulate them because they occur in series along the media path. #### Part (b): Compliance with Clinical Threshold Comparing with the KTU-recommended threshold: $$ L_{total} = 280 \text{ ms} \quad \text{vs.} \quad L_{thresh} = 300 \text{ ms} $$ Since $280 < 300$, the system **just satisfies** the clinical threshold with a **20 ms margin**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In practice, a 20 ms margin is dangerous — jitter spikes (up to 50 ms common on best-effort internet) can push the system over the threshold. KTU exam tip: always recommend a **safety margin of at least 30–50%**. #### Part (c): Error Concealment vs. Retransmission **Retransmission approach (TCP-like):** $$ L_{retrans} = L_{total} + RTT + L_{process} $$ $$ L_{retrans} = 280 + 200 + 20 = 500 \text{ ms} $$ This **fails** the 300 ms threshold → unacceptable for clinical use. **Forward Error Correction (FEC) or error concealment approach:** $$ L_{FEC} = L_{total} + \epsilon $$ where $\epsilon \approx 10$ ms overhead. $$ L_{FEC} = 280 + 10 = 290 \text{ ms} $$ This **passes** the threshold. **Logic:** Retransmission involves round-trip wait, which doubles effective latency. FEC adds redundancy **proactively** so a lost packet can be reconstructed at the receiver without waiting. #### Part (d): Required Network Bandwidth for H.264 SD Video For H.264 Standard Definition (720×576) at 25 fps, the typical encoded bitrate is: $$ B_{video} = R \times W \times H \times CR^{-1} \times f \times \alpha $$ where: - $R$ = bits per pixel (≈ 8 bits, RGB) - $W \times H$ = 720 × 576 = 414,720 pixels - $CR$ = compression ratio (H.264 typically ≈ 50:1 for clinical video) - $f$ = frame rate = 25 fps - $\alpha$ = chroma subsampling factor (4:2:0 → 1.5) Raw rate before compression: $$ B_{raw} = 720 \times 576 \times 25 \times 1.5 \times 8 = 124,416,000 \text{ bps} \approx 124.4 \text{ Mbps} $$ After H.264 compression (CR = 50:1): $$ B_{encoded} = \frac{124.4}{50} \approx 2.49 \text{ Mbps} $$ Adding audio (G.722 = 64 kbps) and protocol overhead (≈ 10%): $$ B_{total} = (2490 + 64) \times 1.10 \approx 2.81 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Conclusion:** A real-time SD clinical video session needs approximately **2.8 Mbps** sustained, well within modern 4G/5G capabilities. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Favored Question Patterns | KTU Question Type | Concept Tested | Typical Marks | Key Answer Points | |-------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------------| | "Differentiate real-time and store-and-forward" | Synchronous vs asynchronous | 3 | Synchrony, latency, use cases | | "Explain H.323 and SIP" | Signalling protocols | 7 | ITU vs IETF, complexity, scalability | | "Latency budget calculation" | Quantitative analysis | 7 | Sum delays, compare with threshold | | "Bandwidth estimation for video" | Codec mathematics | 7 | Bit depth, fps, compression ratio | | "QoS challenges in real-time telemedicine" | Network engineering | 14 | Jitter, packet loss, prioritization | | "Telesurgery requirements vs. teleconsultation" | Comparative analysis | 14 | Latency < 150 ms, haptic feedback, reliability | ## 3.3 Worked Example: ECG Streaming Data Rate **Given:** A 12-lead ECG sampled at 500 Hz with 16-bit resolution per channel. **Find:** Total data rate per second, and required bandwidth after 4:1 wavelet compression. ### Step 1: Compute raw data rate Channels = 12, Sample rate = 500 Hz, Resolution = 16 bits $$ R_{raw} = N_{ch} \times f_s \times b = 12 \times 500 \times 16 $$ $$ R_{raw} = 96{,}000 \text{ bits/second} = 96 \text{ kbps} $$ ### Step 2: Apply 4:1 wavelet compression Compression ratio CR = 4:1 → compressed bitrate: $$ R_{comp} = \frac{R_{raw}}{CR} = \frac{96}{4} = 24 \text{ kbps} $$ ### Step 3: Account for RTP/UDP/IP overhead Overhead ≈ 40 bytes per packet. If we packetize 20 ms of ECG = 10 samples = 240 bytes, overhead ratio = 40/280 ≈ 14.3%. $$ R_{wire} = 24 \times 1.143 \approx 27.4 \text{ kbps} $$ **Interpretation:** ECG streaming is bandwidth-cheap (~27 kbps), but **packet timing must be preserved** — losing 1 sample can corrupt the QRS complex detection. Hence, real-time ECG over UDP requires careful packet design. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Real-Time Telemedicine System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Site
Audio/Video/Data Acquisition] --> B[Encoding Layer
H.264 / G.722 / HL7] B --> C[Signaling Layer
SIP or H.323] B --> D[Transport Layer
RTP over UDP] C --> E[Network Layer
IP / MPLS / 5G / Satellite] D --> E E --> F[Remote Clinician Site
Decoding / Display] F --> G[Clinical Decision
Diagnosis / Guidance] G --> H[EMR / FHIR Record Update] E -.-> I[QoS Monitor
RTCP / Jitter / Loss] I -.-> F ``` **Interpretation:** This diagram shows the **closed-loop** nature of real-time telemedicine. Data flows from patient to clinician (forward path), but control metadata, QoS feedback, and clinical decisions (e.g., medication orders) flow back. The **QoS monitor** is critical — it continuously samples network health and triggers codec adaptation when jitter or loss exceeds thresholds. ## 4.2 Layered Architecture Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L5[Layer 5 - Clinical Application] APP1[Teleconsultation UI] APP2[Tele-ICU Dashboard] APP3[Telesurgery Console] end subgraph L4[Layer 4 - Synchronization and QoS] SYNC1[RTP Timestamping] SYNC2[RTCP Feedback] SYNC3[Jitter Buffer] end subgraph L3[Layer 3 - Transport and Signaling] TR1[SIP Session Setup] TR2[RTP Media Transport] TR3[UDP/IP] end subgraph L2[Layer 2 - Codec] CD1[Video H.264/HEVC] CD2[Audio G.711/G.722] CD3[ECG Compression] end subgraph L1[Layer 1 - Acquisition] ACQ1[Camera / Mic] ACQ2[ECG / SpO2 / Stethoscope] end L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Real-Time vs. Store-and-Forward ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RT[Real-Time Synchronous Path] R1[Patient Speaks] --> R2[Live Audio Capture] R2 --> R3[Encode / Stream] R3 --> R4[Network UDP] R4 --> R5[Decode / Display] R5 --> R6[Doctor Responds Live] R6 --> R1 end subgraph SF[Store-and-Forward Asynchronous Path] S1[Patient Captures Image] --> S2[Store on Server] S2 --> S3[Doctor Reviews Later] S3 --> S4[Reply Sent to Patient] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading tip for KTU exams:** The real-time path has a **closed loop** (continuous bidirectional arrows), while the store-and-forward path is a **one-way with delayed return**. This single diagram, if drawn in an exam, can earn 2–3 marks for differentiation questions. ## 4.4 Latency Budget Waterfall ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Capture
40 ms] --> A1[Encode
60 ms] A1 --> A2[Network Transit
85 ms] A2 --> A3[Jitter Buffer
40 ms] A3 --> A4[Decode
30 ms] A4 --> A5[Display
25 ms] A5 --> A6{Total = 280 ms
under 300 ms limit} ``` **Engineering takeaway:** A waterfall of latency contributions makes it visually clear which component is the bottleneck. In the example above, **encoding (60 ms)** and **network transit (85 ms)** together account for over 50% of total latency — these are the prime targets for optimization. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Real-Time Telemedicine and list its three primary modalities. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Real-Time Telemedicine is the **synchronous, bidirectional delivery of healthcare services** between geographically separated participants using interactive telecommunications technologies, where the end-to-end latency is kept below **300 ms** so that interaction is natural and clinically safe. **Three modalities (1 mark):** 1. **Videoconferencing-based teleconsultation** (doctor–patient or doctor–doctor) 2. **Real-time telemonitoring** (continuous streaming of vital signs like ECG, SpO₂) 3. **Telementoring / Telesurgery** (live surgical guidance or robotic remote intervention) > [!NOTE] > **Mark-splitting tip:** Definition alone = 2 marks, modalities = 1 mark. Always quote the latency number — examiners reward specificity. --- ### Question 2: What is the role of RTP and RTCP in real-time telemedicine? **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **RTP (Real-Time Transport Protocol) (1.5 marks):** Provides end-to-end transport for real-time audio/video over UDP. It adds **sequence numbers** (to detect loss and reorder packets) and **timestamps** (to synchronize playback and remove jitter at the receiver). **RTCP (RTP Control Protocol) (1.5 marks):** Runs alongside RTP and provides **out-of-band QoS statistics** such as packet loss percentage, jitter, and round-trip time. The sender uses RTCP feedback to adapt codec bitrate (e.g., drop from 1 Mbps to 256 kbps if loss increases). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common mistake:** Students often confuse RTP with RTSP. RTP = **transport** (carries media). RTSP = **control** (pause, play, record). KTU examiners deduct marks for this mix-up. --- ## PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme):** Module 2 carries questions in Part B with internal choice. Students must answer **one** of the two questions below. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **five-layer architecture of a real-time telemedicine system**, clearly stating the function of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A real-time teleconsultation uses H.264 video encoded at **25 fps** with a **640×480** resolution and a **4:2:0 chroma subsampling**. Compute the encoded bitrate assuming a compression ratio of **60:1**, and the total bandwidth required including a G.722 audio channel and 10% protocol overhead. State whether the system is suitable for deployment over a **2 Mbps broadband link**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):** A real-time telemedicine system is a stack of **five interconnected layers** that handle everything from capturing patient data to delivering clinical decisions. **Step 2 — Layer 1: Acquisition Layer (1 mark):** Captures raw clinical data — microphone for speech, camera for visual signs, ECG/SpO₂ for vitals. This layer is responsible for **analog-to-digital conversion** of biomedical signals. **Step 3 — Layer 2: Encoding/Compression Layer (1.5 marks):** Reduces data volume for transmission. Video uses **H.264/HEVC**; audio uses **G.711/G.722**; medical signals use wavelet or lossless compression. Goal: reduce bitrate while preserving clinical fidelity. **Step 4 — Layer 3: Transport/Signaling Layer (1.5 marks):** **Signaling** (SIP/H.323) sets up the call. **Transport** (RTP over UDP) carries the media. UDP is preferred over TCP because retransmission delays break real-time continuity. **Step 5 — Layer 4: Network Layer (1 mark):** The actual physical/logical network — IP, MPLS, 4G/5G, satellite. Must support **QoS mechanisms** (DiffServ, traffic shaping) to prioritize telemedicine traffic. **Step 6 — Layer 5: Clinical Application Layer (1 mark):** The end-user interface — teleconsultation dashboard, EMR integration, telesurgery console. Translates raw data into clinical action. *Reference diagram: see SECTION 4.2 Layered Architecture Diagram.* #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Compute raw (uncompressed) bitrate (2 marks):** $$ B_{raw} = W \times H \times f \times \alpha \times b $$ where: - $W \times H$ = 640 × 480 = 307,200 pixels - $f$ = 25 fps - $\alpha$ = 1.5 (4:2:0 subsampling) - $b$ = 8 bits/pixel $$ B_{raw} = 640 \times 480 \times 25 \times 1.5 \times 8 = 92,160,000 \text{ bps} \approx 92.16 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 2 — Apply H.264 compression (2 marks):** $$ B_{video} = \frac{B_{raw}}{CR} = \frac{92.16}{60} \approx 1.536 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 3 — Add audio channel (1.5 marks):** G.722 = 64 kbps = 0.064 Mbps $$ B_{media} = 1.536 + 0.064 = 1.600 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 4 — Add protocol overhead (1.5 marks):** RTP/UDP/IP + SIP = ~10% overhead $$ B_{total} = 1.600 \times 1.10 = 1.76 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $1.76 \text{ Mbps} < 2.0 \text{ Mbps}$, the system **fits** within the 2 Mbps link with ~12% headroom for control traffic. **Deployment is feasible**, though a fiber link with low jitter is recommended. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Forgetting the **chroma subsampling factor (1.5)** is a common 1-mark deduction. > - Not stating the **unit conversions** explicitly (kbps vs Mbps) loses 0.5 mark. > - Writing only the formula without numerical substitution = 0 marks even if the formula is correct. Always plug in numbers. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Differentiate between **synchronous (real-time)** and **asynchronous (store-and-forward)** telemedicine across **six parameters**, and explain with examples when each is clinically appropriate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **network-level challenges** (latency, jitter, packet loss, security) of deploying real-time telemedicine over public internet, and propose **three engineering solutions** for each challenge. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Six differentiating parameters (6 marks, 1 each):** | Parameter | Real-Time (Synchronous) | Store-and-Forward (Asynchronous) | |-----------|-------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Latency** | < 300 ms required | Hours to days acceptable | | **Direction** | Bidirectional, live | Often unidirectional, time-shifted | | **Bandwidth** | High (≥ 384 kbps) | Lower (batch transfer) | | **Examples** | Tele-ICU, telesurgery, emergency triage | Teleradiology, teledermatology, telepathology | | **Technology** | H.323/SIP, RTP/UDP, H.264 | Email/FTP, DICOM PACS, secure web upload | | **Clinical use** | Acute/emergency care, guided procedures | Diagnostic review, second opinion | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Real-time telemedicine is chosen for **time-critical** care; store-and-forward for **diagnostic review** that can tolerate delay. Modern hybrid systems (e.g., teleconsultation with attached images) combine both. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Four challenges + solutions (1.75 marks each):** **1. Latency (0.5 mark challenge + 1.25 marks solutions):** - Challenge: Public internet has variable latency (20–200 ms), breaking the 300 ms budget. - Solutions: (i) **CDN edge servers** to reduce physical distance; (ii) **5G network slicing** with guaranteed latency SLAs; (iii) **Leased MPLS lines** for hospital-to-hospital links; (iv) **Codec tuning** to lower encode/decode delay. **2. Jitter (0.5 + 1.25 marks):** - Challenge: Variable packet inter-arrival times cause audio glitches/video freeze. - Solutions: (i) **Adaptive jitter buffer** at receiver; (ii) **RTCP feedback** to sender for rate adaptation; (iii) **QoS prioritization** (DSCP marking for RTP packets). **3. Packet Loss (0.5 + 1.25 marks):** - Challenge: Loss > 1% corrupts video; > 5% makes audio unintelligible. - Solutions: (i) **Forward Error Correction (FEC)** adds redundant packets proactively; (ii) **Packet loss concealment (PLC)** algorithms in codecs; (iii) **Codec bitrate reduction** when loss detected. **4. Security & Privacy (0.5 + 1.25 marks):** - Challenge: Patient data over public internet vulnerable to eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle, and HIPAA/AERB violations. - Solutions: (i) **SRTP (Secure RTP)** with AES-128 encryption; (ii) **DTLS-SRTP** for key exchange; (iii) **TLS 1.3** for SIP signaling; (iv) **End-to-end mutual authentication** using mTLS. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Vague answers like *"Use better network"* get 0 marks — always name the **specific protocol or mechanism**. > - For "differentiate" questions, students who write paragraphs instead of a **table** often lose 1 mark for poor presentation. > - The "six parameters" wording in (a) means you must list **exactly six** — fewer = deduction; extra acceptable if relevant. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Real-Time Telemedicine (Module 2)** ### **A. Core Definitions (must memorize verbatim)** - ✅ Real-Time Telemedicine = **synchronous, bidirectional** clinical interaction with **latency < 300 ms**. - ✅ Three modalities: **teleconsultation, telemonitoring, telesurgery/telementoring**. - ✅ Telesurgery latency requirement: **< 150 ms**. ### **B. Standards & Protocols (high-weight for KTU)** - ✅ **SIP** (RFC 3261) for session setup; **H.323** is older ITU alternative. - ✅ **RTP/RTCP** for media transport + QoS feedback. - ✅ **H.264/AVC** for video; **G.711/G.722** for audio; **H.265/HEVC** for newer systems. - ✅ **HL7/FHIR** for EMR data; **DICOM** for imaging; **IEEE 11073** for wearable devices. ### **C. Latency Budget Numbers (always quote in numerical questions)** - ✅ Capture ≈ 20–50 ms | Encode ≈ 30–80 ms | Network ≈ 50–100 ms | Decode ≈ 20–40 ms | Display ≈ 10–30 ms - ✅ Total **< 300 ms** clinical; **< 150 ms** telesurgery. - ✅ Jitter tolerable **< 50 ms**; packet loss **< 1%**. ### **D. Codec Math (derivation questions)** - ✅ Raw bitrate: $B_{raw} = W \times H \times f \times \alpha \times b$ - ✅ Encoded: $B_{video} = B_{raw} / CR$ where $CR \approx 50$–$100$ for H.264. - ✅ Add audio + 10% overhead for total bandwidth. - ✅ G.711 = 64 kbps; G.722 = 48/56/64 kbps; Opus = 6–510 kbps adaptive. ### **E. Differentiations to Remember** - ✅ Real-time vs Store-and-forward (6 parameters) - ✅ SIP vs H.323 (IETF vs ITU; lightweight vs monolithic) - ✅ RTP vs RTSP (transport vs control) - ✅ TCP vs UDP in telemedicine (reliable vs low-latency) - ✅ Telemedicine vs Telehealth (clinical vs broader health + education) ### **F. Real-World Examples (use as illustration in answers)** - ✅ **Lindbergh Operation (2001):** Transatlantic laparoscopic cholecystectomy using Zeus robotic system. - ✅ **AIIMS Tele-ICU model:** Real-time monitoring of district hospital ICUs. - ✅ **Telestroke networks:** On-call neurologists review CT scans live and decide on tPA administration. - ✅ **5G Ambulance Trials (India):** Live ECG + video from moving ambulance to cardiologist. ### **G. Common Pitfalls (KTU examiners love to test these)** - ⚠️ Confusing "real-time" with "fast" — speed alone is not the criterion; **synchrony** is. - ⚠️ Saying "telemetry" when meaning "telemonitoring" — they overlap but aren't identical. - ⚠️ Forgetting that real-time telemedicine **does not mean instant** — there's a 300 ms budget, not 0 ms. - ⚠️ Ignoring security — every real-time system discussion must mention **encryption (SRTP/DTLS)**. - ⚠️ Treating all telemedicine as one — always clarify **synchronous vs asynchronous** early in the answer. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** For any "explain" question, **start with a 1-sentence definition, then draw the block diagram, then list standards, then numbers.** This 4-part structure is what KTU examiners reward. For any "calculate" question, **show formula → substitute → unit conversion → comparison with threshold → conclusion.** Skipping the comparison = 1 mark lost.

topics.title: The Internet: Basic Concepts, Security, Secure Socket Layer, Firewalls, Proxies, Data Exchange, Network Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Internet: Basic Concepts, Security, Secure Socket Layer, Firewalls, Proxies, Data Exchange, Network Configuration # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 The Internet — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > The **Internet** is a global, decentralized, packet-switched network of networks that uses the standardized **Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP)** to enable communication and data exchange between heterogeneous computing devices across geographical boundaries. In the context of **Telemedicine**, the Internet serves as the universal transport backbone for the transmission of Electronic Medical Records (EMR), DICOM medical images, real-time vitals, and tele-consultation streams. The Internet is governed by standards published by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** as **Requests for Comments (RFCs)**. Every byte of data transmitted must conform to one of these RFCs for interoperability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Internet as a Postal System | Real World Element | Internet Equivalent | |---|---| | Letter | Data Packet (PDU) | | Envelope with Address | IP Header (Source/Destination IP) | | Postal Code | Subnet Mask / Routing Prefix | | Post Office | Router | | Sorting Hub | Gateway / ISP | | Tracking Number | Sequence Number (TCP) | | Registered Post | TCP (reliable, ordered) | | Ordinary Mail | UDP (best-effort, fast) | | Registered Aadhaar | SSL/TLS Encrypted Session | | Return Address | MAC Address / Source IP | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Telemedicine:** A single tele-echocardiography session may require **TCP** for reliable image transfer and **UDP** for low-latency voice — just like registered post and speed post serve different purposes. ## 1.3 Core Layered Architecture — TCP/IP Model The Internet operates on a **4-layer reference model** (per **RFC 1122**): $$ \text{TCP/IP Stack} = \begin{cases} \text{Application Layer} & \text{(HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DICOM, HL7)} \\ \text{Transport Layer} & \text{(TCP - Port 443, UDP - Port 53)} \\ \text{Internet Layer} & \text{(IPv4 / IPv6, ICMP)} \\ \text{Link / Network Access Layer} & \text{(Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11, Fiber)} \end{cases} $$ **Standard Port Metrics (Bold for emphasis):** - **HTTP** → **Port 80** - **HTTPS** → **Port 443** - **DNS** → **Port 53** - **SSH** → **Port 22** - **DICOM** → **Port 104** (and **Port 11112** for TLS) ## 1.4 Basic Concepts of Internetworking ### 1.4.1 IP Addressing An **IPv4 address** is a **32-bit** logical identifier expressed in **dotted-decimal notation**: $$ \text{IP}_4 = 4 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bits} $$ $$ \text{Address Space}_{IPv4} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ unique addresses} $$ An **IPv6 address** is **128-bit**, expressed in **colon-hexadecimal**: $$ \text{Address Space}_{IPv6} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ addresses} $$ ### 1.4.2 DNS (Domain Name System) — Port 53 Translates human-readable hostnames (e.g., `ehr.hospital.kerala.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses. Operates primarily over **UDP** for queries and **TCP** for zone transfers. ### 1.4.3 Subnetting Divides a large network into smaller sub-networks using a **subnet mask**. $$ \text{No. of usable hosts} = 2^{(32-n)} - 2 $$ where **n** = number of bits in the network prefix. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Internet Security — The CIA Triad > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Every telemedicine data exchange must preserve the **CIA Triad**: > - **C**onfidentiality — only authorized parties read the data > - **I**ntegrity — data is not modified in transit > - **A**vailability — services are accessible when needed ## 2.2 Cryptographic Building Blocks ### 2.2.1 Symmetric Encryption The same secret key is used for encryption and decryption. Speed is high; key distribution is the bottleneck. $$ C = E_K(P) \quad ; \quad P = D_K(C) $$ where $E_K$ = encryption with key $K$, $D_K$ = decryption with key $K$, $P$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext. **Common Algorithms:** **AES (128/192/256-bit)**, **DES (56-bit)**, **3DES**, **Blowfish**. ### 2.2.2 Asymmetric (Public-Key) Encryption A mathematically related **key pair** is used. Slow but solves the key-distribution problem. $$ C = E_{K_{pub}}(P) \quad ; \quad P = D_{K_{priv}}(C) $$ **Common Algorithms:** **RSA (1024/2048/4096-bit)**, **ECC**, **Diffie-Hellman**. ### 2.2.3 Hash Functions One-way mathematical functions producing a fixed-length **message digest**. $$ h = H(M) \quad \text{where} \quad h \text{ is fixed length}, \quad H(M) \text{ is irreversible} $$ **Common Algorithms:** **SHA-256**, **SHA-3**, **MD5 (deprecated)**. ## 2.3 Secure Socket Layer (SSL) / Transport Layer Security (TLS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Concept:** SSL was the original protocol (deprecated since **TLS 1.0**). Modern systems use **TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3** (RFC 5246 / RFC 8446). The term "SSL" is colloquially retained. ### 2.3.1 TLS Handshake Phases **Phase 1 — Negotiation:** - Client sends `ClientHello` (supported cipher suites, TLS version, random nonce). - Server replies with `ServerHello` + **digital certificate** (X.509) + `ServerHelloDone`. **Phase 2 — Key Exchange:** - Client verifies certificate against trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**. - Client generates **Pre-Master Secret**, encrypts with server's public key (RSA) or via **Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral (DHE/ECDHE)**. **Phase 3 — Session Key Derivation:** $$ \text{Master Secret} = \text{PRF}(\text{Pre-Master Secret} \Vert \text{Client Random} \Vert \text{Server Random}) $$ $$ \text{Session Keys} = \text{PRF}(\text{Master Secret} \Vert \text{Server Random} \Vert \text{Client Random}) $$ **Phase 4 — Finished:** - Both parties exchange `Finished` messages encrypted with the new session keys to confirm handshake integrity. ### 2.3.2 Cipher Suite Naming Convention $$ \text{TLS\_ECDHE\_RSA\_WITH\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384} $$ | Field | Meaning | |---|---| | TLS | Protocol | | ECDHE | Ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman key exchange | | RSA | Authentication / signature algorithm | | AES_256_GCM | Bulk symmetric encryption (256-bit, Galois Counter Mode) | | SHA384 | HMAC hash for integrity | ## 2.4 Firewalls > [!NOTE] > A **Firewall** is a network security device (hardware or software) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing traffic based on a defined **security policy**. ### 2.4.1 Types of Firewalls | Type | OSI Layer | Filter Basis | Telemedicine Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Packet-Filtering** | L3/L4 | Source/Dest IP, Port | Block external SSH into EHR server | | **Stateful Inspection** | L3/L4 | Connection state | Track valid TCP sessions for DICOM transfer | | **Application-Layer (Proxy)** | L7 | Protocol content | Inspect HTTP headers in HL7 messages | | **Next-Gen (NGFW)** | L7 + IDS/IPS | Deep packet + behavioural | Detect anomalous tele-ICU traffic | | **WAF** | L7 (Web) | SQL injection, XSS | Protect patient portal | ## 2.5 Proxies ### 2.5.1 Forward Proxy Sits between **clients and the Internet**, hiding internal IPs. Common in hospital networks to enforce access policy. ### 2.5.2 Reverse Proxy Sits in front of **servers**, distributing requests. Used for load-balancing tele-consultation servers (e.g., **Nginx**, **HAProxy**). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | IPv4 address count | $2^{32}$ | ≈ 4.3 billion | | IPv6 address count | $2^{128}$ | ≈ $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | | Usable hosts per subnet | $2^{(32-n)} - 2$ | $-2$ for network & broadcast | | RSA encryption time | $O(k^3)$ | $k$ = key bit length | | AES block size | 128 bits | Fixed | | TLS record max size | $2^{14}$ bytes | = 16 KB | | MD5 digest size | 128 bits | Deprecated, do not use | | SHA-256 digest size | 256 bits | Recommended | | Effective key (3DES) | 112 bits | Due to meet-in-the-middle | | HMAC security bound | $\approx 2^{L/2}$ | $L$ = hash output bits | | Shannon entropy | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In telemedicine, the bandwidth-delay product determines the optimal TCP window size for transmitting DICOM images of size $\geq 100$ MB. $$ \text{Bandwidth-Delay Product} = B \times RTT $$ where $B$ = bandwidth (bits/s), $RTT$ = round-trip time (s). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation — Subnet Mask and Host Calculation **Problem:** A hospital IT department receives the address block **192.168.10.0/26**. Compute the number of usable host addresses per subnet. ### Step 1 — Identify the prefix length $$ n = 26 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute bits reserved for host portion $$ h = 32 - n = 32 - 26 = 6 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute total addresses in the subnet $$ \text{Total} = 2^{h} = 2^{6} = 64 $$ ### Step 4 — Subtract network and broadcast addresses $$ \text{Usable Hosts} = 2^{h} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 $$ ### Step 5 — Identify subnet boundaries $$ \text{Subnet Increment} = 2^{(8-h_{\text{last-octet}})} = 2^{6} = 64 $$ $$ \text{Subnets:} \quad 192.168.10.0, \; 192.168.10.64, \; 192.168.10.128, \; 192.168.10.192 $$ ## 3.2 Derivation — RSA Key Generation (Conceptual) **Step 1:** Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$. **Step 2:** Compute modulus. $$ n = p \times q $$ **Step 3:** Compute Euler's totient. $$ \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) $$ **Step 4:** Choose public exponent $e$ such that: $$ \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1 \quad ; \quad 1 < e < \phi(n) $$ **Step 5:** Compute private exponent $d$ as modular inverse. $$ d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} $$ **Step 6:** Public key = $(e, n)$; Private key = $(d, n)$. ### Encryption / Decryption $$ C \equiv P^{e} \pmod{n} $$ $$ P \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why large primes?** Breaking RSA requires factoring $n$, which is computationally infeasible for $n \geq 2048$ bits using classical computers. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Security for Telemedicine Traffic | Telemedicine Use Case | Protocol | Recommended Cipher Suite | Key Length | Justification | |---|---|---|---|---| | Patient Portal Login | HTTPS | TLS_ECDHE_RSA_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 | RSA 2048 | Balance of speed & security | | DICOM Image Transfer | DICOM-TLS | TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 | ECC 256 | High data volume, low latency | | Video Consultation | WebRTC + DTLS-SRTP | SRTP_AES128_CM_HMAC_SHA1_80 | AES 128 | Real-time constraints | | HL7 v2 Messaging | MLLP over TLS | TLS_DHE_RSA_AES_256_CBC_SHA256 | RSA 2048 | Legacy integration | | HL7 FHIR API | HTTPS + OAuth 2.0 | TLS_ECDHE_RSA_CHACHA20_POLY1305 | RSA 2048 | Mobile/IoT friendly | | Remote Surgery Telemetry | HTTPS + IPsec | Suite B (AES-256, SHA-384, ECDH P-384) | 384-bit ECC | Mission-critical | | Pager / SMS Fallback | None | N/A (use signed SMS gateways) | N/A | Low sensitivity, low bandwidth | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Firewall Rule Evaluation A stateful firewall receives a packet with the following attributes: | Attribute | Value | |---|---| | Source IP | 10.0.5.12 (Internal EHR workstation) | | Destination IP | 142.250.190.46 (Public Google) | | Source Port | 52431 (Ephemeral) | | Destination Port | 443 (HTTPS) | | Protocol | TCP | | ACK flag | Set (ESTABLISHED) | | Direction | Outbound | **Step 1:** Inspect rule base in order: 1. `DENY ALL 0.0.0.0/0 → 10.0.0.0/8` (Inbound) 2. `ALLOW tcp 10.0.0.0/8 → any : 443 ESTABLISHED,RELATED` 3. `DENY ALL` **Step 2:** Match found — Rule 2 (ALLOW outbound HTTPS for internal subnets). **Step 3:** Connection state verified (ESTABLISHED). **Step 4:** Action = **PERMIT**. Log entry created. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Modern firewalls use **state tables** to track TCP handshakes, eliminating the need to allow a wide range of ephemeral inbound ports. ## 3.5 Step-by-Step — Proxy Request Flow (Reverse Proxy for Telemedicine API) **Step 1:** Patient mobile app sends `GET /api/v1/appointments`. **Step 2:** DNS resolves `api.hospital.in` to reverse proxy IP. **Step 3:** Reverse proxy terminates TLS (SSL offloading). **Step 4:** Proxy inspects `X-Forwarded-For`, rate limits, and forwards to upstream server pool via **round-robin**: $$ \text{Server}_i = i \bmod N \quad \text{where } N = \text{upstream count} $$ **Step 5:** Upstream server responds → proxy caches (if policy allows) → returns encrypted response to client. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Internet & Telemedicine Network — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Mobile App
Android iOS] -->|TLS HTTPS| B[Hospital DMZ
Reverse Proxy Nginx] A -->|WebRTC DTLS| C[Telemedicine Web Server
NodeJS] B -->|HTTP Backend| C C -->|HL7 FHIR JSON| D[Application Server
Spring Boot] D -->|SQL TLS| E[(EHR Database
PostgreSQL)] D -->|DICOM TLS Port 11112| F[(PACS Server
DICOM Archive)] D -->|LDAP TLS| G[Active Directory
User Auth] H[Stateful Firewall
pfSense] -->|Filters| B I[IDS IPS Suricata] -->|Inspect| C J[CA Root Certificate
DigiCert] -->|Signs| B K[Remote Specialist
Browser] -->|Mutual TLS| B L[Audit Log Server
SIEM] <-->|Syslog TLS| H L <-->|Syslog TLS| D ``` ## 4.2 TLS Handshake Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Patient App participant Server as Hospital Web Server participant CA as Certificate Authority Client->>Server: ClientHello (TLS version, cipher suites, client random) Server->>Client: ServerHello (chosen cipher, server random) Server->>Client: Certificate (X.509 from CA) Server->>Client: ServerHelloDone Note over Client: Verify certificate against trusted CA root store Client->>Server: ClientKeyExchange (Pre-Master Secret encrypted with server public key) Client->>Server: ChangeCipherSpec Client->>Server: Finished (encrypted hash of handshake) Server->>Client: ChangeCipherSpec Server->>Client: Finished (encrypted hash of handshake) Note over Client,Server: Secure symmetric channel established Client->>Server: Application Data (e.g. POST /login) Server->>Client: Application Data (e.g. JWT token) ``` ## 4.3 Firewall Architecture — DMZ with Telemedicine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INTERNET["Public Internet Zone"] X[Remote Patient] Y[Specialist Consultant] end subgraph DMZ["Hospital DMZ"] W1[Reverse Proxy 1] W2[Reverse Proxy 2] F1[Stateful Firewall
Front-end] end subgraph INTERNAL["Internal Hospital Network"] F2[Internal Firewall
Back-end] S1[Web Server] S2[Application Server] S3[(EHR DB)] S4[(PACS)] end X --> F1 Y --> F1 F1 --> W1 F1 --> W2 W1 --> F2 W2 --> F2 F2 --> S1 F2 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S2 --> S4 ``` ## 4.4 Data Exchange Format Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Medical Data to Transmit] --> B{Real Time Required?} B -->|Yes| C{Binary Image Data?} B -->|No| D{Structured Clinical Data?} C -->|Yes| E[DICOM
Over DICOM-TLS Port 11112] C -->|No| F[WebRTC + SRTP
Audio Video] D -->|Yes| G{Legacy Hospital System?} D -->|No| H[Custom REST API
JSON over HTTPS] G -->|Yes| I[HL7 v2
MLLP over TLS Port 6661] G -->|No| J[HL7 FHIR
JSON XML over HTTPS] A --> K[Audit and Log to SIEM] ``` ## 4.5 Network Configuration — IP Allocation for Telemedicine ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SUBNET_A["10.10.0.0/24 - Patient Wi-Fi VLAN"] A1[Waiting Area Tablets] A2[Patient BYOD] end subgraph SUBNET_B["10.10.1.0/24 - Clinical Workstations"] B1[Doctor PCs] B2[Nurse Stations] end subgraph SUBNET_C["10.10.2.0/24 - Servers DMZ"] C1[Web Server] C2[App Server] C3[Reverse Proxy] end subgraph SUBNET_D["10.10.3.0/24 - Imaging"] D1[PACS] D2[DICOM Router] end subgraph SUBNET_E["10.10.4.0/24 - Management"] E1[SIEM] E2[Backup] E3[Admin Jump Host] end FW[Core Firewall
L3 Switch] --> SUBNET_A FW --> SUBNET_B FW --> SUBNET_C FW --> SUBNET_D FW --> SUBNET_E ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS. Why is HTTPS mandatory for telemedicine applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | HTTP | HTTPS | |---|---|---| | Default Port | 80 | 443 | | Encryption | None | TLS/SSL | | Certificate | Not required | X.509 from CA | | URL Scheme | `http://` | `https://` | | Data Integrity | None | HMAC + AEAD | | Performance | Faster | Slight overhead (TLS handshake) | **Why mandatory for telemedicine (1 mark):** Telemedicine transmits **PHI (Protected Health Information)** which, under the **IT Act 2000 (India)**, **DISHA (Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act)**, and **HIPAA (US)**, must be encrypted in transit. HTTPS via TLS provides confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication — preventing eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks, and patient data tampering during tele-consultations. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain the role of a firewall in securing a hospital network. List any two types of firewalls.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **firewall** is a network security device that enforces an access control policy by inspecting packets flowing between zones of differing trust (e.g., Internet ↔ Hospital LAN). In a hospital network, it: - Blocks unauthorized external access to **EHR/PACS servers**. - Segments the network (e.g., separates **OT (Operation Theatre)** Wi-Fi from guest networks). - Logs all access attempts for **HIPAA / DISHA audit compliance**. **Two types (2 marks):** 1. **Stateful Inspection Firewall** — Tracks TCP connection states (SYN, SYN-ACK, ESTABLISHED). 2. **Application-Layer (Proxy) Firewall** — Inspects HTTP, HL7, DICOM payload contents for malicious patterns. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the two.* --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TLS handshake** process between a client and a telemedicine web server. Discuss how **digital certificates** and **Certificate Authorities (CAs)** establish trust. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain **symmetric** and **asymmetric encryption**. Show how TLS combines both to establish a secure session for transmitting a patient record. **[7 marks]** ### MODEL SOLUTION #### (a) TLS Handshake and Certificate Trust — 7 Marks **Step 1 — ClientHello (1 mark):** The client (e.g., doctor's browser) sends a `ClientHello` message containing: - Highest supported TLS version (e.g., **TLS 1.3**) - List of cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`) - A 32-byte **client random nonce** **Step 2 — ServerHello + Certificate (2 marks):** The server replies with: - Selected cipher suite - 32-byte **server random nonce** - Its **X.509 digital certificate** containing: server's public key, server hostname, issuing CA's name, validity period, and the **CA's digital signature** over the certificate. - `ServerHelloDone` signal **Step 3 — Certificate Verification (1.5 marks):** The client: 1. Builds a **certificate chain**: Server Cert → Intermediate CA → Root CA. 2. Compares the **Root CA's public key** with its locally trusted store (e.g., Windows cert store, Mozilla trust list). 3. Verifies the CA's signature on each certificate using the CA's public key: $$ \text{Valid} \iff \text{Decrypt}(Sig_{CA}) = \text{Hash}(Cert_{Server}) $$ 4. Checks certificate is not expired and hostname matches. **Step 4 — Key Exchange (1.5 marks):** Client generates a **Pre-Master Secret (PMS)**. Encrypts it with the server's public key (RSA) or uses **ECDHE** to derive a shared secret without sending it. **Step 5 — Session Key Derivation + Finished (1 mark):** Both sides compute session keys and exchange `Finished` MAC to confirm handshake integrity. **Trust via CAs (additional 1 mark included in flow):** The chain of trust flows: **Root CA → Intermediate CA → Server Cert**. The client only needs to trust the root CA, which is pre-installed in operating systems and browsers. #### (b) Symmetric + Asymmetric Encryption in TLS — 7 Marks **Symmetric Encryption (2.5 marks):** - Uses a **single shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption. - **Algorithms:** AES-128, AES-256, ChaCha20. - **Advantage:** Extremely fast (suitable for bulk data — e.g., streaming a 50 MB MRI image). - **Disadvantage:** Secure key distribution is hard. $$ C = AES_K(P) \quad ; \quad P = AES_K(C) $$ **Asymmetric Encryption (2.5 marks):** - Uses a mathematically linked **key pair** — public key (anyone can have) + private key (server only). - **Algorithms:** RSA-2048, ECC P-256. - **Advantage:** Solves key distribution. - **Disadvantage:** ~1000× slower than symmetric. $$ C = RSA_{K_{pub}}(P) \quad ; \quad P = RSA_{K_{priv}}(C) $$ **Hybrid Approach in TLS (2 marks):** 1. Asymmetric crypto is used **only during the handshake** to securely exchange a random **Pre-Master Secret**. 2. Both sides derive symmetric **session keys** from the PMS. 3. All subsequent data (including the patient record) is encrypted with **fast symmetric AES-256-GCM**. 4. **Forward secrecy** is achieved with **ECDHE** — even if the server's private key is later compromised, past sessions remain secure. This hybrid approach gives the **best of both worlds** — secure key exchange + high-speed bulk encryption. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the working of **packet-filtering**, **stateful inspection**, and **application-layer firewalls**. Compare them with a suitable table. **[7 marks]** **(b)** What is a **proxy server**? Differentiate between **forward** and **reverse proxies**. With a diagram, show how a reverse proxy enables **load balancing** for a telemedicine consultation platform. **[7 marks]** ### MODEL SOLUTION #### (a) Types of Firewalls — 7 Marks **1. Packet-Filtering Firewall (2 marks):** - Operates at **OSI Layer 3/4**. - Examines each packet in isolation: source IP, destination IP, source port, destination port, protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP). - Decision based on **Access Control List (ACL)** rules. - **Limitation:** No context — cannot detect if a packet belongs to a legitimate session or a port-scanning attack. **2. Stateful Inspection Firewall (2.5 marks):** - Operates at **Layer 3/4** with awareness of TCP/UDP connection **state**. - Maintains a **state table** tracking `SYN`, `SYN-ACK`, `ACK`, `FIN` flags. - Only allows inbound packets that match an existing `ESTABLISHED` or `RELATED` outbound connection. - **Advantage:** Resistant to basic spoofing and unauthorized port access. - **Used for:** General hospital perimeter defence. **3. Application-Layer (Proxy) Firewall (2.5 marks):** - Operates at **OSI Layer 7** — terminates the client connection and initiates a new one to the server. - Inspects the **payload** of protocols such as HTTP, HL7 v2, DICOM. - Can block SQL injection in a patient portal, strip malicious HL7 segments, or reject malformed DICOM C-STORE requests. - **Disadvantage:** Performance overhead due to full packet inspection. **Comparison Table (built into narrative — marks awarded throughout):** | Feature | Packet-Filter | Stateful | Application-Layer | |---|---|---|---| | OSI Layer | 3/4 | 3/4 | 7 | | State Awareness | No | Yes | Yes | | Payload Inspection | No | No | Yes | | Speed | Fastest | Medium | Slowest | | Cost | Low | Medium | High | | Telemedicine Fit | Basic segregation | Default perimeter | HL7 / DICOM validation | #### (b) Proxy Servers — 7 Marks **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **proxy server** is an intermediary that terminates client requests and forwards them on behalf of the client (or to a backend server), often adding caching, filtering, encryption, or load distribution. **Forward Proxy (2 marks):** - Sits in front of **clients** (e.g., hospital staff browsers). - Hides internal IP addresses from external servers. - Enforces acceptable-use policy (e.g., blocks social media on clinical workstations). - **Example:** Hospital-wide **Squid proxy** caching common medical references. **Reverse Proxy (2 marks):** - Sits in front of **servers** (e.g., EHR API cluster). - Performs **SSL/TLS termination** (offloading CPU-intensive crypto from backend). - **Caches** static content, **compresses** responses, hides backend topology. - **Example:** **Nginx** or **HAProxy** in front of FHIR servers. **Load Balancing Diagram (1.5 marks) — text representation:** ``` [Patient App 1] ─┐ [Patient App 2] ─┤──→ [Reverse Proxy:443] ──→ [API Server 1] (Weight 5) [Patient App 3] ─┘ ├──→ [API Server 2] (Weight 5) └──→ [API Server 3] (Weight 3) [Backup] ``` The reverse proxy distributes tele-consultation requests using algorithms such as **Round-Robin**, **Least-Connections**, or **Weighted Round-Robin** — ensuring no single FHIR server is overloaded during peak consultation hours. --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing SSL with TLS** — SSL 3.0 was deprecated in **2015** (RFC 7568). Writing "SSL is used today" is technically wrong. Always state **TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3**. > 2. **Forgetting the Port Number** — Examiners specifically look for **443 (HTTPS)**, **80 (HTTP)**, **53 (DNS)**, **22 (SSH)**, **104 / 11112 (DICOM)**. Missing ports = 0.5–1 mark deduction. > 3. **Not Mentioning Certificates in TLS** — Many students describe the handshake but skip **X.509 certificates and CA trust chain**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question on every KTU paper. > 4. **Writing "Firewall is a wall"** — Vague analogies get 0.5 marks. Use technical terms: *packet filtering, stateful inspection, ACL rules, DMZ*. > 5. **Conflating Forward and Reverse Proxies** — Forward = client-side anonymity/policy. Reverse = server-side protection/load balancing. Examiners will explicitly ask the difference. > 6. **Ignoring Telemedicine Context** — PEBMT862 is a TELEMEDICINE course. Always frame answers around **EHR, HL7, DICOM, PACS, tele-consultation** — not generic banking/email examples. > 7. **Skipping Diagrams** — A neatly labelled TLS handshake or DMZ diagram is worth **2–3 marks** by itself. Always draw with **arrow directions and labels**. > 8. **Not Mentioning Hybrid Encryption in TLS** — The fact that TLS uses **asymmetric for key exchange + symmetric for bulk data** is the single most-tested concept in this module. --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Internet & Security):** > > - ✅ **Internet = Packet-Switched Network using TCP/IP** (RFC 1122) > - ✅ **TCP/IP has 4 layers:** Application, Transport, Internet, Link > - ✅ **IPv4 = 32 bits, IPv6 = 128 bits**; subnet hosts = $2^{(32-n)} - 2$ > - ✅ **HTTP = Port 80, HTTPS = Port 443, DNS = 53, DICOM-TLS = 11112** > - ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability > - ✅ **Symmetric = Fast + shared key (AES)** | **Asymmetric = Slow + key pair (RSA/ECC)** > - ✅ **TLS = modern SSL** (TLS 1.2/1.3 only); SSL 3.0 is **deprecated** > - ✅ **TLS Handshake = ClientHello → Cert → Key Exchange → Finished** > - ✅ **Digital Certificates = X.509 from CA**; trust chain ends at trusted Root CA > - ✅ **TLS uses HYBRID encryption** — asymmetric for key exchange, symmetric for bulk data > - ✅ **Firewall types = Packet-filter / Stateful / Application-layer / NGFW / WAF** > - ✅ **Stateful firewall tracks TCP states** — defends against spoofing > - ✅ **Forward Proxy = client side** | **Reverse Proxy = server side + load balancing** > - ✅ **Data exchange in telemedicine:** HL7 v2 (legacy), **HL7 FHIR (modern JSON/XML over HTTPS)**, **DICOM (imaging over TLS)**, **WebRTC + SRTP (real-time video)** > - ✅ **Network configuration = Subnetting + VLANs + DMZ + dedicated subnets for Patient/Clinical/Server/Imaging/Management zones** > - ✅ **TLS Cipher Suite format:** `TLS_KEYEXCH_AUTH_WITH_BULKCIPHER_HASH` > - ✅ **Remember the bandwdith-delay product** $B \times RTT$ for sizing DICOM TCP windows.

topics.title: Circuit and Packet Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Circuit and Packet Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Switching — The Foundation of Telecommunication Networks

In the context of **Telemedicine** and modern telecommunication infrastructure, **Switching** refers to the technique used to interconnect different communication nodes (hospitals, diagnostic centers, remote clinics, data servers) by establishing temporary or permanent pathways for the transfer of voice, video, and medical data signals across a network.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *Switching is the process of interconnecting network nodes/links to establish, maintain, and release communication paths between a source and a destination. The two principal techniques in telecommunications are **Circuit Switching** and **Packet Switching**.*

There are two principal switching methodologies that form the backbone of all data and voice communication systems used in telemedicine:

1. **Circuit Switching**
2. **Packet Switching**

---

## 1.2 Circuit Switching — Definition

**Circuit Switching** is a switching methodology in which a dedicated physical communication path (a "circuit") is established between the sender and the receiver **before** actual data transmission begins, and this path remains exclusively reserved for the entire duration of the communication session.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** The dedicated path in circuit switching is a *physical* (or logically isolated) end-to-end channel. It is a one-time setup, and the path is torn down only after the conversation ends.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Railway Track Reservation

Imagine you want to travel from **Kochi** to **Thiruvananthapuram** by train.

- You book an **exclusive train track** (the entire route from station A to station B) reserved **only for your journey**.
- The track is reserved from the moment of booking until you reach your destination.
- **No one else** can use that track — even if your train stops temporarily at a station.
- Once you reach, the track is **released** for others.

This is **Circuit Switching**. The "track" is the dedicated bandwidth, and the "stations" are the switching nodes.

In telemedicine, this is analogous to a real-time **live teleconsultation** between a specialist in a tertiary hospital and a patient in a rural PHC, where a continuous, uninterrupted video and voice path is essential.

---

## 1.3 Packet Switching — Definition

**Packet Switching** is a switching methodology in which the data to be transmitted is **broken into smaller units called packets**, each containing source/destination addressing information, payload data, and control headers. These packets are routed **independently** through the network, may take **different paths**, and are **reassembled** at the destination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Definition:**
> *In packet switching, messages are divided into fixed or variable-sized packets. Each packet contains a header with source and destination addresses, and the network routes each packet independently based on routing tables at intermediate nodes.*

### Conceptual Analogy: The Parcel Courier Service

Think of sending a **medical report (100 pages)** to a hospital in another city via a courier service:

- You **split the report** into 10 parcels of 10 pages each.
- Each parcel has a **label** with sender's address, receiver's address, and parcel number ("1 of 10", "2 of 10"...).
- The courier company (network) routes each parcel through **different cities and hubs** based on real-time traffic conditions.
- Some parcels may arrive **earlier** than others; they are **reordered** at the destination.
- Multiple couriers from different customers **share the same highways** — no exclusive route is reserved.

This is **Packet Switching**. In telemedicine, it is used for transmitting **ECG records, MRI images, lab reports, and EHR (Electronic Health Records)** where the data is bursty and bandwidth efficiency is critical.

---

## 1.4 Standard Telemedicine Metrics & Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Significance in Telemedicine |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | **Hz / bps** | Determines quality of video/audio for teleconsultation |
| Propagation Delay | **Tp (seconds)** | Time for signal to traverse the medium |
| Transmission Delay | **Tt (seconds)** | Time to push packet bits onto the link |
| Processing Delay | **Tproc (seconds)** | Time at routers/nodes to process the packet |
| Queuing Delay | **Tq (seconds)** | Time packet waits in router buffer |
| Total Delay | **T = Tt + Tp + Tproc + Tq** | Critical for real-time telemedicine apps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **real-time telemedicine** (live surgery, remote ICU monitoring), **end-to-end delay must be < 150 ms** as per WHO/ITU recommendations. This is a high-yield KTU exam value to remember.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Switching — Operational Phases

Circuit switching operates in **three distinct sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase)

- The source station sends a **connection request** to the destination via intermediate switching nodes.
- Each switching node **reserves a dedicated path** along a chosen route based on availability.
- A confirmation signal is returned to the source once the circuit is established end-to-end.
- **Time consumed:** Called *Call Setup Time* (CST). Pure overhead, no data transmitted yet.

### Phase 2: Data Transfer (Talk Phase)

- The two stations communicate **continuously** over the reserved circuit.
- The full **bandwidth of the channel is dedicated** to this single session.
- Data flows in **real-time** with **constant, predictable delay**.
- Switching nodes simply forward bits — no addressing or routing decisions needed.
- **Time consumed:** Called *Data Transfer Time* or *Holding Time* (H). Productive phase.

### Phase 3: Circuit Disconnect (Teardown Phase)

- One of the stations sends a **disconnect signal**.
- Switching nodes **release the reserved resources** (links, buffers, channel capacity).
- The path is freed for use by other users.
- **Time consumed:** Called *Teardown Time* (TT). Usually negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** In a KTU numerical problem, if a user transmits for `D` seconds, the circuit was established over `N` hops with setup time `S` seconds per hop, the total time the network resources are held:
> `T_total = S × N + D + (teardown time)`

---

## 2.2 Types / Categories within Packet Switching

Packet switching has two principal subtypes, both of which are heavily tested in KTU exams.

### (a) Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless Service)

- Each packet is treated as an **independent entity** (a "datagram").
- No prior path is established; each packet is routed **independently** at every router based on the current routing table.
- Packets of the same message may take **different paths**.
- **Packets may arrive out of order** at the destination; reassembly is the receiver's job.
- Used in the **Internet Protocol (IP)** — the foundation of all modern internet traffic.
- **Analogy:** Sending each page of a report separately through different couriers.

### (b) Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented Service)

- A **logical path** (virtual circuit) is established between source and destination **before** data transfer using a "call request" packet.
- Once established, **all packets follow the same path** through the network.
- Each packet contains a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** instead of full source/destination addresses — this makes routing faster.
- Packets arrive **in order** because they traverse the same path.
- **Analogy:** Booking a specific berth on a train; even if the train uses different tracks, your berth is preserved throughout.
- Examples: **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but Exam-Critical Distinction:**
> - In a *Virtual Circuit*, the path is **logical**, not physical. The actual physical links may be shared with other users.
> - In a *Circuit Switched* connection, the path is **physically dedicated** and exclusive.

---

## 2.3 Hybrid Switching for Telemedicine

In modern telemedicine networks, a **hybrid approach** is used:
- **Circuit-switched behavior** for voice/video (low latency, predictable).
- **Packet-switched behavior** for medical data, imaging, and EHRs (efficient, statistical multiplexing).

This is realized through technologies like **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** and **QoS-enabled IP networks**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrix

### 2.4.1 Key Formulas Table

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmission Delay | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | **seconds** |
| Propagation Delay | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | $d$ = distance, $s$ = signal propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) | **seconds** |
| Total Nodal Delay | $T_{nodal} = T_t + T_p + T_{proc} + T_q$ | Sum of all four delays at a single router | **seconds** |
| End-to-End Delay (N hops) | $T_{e2e} = N \times (T_t + T_p + T_{proc} + T_q)$ | For a packet traversing N identical links | **seconds** |
| Throughput (Circuit Switched) | $\eta_{CS} = \dfrac{D}{S + D}$ | $D$ = data phase, $S$ = setup time | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |
| Packet Switched Delay | $T_{PS} = (N-1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + N \cdot T_p$ | Store-and-forward delay across N hops | **seconds** |
| Effective Bandwidth Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Useful Data Time}}{\text{Total Holding Time}}$ | Efficiency of a circuit-switched session | **dimensionless** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In a packet-switched network with $N$ hops, the delay is $(N-1) \cdot (L/R)$ because the source does not experience a "transmission wait" — only intermediate routers do. Many students incorrectly write $N \cdot (L/R)$.

### 2.4.2 Circuit vs Packet Switching — Master Comparison Table

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching (Datagram) | Packet Switching (Virtual Circuit) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path Setup | Required | Not required | Required |
| Dedicated Physical Path | **Yes** | No | No (logical only) |
| Resource Reservation | Yes (exclusive) | No (statistical) | Yes (logical) |
| Bandwidth Allocation | Fixed | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| Delay Variability | Constant & predictable | Variable | More predictable than datagram |
| Packet Ordering | N/A (continuous stream) | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed (same path) |
| Header Overhead | None | High (per-packet addresses) | Moderate (VCI per packet) |
| Typical Use Case | Voice calls (PSTN) | Internet (IP) | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 |
| Best For Telemedicine | Live teleconsultation, remote surgery | EHR transfer, PACS, email | Real-time + data mix (MPLS) |
| Robustness to Link Failure | **Low** (entire path fails) | **High** (rerouting possible) | Moderate (new VC needed) |
| Congestion Handling | Cannot adapt (already reserved) | Can reroute | More complex |
| Cost of Idle Channel | **High (wasted)** | Low (shared) | Low (shared) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Circuit Switching** powers traditional PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network) and ISDN — still used in telemedicine consultation booths where reliability trumps efficiency.
- **Packet Switching** is the foundation of the internet, 4G/5G data networks, hospital LANs, and PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems).
- **Virtual Circuit Switching** underpins MPLS — the backbone of modern telesurgery networks (e.g., NASA-Mayo Clinic partnerships).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Delay in Packet-Switched Network (Store-and-Forward)

Consider a packet of length $L$ bits traversing a network path of $N$ identical links, each with transmission rate $R$ bps, and each link of length $d$ meters with signal propagation speed $s$ m/s.

**Step 1:** The source begins transmitting the packet onto the first link. The first bit reaches the next router in $T_p = d/s$ seconds. However, the **entire packet** must arrive at the first intermediate router before it can be forwarded. Time for full packet to be transmitted: $T_t = L/R$.

**Step 2:** Therefore, the time for the first bit to leave the source and for the last bit to be received at the **first router** is:
$$
T_{\text{hop1}} = T_t + T_p
$$

**Step 3:** Once the first router has received the full packet, it begins forwarding it to the second link. This introduces another $T_t$ seconds.

**Step 4:** Generalizing, for $N$ links and $(N-1)$ intermediate routers, the total store-and-forward delay is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_p + N \cdot T_{proc} + N \cdot T_q + (N-1) \cdot T_t
$$

**Step 5:** If we ignore processing and queuing delays for simplicity, this reduces to the **store-and-forward delay formula**:

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \dfrac{d}{s} + (N-1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $(N-1)$ and not $N$?**
> Because the source (the sender itself) is **not a router** — it does not need to wait for any packet before transmitting. Only the $N-1$ intermediate routers each wait for $L/R$ seconds to store the entire packet before forwarding. This is a classic KTU board-exam derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Throughput Efficiency of Circuit Switching

**Given:** A circuit-switched telemedicine session between a rural PHC and a city hospital.
- Call setup time per hop: $S$ seconds
- Number of hops: $N$
- Data transfer duration: $D$ seconds
- Teardown time: negligible

**Step 1:** Total setup time (sequential) = $N \cdot S$

**Step 2:** Total session time = $N \cdot S + D$

**Step 3:** Useful data time = $D$

**Step 4:** Throughput efficiency $\eta$ = (Useful time) / (Total time)

$$
\eta = \dfrac{D}{N \cdot S + D}
$$

**Step 5:** If $D \gg N \cdot S$, then $\eta \rightarrow 1$ (highly efficient — typical of long calls). If $D \ll N \cdot S$, then $\eta \rightarrow 0$ (highly inefficient — typical of short Web-like bursts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** This is precisely why **packet switching dominates modern telemedicine data networks** — medical file transfers are typically short, bursty, and do not justify the setup overhead of circuit switching.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 1 (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A hospital transmits a patient's MRI scan (file size = 4 MB) from a rural clinic to a city diagnostic center through a packet-switched network. The path has 4 links, each of 2000 km, with a transmission rate of 2 Mbps and propagation speed of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Assume negligible processing and queuing delay. Find the end-to-end delay.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert file size:
$$
L = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 10^6 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 3.2 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** Transmission delay per link:
$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^6} = 16 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Propagation delay per link:
$$
T_p = \dfrac{d}{s} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the store-and-forward formula with $N = 4$:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_t
$$
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 0.01 + 3 \times 16
$$
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.04 + 48 = 48.04 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Result: The MRI takes approximately **48.04 seconds** to arrive at the city hospital.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 2 (Circuit vs Packet Comparison)

> **Problem:** A teleconsultation session lasts 600 seconds. Setup time per switch is 1.5 s and there are 5 switches in the path. Calculate the throughput efficiency for both circuit and packet switching (assume packet switching has zero setup time but a 0.2% retransmission overhead).

**Solution (Circuit Switching):**

**Step 1:** Total setup time = $5 \times 1.5 = 7.5$ s

**Step 2:** Throughput efficiency:
$$
\eta_{CS} = \dfrac{600}{7.5 + 600} = \dfrac{600}{607.5} \approx 0.9877 = 98.77\%
$$

**Solution (Packet Switching):**

**Step 3:** No setup time, so total time ≈ 600 s + 0.2% retransmission overhead
$$
\text{Overhead time} = 0.002 \times 600 = 1.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Throughput efficiency:
$$
\eta_{PS} = \dfrac{600 - 1.2}{600} = \dfrac{598.8}{600} \approx 0.998 = 99.8\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For this long-duration teleconsultation, both methods are efficient, but packet switching slightly wins due to no setup overhead. However, packet switching has **variable delay**, which is the key reason circuit switching is still preferred for real-time voice/video.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Circuit Switching Architecture (Telemedicine)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the three-phase operation of a circuit-switched telemedicine link between a Rural PHC and a Tertiary City Hospital.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    SW1 --> SW2[Switching Node 2]
    SW2 --> SW3[Switching Node 3]
    SW3 --> CITY_B[Destination: City Hospital]

    PHC_A -. Setup Signal .-> SW1
    SW1 -. Reserve .-> SW2
    SW2 -. Reserve .-> SW3
    SW3 -. ACK .-> CITY_B
    CITY_B -. ACK Back .-> PHC_A

    PHC_A == Continuous Voice/Video Data ==> CITY_B
    CITY_B == Continuous Voice/Video Data ==> PHC_A

    CITY_B -. Teardown Signal .-> PHC_A
```

This diagram shows how different packets of the same MRI file (e.g., Packet 1, Packet 2, Packet 3) may take **completely different physical paths** through a network of routers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

The diagram below illustrates that in Virtual Circuit switching, a **logical path** is established first, and all subsequent packets follow the same logical route (although physical links may be shared with other users).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Q:** Define Circuit Switching and Packet Switching. List two key differences.

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Switching:** A switching technique in which a dedicated physical communication path is established between the source and destination **before** data transmission begins. The path is reserved exclusively for the entire duration of the call, irrespective of whether data is flowing continuously. Example: Traditional telephone network (PSTN).

**Packet Switching:** A switching technique in which data is broken into smaller units called **packets**, each containing source and destination addresses, and these packets are routed **independently** through the network. Example: The Internet (IP-based).

**Two Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| Path Reservation | Dedicated & reserved | Shared, no reservation |
| Delay | Constant after setup | Variable (depends on load) |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]**
**Q:** What are the three phases of circuit switching? Briefly explain each.

**Model Answer:**

The three phases of circuit switching are:

1. **Circuit Establishment Phase:** The source sends a connection request through switching nodes. Each node reserves a dedicated path. A confirmation reaches the source once the end-to-end circuit is ready. (1 mark)

2. **Data Transfer Phase:** Once the circuit is established, data flows continuously between the source and destination over the reserved path. Full bandwidth is available; switching nodes simply forward bits. (1 mark)

3. **Circuit Disconnect Phase:** When communication ends, a teardown signal is sent, and all intermediate nodes release the reserved resources, freeing the path for other users. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
**Q (A):** (a) Explain in detail the three phases of circuit switching with a neat block diagram. Discuss its suitability for telemedicine applications. **(7 marks)**
       (b) Compare and contrast Datagram Packet Switching with Virtual Circuit Packet Switching. Which is more suitable for transmitting Electronic Health Records (EHR) in a telehospital network, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Sub-Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** Circuit switching establishes a **dedicated end-to-end physical path** between the communicating entities before any data is exchanged. It is the classical technique used in PSTN.

**Three Phases (4 marks):**

- **Phase 1 – Circuit Establishment:** A `SETUP` signal travels hop-by-hop from the source to the destination. At each switching node (S1, S2, S3...), a dedicated channel slot is reserved on the outgoing link. When the destination acknowledges, a complete bidirectional circuit exists. Setup time = $N \times S$ (where N = number of hops, S = per-hop setup time).

- **Phase 2 – Data Transfer:** Once established, both stations communicate in real-time. The full channel bandwidth (e.g., 64 kbps in standard voice) is dedicated. Delay is **constant and predictable** — ideal for synchronous medical consultations and telesurgery.

- **Phase 3 – Circuit Disconnect:** A `RELEASE` signal is sent; all reserved resources are freed.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Reference: Diagram 4.1 above]

**Suitability for Telemedicine (1 mark):**
- ✅ **Pros:** Predictable delay, ideal for real-time video consultations, no packet loss, no jitter, secure dedicated path.
- ❌ **Cons:** Inefficient bandwidth use if data is bursty (e.g., transmitting a 5 MB DICOM file), expensive for intermittent traffic.

**Verdict:** Circuit switching is **best for live, real-time telemedicine** (telephonic consultation, video consultation, remote surgery) but **not ideal for bulk data transfer** like medical imaging archives.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Datagram Packet Switching:** Each packet is routed **independently** at each router. No path is pre-established. Different packets of the same message may take different routes. The Internet Protocol (IP) is the canonical example. Connectionless service.

- **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching:** A **logical path** (virtual circuit) is established first using a `CALL REQUEST` packet. All subsequent packets carry a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** and follow the same logical path. Connection-oriented service (e.g., X.25, ATM, Frame Relay).

**Detailed Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Setup Phase | None | Required |
| Addressing | Full source/dest in every packet | Only VCI in each packet |
| Routing Decision | At every hop | Only at setup |
| Packet Order | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed |
| Router Overhead | High (lookup per packet) | Low (VCI lookup) |
| Robustness to Failure | High (rerouting automatic) | Low (VC must be re-established) |
| Congestion Control | Difficult | Easier (flow control per VC) |

**Which is More Suitable for EHR Transmission? (2 marks):**

**Verdict: Virtual Circuit Switching is more suitable for EHR transmission** in a hospital network, because:

1. EHR data is **transactional** and **session-based** (e.g., doctor logs in, retrieves a record, edits it, logs out) — VC's connection-oriented nature matches this perfectly.
2. VCs provide **predictable performance** and **in-order delivery** — critical for medical record consistency.
3. **Lower per-packet overhead** (only VCI, not full IP addresses) makes it efficient within the controlled hospital LAN.
4. However, for **Internet-wide EHR sharing** between geographically separated hospitals, **Datagram (IP)** is preferred due to its robustness and global reachability.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
**Q (B):** (a) With a suitable diagram, explain the operation of Datagram Packet Switching. Derive the formula for end-to-end delay in a packet-switched network with $N$ hops. **(7 marks)**
       (b) A rural telemedicine center transmits a patient's CT scan (50 MB) to a city hospital over a packet-switched network with 5 links, each 1500 km long, transmission rate 5 Mbps, and signal speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the total end-to-end delay, assuming negligible processing and queuing delay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Sub-Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Diagram (2 marks):** [Reference: Diagram 4.2 above] — Source → Router A → {Router B, Router C} → Router D → Destination, with three packets taking different paths.

**Operation Explanation (3 marks):**
1. The message is divided into packets at the source. Each packet contains a **header** (source IP, destination IP, packet number, total packet count) and a **payload** (data).
2. Each packet is dispatched into the network and arrives at the first router.
3. At **every intermediate router**, the packet's destination address is examined, the routing table is consulted, and the packet is forwarded along the best available outgoing link (best = shortest path, lowest congestion, etc.).
4. Crucially, **each router makes an independent decision per packet** — so two consecutive packets from the same message may take different routes.
5. The destination receives the packets, possibly **out of order**, and **reassembles** them using the sequence numbers in the headers.
6. If a packet is lost or corrupted, the destination requests retransmission.

**Derivation (2 marks):** [As derived in Section 3.1]
$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_t
$$
where $T_t = L/R$ and $T_p = d/s$. The $(N-1)$ term reflects that only $N-1$ intermediate routers incur the store-and-forward transmission wait.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert CT scan size to bits.
$$
L = 50 \text{ MB} = 50 \times 10^6 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute transmission delay per link.
$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^6} = 80 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute propagation delay per link.
$$
T_p = \dfrac{d}{s} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.0075 \text{ s} = 7.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the store-and-forward formula with $N = 5$ hops.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_t
$$
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 0.0075 + 4 \times 80
$$
$$
T_{\text{total}} = 0.0375 + 320 = 320.0375 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Interpretation and conclusion.

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx 320.04 \text{ seconds} \approx 5 \text{ minutes and } 20 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** The CT scan takes approximately **5 minutes 20 seconds** to transmit end-to-end. The **transmission delay dominates** (320 s out of 320.04 s ≈ 99.99%), because the file is large (50 MB) and the link rate (5 Mbps) is moderate. This is a typical pattern in telemedicine bulk-data transfer — the bit-transmission time, not the propagation time, is the bottleneck.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Virtual Circuit with Circuit Switching** (–2 marks): A Virtual Circuit is a **logical** path, **not** a dedicated physical path. Always clarify in your answer.
