> 2. **Using $N \cdot (L/R)$ instead of $(N-1) \cdot (L/R)$** (–1 mark): The source does not store-and-forward. Only $N-1$ intermediate routers incur this delay.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert MB to bits** (–1 mark): 1 MB = $8 \times 10^6$ bits, not $10^6$ bits.
> 4. **Confusing propagation speed in fiber (2×10⁸ m/s) with speed of light in vacuum (3×10⁸ m/s)** (–1 mark): Use $2 \times 10^8$ m/s for fiber-optic links unless explicitly stated.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram in circuit switching question** (–1 to –2 marks): A neat diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU Part B.
> 6. **Ignoring the three-phase structure** in circuit switching answers (–2 marks): Always explicitly label: Setup → Transfer → Teardown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — High-Density Bullet Sheet:**

- ✅ Circuit switching has **three phases**: Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown.
- ✅ Packet switching has **two types**: **Datagram** (connectionless) and **Virtual Circuit** (connection-oriented).
- ✅ Virtual Circuit = **logical** path, NOT a **dedicated physical** path. (Most-asked concept!)
- ✅ Datagram = Internet Protocol (IP). Virtual Circuit = ATM, X.25, Frame Relay, MPLS.
- ✅ Store-and-forward delay formula: $T_{e2e} = N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_t$ (Note: $(N-1)$, not $N$).
- ✅ Transmission delay $T_t = L/R$; Propagation delay $T_p = d/s$.
- ✅ Signal speed in optical fiber = $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (NOT $3 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- ✅ Circuit switching throughput efficiency $\eta = D / (N \cdot S + D)$.
- ✅ Circuit switching is **best for real-time telemedicine** (voice/video); Packet switching is **best for bulk data** (EHR, PACS, MRI files).
- ✅ Datagram advantages: **Robust, simple, no setup**; disadvantage: **variable delay, out-of-order delivery**.
- ✅ Virtual Circuit advantages: **In-order delivery, low per-packet overhead**; disadvantage: **setup required, fragile to link failure**.
- ✅ Always convert: **1 MB = 8 × 10⁶ bits**; **1 Mbps = 10⁶ bps**; **1 Gbps = 10⁹ bps**.
- ✅ Real-time telemedicine requires **end-to-end delay < 150 ms** (ITU/WHO recommendation).
- ✅ Key telemedicine use-cases: **Live Consultation → Circuit / MPLS**, **EHR & PACS Transfer → IP Datagram**, **Hospital LAN Interconnect → Virtual Circuit / ATM**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.320 Series (Video Phone Based ISBN), T.120
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** H.320 Series (Video Phone Based ISDN), T.120

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context Clarification:** The syllabus notation "ISBN" in the context of H.320 is a transcription artifact. The correct underlying network for the H.320 Video Phone standard is **ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)**, not ISBN (which is the International Standard Book Number). This entire module treats the topic as **ISDN-based Video Telephony (H.320)**.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 H.320 — Formal Academic Definition

**H.320** is an **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector) Recommendation** titled *"Narrowband visual telephone services and systems."* It defines the complete technical framework, protocol stack, call-control procedures, and interoperability requirements for **circuit-switched video telephony over ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)**.

H.320 is the **first globally interoperable standard for room-based and desktop video conferencing**, serving as the foundational reference for early-generation telemedicine systems, teleradiology consultation rooms, and remote specialist clinics. It is sometimes called the **"Grandfather Standard"** of all modern video conferencing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus High-Yield Definition (verbatim expected):**
> *"H.320 is an ITU-T recommendation that specifies technical requirements for narrowband visual telephone services over ISDN. It addresses video coding, audio coding, multiplexing, signaling, and end-to-end system control for point-to-point and multipoint video conferences."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — H.320 in Plain English

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Express Train Track":**
> Think of **ISDN** as a dedicated express railway track reserved solely for your train. Unlike the public internet (a busy shared road with traffic jams), the ISDN track is **guaranteed to be empty and at fixed speed**. The H.320 standard is the **rulebook** that tells the train (your video call) exactly how fast to run, what shape the locomotive (video codec) should be, how many carriages (data channels) it can pull, and how stations (endpoints) must coordinate so two trains can pass each other smoothly. This is why H.320 telemedicine was the gold standard for **predictable, jitter-free medical video consultation** in the 1990s and 2000s.

### 1.3 T.120 — Formal Academic Definition

**T.120** is a family of ITU-T Recommendations (T.121 through T.128) that collectively specify a **real-time multipoint data-conferencing protocol suite**. Where H.320 handles the *audio and video channels*, **T.120 handles the data channel** — enabling collaborative applications such as shared whiteboards, file transfer, application sharing, and synchronized annotation of medical images (e.g., DICOM X-rays) during a live telemedicine session.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — T.120 in Plain English

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Conference Room Whiteboard":**
> If H.320 is the *video call* between two doctors (the voices and faces), then **T.120 is the shared digital whiteboard** in the same conference room. While H.320 transmits "I can see you and hear you," T.120 transmits "Here is a CT scan I am pointing at, and I have just drawn a red circle around the lesion — you can see my red circle appear live on your screen, and I can see yours too." It is the **collaborative annotation layer** that transforms a simple call into a *working medical consultation*.

### 1.5 Core Standardized Metrics (KTU High-Yield Numerics)

| Parameter | Standardized Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying Network | **ISDN (BRI / PRI)** | Circuit-switched bearer |
| Channel Bandwidth (B channel) | **64 kbps** | Fundamental bearer unit |
| Aggregate H.320 Bandwidth | **64 kbps – 1920 kbps** | Up to 30 bonded B channels |
| Video Codec (H.320 mandatory) | **H.261** (also H.263 optional) | CIF / QCIF resolution |
| Frame Rate | **7.5 – 30 fps** | 30 fps at 384 kbps+ |
| Audio Codec | **G.711 (3.4 kHz), G.722 (7 kHz), G.728 (LD-CELP)** | 56–64 kbps |
| Multiplexing | **H.221** | Frame structure |
| Call Control | **H.242, H.243, H.230** | End-to-end signaling |
| Data Channel Companion | **T.120** | Independent data conferencing |
| ITU-T Approval | **1990 (H.320), 1993–1996 (T.120 series)** | First interoperable standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Examinations:** Always explicitly state the **channel bandwidth of 64 kbps**, the **mandatory H.261 video codec**, and the **companion T.120 data protocol** when defining H.320. Examiners award high marks for these precise numeric anchors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 H.320 Architecture — Layered Operational Breakdown

The H.320 framework is built as a **multi-layer reference architecture**, each layer governed by an associated ITU-T Recommendation. Understanding these layers is critical for KTU Module 3 questions.

**Layer 1 — Network Bearer (ISDN Physical Layer)**
- Provides the digital **B (Bearer) channels** at **64 kbps** each
- BRI (Basic Rate Interface) = 2B + 1D (16 kbps) = 144 kbps
- PRI (Primary Rate Interface) = 23B + 1D (US/Japan) or 30B + 1D (E1, India/EU) = 1.544 Mbps or 2.048 Mbps

**Layer 2 — Audio / Video / Data Coding**
- **Video** → H.261 (mandatory), H.263 (optional, lower bitrate)
- **Audio** → G.711, G.722, G.728
- **Data** → T.120 suite

**Layer 3 — Multiplexing and Framing**
- **H.221** — defines the bit-stuffing frame structure that bundles audio, video, and data into the synchronous 64 kbps B-channel stream
- **H.230** — defines Frame Control and Indication (FCI) bits for channel-by-channel control

**Layer 4 — Call Control and Signaling**
- **H.242** — point-to-point call setup
- **H.243** — multipoint control procedures (MCU bridging)

### 2.2 The H.320 "Family" of Recommendations

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Mapping — KTU Examiners Frequently Test This:**

| Recommendation | Function | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **H.320** | Umbrella standard | The Family Head |
| **H.221** | Frame structure / multiplexer | "**H**ow to **C**ombine" |
| **H.230** | Frame control signals | "**C**ontrol **B**its" |
| **H.242** | Point-to-point call setup | "**P**2**P** handshake" |
| **H.243** | Multipoint control (MCU) | "**M**ulti-**P**oint" |
| **H.244** | Channel aggregation (H.221 bonding) | "**B**onding" |
| **H.261** | Video codec (mandatory) | "**V**ideo **C**ode" |
| **H.263** | Video codec (optional, better) | "**V**ideo **C**ode+ " |
| **H.281** | Far-end camera control (FECC) | "**R**emote **P**an-Tilt" |
| **G.711/G.722/G.728** | Audio codecs | "**A**udio **C**odes" |

### 2.3 T.120 Protocol Suite — Layered Architecture

The T.120 series forms a **four-layer protocol stack**, each layer with a dedicated ITU-T Recommendation:

| T.120 Layer | Recommendation | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Application Services | **T.121** (Templates), **T.126** (Still Image), **T.127** (File Transfer), **T.128** (App Sharing) | User-facing collaborative tools |
| Conference Control | **T.124** — Generic Conference Control (GCC) | Session management, privileges, conference topology |
| Transport / MCS | **T.125** — Multipoint Communication Service | Reliable data delivery to multiple endpoints |
| Network Adaptation | **T.122** (Service Definition), **T.123** (Protocol Stacks over ISDN, PSTN, IP) | Maps T.120 to physical network |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T.120 + H.320 = Full Multimedia Telemedicine Session:**
> The T.120 data channel is carried **in-band within the H.221 multiplex** over ISDN, meaning it shares the same physical B-channels as the video/audio but logically rides on a separate **Low Speed Data (LSD) channel of 4.8 kbps or 6.4 kbps** extracted from the H.221 frame.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** for Module 3 H.320/T.120 problems. **Memorize all rows verbatim.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Boundary / Limiting Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISDN B-channel rate | $R_{B} = 64$ | **kbps** | Fixed by ITU-T G.711 framing |
| 2 | BRI total rate | $R_{BRI} = 2 \cdot 64 + 16 = 144$ | **kbps** | 2B + 1D |
| 3 | PRI (E1) total rate | $R_{PRI,E1} = 30 \cdot 64 + 64 = 2048$ | **kbps** | 30B + 1D (EU/India) |
| 4 | PRI (T1) total rate | $R_{PRI,T1} = 23 \cdot 64 + 64 = 1544$ | **kbps** | 23B + 1D (US/Japan) |
| 5 | H.320 total bandwidth for *n* B-channels | $B_{H320} = n \cdot 64$ | **kbps** | $n \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6,12,24,30\}$ |
| 6 | Video payload rate (H.320) | $R_{video} = B_{H320} - R_{audio} - R_{LSD}$ | **kbps** | Audio + LSD carve out |
| 7 | Audio rate (G.711) | $R_{audio,G711} = 64$ | **kbps** | Worst-case audio |
| 8 | Audio rate (G.722) | $R_{audio,G722} = 48 \text{ or } 56$ | **kbps** | Wideband 7 kHz |
| 9 | Audio rate (G.728) | $R_{audio,G728} = 16$ | **kbps** | LD-CELP low bitrate |
| 10 | LSD rate (T.120 channel) | $R_{LSD} = 4.8 \text{ or } 6.4$ | **kbps** | Extracted from H.221 |
| 11 | H.261 frame format | $CIF = 352 \times 288$, $QCIF = 176 \times 144$ | **pixels** | YCbCr 4:2:0 |
| 12 | H.261 bit rate ceiling | $R_{H261} \leq 2048$ | **kbps** | p \times 64 |
| 13 | Frame rate ceilings | $30$ fps at $384$ kbps+; $15$ fps at $128$ kbps; $7.5$ fps at $64$ kbps | **fps** | Codec-dependent |
| 14 | H.320 end-to-end delay budget | $D_{H320} \leq 400$ | **ms** | G.114 one-way limit |
| 15 | MCU port count (typical) | $N_{ports} \in \{4,8,16,24\}$ | **endpoints** | Multipoint bridge |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern telemedicine practice, H.320 + T.120 systems powered the **first generation of telestroke, teledermatology, and telepathology** deployments worldwide. While largely superseded by H.323 and SIP-based systems on IP, H.320 ISDN infrastructure **remains in active use in rural India, defense medical services, and maritime telemedicine** where IP QoS cannot be guaranteed. **T.120, by contrast, is alive and well** — it underpins the data-collaboration layer of virtually every modern video conferencing client (Zoom, Microsoft Teams, Webex) under the name **"data conferencing"** or **"application sharing channel."**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — H.320 Available Video Bandwidth (Standard Problem Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Problem Template:** *"An H.320 telemedicine system uses **n** ISDN B-channels. Audio is G.711 (64 kbps) and the T.120 LSD channel is 4.8 kbps. Calculate the **available video bandwidth** and the **H.261 frame size in kB at QCIF**."*

**Given:**
- Number of bonded B-channels: $n = 6$
- Audio codec: G.711
- LSD (T.120) rate: 4.8 kbps

**Step 1 — Total H.320 Aggregate Bandwidth**

$$B_{H320} = n \cdot R_{B} = 6 \times 64 \text{ kbps} = 384 \text{ kbps}$$

*Explanation:* Each B-channel is 64 kbps; H.320 bonds them via H.221 framing. With 6 channels the aggregate bearer is 384 kbps.

**Step 2 — Carve out Audio + Data Sub-Channels**

$$R_{audio} + R_{LSD} = 64 + 4.8 = 68.8 \text{ kbps}$$

*Explanation:* The audio codec G.711 consumes one full 64 kbps sub-channel, and the T.120 LSD occupies 4.8 kbps extracted from the H.221 frame.

**Step 3 — Available Video Payload**

$$R_{video} = B_{H320} - R_{audio} - R_{LSD} = 384 - 68.8 = 315.2 \text{ kbps}$$

*Explanation:* The remainder of the multiplex is available for H.261 compressed video. This is the **bandwidth the H.261 encoder can consume per frame**.

**Step 4 — H.261 Frame Size at QCIF, 30 fps (KTU Check)**

$$S_{frame} = \frac{R_{video}}{f_{ps}} = \frac{315.2 \text{ kbps}}{30 \text{ fps}} = 10.5 \text{ kB/frame}$$

*Explanation:* At 30 frames per second, each QCIF frame budget is approximately 10.5 kilobytes, well within H.261's recommended intra-frame target of 10–12 kB at QCIF.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Upgrade for Diagnostic Telepathology (H.320)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Problem Template:** *"A teleradiology workstation requires diagnostic-quality 30 fps CIF video. The audio (G.728) needs 16 kbps and LSD is 6.4 kbps. How many B-channels are required?"*

**Step 1 — Minimum H.261 Bit Rate for 30 fps CIF**

For diagnostic-grade 30 fps CIF under H.261:

$$R_{video,min} \geq 384 \text{ kbps}$$

*Explanation:* CIF (352 \times 288) at 30 fps requires substantially more bandwidth than QCIF. Empirically, 384 kbps is the threshold for clinically acceptable motion fidelity.

**Step 2 — Total Aggregate Bandwidth Needed**

$$B_{H320} = R_{video,min} + R_{audio} + R_{LSD} = 384 + 16 + 6.4 = 406.4 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Bonded B-Channels**

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{B_{H320}}{R_{B}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{406.4}{64} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.35 \rceil = 7$$

*Explanation:* Since ISDN channels cannot be fractional, we round up to the next integer. 7 \times 64 = 448 kbps, providing 41.6 kbps of overhead margin.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — ISDN BRI vs PRI H.320 Telemedicine Sizing

**Step 1 — BRI Maximum H.320 Channels**

$$n_{BRI} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{BRI} = 128 \text{ kbps (excluding D-channel)}$$

*Conclusion:* BRI supports up to 2 B-channels = 128 kbps H.320 — sufficient for **desktop telemedical consultation at QCIF, 15 fps**.

**Step 2 — PRI Maximum H.320 Channels**

$$n_{PRI,E1} = 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{PRI} = 1920 \text{ kbps}$$

*Conclusion:* PRI supports up to 30 B-channels = 1.92 Mbps H.320 — sufficient for **diagnostic teleradiology, telesurgery, and medical grand rounds at full CIF, 30 fps**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use H.320 vs. H.323/SIP

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Justify the use of H.320 in a specific telemedicine scenario."* Use this matrix.

| Scenario | Recommended Standard | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Rural PHC with no broadband, only PSTN/ISDN | **H.320** | ISDN is the only guaranteed bearer |
| Maritime / Defense telemedicine vessel | **H.320** | Satellite ISDN with predictable QoS |
| Metropolitan tertiary hospital LAN | **H.323 / SIP** | High bandwidth, IP-native |
| Mobile ambulance-to-hospital link | **3G/4G + H.323** | IP-based wireless infrastructure |
| Multiparty international grand rounds | **H.323 with MCU** | IP bandwidth scalable |
| Telepathology live annotation of biopsy | **H.320 + T.120** | Guaranteed LSD channel for shared annotation |
| Teleradiology async DICOM transfer | **DICOM over IP** | Not real-time; bandwidth-tolerant |

### 3.5 Cross-Reference Matrix — H.320 Series Coverage Map

| H.320 Sub-Standard | KTU 2024 Weight | Likely Exam Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| H.320 umbrella | High | Definition, 2-mark Q |
| H.221 framing | Medium | 7-mark derivation |
| H.230 control | Low | Optional |
| H.242 / H.243 call control | Medium | 14-mark part (a) |
| H.261 / H.263 codecs | High | Numericals (kbps) |
| H.281 FECC | Low | Mention only |
| T.120 series | High | Definition + role (7-mark) |
| T.124 GCC / T.125 MCS | Medium | Architecture diagram |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 H.320 Protocol Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **H.320 protocol stack** from the telemedicine application down to the ISDN physical bearer. It illustrates how audio, video, and T.120 data are multiplexed by H.221, framed by H.230, call-controlled by H.242/H.243, and finally bonded onto ISDN B-channels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1[Medical Application Layer] --> H320
    APP2[T.120 Data Conferencing Suite] --> H320
    APP3[Electronic Patient Record GUI] --> H320

    subgraph H320[H.320 System Core]
        direction TB
        VID[Video Codec H.261 H.263]
        AUD[Audio Codec G.711 G.722 G.728]
        DAT[T.120 Data Channel LSD 4.8 to 6.4 kbps]
        MUX[H.221 Multiplexer and Demultiplexer]
        CTRL[H.230 Frame Control and Indication]
        CC[H.242 Point to Point and H.243 Multipoint Call Control]
    end

    VID --> MUX
    AUD --> MUX
    DAT --> MUX
    CTRL --> MUX
    MUX --> CC

    CC --> ISDN[ISDN Physical Layer B Channels 64 kbps Each]
    ISDN --> EXT[External Telemedicine Endpoint via ISDN Switched Network]

    style H320 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style MUX fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:1.5px
    style CTRL fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:1.5px
    style CC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1.5px
    style ISDN fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:1.5px
```

### 4.2 T.120 Protocol Stack — Layered Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        A1[T.121 Generic Application Templates]
        A2[T.126 Still Image Annotation and Markup]
        A3[T.127 Multipoint Binary File Transfer]
        A4[T.128 Application Sharing]
    end

    subgraph CTL[Conference Control Layer]
        C1[T.124 Generic Conference Control GCC]
    end

    subgraph TRN[Transport Layer]
        T1[T.125 Multipoint Communication Service MCS]
    end

    subgraph NET[Network Adaptation Layer]
        N1[T.122 Multipoint Service Definition]
        N2[T.123 Protocol Stacks over ISDN PSTN IP]
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    A4 --> C1
    C1 --> T1
    T1 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> PHY[ISDN B Channel or IP Network]

    style APP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style CTL fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style TRN fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style NET fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 H.320 + T.120 Telemedicine End-to-End Session Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PHC as Rural PHC Doctor
    participant ISDN1 as ISDN Switch 1
    participant MCU as Multipoint Control Unit
    participant ISDN2 as ISDN Switch 2
    participant HOSP as Tertiary Hospital Specialist

    PHC->>ISDN1: Q.931 SETUP n B channels
    ISDN1->>MCU: ISDN PRI 30B bonding H.221
    MCU->>ISDN2: H.243 multipoint cascade
    ISDN2->>HOSP: Q.931 SETUP ring

    Note over PHC,HOSP: H.242 Capability Exchange via H.230 FCI
    PHC->>MCU: H.261 video stream G.711 audio
    MCU->>HOSP: H.261 video stream G.711 audio
    HOSP-->>MCU: H.281 far end camera control
    MCU-->>PHC: H.281 pan tilt zoom applied

    Note over PHC,HOSP: T.120 data channel established on LSD
    PHC->>HOSP: T.126 DICOM X ray annotation
    HOSP->>PHC: T.127 patient file transfer
    PHC->>HOSP: T.121 shared markup

    PHC->>ISDN1: Q.931 DISCONNECT
    ISDN1->>MCU: Release n B channels
    MCU->>ISDN2: Release call
    ISDN2->>HOSP: Q.931 DISCONNECT confirmed
```

### 4.4 H.221 Super-Frame Structure (Conceptual Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frame Alignment 8 bits FAS] --> B[Bit Rate Allocation Signal 8 bits BAS]
    B --> C[Audio Sub Channel 64 kbps G.711]
    B --> D[Video Sub Channel Variable H.261]
    B --> E[Low Speed Data LSD 4.8 to 6.4 kbps T.120]
    B --> F[Encryption and FEC Optional]

    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffccff,stroke:#993399,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Examinations:** The FAS (Frame Alignment Signal) and BAS (Bit-rate Allocation Signal) of H.221 occupy the **first 16 bits** of every H.221 super-frame. These are NOT available for user data; they are *control overhead* and are sometimes tested as "what is the H.221 overhead per 80-octet super-frame."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Q1] [Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q.** Define the H.320 ITU-T Recommendation. What is the underlying network, and what video codec does it mandate?

**Model Answer:**

> H.320 is an ITU-T Recommendation (first approved in 1990) that defines the **technical requirements for narrowband visual telephone services and systems** over **ISDN (Integrated Services Digital Network)**. It specifies the video codec, audio codec, multiplexing, signaling, and end-to-end call-control procedures for point-to-point and multipoint video conferences. The **mandatory video codec is H.261**, which supports CIF (352 \times 288) and QCIF (176 \times 144) resolutions at frame rates from 7.5 to 30 fps over 64 kbps to 1920 kbps aggregate bandwidth formed by bonding multiple 64 kbps B-channels.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Q2] [Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q.** List any **four T.120-series Recommendations** with their functions.

**Model Answer:**

> | Recommendation | Function |
> |---|---|
> | **T.124** | Generic Conference Control (GCC) — manages conference creation, joining, and termination |
> | **T.125** | Multipoint Communication Service (MCS) — reliable data delivery to all participants |
> | **T.126** | Still Image Annotation and Markup — shared annotation of medical images |
> | **T.127** | Multipoint Binary File Transfer — exchange of patient records, DICOM files |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — H.320 Deep Architecture**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 14-Mark Q]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **H.320 protocol family**. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of H.221, H.230, H.242, and H.243 in establishing and managing an H.320 video conference over ISDN. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The H.320 Recommendation is an **umbrella standard** that integrates a family of ITU-T sub-Recommendations to deliver end-to-end narrowband video telephony over ISDN. Its architecture is layered, with each layer governed by a specific Recommendation.

**1. Network Bearer — ISDN:**
The base network is **ISDN**, which provides **B-channels of 64 kbps** each and a **D-channel of 16 kbps (BRI)** or 64 kbps (PRI) for signaling. H.320 bonds multiple B-channels to provide aggregate bandwidth of 64 kbps to 1920 kbps (up to 30 B-channels on an E1 PRI).

**2. H.221 — Multiplexing and Framing:**
H.221 is responsible for **bit-multiplexing** audio, video, and data into a synchronous stream across one or more B-channels. It defines a **80-octet super-frame structure** containing the **Frame Alignment Signal (FAS, 8 bits)** and **Bit-rate Allocation Signal (BAS, 8 bits)** at the start, followed by payload octets carrying audio (G.711/G.722/G.728), video (H.261/H.263), and the **Low-Speed Data (LSD) channel** that carries T.120.

**3. H.230 — Frame Control and Indication (FCI):**
H.230 specifies the **control bits** that ride inside the BAS portion of the H.221 frame. These bits signal capabilities (e.g., "I support H.263"), mode changes (audio/video encryption, broadcast mode), and synchronization events between endpoints. Without H.230, dynamic channel reallocation is impossible.

**4. H.242 — Point-to-Point Call Setup:**
H.242 defines the **end-to-end capability exchange and call-establishment procedure** for **two-endpoint** H.320 sessions. After Q.931 ISDN signaling establishes the physical B-channels, H.242 negotiates audio codec (G.711/G.722/G.728), video codec (H.261/H.263), data rate, encryption mode, and frame format (CIF/QCIF) between the two endpoints. H.242 also supports **dynamic mode switching** during the call.

**5. H.243 — Multipoint Control (MCU Bridging):**
H.243 extends H.242 for **multi-endpoint conferences** through the **Multipoint Control Unit (MCU)**. H.243 procedures allow endpoints to join/leave a multipoint conference, perform **chair-control token passing**, and select which endpoint is currently broadcasting. The MCU uses H.243 to maintain a consistent conference topology across all H.221 streams.

```
Block Diagram Reference: See Section 4.1 above
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students write "H.320 uses IP" — **this is WRONG**. H.320 is **ISDN-based**, not IP-based. H.323 is the IP-based counterpart.
> 2. Do not confuse **H.221 (framing)** with **H.230 (control bits)** — these are different layers, and examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. Always mention **H.261 as the mandatory codec**. Saying only "H.263" loses 1 mark.
> 4. The phrase "H.242 is for H.323" is incorrect — H.242 belongs to H.320.

---

**(b)** A telemedicine consultation room uses an H.320 system with **6 ISDN B-channels (384 kbps)**. The audio codec is **G.722 at 56 kbps**, and the **T.120 LSD channel is 6.4 kbps**. Calculate the available video bandwidth, the per-frame budget at 30 fps, and determine whether this is suitable for **diagnostic teleradiology at 30 fps CIF**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of B-channels: $n = 6$
- Audio: G.722 at $R_{audio} = 56$ kbps
- LSD (T.120): $R_{LSD} = 6.4$ kbps
- Frame rate target: $f = 30$ fps
- Resolution: CIF (352 \times 288)

**Step 1 — Total H.320 Aggregate Bandwidth**

$$B_{H320} = n \cdot 64 = 6 \times 64 = 384 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Available Video Payload**

$$R_{video} = B_{H320} - R_{audio} - R_{LSD} = 384 - 56 - 6.4 = 321.6 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Per-Frame Bit Budget at 30 fps**

$$R_{frame} = \frac{R_{video}}{f} = \frac{321.6 \text{ kbps}}{30 \text{ fps}} = 10.72 \text{ kbps/frame}$$

Converting to bytes:

$$S_{frame} = \frac{10.72}{8} = 1.34 \text{ kB/frame}$$

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Suitability Assessment**

For diagnostic-quality teleradiology at 30 fps CIF, the empirical minimum is approximately **384 kbps of net video payload** (per H.261 practical limits). The available **321.6 kbps falls short by ~62 kbps**.

**Conclusion:** This configuration is **adequate for clinical consultation at QCIF or for CIF at reduced frame rate (15–20 fps)**, but **NOT sufficient for diagnostic teleradiology at 30 fps CIF**. To upgrade, the system should be reconfigured to **8 B-channels (512 kbps)**, yielding a video payload of:

$$R_{video,upgraded} = 512 - 56 - 6.4 = 449.6 \text{ kbps}$$

which comfortably supports 30 fps CIF diagnostic quality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — T.120 Series Deep Dive (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **T.120 family of Recommendations**. With a layered diagram, describe the role of **T.121, T.124, T.125, and T.123** in a multipoint telemedicine data-conferencing session. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **T.120 series** of ITU-T Recommendations defines a **multipoint data-conferencing framework** that operates alongside H.320 (or H.323/SIP) to provide **real-time collaborative data services** in a multimedia session. In telemedicine, T.120 is essential for applications such as **shared annotation of DICOM medical images, multipoint file transfer of patient records, and synchronized whiteboard markup between specialists**.

The T.120 architecture is organized into **four hierarchical layers**, each specified by a dedicated Recommendation:

**1. T.123 — Network Adaptation Layer (Bottom):**
T.123 defines the **protocol stacks that map T.120 onto various physical and link-layer networks**, including **ISDN (X.25/LAPB), PSTN (V.120/V.34), and IP (TCP/UDP)**. T.123 is the only layer that depends on the underlying network; all upper layers are network-agnostic. In an H.320 telemedicine session, T.123 typically rides on the LSD (Low-Speed Data) channel extracted from the H.221 multiplex.

**2. T.125 — Multipoint Communication Service (MCS) Layer:**
T.125 provides **reliable, ordered, multipoint data delivery** to all conference participants. It manages **logical channels** called MCS channels, with **token-passing topology control** to avoid collisions. T.125 guarantees that an annotation made on a CT scan by the remote radiologist appears identically and synchronously on the local physician's screen.

**3. T.124 — Generic Conference Control (GCC) Layer:**
T.124 is the **session manager** of T.120. It handles **conference creation, joining, leaving, privilege assignment (chair/panel rights), and conference termination**. T.124 is invoked by the higher-layer T.121 application services and uses T.125 for transport. T.124 also implements **conference roster management** and **capability negotiation**.

**4. T.121 — Application Template Layer (Top):**
T.121 provides **generic application service templates** that specific applications (T.126, T.127, T.128) inherit and extend. T.121 defines the **Generic Application Service (GAS)** interface, which standardizes how applications register, send/receive data, and terminate within a T.120 conference. This allows vendors to add proprietary telemedicine tools (e.g., a custom ECG-waveform sharer) that interwork with the standard T.120 stack.

```
Layered Diagram Reference: See Section 4.2 above
```

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast **H.320 with T.120**. Why is T.120 considered the "data companion" of H.320? Give **two telemedicine-specific use cases** of T.120 in a live H.320 consultation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Parameter | H.320 | T.120 |
|---|---|---|
| ITU-T Series | H-series (multimedia systems) | T-series (data conferencing) |
| Primary Function | **Real-time video and audio** | **Real-time data sharing** |
| Network Bearer | ISDN (64 kbps B-channels) | ISDN / PSTN / IP (via T.123) |
| Channel Allocation | Dominant bandwidth (most B-channels) | Low-Speed Data (4.8 or 6.4 kbps) |
| Multiplexing | H.221 frame structure | Independent logical channels (T.125) |
| Standards Family | H.221, H.230, H.242, H.243, H.261 | T.121, T.124, T.125, T.123, T.126, T.127 |
| Typical Use | See the patient and hear the doctor | Share images, annotate, transfer files |

**Why T.120 is the "Data Companion" of H.320:**

H.320 alone provides only **audio and video** channels; it has **no native mechanism for sharing images, whiteboards, or files** in real time. T.120 fills this gap by providing a **parallel, standards-based data channel** that rides *alongside* H.320 audio/video in the same ISDN session. Together, they form a **complete multimedia telemedicine session**: H.320 carries the *voice and face*, T.120 carries the *content and collaboration*.

**Two Telemedicine-Specific T.120 Use Cases:**

1. **Live DICOM Image Annotation (T.126):** A teleradiologist at a tertiary hospital receives a chest X-ray from a rural PHC. During the live H.320 audio/video call, the local physician opens the X-ray in the T.126-compatible viewer. The radiologist **draws a red circle around a suspected lesion** using a stylus — the annotation is **synchronized via T.125 MCS** to the local viewer's screen in real time, enabling a **live "see-what-I-see" diagnostic exchange**.

2. **Patient Record File Transfer (T.127):** While the H.320 video consultation is in progress, the specialist requests the patient's prior ECG strip and lab report. The T.127 file-transfer service **multicasts the file** from the PHC to all conference participants (e.g., for recording in the hospital EMR) with **reliable delivery guarantees** from T.125 MCS, ensuring no partial or corrupted medical data is delivered.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — T.120 vs. H.320 Confusion:**
> 1. Do **NOT** claim T.120 carries video. T.120 is **exclusively for data** (files, annotations, application sharing).
> 2. Do **NOT** describe T.120 as an "alternative" to H.320 — it is a **complementary companion** that runs *alongside* H.320 (or H.323/SIP).
> 3. **T.124 ≠ T.125** — many students swap them. T.124 is *conference control*; T.125 is *data transport*. Examiners test this distinction specifically.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist for H.320 / T.120**

- **H.320** is the ITU-T standard for **narrowband video telephony over ISDN**, approved in **1990**.
- Underlying network is **ISDN**, with **B-channels of 64 kbps** each (BRI: 2B+D; PRI E1: 30B+D).
- Aggregate H.320 bandwidth ranges from **64 kbps to 1920 kbps**.
- **Mandatory video codec: H.261**. Optional: **H.263**. Resolutions: **CIF (352×288)** and **QCIF (176×144)**.
- **Audio codecs: G.711 (64 kbps), G.722 (48/56 kbps), G.728 (16 kbps)**.
- **H.221** handles framing/multiplexing. **H.230** carries control bits. **H.242** does point-to-point setup. **H.243** handles multipoint/MCU.
- **H.281** is for **Far-End Camera Control (FECC)** — pan, tilt, zoom.
- **T.120** is the **data-conferencing companion** of H.320; it shares the LSD (4.8 or 6.4 kbps) channel of the H.221 frame.
- **T.121** — Application templates. **T.123** — Network adaptation. **T.124** — Generic Conference Control. **T.125** — Multipoint Communication Service. **T.126** — Still image annotation. **T.127** — File transfer. **T.128** — Application sharing.
- **T.124 (GCC)** is the conference manager; **T.125 (MCS)** is the data transport; **T.123** is the network adapter.
- **H.320 ≠ H.323**: H.320 is ISDN-based; H.323 is IP-based. This is a top-favorite KTU trick question.
- **T.120 still exists today** in modern clients (Zoom, Teams) under the "application sharing" feature.
- **Common KTU numerics to memorize:** 64 kbps (B-channel), 144 kbps (BRI), 2.048 Mbps (E1 PRI), 1.544 Mbps (T1 PRI), 352×288 (CIF), 176×144 (QCIF), 30 fps at 384 kbps+ (diagnostic threshold).
- **Telemedicine use cases** of T.120: live DICOM annotation, multipoint file transfer of patient records, shared ECG waveform viewing.
- **ISDN still relevant** in rural India, defense, maritime, and satellite-link telemedicine deployments.
- **End-to-end H.320 delay budget** is **≤ 400 ms** per ITU-T G.114 one-way voice limit.
- For **diagnostic teleradiology 30 fps CIF**, you need **≥ 8 B-channels (512 kbps)** with G.722 audio.
- **LSD channel** is the sub-channel within H.221 that carries **T.120 data**; rate is **4.8 kbps or 6.4 kbps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** When a question mentions H.320, **always draw the H.221 frame and the layered protocol stack**. Examiners at KTU consistently award 2–3 extra marks for a clean labeled diagram in 7-mark and 14-mark answers. Pair every H.320 answer with a T.120 mention (data channel) to demonstrate integrated understanding — this signals to the examiner that you have mastered the full Module 3 scope.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: H.324 (Video Phone Based PSTN): Video Conferencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** H.324 (Video Phone Based PSTN): Video Conferencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**H.324** is an umbrella recommendation by the **ITU-T (International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector)** that defines the requirements for low-bit-rate multimedia communication over the **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)**. It is officially titled *"Terminal for low bit-rate multimedia communication"* and is the foundational standard for legacy **video telephony** and **desktop video conferencing** on analog dial-up connections.

The standard integrates:
- **H.263 (and H.261)** for video compression
- **G.723.1 / G.729** for speech compression
- **H.223** for multiplexing/demultiplexing
- **H.245** for call control and signalling
- **V.34 modem (up to 33.6 kbps)** as the physical layer
- **V.8 / V.25** for terminal initiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In telemedicine, H.324 is significant because it enabled **first-generation teleconsultation systems** that could be deployed in remote/rural clinics where broadband (ISDN, DSL, fibre) was unavailable. It uses the existing copper telephone wire — a *huge* advantage in low-resource settings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have only a **single, narrow garden hose** (the PSTN copper line) connecting your house to the water tank (the remote hospital). You want to simultaneously send **two streams** — clean drinking water (audio of the doctor's voice) and **shampoo foam** (video of the patient's wound). The hose is too narrow to carry both separately, so you:

1. **Squeeze the video (H.263)** — drop frames, reduce resolution to 176×144 (QCIF), throw away redundant data.
2. **Squeeze the audio (G.723.1)** — eliminate silent gaps, model only the human vocal tract.
3. **Mix both into one ribbon (H.223 Multiplexer)** — packets alternate: 1 video, 1 audio, 1 control, 1 video, 1 audio...
4. **Push the ribbon through the hose (V.34 Modem)** — converts digital bits into analog tones that can travel over voice-grade lines at **33.6 kbps**.
5. **At the receiving end, the ribbon is unrolled (H.223 Demultiplexer)**, foam and water are separated back into two streams.

That entire "push-pull" pipeline is **H.324** — a system that gives you *acceptable* audio-video communication using just a **regular telephone line** and a **modem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandwidth Reality Check:** A typical H.324 connection runs at **28.8 kbps to 33.6 kbps**. Compare this with today's Zoom call running at **2–5 Mbps** — H.324 is roughly **100× slower**, which is why the video looks like a *slow slideshow* (typically **5–15 frames per second** at QCIF resolution of **176 × 144 pixels**).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Maximum Modem Speed (V.34):** **33.6 kbps** (theoretical); practical around **26–30 kbps**
- **Video Frame Rate:** **5–15 fps** at QCIF (176 × 144)
- **Audio Bit-rate (G.723.1):** **5.3 or 6.3 kbps**
- **Video Bit-rate (H.263):** typically **20–25 kbps** of the remaining bandwidth
- **End-to-End Latency:** **300 – 800 ms** (chatty but usable)
- **Call Setup Time:** **10 – 30 seconds** (modem handshake is the bottleneck)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **H.324M (Mobile Extension):** When H.324 was adapted for **3G mobile networks** (where the underlying link is digital, not analog modem), it was called **H.324 Annex C** or informally **H.324M**. It is the technology behind **3G video calling** on early smartphones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The H.324 Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

H.324 is not a single protocol; it is a **framework recommendation** that ties together several sub-recommendations. Think of it as a *recipe* — the dish is "video call over PSTN", and the ingredients are individual ITU-T standards.

| OSI Layer | H.324 Component | Function | KTU Keyword |
|-----------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| Application | User applications (telemedicine, video call) | End-user software | Telemedical consultation |
| Presentation | H.263 / H.261 | Video compression | Video codec |
| Presentation | G.723.1 / G.729 | Audio compression | Speech codec |
| Session | H.245 | Call control, capability exchange, channel management | Signalling |
| Data Link | H.223 | Multiplexing/demultiplexing of A/V + data + control | Multiplexer |
| Physical | V.34 (or V.90) modem + PSTN | Analog transmission over copper pair | Modem |
| Physical | V.8 / V.25 | Call initiation, modem handshake | Dial-up setup |

### 2.2 Detailed Sub-Protocol Breakdown

#### A. H.263 — The Video Codec
- Designed for **low bit-rates (< 64 kbps)**.
- Operates on **QCIF (176×144)**, **CIF (352×288)**, and **Sub-QCIF (128×96)**.
- Uses **DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform)** + **Motion Compensation** + **Entropy Coding**.
- **Annexes (D, F, etc.)** add features like error resilience — *critical* over noisy PSTN lines.

#### B. G.723.1 — The Audio Codec
- Operates at **5.3 kbps (ACELP)** or **6.3 kbps (MP-MLQ)**.
- Frame duration: **30 ms** with a **7.5 ms look-ahead** → 30 ms of audio becomes a packet every **37.5 ms**.
- Optimized for human speech (300–3400 Hz bandwidth).

#### C. H.223 — The Multiplexer
- Packs video, audio, data, and control into a single stream.
- Uses **Adaptation Layer (AL1, AL2, AL3)** to provide error protection and sequence numbering.
- **AL3** is mandatory — provides **8-bit CRC** and **retransmission** for error-prone PSTN links.
- **H.223 Annex A, B, C** add increasing levels of robustness.

#### D. H.245 — The Signalling Protocol
- Performs:
  1. **Capability Exchange** — what codecs/resolutions each end supports.
  2. **Master–Slave Determination** — avoids conflicts (e.g., who is "boss" of the call).
  3. **Logical Channel Signalling** — opens/closes audio and video channels.
  4. **Multipoint Signalling** — for H.324 conferences with >2 parties (uses MCU).
  5. **Call Closure** — clean tear-down.

#### E. V.34 — The Modem
- The bottleneck of H.324. A V.34 modem achieves **33.6 kbps** over PSTN using **trellis-coded modulation** across **0–3429 Hz** audio band.
- Theoretical limit is **33.6 kbps**; **Shannon's channel capacity theorem** is the reason.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits per second |
| **PSTN Audio Bandwidth** | $B = 3000$ to $3400$ | Hz |
| **V.34 Max Bit-rate** | $C_{V.34} \approx 33600$ | bps |
| **H.223 Frame Rate** | One mux-frame per ~10 ms | ms |
| **G.723.1 Frame Size** | 30 ms audio + 7.5 ms look-ahead = 37.5 ms | ms |
| **H.263 Common Resolutions** | Sub-QCIF = 128×96 ; QCIF = 176×144 ; CIF = 352×288 | pixels |
| **H.263 Default Frame Rate** | $\approx 7.5$ to $15$ | fps |
| **Total H.324 Payload (typical)** | $R_{total} = R_{audio} + R_{video} + R_{control} \le 33.6$ | kbps |
| **G.723.1 Bit-rates** | $5.3$ (ACELP) or $6.3$ (MP-MLQ) | kbps |
| **End-to-End Latency Budget** | $\approx 400$ ms (acceptable), $>800$ ms (poor) | ms |
| **MCU Conference Capacity** | $N$ parties $\Rightarrow$ bandwidth = $N \cdot 33.6$ kbps uplink (centralized MCU) | kbps |
| **Asymmetric Modem (V.90/V.92)** | Downstream: 56 kbps ; Upstream: 33.6 kbps | kbps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shannon's Limit on PSTN:** With $B = 3400$ Hz and typical telephone line SNR $\approx 40$ dB ($10^{4}$), the maximum theoretical capacity is:
> $$C = 3400 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10000) \approx 3400 \cdot 13.29 \approx 45186 \text{ bps}$$
> V.34's **33.6 kbps** reaches about **74% of Shannon's limit** — a remarkable engineering achievement for 1990s DSP technology.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of H.324 |
|--------------------|--------------|
| **Telemedicine (KTU focus)** | First-generation rural teleconsultation kiosks; portable video-phones for ambulance-to-hospital links |
| **3G Mobile Video Calling** | H.324M was the *de facto* video-call standard for UMTS/3G handsets (2003–2010) |
| **Disaster Response** | Field hospitals where only analog lines survive (fibre cut, towers down) |
| **Legacy Tele-education** | Distance learning over dial-up in the late 1990s and early 2000s |
| **Video Phones** | Standalone consumer devices (e.g., 8x8, D-Link, Intel Video Phone) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Bandwidth Budget Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A telemedicine H.324 link is established over a V.34 modem at **28.8 kbps**. The audio codec is G.723.1 at **6.3 kbps**. H.245 control uses **3 kbps** (steady-state average). H.223 framing overhead is **5%** of the total bit-rate. Calculate the **available bit-rate for the H.263 video channel**.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify total usable bit-rate**
The modem provides a raw pipe of $R_{raw} = 28.8$ kbps. H.223 must add a small header overhead per mux-frame, but the problem states this as **5%** of the *total* bit-rate (i.e., the *net useful* bit-rate is 95% of 28.8 kbps).

$$
R_{net} = R_{raw} \times (1 - 0.05) = 28.8 \times 0.95
$$

$$
R_{net} = 27.36 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 2: Subtract audio bit-rate**
G.723.1 MP-MLQ mode consumes $R_{audio} = 6.3$ kbps.

$$
R_{after\_audio} = R_{net} - R_{audio} = 27.36 - 6.3
$$

$$
R_{after\_audio} = 21.06 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract H.245 control overhead**
H.245 exchanges keep-alive and control frames; on average this consumes $R_{control} = 3$ kbps.

$$
R_{video} = R_{after\_audio} - R_{control} = 21.06 - 3
$$

$$
R_{video} = 18.06 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4: Final Result**

$$
\boxed{R_{video} \approx 18.06 \text{ kbps}}
$$

This means H.263 will compress the video to roughly **18 kbps** — sufficient for **QCIF (176×144)** at around **6–8 fps** with modest motion (acceptable for a static dermatology consult, but inadequate for endoscopy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for telemedicine:** The examiner often tests whether the student realizes that *every kbps* matters at PSTN speeds. Reducing audio from 6.3 to 5.3 kbps (ACELP) frees up **1 kbps** for video — enough to bump frame rate by ~1 fps.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Shannon Capacity Verification

**Problem Statement:** A rural Indian PSTN line has an audio passband of $B = 3200$ Hz and a measured SNR of $35$ dB. Compute the **theoretical Shannon capacity** and compare it with V.34's achieved bit-rate of 33.6 kbps. What is V.34's **spectral efficiency**?

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear scale**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{35/10} = 10^{3.5}
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} \approx 3162.28
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon–Hartley theorem**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{linear})
$$

$$
C = 3200 \cdot \log_2(1 + 3162.28)
$$

$$
C = 3200 \cdot \log_2(3163.28)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the log base 2**

$$
\log_2(3163.28) = \frac{\ln(3163.28)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{8.0596}{0.6931} \approx 11.628
$$

**Step 4: Final capacity**

$$
C = 3200 \times 11.628
$$

$$
C \approx 37211 \text{ bps} \approx 37.21 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 5: Compute spectral efficiency**
Spectral efficiency $\eta$ is defined as:

$$
\eta = \frac{R}{B} = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

$$
\eta \approx 11.628 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

**Step 6: Compare with V.34**

$$
\text{V.34 efficiency} = \frac{33600}{3200} = 10.5 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

**Step 7: V.34 vs Shannon — efficiency ratio**

$$
\frac{\eta_{V.34}}{\eta_{Shannon}} = \frac{10.5}{11.628} \approx 0.903 = 90.3\%
$$

#### Final Result Table

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Shannon Capacity $C$ | **37.21** | kbps |
| V.34 Achieved Rate | **33.6** | kbps |
| Spectral Efficiency (Shannon) | **11.63** | bits/s/Hz |
| Spectral Efficiency (V.34) | **10.5** | bits/s/Hz |
| **V.34 / Shannon Ratio** | **90.3 %** | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** V.34 reaches over **90% of Shannon's limit** — a near-optimal modem design. Further speed gains (V.90 → 56 kbps downstream) required breaking the analog assumption and exploiting the **digital hybrid** in the central office exchange.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Latency Calculation for Tele-ECG over H.324

**Problem Statement:** A patient-side H.324 terminal captures a 12-lead ECG waveform (audio channel) and sends it to a cardiologist. Given:
- Audio packet size = **24 bytes** (G.723.1 frame)
- Modem rate = **26.4 kbps**
- H.223 mux-frame header = **4 bytes** per packet
- Propagation delay over PSTN (single hop) = **30 ms** (one-way)
- Modem buffering = **20 ms**

Calculate the **one-way audio latency** of a single packet.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Total bytes per audio packet on the wire**

$$
\text{Bytes}_{total} = \text{Audio} + \text{H.223 header} = 24 + 4 = 28 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to bits**

$$
\text{Bits}_{total} = 28 \times 8 = 224 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Serialization delay over the modem**

$$
t_{serial} = \frac{\text{Bits}_{total}}{R_{modem}} = \frac{224}{26400}
$$

$$
t_{serial} \approx 0.00848 \text{ s} = 8.48 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Add fixed delays**

$$
t_{total} = t_{serial} + t_{propagation} + t_{buffering}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 8.48 + 30 + 20
$$

$$
t_{total} = 58.48 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Round-trip time (RTT) for interactive ECG review**

$$
\text{RTT} = 2 \times t_{total} = 2 \times 58.48
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RTT} \approx 117 \text{ ms}}
$$

This is well within the **300 ms threshold** for acceptable real-time voice — H.324 is *viable* for stethoscope/ECG auscultation audio, *not* for real-time diagnostic video of moving subjects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 H.324 System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Camera and Microphone] --> B[Video Codec H.263]
    A1[Microphone Only] --> C[Audio Codec G.723.1]
    B --> D[Video Channel]
    C --> E[Audio Channel]
    F[User Data and Control] --> G[H.245 Signalling]
    D --> H[H.223 Multiplexer]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[V.34 Modem]
    I --> J[(PSTN Copper Line)]
    J --> K[V.34 Modem Receiver]
    K --> L[H.223 Demultiplexer]
    L --> M[Video Channel RX]
    L --> N[Audio Channel RX]
    L --> O[H.245 Signalling RX]
    M --> P[H.263 Decoder]
    N --> Q[G.723.1 Decoder]
    P --> R[Display and Speaker]
    Q --> R
    O --> S[Call Controller]
    S --> T[End User Application]
```

### 4.2 H.324 Call Setup Sequence (Signalling Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Calling H.324 Terminal
    participant ModemC as Calling V.34 Modem
    participant PSTN as PSTN Network
    participant ModemR as Receiving V.34 Modem
    participant Callee as Called H.324 Terminal

    Caller->>ModemC: Off-hook, dial number
    ModemC->>PSTN: V.8 handshake tones
    PSTN->>ModemR: Ring signal
    ModemR->>Callee: Alert (ring)
    Callee->>ModemR: Off-hook
    ModemC-->>ModemR: V.34 modem training (33.6 kbps)
    ModemR-->>Callee: Physical link ready

    Caller->>Callee: H.245 TerminalCapabilitySet
    Callee->>Caller: H.245 TerminalCapabilitySetAck
    Caller->>Callee: H.245 MasterSlaveDetermination
    Callee->>Caller: H.245 MasterSlaveAck
    Caller->>Callee: H.245 OpenLogicalChannel (Audio)
    Callee->>Caller: H.245 OpenLogicalChannelAck
    Caller->>Callee: H.245 OpenLogicalChannel (Video)
    Callee->>Caller: H.245 OpenLogicalChannelAck

    loop Multiplexed Media Stream
        Caller->>Callee: H.223 mux-frames (audio + video + control)
    end

    Caller->>Callee: H.245 EndSessionCommand
    PSTN->>ModemC: Hang-up
    PSTN->>ModemR: Hang-up
```

### 4.3 H.324 Multiplexing Concept (AL3 Frame Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    StreamIn[Multiple Logical Channels: Audio, Video, Control, Data] --> MuxLayer[H.223 Multiplexer]

    subgraph FrameStructure["H.223 Mux Frame Structure (AL3)"]
        direction TB
        H[High-Level Overhead: 4 bits]
        MUX[Mux Code Field: variable]
        CRC[8-bit CRC Header Check]
        Pay[Payload: Audio Frames, Video Frames, Control Frames]
    end

    MuxLayer --> FrameStructure
    FrameStructure --> Modem[V.34 Modem]
    Modem --> Line[(PSTN)]
```

### 4.4 H.324 vs H.320 vs H.323 vs H.324M — Comparative Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Parent[ITU-T Multimedia Conferencing Family] --> H320[H.320 - ISDN Video Conferencing]
    Parent --> H323[H.323 - IP / LAN Video Conferencing]
    Parent --> H324[H.324 - PSTN Video Phone]
    Parent --> H324M[H.324M - 3G Mobile Video Call]
    Parent --> H310[H.310 - ATM / Broadband]

    H320 --> Notes1[Digital 64 to 1920 kbps, H.261/H.263, G.711/G.722/G.728]
    H323 --> Notes2[Packet-based, H.261/H.263/H.264, G.711/G.722/G.723.1, RAS and H.225]
    H324 --> Notes3[Analog PSTN, H.263, G.723.1, V.34 modem at 33.6 kbps]
    H324M --> Notes4[Mobile 3G, replaces V.34 with WCDMA channel at 64 kbps]
    H310 --> Notes5[ATM-based broadband multimedia]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid:** Always remember — H.324 = **Plain-old phone line**, H.320 = **ISDN digital line**, H.323 = **Packet network (LAN/Internet)**, H.324M = **3G mobile**. They share video/audio codecs but differ in the **transport layer**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the major ITU-T sub-recommendations that constitute the H.324 standard and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The H.324 standard is a **framework** that integrates five key ITU-T sub-recommendations:

| Sub-Recommendation | Function |
|--------------------|----------|
| **H.263 (or H.261)** | Video compression and decompression |
| **G.723.1 (or G.729)** | Audio/speech compression at 5.3 or 6.3 kbps |
| **H.223** | Multiplexing/demultiplexing of audio, video, data, and control into a single stream |
| **H.245** | Call control — capability exchange, master-slave determination, logical channel management |
| **V.34 (with V.8 and V.25)** | Modem standard providing physical-layer transmission at up to 33.6 kbps over analog PSTN |

*(Full marks: 1 mark for each correctly named sub-recommendation with its function, plus 1 mark for mentioning H.324 is a "framework" / "umbrella" recommendation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why H.324 video quality is significantly lower than modern video conferencing standards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Limited bandwidth:** H.324 uses a V.34 modem at a maximum of **33.6 kbps**, whereas modern H.323/SIP video calls use **384 kbps to 2 Mbps+** — roughly **10–60×** more bandwidth *(1 mark)*.

2. **Low resolution and frame rate:** H.263 codec typically operates at **QCIF (176×144) at 5–15 fps**, producing a "slide-show" feel *(1 mark)*.

3. **High error rate on PSTN:** Analog lines are subject to noise, echo, and fading; H.223 AL3 headers and CRC add overhead and limit achievable throughput *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse H.324 with H.320** (ISDN) or H.323 (LAN). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - **Always state the bit-rate** of the audio codec (G.723.1 → 5.3/6.3 kbps) when discussing bandwidth budget.
> - **Do NOT mention "H.264" in an H.324 context** — H.264 (H.264/AVC) is an H.323/IMS codec. H.324 standard predates H.264 and uses H.263.
> - **Mention H.223 AL3** (not just H.223) to score full marks on error resilience.
> - **Never write the modem speed as 56 kbps for H.324** — that is V.90/V.92 and is **asymmetric**; H.324 is symmetric at 33.6 kbps.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **H.324 protocol stack** and the role of each component in enabling a video call over the PSTN. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A telemedicine consultation between a rural Primary Health Centre (PHC) and a city hospital uses H.324 over a V.34 modem link at **26.4 kbps**. The H.223 multiplexer uses **AL3** with a **4-byte header per packet**. Audio is encoded with **G.723.1 at 5.3 kbps** using 30-byte frames, and **H.245 control** consumes **2.5 kbps** on average. Calculate the **net bit-rate available for the H.263 video channel**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — H.324 Protocol Stack (7 marks):**

H.324 is an **umbrella recommendation** (1 mark) by ITU-T that integrates the following components in a layered architecture (1 mark for diagram/architecture mention):

1. **Physical Layer — V.34 Modem (1 mark):** Provides analog transmission over the PSTN's voice-grade copper pair, achieving up to 33.6 kbps using trellis-coded modulation. V.8 handles initial modem handshake; V.25 manages call setup tones.

2. **Data Link Layer — H.223 Multiplexer (1 mark):** Combines multiple logical channels (video, audio, control, user data) into a single serial bit-stream. Uses **AL3 (Adaptation Layer 3)** with 8-bit CRC for error detection and retransmission — critical for noisy PSTN.

3. **Session Layer — H.245 Signalling (1 mark):** Manages call control. Functions include:
   - **Terminal Capability Exchange** — advertises supported codecs and resolutions.
   - **Master–Slave Determination** — resolves conflicts.
   - **Logical Channel Signalling** — opens/closes audio/video channels dynamically.
   - **End-Session** — clean call tear-down.

4. **Presentation Layer — Audio/Video Codecs (1 mark):**
   - **G.723.1** speech codec at 5.3/6.3 kbps; 30 ms frame size.
   - **H.263** video codec at QCIF (176×144) resolution; uses DCT + motion compensation.

5. **Application Layer — Telemedicine Software (1 mark):** End-user video conferencing application that integrates clinical workflow (patient records, stethoscope, dermatoscope inputs).

6. **Why H.324 matters for telemedicine (1 mark):** It enables video consultation over **existing analog telephone lines** — critical for rural clinics in India where broadband rollout is incomplete. Despite low bandwidth, it supports **tele-dermatology, tele-ECG (audio), and tele-OPG** (audio + still images).

---

**Part (b) — Bandwidth Budget Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Modem rate: $R_{raw} = 26.4$ kbps
- G.723.1 audio frame: 30 bytes at 5.3 kbps
- H.245 control overhead: $R_{ctrl} = 2.5$ kbps
- H.223 AL3 header: 4 bytes per packet

**Step 1: Audio packetization overhead (2 marks)**

The G.723.1 audio frame is 30 bytes = 240 bits. H.223 adds 4 bytes = 32 bits of header per packet.

$$
\text{Total audio per packet} = 240 + 32 = 272 \text{ bits}
$$

Since the audio codec operates at 5.3 kbps, the effective on-wire audio bit-rate is:

$$
R_{audio, wire} = 5.3 \times \frac{272}{240} = 5.3 \times 1.1333 = 6.006 \text{ kbps}
$$

*Alternatively, accept the simple model: audio uses 5.3 kbps of payload and 4 bytes of header per frame. The header overhead depends on packetization period.*

**Step 2: Total consumed bandwidth (audio + control) (2 marks)**

$$
R_{used} = R_{audio,wire} + R_{control} = 6.006 + 2.5
$$

$$
R_{used} = 8.506 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3: Compute available bit-rate for video (2 marks)**

$$
R_{video} = R_{raw} - R_{used} = 26.4 - 8.506
$$

$$
R_{video} \approx 17.89 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4: Final Answer + Interpretation (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{R_{video} \approx 17.9 \text{ kbps}}
$$

This is sufficient for **H.263 at QCIF (176×144)** at approximately **6–7 fps** — usable for **static or slow-moving** medical video such as dermatological lesion inspection or wound assessment, but **not** for real-time endoscopy or echocardiography.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **H.324 system architecture** and describe the **call establishment procedure** including V.8/V.34 modem handshake, H.245 capability exchange, and logical channel opening. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **V.34 modem** operates on a PSTN line with audio passband $B = 3200$ Hz and SNR of **30 dB**. Compute the **theoretical Shannon capacity** and determine what percentage of this capacity is achieved by V.34's **33.6 kbps** design. Comment on whether **V.90 (56 kbps)** is theoretically possible. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — H.324 Architecture and Call Establishment (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The H.324 terminal consists of the following functional blocks:

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Video Codec (H.263)** | Encodes camera input → video bitstream |
| **Audio Codec (G.723.1)** | Encodes microphone input → audio bitstream |
| **H.223 Multiplexer** | Combines A/V + data + control into a single stream |
| **H.245 Signalling** | Manages call control (capability, master-slave, channels) |
| **V.34 Modem** | Converts digital bitstream ↔ analog tones on PSTN |
| **PSTN** | The two-wire analog telephone network |
| **H.223 Demultiplexer** | Separates the received stream back into A/V + data + control |
| **V.34 Modem (RX)** | Demodulates the analog signal back into digital bits |

**Call Establishment Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **V.8 / V.25 Dialling Phase (1 mark):** The calling terminal goes off-hook, dials the number using DTMF tones, and a V.8 handshake exchange (ANS / ANSam / CM) occurs to identify both ends as multimedia-capable H.324 terminals. The receiving terminal goes off-hook and the modem begins V.34 training.

2. **V.34 Modem Training (1 mark):** Both modems perform a handshaking sequence to train the echo canceller, equalizer, and symbol rate. The final negotiated line speed is typically **26.4 to 33.6 kbps** depending on line quality.

3. **H.245 Capability Exchange (1 mark):** Each terminal sends a `TerminalCapabilitySet` message declaring its supported codecs (e.g., "I support H.263 QCIF, G.723.1 at 6.3 kbps"). Each side acknowledges with `TerminalCapabilitySetAck`. Common capabilities are then selected.

4. **Logical Channel Opening (1 mark):** Both terminals exchange `OpenLogicalChannel` messages — one for audio (G.723.1) and one for video (H.263). After the `OpenLogicalChannelAck` is received, the H.223 multiplexer begins carrying live media. The call is now active. Tear-down uses `EndSessionCommand`.

---

**Part (b) — Shannon Capacity Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear (1 mark)**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon–Hartley theorem (2 marks)**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{linear})
$$

$$
C = 3200 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3200 \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
C = 3200 \cdot \frac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = 3200 \cdot \frac{6.908}{0.6931} = 3200 \cdot 9.966
$$

$$
C \approx 31892 \text{ bps} \approx 31.89 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3: V.34 efficiency vs Shannon (1 mark)**

$$
\eta_{V.34} = \frac{33.6}{31.89} \times 100\% \approx 105.4\%
$$

> **Observation:** V.34's nominal 33.6 kbps **exceeds** Shannon's 31.89 kbps for SNR = 30 dB. This is because **real PSTN lines often have SNR > 30 dB** (e.g., 35–40 dB on short, well-conditioned local loops). The "30 dB" assumption in the problem is conservative.

**Step 4: Recalculate for SNR = 35 dB (1 mark)**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{3.5} = 3162.3
$$

$$
C = 3200 \cdot \log_2(3163.3) = 3200 \cdot 11.628 \approx 37.21 \text{ kbps}
$$

Now V.34's 33.6 kbps is $\frac{33.6}{37.21} \times 100\% \approx 90.3\%$ of Shannon's limit — a near-optimal design.

**Step 5: Is V.90 (56 kbps) theoretically possible? (2 marks)**

**No — not on a fully analog PSTN.** Shannon's theorem with $B = 3200$ Hz and $\text{SNR} \le 40$ dB caps capacity at approximately:

$$
C_{max} = 3200 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10^{4}) = 3200 \cdot 13.29 \approx 42.5 \text{ kbps}
$$

V.90's **56 kbps** downstream is **impossible** on a pure analog link. V.90 works only because the **central office (CO) line card** is **digital** (TDM PCM at 64 kbps, G.711), so the downstream path is **only one analog hop** (CO to subscriber) and the CO is the digital source. This breaks the symmetric-analog assumption of Shannon's classic channel. Hence, V.90 is **asymmetric**: 56 kbps downstream, 33.6 kbps upstream (still limited to Shannon for the two-analog-hop path).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — H.324 (PSTN Video Conferencing)**

- **H.324** = ITU-T standard for **low-bit-rate multimedia over PSTN (analog dial-up)**.
- H.324 is a **framework (umbrella)** recommendation, not a single protocol.
- **Components:** H.263 (video) + G.723.1 (audio) + H.223 (mux) + H.245 (control) + V.34 (modem).
- **V.34 modem** speed: **33.6 kbps** (practical 26–30 kbps).
- **G.723.1** audio bit-rates: **5.3 kbps (ACELP)** or **6.3 kbps (MP-MLQ)**; frame size **30 ms**.
- **H.263 video** at QCIF **(176×144)** at **5–15 fps**.
- **H.223 AL3** is mandatory for error-resilient operation over noisy PSTN.
- **H.245** functions: Capability exchange, Master–Slave determination, Logical channel signalling, End-session.
- **Shannon limit** at $B = 3200$ Hz, SNR = 35 dB: **~37.2 kbps** → V.34 achieves **~90 %** of limit.
- **H.320** (ISDN) and **H.323** (LAN/IP) are *siblings* — H.324 is the PSTN-specific member of the family.
- **H.324M (Annex C)** = mobile extension used in **3G video calling** on UMTS handsets.
- **Telemedicine use cases:** rural teleconsultation, tele-dermatology, tele-ECG (audio), disaster response.
- **Latency** of H.324 = **300–800 ms** (acceptable for voice; not for real-time surgical video).
- **V.90/V.92** = 56 kbps **downstream only**; relies on digital central-office line card; not strictly "H.324-grade" analog.
- **V.8** = call initiation/handshake; **V.25** = answer tones; **V.34** = high-speed modem training.
- **H.264 is NOT part of H.324** — H.264 belongs to H.323/SIP/IMS era.
- **MCU (Multipoint Control Unit)** required for conferences with >2 H.324 parties.
- **Advantage of H.324 for telemedicine:** works over **any copper phone line** — no need for fibre, DSL, or 3G/4G coverage.
- **Disadvantage:** very low bandwidth → low-resolution, low-fps video → **not suitable for real-time imaging modalities** (endoscopy, ultrasound) that require fine motion detail.
- **Modern successor:** **H.323/SIP over 4G/5G** with H.264/AVC or H.265/HEVC at 1+ Mbps; H.324 is now considered legacy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When asked to "compare H.324, H.320, and H.323", always structure your answer as a **table** with columns: *Transport Network, Bandwidth, Video Codec, Audio Codec, Multiplexer/Signalling, Application*. Examiners award 1 mark per correctly-filled row.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Security and Standards: Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Security and Standards: Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encryption** is the cryptographic process of transforming plaintext (readable medical data such as patient records, DICOM images, or live biosignal streams) into ciphertext (unreadable encoded form) using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a key, such that only authorized parties possessing the corresponding decryption key can recover the original information.

In the **Telemedicine** context, encryption is the foundational pillar of the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** for Protected Health Information (PHI) transmitted across untrusted public networks (e.g., the internet, 4G/5G, Wi-Fi).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Mathematical Form:** Let $P$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext, $E$ = encryption function, $D$ = decryption function, and $K$ = key.
> - Encryption: $C = E_K(P)$
> - Decryption: $P = D_K(C)$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Prescription Pouch"

Imagine a doctor writing a prescription for a remote patient. Instead of handing the paper openly (plaintext), the doctor places it inside a **tamper-proof, locked steel pouch** and ships it via a public courier (the internet). 

- The **steel pouch** = the *cipher algorithm* (e.g., AES, RSA).
- The **key/lock combination** = the *cryptographic key*.
- The **unreadable gibberish** inside if intercepted = the *ciphertext*.
- Only the patient (holding the matching key) can unlock and read the prescription.

If a thief snatches the pouch mid-transit, all they see is a metal box — useless without the key. This is exactly how encryption protects an **ECG report sent from a remote village clinic to a cardiologist in a tertiary hospital**.

## 1.3 Core Security Goals in Telemedicine

| Security Goal | Meaning | Telemedicine Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Only authorized entities can read data | Patient's HIV status visible only to the consulting doctor |
| **Integrity** | Data has not been altered in transit | ECG waveform has not been tampered with |
| **Authentication** | Sender identity is verified | Confirmation that the prescription truly came from a registered physician |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Sender cannot deny sending the message | Patient cannot deny receiving medical advice |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants in Cryptography

- **Bit Security Level:** Modern encryption standards enforce a minimum of **128-bit** key length for symmetric and **2048-bit** for RSA-based asymmetric systems.
- **Computational Hardness:** Security of RSA relies on the integer factorization problem; ECC relies on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between *symmetric* (single shared key) and *asymmetric* (public-private key pair) encryption, and understand the role of **Digital Signatures** and **PKI** in telemedicine interoperability standards (HL7, DICOM, HIPAA).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Encryption Techniques

Encryption systems are broadly classified into three families, each serving a distinct role in a secure telemedicine pipeline.

### A. Symmetric-Key Encryption (Secret-Key Cryptography)

- **Operational Principle:** The *same* secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
- **Speed:** Extremely fast (suited for encrypting large DICOM images or 24-hour Holter ECG recordings).
- **Key Distribution Problem:** Both sender and receiver must securely share the key beforehand — impractical for telemedicine where the patient and doctor may never meet physically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Ciphers** process data in fixed-size blocks (e.g., 128 bits for **AES**). **Stream Ciphers** process data one bit/byte at a time (e.g., RC4 — now deprecated).

### B. Asymmetric-Key Encryption (Public-Key Cryptography)

- **Operational Principle:** Uses a mathematically linked key pair — a **Public Key** (shared openly) and a **Private Key** (kept secret by the owner).
- **Key Distribution:** Solves the symmetric key-exchange problem; the public key can be posted in a digital certificate.
- **Drawback:** ~1000× slower than symmetric encryption; therefore used to encrypt small payloads (e.g., the symmetric session key itself).

### C. Hybrid Encryption (The De-Facto Standard)

In practice (e.g., **TLS 1.3**, the backbone of secure web-based telemedicine portals), a hybrid scheme is used:
1. Asymmetric cryptography (RSA/ECDHE) securely exchanges a randomly generated symmetric session key.
2. Symmetric cryptography (AES-256) encrypts the bulk medical data using that session key.

## 2.2 Why and How — Structured Logic

- **Why encryption in telemedicine?** Wireless channels (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular) are inherently broadcast media; passive eavesdropping is trivial without cryptographic protection.
- **How is integrity ensured?** Through **Hash Functions** (SHA-256) and **Message Authentication Codes (HMAC)** that produce a unique fixed-size "fingerprint" of the data.
- **How is authentication + non-repudiation achieved?** Through **Digital Signatures**, which mathematically bind the sender's identity to the message content.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Cryptographic Element | Formula / Notation | Purpose | Typical Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric Encryption** | $C = E_K(P)$,  $P = D_K(C)$ | Bulk data confidentiality | AES-128 / AES-256 |
| **Asymmetric Encryption** | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$,  $P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$ | Secure key exchange | RSA-2048, ECC-256 |
| **Digital Signature** | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ | Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation | ECDSA, RSA-PSS |
| **Hash Function** | $h = H(M)$, where $h$ is fixed-length | Integrity check, fingerprinting | SHA-256 (256-bit output) |
| **HMAC** | $HMAC(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | Keyed integrity | HMAC-SHA256 |
| **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange** | Shared Secret $= (g^a \bmod p)^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Establish shared secret over insecure channel | DH-2048 / ECDHE |
| **RSA Key Generation** | $n = p \times q$,  $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Foundation of public-key crypto | $p,q$ are large primes |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the literal vertical bar $\vert$ inside a math expression — use $\backslash$vert$ or $\backslash$mid$ instead to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** AES-256 encrypts Electronic Health Records (EHR) at rest in databases.
- **Tele-radiology:** DICOM images transmitted via PACS are wrapped in TLS tunnels using RSA+AES hybrid encryption.
- **IoMT (Internet of Medical Things):** Wearable ECG/BP monitors use **ECC** (smaller keys, lower power) for secure pairing with smartphones.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Encryption at rest and in transit is mandated by **HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR §164.312)** and India's **DISHA / ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission)** standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Encryption — Exhaustive Derivation

RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977) is the most widely taught public-key cryptosystem in KTU modules. Below is the full derivation a board examiner expects.

### Step 1: Key Generation

Choose two large distinct prime numbers $p$ and $q$. Compute the modulus $n$ and Euler's totient $\phi(n)$:

$$
n = p \times q
$$

$$
\phi(n) = (p - 1) \times (q - 1)
$$

**Logic:** $\phi(n)$ counts integers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$. For a product of two primes, this simplifies to the product of $(p-1)$ and $(q-1)$.

### Step 2: Choose Public Exponent

Select a public exponent $e$ such that:

$$
1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1
$$

**Logic:** $e$ must be coprime to $\phi(n)$ so that a modular inverse exists. Common choice: $e = 65537$ (a Fermat prime, $2^{16} + 1$).

### Step 3: Compute Private Exponent

Find the private exponent $d$ as the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$
d \times e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$

This is solved using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**: $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$.

### Step 4: Encryption

The sender converts the plaintext message $M$ (an integer, $0 \le M < n$) into ciphertext $C$ using the receiver's public key $(n, e)$:

$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}
$$

### Step 5: Decryption

The receiver recovers $M$ using their private key $(n, d)$:

$$
M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}
$$

### Step 6: Correctness Proof — Why Decryption Works

By Euler's Theorem, if $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$
M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Substituting $C = M^e$ into the decryption formula:

$$
C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}
$$

Since $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$:

$$
M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}
$$

**Result:** $C^d \equiv M \pmod{n}$, confirming round-trip correctness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RSA is "hard to break":** Inverting RSA requires computing $d$ from $(e, n)$, which means factoring $n$ into $p \times q$. For $n \approx 2048$ bits, classical factoring is computationally infeasible ($\sim 2^{112}$ operations).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Encrypt $M = 7$ using RSA with $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $e = 7$. Show decryption recovers $M$.

**Step (a) — Compute $n$ and $\phi(n)$:**

$$
n = p \times q = 11 \times 13 = 143
$$

$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120
$$

**Step (b) — Verify $e$:** $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ ✔ (since $7$ is prime and $7 \nmid 120$).

**Step (c) — Encryption:** Compute $C = 7^{7} \bmod 143$.

$$
7^2 = 49
$$

$$
7^3 = 49 \times 7 = 343 = 2 \times 143 + 57, \text{ so } 7^3 \equiv 57 \pmod{143}
$$

$$
7^6 \equiv 57^2 = 3249 = 22 \times 143 + 103, \text{ so } 7^6 \equiv 103 \pmod{143}
$$

$$
7^7 = 7^6 \times 7 \equiv 103 \times 7 = 721 = 5 \times 143 + 6
$$

$$
C \equiv 6 \pmod{143}
$$

**Step (d) — Compute Private Key $d$:** Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using Extended Euclidean: $120 = 17 \times 7 + 1 \Rightarrow 1 = 120 - 17 \times 7$, so $d = -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$.

**Step (e) — Decryption:** $M = C^d = 6^{103} \bmod 143$. (Reduced via successive squaring: $6^{103} \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$ ✔)

**Result:** Original message $M = 7$ is recovered. **Ciphertext $C = 6$.**

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric

| Parameter | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (RSA) | Elliptic Curve (ECC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key Count** | 1 shared key | Key pair (public + private) | Key pair |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slow | Moderate |
| **Key Length (Equivalent Security)** | 128-bit | 2048-bit | 256-bit |
| **Confidentiality** | ✔ (bulk data) | ✔ (small data / keys) | ✔ |
| **Digital Signature** | ✘ (via HMAC only) | ✔ (RSA-PSS) | ✔ (ECDSA) |
| **Key Distribution Problem** | Yes | No | No |
| **Telemedicine Use Case** | Encrypting DICOM files | TLS handshake, digital certificates | IoMT wearables, mobile apps |

## 3.4 Digital Signature Workflow (Step-by-Step)

1. **Sender** computes the hash of the medical report: $h = H(M)$, where $H$ is SHA-256.
