2. Sender encrypts the hash with their **private key**: $S = E_{K_{priv}}(h)$. This $S$ is the *digital signature*.
3. Sender transmits $(M, S)$ to the receiver.
4. **Receiver** decrypts $S$ using the sender's **public key** to obtain $h' = D_{K_{pub}}(S)$.
5. Receiver independently computes $h'' = H(M)$.
6. If $h' = h''$, the signature is **valid** — proving both *integrity* and *authentication*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Plaintext P] -- "Encrypt with Key K" --> nodeB[Ciphertext C]
    nodeB -- "Decrypt with Key K" --> nodeC[Plaintext P]
    style nodeA fill:#cfe2ff
    style nodeC fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeB fill:#f8d7da
```

**Interpretation:** Both directions use the *same* key $K$. Anyone intercepting $C$ sees only gibberish.

## 4.2 Asymmetric Encryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Plaintext M] -- "Encrypt with Receiver's Public Key" --> nodeQ[Ciphertext C]
    nodeQ -- "Decrypt with Receiver's Private Key" --> nodeR[Plaintext M]
    style nodeP fill:#cfe2ff
    style nodeQ fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeR fill:#d1e7dd
```

**Interpretation:** Even if an attacker intercepts $C$, they cannot decrypt without the *private* key, which never leaves the receiver's device.

## 4.3 Digital Signature Generation & Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeS["SENDER SIDE"]
        nodeD1[Medical Record M] --> nodeD2["Hash h = H(M)"]
        nodeD2 --> nodeD3["Sign: S = E_Priv(h)"]
        nodeD3 --> nodeD4[Transmit M and S over network]
    end
    nodeD4 --> nodeV1
    subgraph nodeV["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        nodeV1[Receive M and S] --> nodeV2["Recover h1 = D_Pub(S)"]
        nodeV1 --> nodeV3["Compute h2 = H(M)"]
        nodeV2 --> nodeV4{Compare h1 == h2}
        nodeV3 --> nodeV4
        nodeV4 -- "Yes" --> nodeV5[Signature Valid: Integrity and Authenticity Confirmed]
        nodeV4 -- "No" --> nodeV6[Signature Rejected: Data Tampered or Forged]
    end
    style nodeD4 fill:#fff3cd
    style nodeV5 fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeV6 fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.4 Hybrid Encryption in TLS — Telemedicine Portal Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeH1["TLS HANDSHAKE - Asymmetric Phase"]
        nodeH2[Patient Browser] -- "ClientHello" --> nodeH3[Hospital Telemedicine Server]
        nodeH3 -- "ServerHello + Digital Certificate" --> nodeH2
        nodeH2 -- "Verify Certificate via CA" --> nodeH4[Trusted CA Root Store]
        nodeH2 -- "Generate Pre-Master Secret, encrypt with Server Public Key" --> nodeH3
        nodeH2 --> nodeH5[Derive Symmetric Session Key]
        nodeH3 --> nodeH5
    end
    nodeH5 --> nodeH6
    subgraph nodeH7["DATA TRANSFER - Symmetric Phase"]
        nodeH6[Secure AES-256 Channel Established] --> nodeH8[Encrypt Patient Vitals, DICOM Images, Video Consult]
        nodeH8 --> nodeH9[Server Decrypts with Same Session Key]
    end
    style nodeH1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style nodeH7 fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeH6 fill:#fff3cd
```

**Real-World Mapping:** This is exactly the sequence executed when a patient in Kannur logs into a tele-cardiology portal in Thiruvananthapuram — every byte of their 12-lead ECG is protected by AES-256, while the session bootstrapping is done via ECDHE-RSA.

## 4.5 PKI Hierarchy for Digital Certificates

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeR[Root CA - Government of India CCA] --> nodeI1[Intermediate CA - NHA under ABDM]
    nodeI1 --> nodeI2[Issuing CA - Hospital Telemedicine Department]
    nodeI2 --> nodeI3[Doctor Digital Certificate]
    nodeI2 --> nodeI4[Patient ABHA-linked Certificate]
    nodeI2 --> nodeI5[Medical Device IoMT Certificate]
    style nodeR fill:#f8d7da
    style nodeI1 fill:#fff3cd
    style nodeI2 fill:#cfe2ff
    style nodeI3 fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeI4 fill:#d1e7dd
    style nodeI5 fill:#d1e7dd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** is the trust framework. Each certificate is signed by the CA above it, forming an unbroken chain of trust back to the Root CA — analogous to how a passport is verified via the Ministry of External Affairs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> Differentiate between **symmetric** and **asymmetric** encryption. State one advantage and one limitation of each in the context of telemedicine.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Used** | Single shared secret key | Public-private key pair |
| **Speed** | Very fast (bulk data) | Slow (key exchange, signatures) |
| **Advantage** | High speed — ideal for encrypting large DICOM files | Solves the key distribution problem over public networks |
| **Limitation** | Key must be shared securely beforehand | Computationally expensive; not suited for large data |
| **Algorithm** | AES-128/256, 3DES | RSA-2048, ECC-256 |

In telemedicine, **symmetric encryption** is preferred for encrypting real-time video consultations and large DICOM images, while **asymmetric encryption** is used during the TLS handshake to exchange the session key.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> What is a **hash function**? List any two properties that make SHA-256 suitable for verifying the integrity of medical data.

**Model Answer:**
A **hash function** $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input message $M$ to a fixed-length output $h = H(M)$ called the *message digest* or *fingerprint*.

**Key Properties of SHA-256:**
1. **Deterministic:** Same input $M$ always yields the same hash $h$.
2. **Avalanche Effect:** A single-bit change in $M$ produces a completely different $h$.
3. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$.
4. **Collision Resistance:** It is extremely difficult to find two distinct messages $M_1 \ne M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

In telemedicine, SHA-256 generates a unique 256-bit fingerprint of a patient's ECG report; if the report is altered even by 1 bit during transmission, the recomputed hash will not match the transmitted hash, instantly detecting tampering.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]`

> **(a)** Explain the **RSA algorithm** in detail. Describe key generation, encryption, and decryption with suitable equations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A telemedicine system uses RSA with $p = 5$ and $q = 11$. Take $e = 3$. Compute $n$, $\phi(n)$, and $d$. Encrypt the message $M = 9$ and verify the decryption. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977) is a public-key cryptosystem whose security is based on the computational difficulty of factoring large composite numbers.

**2. Key Generation Steps (3 marks):**
- Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
- Compute $n = p \times q$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

**3. Encryption (1.5 marks):**

$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}
$$

**4. Decryption (1.5 marks):**

$$
M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}
$$

**Why RSA works:** By Euler's Theorem, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$, so $C^{d} = (M^{e})^{d} = M^{ed} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute $n$ and $\phi(n)$.

$$
n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55
$$

$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40
$$

**Step 2:** Verify $e = 3$. $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$ ✔ (since $3$ is prime and $3 \nmid 40$).

**Step 3:** Compute $d$ from $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Using Extended Euclidean: $40 = 13 \times 3 + 1 \Rightarrow 1 = 40 - 13 \times 3$, so $d = -13 \equiv 27 \pmod{40}$.

**Verification:** $3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1$ ✔

**Step 4:** Encrypt $M = 9$.

$$
C = 9^{3} \bmod 55 = 729 \bmod 55
$$

$$
729 = 13 \times 55 + 14
$$

$$
C = 14
$$

**Step 5:** Decrypt $C = 14$.

$$
M = 14^{27} \bmod 55
$$

Using repeated squaring: $14^2 = 196 = 3 \times 55 + 31 \equiv 31 \pmod{55}$. Continuing: $14^4 \equiv 31^2 = 961 = 17 \times 55 + 26 \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$. After successive reductions:

$$
14^{27} \equiv 9 \pmod{55} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** Original message $M = 9$ is successfully recovered.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper Pattern]`

> **(a)** What is **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**? With a neat block diagram, explain the role of **Certificate Authority (CA)**, **Registration Authority (RA)**, and **Digital Certificate** in securing telemedicine transactions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** workflow. How does it provide **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation** for an electronic prescription transmitted from a doctor to a patient? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** **PKI** is the framework of policies, hardware, software, and procedures needed to create, manage, distribute, use, store, and revoke **digital certificates**. It binds public keys to verified identities, enabling trust in open networks.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the PKI hierarchy diagram in Section 4.5 — Root CA → Intermediate CA → Issuing CA → End Entity Certificates (Doctor, Patient, IoMT Device).

**Roles (2 marks):**
- **Certificate Authority (CA):** Trusted third party that issues and signs digital certificates (e.g., CCA-India, eMudhra).
- **Registration Authority (RA):** Verifies the identity of the certificate applicant before the CA issues a certificate.
- **Digital Certificate:** An electronic document (X.509 standard) containing the owner's public key, identity, CA's digital signature, and validity period.

In **ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission)**, a doctor's MCI-registration number is verified by the RA, after which the CA issues an ABDM-compliant digital certificate, enabling the doctor to sign tele-consultation prescriptions legally.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**DSS Workflow (4 marks):**

1. **Hashing:** Sender computes $h = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ where $M$ is the electronic prescription.
2. **Signing:** Sender encrypts $h$ with their private key: $S = E_{K_{priv}}(h)$. The pair $(M, S)$ is transmitted.
3. **Verification:** Receiver computes $h' = D_{K_{pub}}(S)$ and independently $h'' = \text{SHA-256}(M)$.
4. **Comparison:** If $h' = h''$, the signature is valid.

**Security Properties (3 marks):**
- **Authentication:** Only the doctor possesses the private key, so a valid signature proves the doctor's identity.
- **Integrity:** If $M$ is altered in transit, $h'' \ne h'$, and the signature is rejected.
- **Non-Repudiation:** Since the private key is unique to the doctor, they cannot later deny signing the prescription. The signed prescription is legally admissible under India's **Information Technology Act, 2000 (§3A)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *encryption* (which provides confidentiality) with *signing* (which provides authentication + integrity). A common mistake is to state "digital signatures encrypt the message" — they only encrypt the **hash**, not the message itself.
> - In RSA derivations, forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ loses 1 mark.
> - Failing to state the **legal/regulatory binding** (IT Act, HIPAA, ABDM) costs marks in "application" questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3: Encryption)**

- 🔑 **Encryption** converts plaintext to ciphertext using a key: $C = E_K(P)$.
- ⚖️ **Symmetric (AES)** = fast, single key, used for bulk data (DICOM, video).
- ⚖️ **Asymmetric (RSA/ECC)** = slow, key pair, used for key exchange & digital signatures.
- 🔗 **Hybrid Encryption** = Asymmetric to exchange session key + Symmetric for data → used in **TLS 1.3**.
- 🧮 **RSA correctness** rests on **Euler's Theorem**: $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.
- #️⃣ **Hash Functions** (SHA-256) provide *integrity*; **HMAC** provides *keyed integrity*.
- ✍️ **Digital Signatures** = $S = E_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ → provide *authentication, integrity, non-repudiation*.
- 🏛️ **PKI** = CA + RA + Digital Certificates (X.509) → framework of trust.
- 📜 **Regulatory Hooks:** HIPAA (US), DISHA/ABDM (India), GDPR (EU) all mandate encryption for PHI.
- 🏥 **Telemedicine Relevance:** TLS secures web portals; AES secures stored EHRs; ECC secures IoMT wearables; PKI binds doctors' identities to ABDM-compliant prescriptions.
- ⚠️ **Never confuse** *confidentiality* (encryption) with *integrity* (hashing) — they are orthogonal goals.
- 📐 **KTU Board Tip:** Always show $n$, $\phi(n)$, $d$, encryption, and decryption in RSA problems — partial steps lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cryptography** is the science and art of designing mathematical techniques and algorithms to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** of information by transforming readable data (**plaintext**) into an unreadable form (**ciphertext**) and back again, using secret keys.

In the specific context of **Telemedicine**, cryptography is the foundational security mechanism that protects electronic Protected Health Information (**ePHI**) — such as diagnostic images, Electronic Medical Records (**EMR**), real-time video consultation streams, and IoT-based vital sign data — as it traverses public, untrusted networks (e.g., the Internet, Wi-Fi, cellular 4G/5G).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> Cryptography is the practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversarial behavior. It is fundamentally built upon three pillars:
> 1. **Confidentiality** – Only authorized parties can read the data.
> 2. **Integrity** – Data has not been altered during transit.
> 3. **Authentication** – The sender/receiver identity is verified.

## 1.2 The CIA Triad in Telemedicine

| Security Pillar | Telemedicine Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | A psychiatrist's video session with a patient cannot be eavesdropped by a hacker. |
| **Integrity** | A blood test report forwarded from a lab cannot be maliciously altered to change medication dosage. |
| **Authentication** | The remote patient is verified to be the actual registered user, and the doctor prescribing the drug is a licensed practitioner. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to send a private love letter to a friend living in a dormitory filled with curious roommates.

- **Plaintext** = The love letter written in plain English.
- **Encryption** = You write the letter in a secret code (e.g., Caesar Cipher: every letter shifted by 3 places, so A becomes D, B becomes E, etc.).
- **Ciphertext** = The coded, unreadable letter.
- **Key** = The "shift of 3" — the secret instruction needed to decode.
- **Decryption** = Your friend applies the reverse shift to recover the original letter.

In telemedicine, the "letter" is your patient's ECG waveform, the "dormitory full of roommates" is the open Internet, and the "secret code" is a **cryptographic algorithm** like **AES-256** or **RSA-2048**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Plaintext (P):** Original, human-readable data.
> - **Ciphertext (C):** Encrypted, unintelligible data.
> - **Encryption (E):** Transformation $P \rightarrow C$ using key $K$.
> - **Decryption (D):** Reverse transformation $C \rightarrow P$ using key $K$.
> - **Key (K):** Secret parameter controlling encryption/decryption.
> - **Cryptosystem:** The complete set of algorithms, keys, and protocols.

## 1.4 Why Cryptography is Indispensable in Telemedicine

- **HIPAA / DISHA 2021 Compliance:** The Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA) in India and HIPAA in the US mandate strict cryptographic protection of patient data.
- **Cyber-Attack Mitigation:** Prevents **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks on teleconsultation platforms.
- **IoMT (Internet of Medical Things) Security:** Wearables like ECG patches and smart insulin pens stream sensitive data that must be cryptographically sealed.
- **Legal Validity:** Digitally signed prescriptions carry legal weight only if backed by strong cryptographic non-repudiation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Branches of Cryptography

### 2.1.1 Symmetric Key Cryptography (Secret Key)
- **Concept:** The **same single key** $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
- **Equation:** $C = E_K(P)$ and $P = D_K(C)$, where $E = D$ operations are mirror images.
- **Telemedicine Use Case:** Bulk encryption of stored EMR databases (data-at-rest).
- **Standard Algorithms:** **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)**, DES, 3DES, Blowfish.
- **Advantages:** Extremely fast (1000x faster than asymmetric), ideal for streaming video and high-throughput sensor data.
- **Disadvantage:** **Key Distribution Problem** — how to securely share $K$ over an open network.

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Key Cryptography (Public Key)
- **Concept:** Uses a **key pair** — a public key $K_{pub}$ (advertised openly) and a private key $K_{prv}$ (kept secret).
- **Equation:** $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$ and $P = D_{K_{prv}}(C)$.
- **Telemedicine Use Case:** Secure exchange of symmetric session keys, digital signatures on prescriptions, and SSL/TLS handshakes for telemedicine portals.
- **Standard Algorithms:** **RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman)**, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), Diffie-Hellman.
- **Advantages:** Solves the key distribution problem; enables digital signatures.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally slow (used mainly to encrypt small payloads or session keys).

### 2.1.3 Hash Functions (One-Way Cryptography)
- **Concept:** A mathematical function $H$ that converts arbitrary-length input into a **fixed-length fingerprint** (digest). It is **one-way** (irreversible).
- **Equation:** $h = H(M)$, where $h$ is the fixed-length hash (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256).
- **Telemedicine Use Case:** Verifying integrity of medical images (DICOM), password storage, blockchain for medical records.
- **Standard Algorithms:** **SHA-256**, SHA-3, MD5 (deprecated).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Cryptographic Entity | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Generic Encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ | Transforms plaintext to ciphertext |
| Generic Decryption | $P = D_K(C)$ | Recovers plaintext from ciphertext |
| Symmetric Identity | $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ | Round-trip verification |
| RSA Key Generation | $n = p \cdot q$, where $p,q$ are large primes | Foundation of public key modulus |
| RSA Public Exponent | $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Encryption exponent |
| RSA Private Exponent | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Decryption exponent |
| Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Modulus arithmetic in RSA |
| RSA Encryption | $C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$ | Ciphertext computation |
| RSA Decryption | $P \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | Plaintext recovery |
| Hash Function | $h = H(M)$ with $H$ irreversible | Integrity / fingerprinting |
| Digital Signature | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{prv}}(H(M))$ | Sender authentication, non-repudiation |
| Signature Verification | $\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(S) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ | Receiver confirms origin |
| Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = (g^b)^a \pmod{p} = (g^a)^b \pmod{p}$ | Secure key exchange over public channel |
| AES Block Size | **128 bits** | Standardized encryption block |
| RSA Recommended Key | **2048 or 4096 bits** | Modern security baseline |
| SHA-256 Output | **256 bits** | Standard hash length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constants (Memorize These):**
> - AES Block Size = **128 bits**
> - AES Standard Key Sizes = **128, 192, or 256 bits**
> - SHA-256 Digest Length = **256 bits (32 bytes)**
> - RSA Recommended Key Size = **2048 bits** minimum (as of 2024)

## 2.3 Why the Hybrid Approach Dominates Telemedicine

In real-world telemedicine platforms (e.g., a video consultation between a doctor in Kochi and a patient in London), a **hybrid cryptosystem** is used:

1. **Asymmetric (RSA/ECC)** securely exchanges a randomly generated **symmetric session key**.
2. **Symmetric (AES-256)** then encrypts the high-volume bulk data (video, audio, EMR) using that session key.

This combines the **secure key exchange** of asymmetric cryptography with the **speed** of symmetric cryptography — the exact architecture used in **TLS 1.3**, the backbone of HTTPS-based telemedicine portals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **WhatsApp / Signal / Zoom for Telemedicine:** Use the **Signal Protocol** (Double Ratchet Algorithm) combining X3DH (asymmetric) + AES-256 (symmetric) + HMAC-SHA256 (hashing).
- **Hospital PACS Servers:** Encrypt DICOM images with AES-256-CBC at rest; transmit over TLS.
- **Smart Card Health IDs (e.g., India's ABHA):** Use ECC-based digital signatures for patient identity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation: Modular Arithmetic

All public-key cryptography relies on arithmetic performed **modulo $n$**, where results wrap around after reaching $n$.

**Definition:**

$$a \equiv b \pmod{n} \iff (a - b) \text{ is divisible by } n$$

**Example:** $17 \equiv 5 \pmod{12}$ because $17 - 5 = 12$, which is a multiple of 12.

## 3.2 Exhaustive RSA Key Generation, Encryption, and Decryption

RSA derives its security from the **computational infeasibility of factoring the product of two very large prime numbers**. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation expected in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1: Choose Two Large Primes

Let $p = 61$ and $q = 53$ (small for illustration; real systems use 1024-bit primes).

### Step 2: Compute the Modulus $n$

$$n = p \cdot q$$

$$n = 61 \times 53$$

$$n = 3233$$

### Step 3: Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

For $n = p \cdot q$ where $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

$$\phi(3233) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1)$$

$$\phi(3233) = 60 \times 52$$

$$\phi(3233) = 3120$$

### Step 4: Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Select $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.

Let $e = 17$.

**Check:** $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$. ✓ (17 is prime and does not divide 3120)

The **public key** is now $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$.

### Step 5: Compute the Private Exponent $d$

We need $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

That is, $d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$.

We compute $d$ using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

We seek $d$ satisfying $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

Apply the Euclidean Algorithm:

$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$$

$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$$

$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$$

$$8 = 8 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$

$$1 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17$$

$$1 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17$$

$$1 = 2 \times 3120 - 366 \times 17 - 1 \times 17$$

$$1 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

So $-367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, meaning:

$$d \equiv -367 \pmod{3120}$$

$$d \equiv 3120 - 367$$

$$d \equiv 2753$$

The **private key** is $d = 2753$.

### Step 6: Encryption of a Plaintext Message

Suppose the doctor wants to send the **patient ID $P = 65$** securely.

The receiver's public key is $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$.

**Encryption formula:**

$$C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$$

$$C \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

**Modular exponentiation** (square-and-multiply):

Step 1: $65^1 \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$

Step 2: $65^2 \equiv 4225 \pmod{3233} \equiv 992 \pmod{3233}$

Step 3: $65^4 \equiv 992^2 \pmod{3233} \equiv 984064 \pmod{3233}$

$984064 / 3233 = 304$ remainder $984064 - 304 \times 3233 = 984064 - 982832 = 1232$

So $65^4 \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$

Step 4: $65^8 \equiv 1232^2 \pmod{3233} \equiv 1517824 \pmod{3233}$

$1517824 / 3233 = 469$ remainder $1517824 - 469 \times 3233 = 1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$

So $65^8 \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$

Step 5: $65^{16} \equiv 1547^2 \pmod{3233} \equiv 2393209 \pmod{3233}$

$2393209 / 3233 = 740$ remainder $2393209 - 740 \times 3233 = 2393209 - 2392420 = 789$

So $65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$

**Combine for $65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65^1$:**

$$C \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$

$$C \equiv 51285 \pmod{3233}$$

$51285 / 3233 = 15$ remainder $51285 - 15 \times 3233 = 51285 - 48495 = 2790$

$$\boxed{C = 2790}$$

The **ciphertext** sent over the network is $2790$.

### Step 7: Decryption of the Ciphertext

The receiver uses their private key $d = 2753$:

**Decryption formula:**

$$P \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$$

$$P \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, since $\gcd(P, n) = 1$ and $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$:

$$P \equiv C^d \equiv (P^e)^d \equiv P^{ed} \equiv P^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv P \cdot (P^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv P \cdot 1^k \equiv P \pmod{n}$$

So mathematically, decryption recovers $P = 65$. ✓

## 3.3 Digital Signature Generation and Verification (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Sender (Doctor) Action | Receiver (Pharmacy) Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute message digest $h = H(M)$ where $H =$ SHA-256. | — |
| 2 | Encrypt digest with doctor's private key: $S = h^{d_{doc}} \pmod{n_{doc}}$. | — |
| 3 | Send $(M, S)$ to the pharmacy. | Receive $(M, S)$. |
| 4 | — | Compute fresh digest: $h' = H(M)$. |
| 5 | — | Decrypt signature using doctor's public key: $h_{recovered} = S^{e_{doc}} \pmod{n_{doc}}$. |
| 6 | — | If $h_{recovered} = h'$, signature is **valid**; otherwise **reject**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Only the doctor's private key could have produced a signature that the doctor's public key decrypts correctly. This guarantees **non-repudiation** — the doctor cannot later deny issuing the prescription.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Cryptosystem Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PLAINTEXT P] --> nodeB[ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM E]
    nodeK[SECRET KEY K] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC[CIPHERTEXT C]
    nodeC --> nodeD[TRANSMISSION CHANNEL]
    nodeD --> nodeE[DECRYPTION ALGORITHM D]
    nodeK2[SAME SECRET KEY K] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P]
    nodeAtt[ADVERSARY EAVESDROPPER] -.-> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted red arrow represents the adversary attempting to intercept $C$. Even if successful, without key $K$, the ciphertext remains unintelligible.

## 4.2 Asymmetric Cryptography Flow (RSA / ECC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph senderA [SENDER SIDE]
        nodeP1[PLAINTEXT P] --> nodeEnc1[ENCRYPT WITH PUBLIC KEY OF RECEIVER]
        nodePB[RECEIVER PUBLIC KEY Kpub] --> nodeEnc1
        nodeEnc1 --> nodeCT[CIPHERTEXT C]
    end
    nodeCT --> nodeChan[INSECURE PUBLIC CHANNEL INTERNET]
    nodeChan --> nodeDec1
    subgraph receiverB [RECEIVER SIDE]
        nodeDec1[DECRYPT WITH PRIVATE KEY OF RECEIVER] --> nodeP2[RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P]
        nodePV[RECEIVER PRIVATE KEY Kprv] --> nodeDec1
    end
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Symmetric vs Asymmetric vs Hashing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph symBlock [SYMMETRIC KEY]
        nodeS1[PLAIN P] --> nodeSE[ENCRYPT E_K]
        nodeSK[SHARED SECRET KEY K] --> nodeSE
        nodeSE --> nodeSC[CIPHER C]
        nodeSC --> nodeSD[DECRYPT D_K]
        nodeSD --> nodeSP[PLAIN P]
    end
    subgraph asymBlock [ASYMMETRIC KEY]
        nodeA1[PLAIN P] --> nodeAE[ENCRYPT E_Kpub]
        nodeAKpub[RECEIVER PUBLIC KEY] --> nodeAE
        nodeAE --> nodeAC[CIPHER C]
        nodeAC --> nodeAD[DECRYPT D_Kprv]
        nodeAKprv[RECEIVER PRIVATE KEY] --> nodeAD
        nodeAD --> nodeAP[PLAIN P]
    end
    subgraph hashBlock [HASH FUNCTION]
        nodeH1[ARBITRARY MESSAGE M] --> nodeHF[ONE WAY HASH FUNCTION SHA 256]
        nodeHF --> nodeH2[FIXED 256 BIT DIGEST H]
    end
```

## 4.4 Hybrid Cryptosystem Architecture Used in Telemedicine (TLS Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Doc as DOCTOR CLIENT
    participant Server as HOSPITAL SERVER
    participant Eve as ADVERSARY
    
    Doc->>Server: HELLO REQUEST
    Server->>Doc: SEND SERVER CERTIFICATE WITH PUBLIC KEY RSA
    Doc->>Doc: GENERATE RANDOM AES 256 SESSION KEY Ks
    Doc->>Server: ENCRYPT Ks WITH SERVER PUBLIC KEY
    Server->>Server: DECRYPT Ks USING SERVER PRIVATE KEY
    Note over Doc,Server: BOTH PARTIES NOW HOLD SHARED SYMMETRIC KEY Ks
    Doc->>Server: ENCRYPT EMR DATA USING AES 256 WITH Ks
    Server->>Doc: ENCRYPT RESPONSE USING AES 256 WITH Ks
    Eve-->>Eve: CANNOT DECRYPT Ks WITHOUT PRIVATE KEY
```

## 4.5 Digital Signature Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDoc1[DOCTOR COMPUTES HASH OF PRESCRIPTION] --> nodeDoc2[SIGN HASH WITH DOCTOR PRIVATE KEY]
    nodeDoc2 --> nodeDoc3[ATTACH SIGNATURE TO PRESCRIPTION]
    nodeDoc3 --> nodeNet[SEND OVER NETWORK]
    nodeNet --> nodePharm1[PHARMACY RECEIVES PRESCRIPTION]
    nodePharm1 --> nodePharm2[PHARMACY RECOMPUTES HASH FRESHLY]
    nodePharm2 --> nodePharm3[DECRYPT SIGNATURE USING DOCTOR PUBLIC KEY]
    nodePharm3 --> nodePharm4{COMPARE TWO HASHES}
    nodePharm4 -->|MATCH| nodePharm5[SIGNATURE VALID INTEGRITY CONFIRMED]
    nodePharm4 -->|MISMATCH| nodePharm6[REJECT TAMPERED DOCUMENT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric Key Cryptography. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | Single shared key | Key pair (public + private) |
| Speed | Very fast | Slow |
| Key Distribution | Difficult (must be secret) | Easy (public key is public) |
| Example Algorithm | AES-256 | RSA-2048 |
| Telemedicine Use | Bulk EMR encryption | Secure key exchange, digital signatures |

(1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for the difference table highlight, 1 mark for the example algorithms.)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Cryptography. List and briefly explain the three primary goals of cryptography in a telemedicine system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Cryptography** is the science of securing information by transforming it into an unreadable format using mathematical algorithms and keys.

**Three Goals:**
1. **Confidentiality:** Ensuring that patient data (e.g., teleconsultation video, EMR) is accessible only to authorized users. *(1 mark)*
2. **Integrity:** Guaranteeing that medical data is not tampered with during transmission between hospital and remote clinic. *(1 mark)*
3. **Authentication & Non-Repudiation:** Verifying the identity of the doctor/patient and ensuring the sender cannot later deny issuing a digital prescription. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the RSA Algorithm in detail. Describe the steps for key generation, encryption, and decryption. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A telemedicine portal uses RSA with $p = 5$ and $q = 11$. Compute the public and private keys, and encrypt the plaintext message $P = 9$. Show all modular arithmetic steps. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to B-A

**Part (a) — RSA Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

RSA is an **asymmetric public-key cryptosystem** invented by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman in 1977. Its security relies on the practical difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers.

**Key Generation Steps:**
1. Select two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$. *(1 mark)*
2. Compute modulus $n = p \cdot q$. *(1 mark)*
3. Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. *(1 mark)*
4. Choose public exponent $e$ with $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. *(1 mark)*
5. Compute private exponent $d$ satisfying $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. *(1 mark)*

**Encryption and Decryption:**
- Public Key: $(e, n)$ — distributed openly.
- Private Key: $(d, n)$ — kept secret.
- Encryption: $C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$ *(1 mark)*
- Decryption: $P \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ *(1 mark)*

**Telemedicine Relevance:** Used in TLS handshakes of telemedicine portals, digital signatures on e-prescriptions, and ABHA health ID authentication.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $P = 9$.

**Step 1: Compute $n$**

$$n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

**Step 2: Compute $\phi(n)$**

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

**Step 3: Choose $e$**

We need $1 < e < 40$ and $\gcd(e, 40) = 1$. Let $e = 7$.

Check: $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$. ✓

**Public Key = $(e, n) = (7, 55)$.** *(2 marks for steps 1–3)*

**Step 4: Compute $d$**

We need $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

$40 = 5 \times 7 + 5$
$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2$
$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1$
$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$

Back-substitute:
$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$
$1 = 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$
$1 = 3 \times (40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7$

So $-17 \times 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, giving $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

**Private Key $d = 23$.** *(2 marks for step 4)*

**Step 5: Encrypt $P = 9$**

$$C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$$

$$C \equiv 9^7 \pmod{55}$$

Compute modular exponentiation:
$9^2 = 81 \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$
$9^4 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 676 - 12 \times 55 = 676 - 660 = 16 \pmod{55}$
$9^7 = 9^4 \times 9^2 \times 9^1 \equiv 16 \times 26 \times 9 \pmod{55}$

$16 \times 26 = 416 \equiv 416 - 7 \times 55 = 416 - 385 = 31 \pmod{55}$

$31 \times 9 = 279 \equiv 279 - 5 \times 55 = 279 - 275 = 4 \pmod{55}$

**Ciphertext $C = 4$.** *(2 marks for step 5)*

**Step 6: Verify by Decryption**

$$P \equiv C^d \pmod{n} = 4^{23} \pmod{55}$$

$4^2 = 16$
$4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 4 \times 55 = 36 \pmod{55}$
$4^8 \equiv 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 23 \times 55 = 1296 - 1265 = 31 \pmod{55}$
$4^{16} \equiv 31^2 = 961 \equiv 961 - 17 \times 55 = 961 - 935 = 26 \pmod{55}$
$4^{23} = 4^{16} \times 4^4 \times 4^2 \times 4^1 \equiv 26 \times 36 \times 16 \times 4 \pmod{55}$

$26 \times 36 = 936 \equiv 936 - 17 \times 55 = 936 - 935 = 1 \pmod{55}$

$1 \times 16 = 16$

$16 \times 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 1 \times 55 = 9 \pmod{55}$

**Recovered $P = 9$. ✓** *(1 mark for verification)*

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the three types of cryptographic systems: Symmetric, Asymmetric, and Hash functions. Compare their use cases in a telemedicine environment. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A doctor issues a digital prescription. Explain in detail the process of generating and verifying a digital signature using hash functions and public key cryptography. Why is non-repudiation achieved? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to B-B

**Part (a) — Three Cryptographic Systems (7 marks):**

**1. Symmetric Key Cryptography:** *(2 marks)*
- Uses a **single shared secret key** $K$ for both encryption and decryption.
- $C = E_K(P)$ and $P = D_K(C)$.
- **Algorithms:** AES-128, AES-256, DES (legacy), 3DES.
- **Telemedicine Use:** Bulk encryption of EMR databases, real-time encryption of high-definition telemedicine video streams due to its high speed (operates at gigabit-per-second throughput on modern CPUs).
- **Drawback:** Key distribution problem over open networks.

**2. Asymmetric Key Cryptography:** *(2 marks)*
- Uses a mathematically linked **key pair** — public key $K_{pub}$ and private key $K_{prv}$.
- $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$; $P = D_{K_{prv}}(C)$.
- **Algorithms:** RSA, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), Diffie-Hellman.
- **Telemedicine Use:** Securing the TLS handshake of telemedicine portals, securing email exchanges of medical reports, and digital signatures on e-prescriptions.
- **Advantage:** Solves key distribution; enables authentication.

**3. Hash Functions:** *(2 marks)*
- One-way functions producing a **fixed-length message digest** from variable-length input.
- $h = H(M)$ with $H$ irreversible and collision-resistant.
- **Algorithms:** SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 (broken, deprecated).
- **Telemedicine Use:** Integrity verification of DICOM medical images, password hashing in patient login portals, blockchain-based medical record systems.
- **Property:** Even a single-bit change in $M$ produces a completely different $h$ (avalanche effect).

**Comparison Summary Table:** *(1 mark)*

| Feature | Symmetric | Asymmetric | Hash |
|---|---|---|---|
| Keys | 1 shared | 2 (pair) | 0 (keyless) |
| Reversible | Yes | Yes | No (one-way) |
| Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Fast |
| Primary Use | Confidentiality | Key exchange, signatures | Integrity |

---

**Part (b) — Digital Signature Process (7 marks):**

A digital signature on an e-prescription cryptographically binds the doctor's identity to the document, ensuring **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation**.

**Generation (at Doctor's Side):** *(3 marks)*

1. The doctor authors the prescription message $M$ (drug, dosage, patient ID, timestamp).
2. The doctor's system computes a hash digest: $h = H(M)$ using SHA-256. This produces a 256-bit fingerprint of $M$.
3. The doctor encrypts this hash with their **private key** $K_{prv}^{doc}$:

$$S = E_{K_{prv}^{doc}}(h) = h^{d_{doc}} \pmod{n_{doc}}$$

4. The signed document $(M, S)$ is transmitted to the pharmacy.

**Verification (at Pharmacy's Side):** *(3 marks)*

1. The pharmacy receives $(M, S)$.
2. They independently recompute the hash of the received message: $h' = H(M)$ using SHA-256.
3. They decrypt the signature $S$ using the doctor's **public key** $K_{pub}^{doc}$ (obtained from a trusted Certificate Authority):

$$h_{recovered} = D_{K_{pub}^{doc}}(S) = S^{e_{doc}} \pmod{n_{doc}}$$

4. Compare $h'$ with $h_{recovered}$:
   - If $h' = h_{recovered}$ → **signature is valid**, integrity confirmed, and the message undeniably originated from the doctor.
   - If $h' \neq h_{recovered}$ → **rejection**: the document has been tampered with OR was not signed by the claimed doctor.

**Why Non-Repudiation is Achieved:** *(1 mark)*

Only the doctor possesses $K_{prv}^{doc}$. Since the public key correctly decrypts the signature, the signature could only have been produced using the matching private key. The doctor **cannot later deny** signing the prescription — a critical legal requirement for telemedicine prescriptions under India's DISHA Act 2021 and Information Technology Act 2000.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $e$ and $d$:** Many students wrongly assign $e$ as the decryption key. **Remember:** $e$ is for encryption (public), $d$ is for decryption (private).
> 2. **Forgetting $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$:** If $e$ shares a factor with $\phi(n)$, the modular inverse $d$ will not exist. State this condition explicitly in key generation steps.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in modular exponentiation:** A single wrong reduction modulo $n$ cascades through the entire computation. Show **intermediate remainders** at every step to earn full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** Always decrypt $C$ to recover $P$ as proof that your key generation and encryption are correct. Examiners award the final 1–2 marks for verification.
> 5. **Omitting the purpose/telemedicine context:** A pure mathematical answer without linking to ePHI, e-prescriptions, or TLS earns partial credit only. Always add 1–2 lines on real-world telemedicine relevance.
> 6. **Mixing up digital signature and digital certificate:** A digital signature proves *who sent* the message; a digital certificate (X.509) proves *who owns the public key*. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cryptography for KTU Module 3**

- **Core Definition:** Cryptography = science of secure communication via encryption $E_K(P) \to C \to D_K(C) \to P$.
- **Three Pillars:** **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication** (CIA Triad).
- **Symmetric Cryptography:** Single shared key, fast, used for bulk data (AES-256). Suffers from key distribution problem.
- **Asymmetric Cryptography:** Public + private key pair, slower, solves key distribution, enables digital signatures (RSA, ECC).
- **Hash Functions:** One-way, fixed-length digest, irreversible, used for integrity (SHA-256).
- **RSA Key Generation Recipe:** $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, choose $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- **RSA Encryption:** $C \equiv P^e \pmod{n}$.
- **RSA Decryption:** $P \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$.
- **Digital Signature:** Sign hash with private key; verify by decrypting signature with public key and comparing hashes.
- **Non-Repudiation:** Property guaranteed by asymmetric cryptography — the signer cannot deny their signature.
- **Hybrid Model:** Telemedicine systems (TLS 1.3) use asymmetric crypto to exchange a symmetric session key, then symmetric crypto for bulk data.
- **Standard Algorithms to Memorize:** AES (symmetric), RSA / ECC (asymmetric), SHA-256 (hashing).
- **Key Sizes (2024 Standard):** AES ≥ 128 bits, RSA ≥ 2048 bits, ECC ≥ 256 bits, SHA ≥ 256 bits.
- **Telemedicine Applications:** EMR encryption, secure video consultation (Signal Protocol), digital e-prescriptions, ABHA health ID, IoMT data security, HIPAA/DISHA compliance.
- **Famous Historical Ciphers to Mention (for 1-mark questions):** Caesar Cipher, Vigenère Cipher, Enigma Machine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Teleradiology: Basic Part of a Teleradiography System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Teleradiology** is the branch of telemedicine that involves the **electronic transmission of radiological images** (such as X-rays, CT scans, MRI scans, and ultrasound images) from one geographic location to another for the purposes of **interpretation, consultation, diagnosis, and archival**, enabling a radiologist to analyze patient images without being physically present at the acquisition site.

A **Teleradiography System** (more correctly, a *Teleradiology System*) is the integrated hardware-software-communication infrastructure that performs the **acquisition, digitization, transmission, reception, display, and interpretation** of radiological images across distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** The term "Teleradiography" historically referred to *transmission of X-ray images by telegraph/telephone lines*. In the modern KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is used interchangeably with **Teleradiology**, encompassing all digital radiological modalities (CR, DR, CT, MRI, USG, PET).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you took an X-ray in a small rural clinic in Kerala at 2 AM. The local doctor suspects a **hairline fracture** but isn't certain. Instead of waiting until morning or transferring the patient to a distant tertiary hospital, the **X-ray image is electronically sent** through internet/telecom lines to a senior radiologist 500 km away in a metro city. Within minutes, the expert reads the image, dictates a report, and sends it back. **This entire workflow is teleradiology.**

> **Think of it as "Swiggy/Zomato for medical images"** — the image (food) is prepared (acquired) at one place, transmitted through a digital pipeline (delivery network), and consumed (interpreted) at another location by the appropriate expert.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following engineering metrics govern a teleradiology system:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Diagnostic display resolution | **≥ 2.5 megapixels (MP)** for CR/DR; **≥ 5 MP** for mammography | KTU-defined minimum for primary diagnosis |
| Grayscale bit depth | **8 to 16 bits per pixel** | Determines dynamic range of image |
| DICOM standard | **3.0 / 2024x** | Universal medical image format |
| Typical CR/DR image size | **~ 10–30 MB** (uncompressed) | Affects transmission time |
| Typical CT series | **~ 100–500 MB** | Multi-slice volume |
| Lossless compression ratio | **~ 2:1 to 3:1** (JPEG 2000 / DICOM Lossless) | Preserves diagnostic fidelity |
| Lossy compression ratio | **~ 10:1 to 20:1** (JPEG / Wavelet) | Acceptable for screening only |
| Network bandwidth minimum | **≥ 50 Mbps** (DICOM recommended) | For near real-time transfer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Acquisition vs Image Transmission vs Image Interpretation** are the THREE distinct engineering stages in any teleradiology system. Each requires separate hardware, software, and quality assurance.

### 1.4 Scope within KTU Module 4

This topic lays the **foundational block diagram** of a teleradiology system — every later concept (telepathology, tele-cardiology, tele-ICU) reuses this **Acquire → Process → Transmit → Display → Archive** architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Architecture — The Five Pillars

A basic teleradiography system consists of **five tightly-coupled functional blocks**. Each block performs a specific role, and the failure of any one compromises the diagnostic chain.

#### **Block 1: Image Acquisition Subsystem**

This is the *front-end* where the patient's radiological data is captured.

- **Conventional (Film-Screen) Radiography** — analog X-ray film; requires a **film digitizer** (CCD scanner / laser scanner) to convert to digital form.
- **Computed Radiography (CR)** — uses **photostimulable phosphor (PSP) plates**; image is read out by a CR reader (resolution ~ 100–200 µm pixel pitch).
- **Digital Radiography (DR)** — direct digital capture using **Flat Panel Detectors (FPDs)** — either *indirect* (CsI scintillator + a-Si TFT) or *direct* (a-Se photoconductor). Resolution ~ 140–200 µm.
- **Cross-sectional modalities** — CT, MRI, PET, USG are inherently digital.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** For film-based systems, the **digitizer (film scanner) is part of the acquisition subsystem**, not a separate block. For CR/DR/MRI/CT, digitization is built into the modality.

#### **Block 2: Image Processing & Digitization Subsystem**

Once the raw image data is acquired, it undergoes:

- **Pre-processing** — flat-field correction, dark-current subtraction, bad-pixel correction.
- **Image enhancement** — window/level adjustment, edge sharpening, noise reduction.
- **DICOM encapsulation** — wrapping the pixel data with patient metadata (name, ID, modality, acquisition parameters) following the **DICOM 3.0 standard**.
- **Compression** — either *lossless* (preserves all data, ~ 2:1 ratio) or *lossy* (irreversible, ~ 10–20:1 ratio). Standards: **JPEG, JPEG 2000, JPEG-LS, PNG, DICOM-specific codecs**.

#### **Block 3: Communication / Transmission Subsystem**

The **network and protocol layer** that ferries the image from sender to receiver.

- **Physical media** — twisted pair, coaxial cable, fiber-optic, satellite, wireless (4G/5G/Wi-Fi).
- **Protocol stack** — TCP/IP at lower layers; **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (DULP)** over **HTTP/HTTPS** at the application layer (DICOMweb: WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS).
- **Security** — **TLS/SSL encryption**, **VPN tunnels**, **AES-256** for data-at-rest, two-factor authentication.
- **Bandwidth shaping** — store-and-forward vs. real-time streaming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Store-and-Forward** is the dominant model in KTU syllabus. Image is fully acquired, packaged, and sent asynchronously. Contrast with **Real-Time / Video Conferencing** model (used in tele-ICU).

#### **Block 4: Display / Interpretation Subsystem**

The **receiving-end workstation** used by the radiologist.

- **Diagnostic monitor** — medical-grade, FDA-cleared, luminance ≥ 350 cd/m², calibrated to **DICOM Part 14 GSDF** (Grayscale Standard Display Function).
- **Workstation hardware** — multi-core CPU, dedicated GPU, ≥ 8 GB RAM, ≥ 4 TB storage, color-accurate IPS/OLED panel.
- **Software** — DICOM viewer (OsiriX, RadiAnt, Horos, Carestream, GE AW), 3D reconstruction, MPR (multi-planar reconstruction), AI-assisted detection.

#### **Block 5: Archival / Storage Subsystem (PACS)**

**Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS)** — the long-term image repository.

- **Short-term storage** — SSD/NAS on the local workstation.
- **Long-term storage** — enterprise PACS server, RAID-6, **DICOM-compliant**, with **HL7 integration** to the Hospital Information System (HIS) / Radiology Information System (RIS).
- **Redundancy** — 3-2-1 backup rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite/cloud).
- **Cloud PACS** — emerging trend; HIPAA/GDPR-compliant vendors.

### 2.2 Why Each Block Exists — The Engineering Rationale

| Block | Failure Without It | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition | No data to transmit | Patient imaging is the source of truth |
| Processing | Raw analog data is non-portable | Digital + standardized format is required for cross-vendor communication |
| Communication | Local-only interpretation | Telemedicine requires distance-spanning transmission |
| Display | Receiver cannot interpret | Diagnostic accuracy depends on calibrated display |
| Archive | No longitudinal comparison / legal record | Medical records must persist 5–25 years per regulations |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **routinely asked in KTU Part A (3-mark)** and **Part B (14-mark)** problems for Teleradiology.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uncompressed image size | $S = W \times H \times B \times N \div 8$ | Bytes | $W,H$ = width, height (pixels); $B$ = bit depth; $N$ = number of images |
| 2 | Transmission time | $T = S \div R$ | Seconds | $R$ = link bandwidth (bits/sec); $S$ in bits |
| 3 | Compression ratio | $C_r = S_{orig} \div S_{comp}$ | Dimensionless | $S_{orig}$ = original size, $S_{comp}$ = compressed size |
| 4 | Bits per pixel (effective) | $B_{eff} = B \div C_r$ | bits/pixel | After compression |
| 5 | Required bandwidth (real-time) | $R = S \times f \times 8$ | bps | $f$ = frames per second (for streaming) |
| 6 | Spatial resolution (line pairs) | $LP \div mm = 1 \div (2 \times p)$ | lp/mm | $p$ = pixel pitch in mm |
| 7 | SNR (detector) | $SNR = \mu \div \sigma$ | Dimensionless | $\mu$ = mean signal, $\sigma$ = std. deviation |
| 8 | File count for a CT series | $N_{slices}$ | Count | Determines total dataset size |
| 9 | Storage for 1 year | $Storage = N_{studies} \times \bar{S} \times 12$ | MB / year | $\bar{S}$ = average study size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In formula 1, students forget to multiply by **$N$** (number of images) for multi-slice CT/MRI. The exam may give a CT with 200 slices — every slice is a separate image.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Healthcare** — district hospitals in Kerala can access AIIMS-level radiology expertise.
- **Off-Hours Coverage** — "Nighthawk" / "Teleradiology night service" model: US/UK radiologists read overnight scans from India/Philippines (time-zone arbitrage).
- **Subspecialty Routing** — a chest X-ray is auto-routed to a thoracic radiologist; a pediatric case to a pediatric radiologist.
- **Disaster Response** — mobile CT vans transmit scans to central hospitals during epidemics/floods.
- **AI Triage** — modern systems embed **deep-learning triage** (e.g., stroke detection, pneumothorax flagging) before the human radiologist opens the case.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uncompressed Image Size and Transmission Time (Worked Example)

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Teleradiology problems.

**Problem Statement (typical KTU style):**
A chest X-ray is acquired on a CR system with image matrix $2048 \times 2048$ pixels, 12-bit depth. Calculate (a) the uncompressed size, (b) the transmission time over a 4 Mbps link, (c) the new transmission time if a 3:1 lossless compression is applied.

#### **Part (a) — Uncompressed Image Size**

$$
S = W \times H \times B \div 8
$$

Step 1 — Identify parameters:
- $W = 2048$ pixels
- $H = 2048$ pixels
- $B = 12$ bits/pixel

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2048 \times 2048 \times 12 \div 8 \\[4pt]
  &= 4{,}194{,}304 \times 12 \div 8 \\[4pt]
  &= 50{,}331{,}648 \div 8 \\[4pt]
  &= 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Convert to MB:

$$
S = 6{,}291{,}456 \div (1024 \times 1024) \approx 6.00 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $\approx 6$ MB uncompressed.

#### **Part (b) — Transmission Time over 4 Mbps**

Step 1 — Convert size to bits:

$$
S_{bits} = 6{,}291{,}456 \times 8 = 50{,}331{,}648 \text{ bits}
$$

Step 2 — Apply transmission formula $T = S \div R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 50{,}331{,}648 \div 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
  &= 12.58 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $\approx 12.6$ seconds.

#### **Part (c) — Time after 3:1 Compression**

Step 1 — Compressed size:

$$
S_{comp} = 6{,}291{,}456 \div 3 \approx 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} \approx 2 \text{ MB}
$$

Step 2 — Transmission time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{comp} &= (2{,}097{,}152 \times 8) \div (4 \times 10^{6}) \\[4pt]
         &= 16{,}777{,}216 \div 4 \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
         &= 4.19 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (c):** $\approx 4.2$ seconds — a **3× speed-up** with no diagnostic loss (lossless).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Slice CT Dataset (Multi-Image Scenario)

**Problem Statement:**
A chest CT produces 350 slices, each at $512 \times 512$ pixels, 16-bit depth. The link is 10 Mbps. Calculate (a) total raw size, (b) total transmission time, (c) compressed transmission time with 5:1 lossy compression.

#### **Part (a) — Total Raw Size**

Step 1 — Size of one slice:

$$
S_{slice} = 512 \times 512 \times 16 \div 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 0.5 \text{ MB}
$$

Step 2 — Total for 350 slices:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 350 \times 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
          &= 183{,}500{,}800 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
          &\approx 175 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Transmission Time at 10 Mbps**

Step 1 — Convert to bits:

$$
S_{bits} = 183{,}500{,}800 \times 8 = 1.468 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits}
$$

Step 2 — Compute time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 1.468 \times 10^{9} \div 10 \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
  &= 146.8 \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 2 \text{ min } 27 \text{ sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Part (c) — Compressed Time (5:1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{comp} &= 175 \div 5 = 35 \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
T_{comp} &= (35 \times 10^{6} \times 8) \div (10 \times 10^{6}) \\[4pt]
         &= 280 \div 10 \\[4pt]
         &= 28 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** 175 MB raw; 2 min 27 sec uncompressed; 28 sec with 5:1 lossy compression.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spatial Resolution and Detector Pixel Pitch

**Problem Statement:**
A mammography FPD has a pixel pitch of 85 µm. Compute the limiting spatial resolution in line pairs per mm (lp/mm).

Step 1 — Convert pitch to mm:

$$
p = 85 \, \mu m = 0.085 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Nyquist limit (one line pair = 2 pixels):

$$
\begin{aligned}
LP \div mm &= 1 \div (2 \times p) \\[4pt]
           &= 1 \div (2 \times 0.085) \\[4pt]
           &= 1 \div 0.17 \\[4pt]
           &\approx 5.88 \text{ lp/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $\approx 5.88$ lp/mm — sufficient for microcalcification detection in mammography.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Acquisition Modalities

| Modality | Data Type | Native Resolution | Bit Depth | Typical Study Size | Compression Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Film-Screen X-ray | Analog → digitized | Up to 4K × 4K | 12-bit | 8–16 MB | Lossless |
| CR (PSP plate) | Digital | 2.5K × 2K to 4K × 4K | 12-bit | 6–20 MB | Lossless / 2:1 lossy |
| DR (FPD) | Digital | 3K × 3K to 4K × 4K | 14-bit | 10–30 MB | Lossless |
| CT | Digital volumetric | 512 × 512 × 200–2000 slices | 12–16 bit | 100–500 MB | 3:1 lossy common |
| MRI | Digital volumetric | 256 × 256 to 1024 × 1024 | 12–16 bit | 50–300 MB | Lossless preferred |
| Ultrasound | Digital video + stills | 640 × 480 | 8-bit (RGB 24) | 5–50 MB | 10:1 lossy acceptable |
| Mammography | Digital | 4K × 5K to 5K × 6K | 14-bit | 30–60 MB | Lossless required |
| PET | Digital volumetric | 128 × 128 × 200+ | 16-bit | 50–200 MB | Lossy OK for screening |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define teleradiology. List the basic components of a teleradiography system.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Teleradiology is the electronic transmission of radiological images (X-ray, CT, MRI, USG) from one location to another for the purpose of interpretation, consultation, and diagnosis by a remote radiologist, eliminating the need for physical presence of the specialist at the acquisition site.

**Basic Components (1.5 marks):**
1. **Image Acquisition Subsystem** — X-ray unit, CR/DR detector, CT/MRI scanner
2. **Image Processing Subsystem** — digitizer, DICOM encoder, compression engine
3. **Communication Subsystem** — network, protocols (DICOM, TCP/IP), security
4. **Display Subsystem** — diagnostic workstation with calibrated medical-grade monitor
5. **Archival Subsystem** — PACS server for long-term storage

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only 3 components (acquisition, transmission, display), missing **processing** and **archival**. For full 3 marks, **all 5 must be mentioned**.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between lossless and lossy compression in teleradiology. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lossless | Lossy |
|---|---|---|
| Data preservation | 100% — exact reconstruction | Irreversible data loss |
| Compression ratio | Low (2:1 to 3:1) | High (10:1 to 30:1) |
| Diagnostic fidelity | Preserved | May degrade if ratio too high |
| Use case | Primary diagnosis, mammography | Triage, screening, thumbnails |
| Example | **JPEG 2000 Lossless**, PNG, DICOM-lossless-JPEG | **JPEG baseline**, Wavelet, JPEG 2000 lossy |
| Standard | DICOM PS3.5 | DICOM PS3.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students write "lossy compression is always bad" — that is **incorrect**. For *teleradiology triage* and *emergent screening*, lossy compression with **ratio ≤ 10:1** is clinically validated and FDA-approved.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Teleradiology System Architecture**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **basic parts of a teleradiography system** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each block. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **DICOM standard** and its role in teleradiology. Discuss the **PACS architecture** and its integration with HIS/RIS. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Basic Parts of a Teleradiography System [7 marks]**

A basic teleradiography system consists of **five functional blocks** working in sequence:

**1. Image Acquisition Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
This is the front-end where the patient's radiological image is generated. It can be:
- **Conventional radiography** — X-ray film; needs a film digitizer (CCD/laser scanner) to convert to digital form.
- **Computed Radiography (CR)** — uses photostimulable phosphor plates; the CR reader converts latent image to digital.
- **Digital Radiography (DR)** — flat-panel detectors (FPD) give direct digital output.
- **Cross-sectional modalities** — CT, MRI, PET, USG are inherently digital.

The acquisition subsystem determines the **spatial resolution, contrast, and noise** of the source image. Without proper acquisition, no downstream block can recover diagnostic information.

**2. Image Processing and Digitization Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- For film-based systems, a **film digitizer** is used.
- For digital modalities, **pre-processing** (flat-field correction, bad-pixel correction) is applied.
- **Image enhancement** — window/level, edge sharpening, noise filtering.
- Output is **DICOM-formatted** image with patient metadata.

**3. Communication / Transmission Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- Uses **TCP/IP over LAN/WAN**, fiber-optic, 4G/5G, or satellite.
- **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol** (DULP) ensures standardized transfer.
- Security via **TLS/SSL, VPN, AES-256 encryption**.
- Two modes: **store-and-forward** (asynchronous, common) and **real-time streaming** (urgent cases).

**4. Display / Interpretation Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- **Medical-grade diagnostic monitor** — ≥ 2.5 MP (CR/DR), ≥ 5 MP (mammography).
- Calibrated to **DICOM Part 14 GSDF** (Grayscale Standard Display Function).
- Workstation with DICOM viewer, MPR, 3D reconstruction, AI-assisted triage.
- Radiologist interprets and dictates report.

**5. Archival Subsystem — PACS (1 mark)**
- Long-term storage of DICOM images.
- **RAID-6** redundant storage, **3-2-1 backup rule**.
- Integrates with **HIS/RIS** via **HL7** protocol.

**Block Diagram** (see Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid version — students must draw a hand-drawn equivalent in the exam).

---

**Part (b) — DICOM Standard and PACS Architecture [7 marks]**

**DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) [3 marks]**
- **Definition:** DICOM is the universal standard for medical imaging — it defines both the **file format** (pixel data + metadata) and the **network communication protocol** for medical images.
- **Maintained by:** NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) — current version **DICOM 3.0**, with annual supplements.
- **Structure:** A DICOM file = **header (metadata)** + **pixel data**. Header contains patient ID, study UID, modality, acquisition parameters, etc.
- **DICOM Network Services:** C-STORE (push), C-FIND (query), C-MOVE / C-GET (retrieve), WADO (web access), STOW, QIDO.
- **Role in teleradiology:** Ensures **interoperability** between vendors (GE, Siemens, Philips) so a Siemens CT image can be read on a Philips workstation across the country.

**PACS Architecture [4 marks]**
- **PACS = Picture Archiving and Communication System** — the digital image repository of a hospital.
- **Three-tier architecture:**
  - **Tier 1 — Acquisition gateway** — DICOM routers pulling images from modalities.
  - **Tier 2 — Archive server** — centralized storage (RAID, NAS, SAN, or cloud).
  - **Tier 3 — Display stations** — radiologist and clinician workstations.
- **Integration with HIS/RIS:**
  - **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** messaging standard bridges the **administrative/clinical** (HIS/RIS) and **imaging** (PACS) worlds.
  - Example: Patient admission in HIS → order created in RIS → modality pulls worklist → image acquired → pushed to PACS → report dictated → pushed back to RIS → visible to referring clinician in HIS.
- **Cloud PACS** — modern extension enabling teleradiology at scale.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Teleradiology Workflow, Compression & Transmission**

**(a)** Explain the **image acquisition techniques** used in teleradiology. Compare **CR and DR** systems. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a **suitable numerical example**, explain the calculation of **uncompressed image size and transmission time** in a teleradiology system. Discuss the role of **compression** in reducing transmission time. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Image Acquisition Techniques [7 marks]**

**Acquisition Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Film-Screen Radiography (Conventional):**
   - X-ray film + intensifying screen.
   - Analog; requires a **film digitizer** to enter the digital teleradiology pipeline.
   - Resolution: up to 10 lp/mm; dynamic range ~ 1:100.
   - Becoming obsolete in modern hospitals.

2. **Computed Radiography (CR):**
   - Uses **photostimulable phosphor (PSP) plates** (BaFBr:Eu²⁺).
   - Latent image stored in phosphor is read by a **CR reader** using laser stimulation → photomultiplier tube detection.
   - Digital output; reusable plates.
   - Spatial resolution ~ 100–200 µm pixel pitch; ~ 5 lp/mm.
   - Replaces film in many mid-tier hospitals.

3. **Digital Radiography (DR):**
   - **Flat Panel Detectors (FPD)** — direct digital capture.
   - **Indirect DR** — CsI scintillator + a-Si TFT photodiode array.
   - **Direct DR** — a-Se photoconductor; X-ray directly converted to charge.
   - Highest resolution, lowest dose, instant preview.
   - Resolution ~ 140–200 µm.

4. **Cross-sectional modalities:**
   - **CT** — X-ray source rotates around patient; reconstructs axial slices.
   - **MRI** — magnetic field + RF pulses; multi-sequence imaging.
   - **USG** — high-frequency sound waves; real-time.
   - **PET** — radiotracer-based functional imaging.

**Comparison: CR vs DR (3 marks):**

| Feature | CR | DR |
|---|---|---|
| Detector | PSP plate (reusable) | Flat Panel Detector (FPD) |
| Image readout | Separate CR reader | Direct / inline |
| Workflow speed | Slower (cassette handling) | Faster (instant preview) |
| Radiation dose | Higher (less efficient) | Lower (better DQE) |
| Image quality | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Portability | Plates are portable | FPD integrated into equipment |
| Teleradiology fit | Acceptable | Excellent (real-time) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical on Size & Transmission Time [7 marks]**

**Worked Numerical (5 marks):**

> A digital mammogram is acquired at a matrix of $4096 \times 5120$ pixels with 14-bit depth. The available transmission link is 8 Mbps. Calculate:
> (i) Uncompressed image size in MB
> (ii) Transmission time
> (iii) Transmission time after 4:1 lossless compression

**Step 1 — Uncompressed Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= W \times H \times B \div 8 \\
  &= 4096 \times 5120 \times 14 \div 8 \\
  &= 20{,}971{,}520 \times 14 \div 8 \\
  &= 293{,}601{,}280 \div 8 \\
  &= 36{,}700{,}160 \text{ bytes} \\
  &\approx 35.0 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Time at 8 Mbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (35.0 \times 10^6 \times 8) \div (8 \times 10^6) \\
  &= 35 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Transmission Time with 4:1 Compression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{comp} &= 35.0 \div 4 \approx 8.75 \text{ MB} \\
T_{comp} &= (8.75 \times 10^6 \times 8) \div (8 \times 10^6) \\
         &= 8.75 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of Compression in Teleradiology (2 marks):**

- **Reduces file size** → less bandwidth required, faster transmission.
- **Lossless** (e.g., JPEG 2000 lossless) is mandatory for **primary diagnosis** — preserves all diagnostic information.
- **Lossy** (e.g., JPEG) at **≤ 10:1 ratio** is acceptable for **triage and screening**; reduces bandwidth needs by an order of magnitude.
- **Regulatory caveat:** Mammography and pediatric imaging legally require lossless transfer in most jurisdictions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Mark-Loss Hotspots

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Teleradiology Questions:**
> 1. **Missing DICOM mention** (–2 marks). Every teleradiology answer without DICOM loses marks.
> 2. **Confusing CR with DR** (–1 mark). Always state the **detector technology** explicitly.
> 3. **No units in numerical answers** (–1 to –2 marks). "35 seconds" gets full marks; "35" alone loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting bit-to-byte conversion** (÷8 step missing) — entire numerical goes wrong.
> 5. **Skipping the PACS block** in the architecture diagram (–1 mark).
> 6. **Conflating store-and-forward with real-time** (–1 mark). State the mode explicitly.
> 7. **Not mentioning DICOM Part 14 GSDF** for diagnostic monitors (–0.5 mark).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Teleradiology** = electronic transmission of radiological images for remote interpretation.
- ✅ A **basic teleradiography system** has **5 blocks**: Acquisition → Processing → Transmission → Display → Archival (PACS).
- ✅ **Acquisition modalities** — Film, CR (PSP plate), DR (FPD), CT, MRI, USG, PET.
- ✅ **Digitization** is built-in for CR/DR/CT/MRI; **film digitizer needed** for film-screen.
- ✅ **DICOM 3.0** is the **universal medical imaging standard** — both file format and network protocol.
- ✅ **Compression types:** **Lossless** (2:1–3:1, mandatory for diagnosis) and **Lossy** (10:1–20:1, acceptable for screening).
- ✅ **Diagnostic monitor** ≥ 2.5 MP for CR/DR; ≥ 5 MP for mammography; calibrated to **DICOM Part 14 GSDF**.
- ✅ **PACS** = Picture Archiving and Communication System; **HIS/RIS** = Hospital/Radiology Information System; **HL7** bridges them.
- ✅ **Store-and-Forward** = asynchronous (default model); **Real-Time** = synchronous video (urgent cases).
- ✅ **Image size formula:** $S = W \times H \times B \div 8$ bytes; **transmission time** $T = S_{bits} \div R$.
- ✅ **Spatial resolution:** $LP \div mm = 1 \div (2 \times p)$, where $p$ = pixel pitch.
- ✅ **Network security** essentials — **TLS/SSL, VPN, AES-256**; **DICOMweb** (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS) is the modern web-friendly protocol suite.
- ✅ **3-2-1 backup rule** for PACS — 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite/cloud.
- ✅ **Lossy compression ratio** ≤ 10:1 is FDA-accepted for triage; **lossless is mandatory** for primary diagnosis, mammography, and pediatrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Acquisition and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Acquisition and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Acquisition and Management** in telemedicine refers to the end-to-end pipeline encompassing the capture, digitization, encoding, storage, retrieval, transmission, and display of medical images across geographically distributed clinical environments. It integrates physical sensing modalities, standardized communication protocols (DICOM / HL7), archiving systems (PACS), and clinical workflow engines to ensure lossless, secure, and standards-compliant medical image lifecycle management.

The discipline sits at the intersection of three engineering pillars:
1. **Medical Imaging Physics** — the modality-specific physical phenomenon that encodes tissue information into measurable signals.
2. **Biomedical Signal/Image Processing** — the mathematical operations applied to raw sensor data to produce diagnostically meaningful images.
3. **Health Informatics** — the standards, networks, and database architecture that enable interoperability between acquisition devices, diagnostic workstations, and remote consultation endpoints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Digital Photo Studio for Hospitals":**
> Imagine a hospital as a giant photography studio. Each imaging room (X-ray, MRI, CT) is a different "camera" designed to "see" inside the human body using a unique kind of light — X-rays, magnetic fields, or sound waves. The technician (radiographer) presses the shutter, the raw capture goes into a "digital darkroom" (image processing pipeline) where contrast, brightness, and noise are adjusted, and the final polished image is filed in a "smart album" (PACS archive) with a unique barcode (DICOM tag). When a doctor in another city needs to see the picture, the system securely mails a high-resolution copy over the internet to their phone or workstation. **Image Acquisition and Management is the entire workflow — from pressing the shutter to the doctor opening the picture on their screen.**

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Engineering Units)

- **Spatial Resolution** — measured in **line pairs per millimeter (lp/mm)** or **pixels per millimeter (px/mm)**.
- **Bit Depth (Radiometric Resolution)** — expressed in **bits per pixel (bpp)**, typically 8, 12, or 16 for medical images.
- **Dynamic Range** — quantified in **decibels (dB)** or as a ratio (e.g., 4096:1 for 12-bit).
- **Acquisition Frame Rate** — measured in **frames per second (fps)** for dynamic modalities (ultrasound, fluoroscopy).
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — dimensionless ratio, often expressed in **dB**.
- **DICOM File Size** — typically **0.5 MB to 50 MB per slice**; volumetric studies can exceed **2 GB**.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Medical Image Acquisition Modalities

| Modality Class | Physical Principle | Diagnostic Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiographic (Projection)** | Differential X-ray attenuation | Bone fractures, chest screening |
| **Fluoroscopic** | Real-time X-ray | Catheter guidance, GI tract |
| **Computed Tomography (CT)** | Rotational X-ray + tomographic reconstruction | Trauma, oncology, stroke |
| **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** | Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) relaxation | Soft tissue, neurology, MSK |
| **Ultrasound (US)** | Acoustic echo reflection (piezoelectric) | Obstetrics, cardiac, vascular |
| **Nuclear Medicine (PET, SPECT)** | Radiotracer gamma emission | Metabolic, oncologic imaging |
| **Endoscopy / Microscopy** | Visible light + fiber optics | Internal mucosal, cellular pathology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this module emphasizes the student's ability to *describe, compare, and select appropriate image acquisition modalities* and *explain the role of DICOM, PACS, and HL7 in clinical workflow integration*. Master the standards, not just the physics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Stages of the Medical Image Pipeline

The complete image acquisition and management chain is decomposed into **six sequential stages**. Each stage has well-defined inputs, transformations, and outputs.

### Stage 1 — Physical Signal Generation
- The modality-specific energy source (X-ray photon, RF pulse, acoustic wave) interacts with tissue.
- Attenuation, reflection, or emission produces a spatially varying **signal field** $S(x, y, z, t)$.
- The signal is captured by a **detector array** (e.g., solid-state flat-panel, phased-array ultrasound transducer, RF coil).

### Stage 2 — Analog Front-End Conditioning
- The raw analog signal is amplified, filtered, and sampled.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** (low-pass) removes frequencies above the **Nyquist limit** to prevent aliasing artifacts.
- **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** quantizes the continuous signal into discrete integer values.

### Stage 3 — Image Reconstruction & Processing
- **Projection modalities (X-ray, fluoroscopy):** Minimal processing — direct digital capture.
- **CT:** Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) or iterative reconstruction from 1D projections.
- **MRI:** 2D/3D Inverse Fourier Transform of k-space data.
- **Ultrasound:** Beamforming, envelope detection, log compression.
- **PET/SPECT:** Iterative reconstruction (MLEM, OSEM) with attenuation correction.

### Stage 4 — DICOM Encoding & Metadata Attachment
- Reconstructed pixel data is wrapped in a **DICOM object** containing:
  - Patient identifiers (Name, ID, DOB)
  - Study/Series/SOP Instance UID
  - Modality, acquisition parameters, image dimensions
  - Pixel data (uncompressed or compressed via JPEG/JPEG-LS/JPEG 2000)

### Stage 5 — Storage & Archiving (PACS)
- DICOM objects are pushed to a **PACS archive** using the **DICOM C-STORE** service class.
- Indexed by Patient → Study → Series → Instance hierarchy.
- Backed by **RAID** or **HSM (Hierarchical Storage Management)** tiers.

### Stage 6 — Distribution & Display
- Radiologist retrieves studies via **DICOM C-FIND / C-MOVE / WADO-RS**.
- Display workstations perform **DICOM Grayscale Standard Display Function (GSDF)** calibration to ensure perceptual linearity.
- Remote teleradiology consultation uses **VPN-secured DICOM transmission** or **secure HTTPS WADO endpoints**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 The Continuous-to-Discrete Imaging Equation

A medical imaging system can be modeled as a **linear shift-invariant (LSI) operator** $\mathcal{H}$ acting on an input object $f(x, y, z)$:

$$
g(x, y, z) = \mathcal{H}\{f(x, y, z)\} + n(x, y, z)
$$

where:
- $f(x, y, z)$ — ideal continuous object function (tissue property distribution: attenuation coefficient $\mu$, spin density $\rho$, echogenicity $E$, etc.)
- $\mathcal{H}$ — system transfer function (point spread function / PSF in spatial domain)
- $n(x, y, z)$ — additive measurement noise (quantum, electronic, thermal)
- $g(x, y, z)$ — degraded observed image

### 2.2.2 Spatial Resolution and the Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)

The **Point Spread Function (PSF)** $h(x, y)$ characterizes the blur kernel. Its 2D Fourier transform is the **Optical Transfer Function (OTF)**:

$$
\text{OTF}(u, v) = \iint_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (ux + vy)} \, dx \, dy
$$

The **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)** is the magnitude of the OTF:

$$
\text{MTF}(u, v) = \vert \text{OTF}(u, v) \vert
$$

where $(u, v)$ are spatial frequencies in **line pairs per mm (lp/mm)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** MTF at 10% (0.1) is conventionally the **limiting resolution** of an imaging system. A higher MTF curve means a sharper, more diagnostic image.

### 2.2.3 Image Sampling and the Nyquist Criterion

For a detector with **pixel pitch** $\Delta$ (in mm), the maximum unaliased spatial frequency is:

$$
f_{\text{Nyquist}} = \frac{1}{2 \Delta} \quad [\text{lp/mm}]
$$

To faithfully sample a structure of period $T$ mm:

$$
\Delta \le \frac{T}{2}
$$

Violation causes **aliasing artifacts** (Moiré patterns, staircase edges).

### 2.2.4 Bit Depth and Dynamic Range

An ADC with $b$ bits produces:

$$
N = 2^b \quad \text{quantization levels}
$$

Dynamic range (DR) in dB:

$$
\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(2^b) = 6.02 \, b \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

Common medical bit depths:

| Bit Depth | Levels | Dynamic Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 bits | 256 | **48.1 dB** | Display buffers |
| 12 bits | 4 096 | **72.2 dB** | CT, digital X-ray |
| 16 bits | 65 536 | **96.3 dB** | MRI, digital mammography |
| 32 bits | 4.29 × 10⁹ | **192.7 dB** | Computational / floating-point |

### 2.2.5 DICOM Compression — Lossless vs Lossy

**Lossless compression** (JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 lossless mode) guarantees bit-exact reconstruction. Compression ratio is typically **2:1 to 3:1** for medical images.

**Lossy compression** (JPEG, JPEG 2000 lossy) achieves higher ratios (10:1 to 30:1) but is **regulatory-restricted** — DICOM PS3.15 specifies maximum acceptable ratios per modality:

| Modality | Max Acceptable Lossy Ratio |
|---|---|
| Mammography | **20:1** |
| Chest Radiography | **10:1** |
| CT, MRI | **5:1** to **8:1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Callout:** Lossy compression of original diagnostic data is generally **prohibited** in most jurisdictions; lossy is permitted only for **secondary capture** (referenced images for review).
