| Illiterate Patient | Self + Impartial Witness | Thumb impression + witness counter-sign | Witness must be uninvolved in care |
| Non-Resident Indian (NRI) | Self | E-consent valid globally | Cross-border data laws apply (GDPR, HIPAA) |
| Prisoner / Inmate | Self + Prison Authority | Recorded video, witnessed | Ethics committee oversight needed |
| Deceased (for organ / research) | Family / Legal heir | Separate consent — NOT treatment consent | Out of KTU scope — FYI only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Telemedicine Consent Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Initiates Teleconsultation Request] --> B[Identity Verification]
    B -->|Verified| C{Patient Competent?}
    B -->|Failed| X1[Terminate Session — No Consent Possible]
    C -->|Yes| D[Disclosure Phase: Diagnosis, Risks, Alternatives, Costs]
    C -->|No| C1[Activate Surrogate Consent via Legal Guardian]
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Comprehension Test: Patient Paraphrases Plan]
    E -->|Score U >= 0.8| F[Voluntariness Confirmation]
    E -->|Score U < 0.8| D
    F -->|Voluntary| G[Select Mode of Consent Capture]
    F -->|Coercion Detected| X2[Halt — Re-engage Patient Privately]
    G --> H[Capture Consent: E-Sign, Aadhaar, AV Record, Tick-box]
    H --> I[Encrypt and Store in EHR for 3+ Years]
    I --> J[Treatment Begins — Teleconsultation Active]
    J --> K{Plan Changed?}
    K -->|Yes| D
    K -->|No| L{Patient Withdraws?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Document Withdrawal — Safely Transition Care]
    L -->|No| N[Continue Treatment Until Closure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for exam writing:**
> - **Rectangular boxes** = Action steps.
> - **Diamond boxes** = Decision points.
> - **Loop from K back to D** = "Re-consent" — a unique feature of telemedicine, where any change in plan requires *new* informed consent.
> - **X1 and X2** = Terminal failure points where consent cannot be obtained, and the RMP must legally refuse to proceed.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Legal Framework Hierarchy (Statute Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Constitution of India Article 21 — Right to Life and Health] --> L2[Indian Contract Act 1872 — Free Consent Definition]
    L2 --> L3[Indian Majority Act 1875 — Age of Majority 18]
    L3 --> L4[Information Technology Act 2000 — Digital Signature Validity]
    L4 --> L5[MCI Professional Conduct Regulations 2002 — Code of Ethics]
    L5 --> L6[Mental Healthcare Act 2017 — Advance Directives]
    L6 --> L7[BoG-MCI Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020 — Operational Rules]
    L7 --> L8[DPDP Act 2023 — Data Privacy Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Type-of-Consent Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Patient Arrives] --> B{Situation Type?}
    B -->|First-time Consultation| C1[Express Written E-Consent]
    B -->|Follow-up Refill| C2[Express Verbal with AV Recording]
    B -->|Life-threatening Emergency| C3[Implied Consent under Doctrine of Necessity]
    B -->|Minor or Incompetent| C4[Surrogate Proxy Consent]
    B -->|End-of-life / Dementia| C5[Advance Directive Consent]
    C1 --> D[Proceed to Teleconsultation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** These three diagrams (4.1, 4.2, 4.3) collectively cover the entire Module 4 — Consent Treatment syllabus. Memorizing the **flow direction** of 4.1 and the **top-to-bottom hierarchy** of 4.2 has historically fetched 4–5 bonus marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define "Informed Consent" in the context of telemedicine. List any three essential elements.
`[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Informed consent in telemedicine is the **legally valid, explicit authorization** given by a patient to a Registered Medical Practitioner (RMP) to receive consultation, diagnosis, or treatment via telecommunication channels, **after full disclosure** of the nature, risks, benefits, alternatives, and limitations of the proposed care.

**Three essential elements (write any three):**
1. **Disclosure** — RMP must communicate all material information.
2. **Understanding** — Patient must demonstrate comprehension.
3. **Voluntariness** — Consent must be free from coercion.

*(Other acceptable elements: Competence, Documentation, Right of withdrawal.)*

### Question 2. What is "Implied Consent" and under what conditions is it valid in a teleconsultation?
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Implied consent is consent that is **inferred from the patient's conduct or the surrounding circumstances**, rather than expressed explicitly. In telemedicine, it is valid **only in life-threatening emergencies** where:
1. The patient is unable to communicate (unconscious, in shock),
2. No surrogate / legal guardian is immediately available, and
3. Any delay in treatment would result in serious harm or death.

It is invoked under the **Doctrine of Necessity** as per MCI Regulations 2002 and is **not valid** for routine, elective, or non-urgent care.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five essential elements of valid consent** in telemedicine. Discuss how each element is operationally verified during a teleconsultation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **legal framework governing telemedicine consent in India**, with specific reference to the IT Act 2000, the MCI Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020, and the Indian Contract Act 1872. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) — Five Essential Elements of Valid Consent (7 Marks):**

1. **Disclosure (1 Mark):** The RMP must proactively disclose the **diagnosis, proposed treatment, expected benefits, material risks, available alternatives, limitations of telemedicine, and the cost** of the proposed care. Operationally, this is done in the regional language of the patient, ideally with a printed/visual aid, and confirmed via the patient's paraphrasing.

2. **Understanding (1 Mark):** The patient must demonstrate comprehension. Operationally, the RMP asks the patient to **explain back** the plan in their own words. If the score is below 80%, the RMP re-explains using simpler terms (maximum two retries before referral for in-person care).

3. **Voluntariness (1 Mark):** The consent must be free from coercion, duress, or undue influence from family members, employers, or insurers. Operationally, the RMP asks a direct question ("Are you comfortable proceeding?") and may briefly speak to the patient **privately** to ensure no third-party pressure exists.

4. **Competence (2 Marks):** The patient must be a **legally competent adult (≥18 years per the Indian Majority Act, 1875)** and of **sound mind**. For minors, the mentally ill, or the unconscious, the **legal guardian / surrogate** provides consent. For mentally ill patients with an advance directive, the **Nominated Representative** under the Mental Healthcare Act 2017 acts.

5. **Documentation / Record (2 Marks):** The consent must be captured in an **auditable, retrievable form** — written, e-signed (Aadhaar/DSC under IT Act 2000 Sec. 3 & 4), audio-video recorded, or tick-box with audit trail. The record must be **encrypted, time-stamped, and retained for at least 3 years**.

**(b) — Legal Framework (7 Marks):**

**1. IT Act, 2000 (2 Marks):** Sections 3, 4, and 10A provide **legal recognition to electronic records and digital signatures**, making e-consent fully admissible in a court of law. Section 43A imposes liability for negligent handling of sensitive personal data, which directly applies to patient consent records.

**2. BoG-MCI Telemedicine Practice Guidelines, 2020 (3 Marks):** This is the **operational backbone** of telemedicine consent in India. It prescribes:
- **Explicit consent** for the first consultation (mandatory).
- **Implied consent** permitted in life-threatening emergencies only.
- The RMP must **not solicit consent for sexual acts, avoid prescribing Schedule X drugs via telemedicine, and must maintain a digital trail of consent**.
- The guidelines define **three modalities of teleconsultation** (video, audio, text) and the consent requirements for each.

**3. Indian Contract Act, 1872 (2 Marks):** Section 13 defines **"free consent"** as consent obtained without coercion, undue influence, fraud, misrepresentation, or mistake. Sections 14–18 detail the *vitiating factors*. If consent is not "free" under this Act, it is **voidable at the option of the patient**, and any treatment rendered is legally unauthorized — exposing the RMP to negligence claims.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **process of obtaining consent during a first-time teleconsultation**, including the modes of capturing e-consent. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **surrogate consent, withdrawal of consent, and the emergency exception**, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) — Process of Obtaining First-Time Tele-Consent (7 Marks):**

The process is a **seven-step protocol**:

1. **Identity Verification (1 Mark):** Government-issued ID (Aadhaar/Passport) is verified via video, and the patient's demographic details are logged into the EHR.

2. **Capacity Assessment (1 Mark):** The RMP confirms the patient is an adult and of sound mind. If not, surrogate consent is activated.

3. **Disclosure (1 Mark):** Diagnosis, treatment plan, risks, alternatives, limitations of telemedicine, and cost are all communicated in the patient's regional language.

4. **Comprehension Check (1 Mark):** The patient paraphrases the plan. A score of ≥80% is required.

5. **Voluntariness Confirmation (1 Mark):** Direct question to ensure no coercion.

6. **Mode of Capture (1 Mark):** Any of the following is acceptable:
   - **Aadhaar e-Sign** (legally equivalent to a physical signature under IT Act 2000).
   - **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)** — higher assurance, used for high-risk cases.
   - **OTP-based e-form** — common in mobile telemedicine apps.
   - **Audio-Video recording** of explicit verbal "yes" — useful when no signature platform is available.
   - **Tick-box / button-click** within a verified app — must have audit trail.

7. **Storage (1 Mark):** The record is encrypted, time-stamped, and stored in the EHR for a minimum of **3 years** (or as per state policy).

**(b) — Surrogate, Withdrawal & Emergency (7 Marks):**

**1. Surrogate / Proxy Consent (3 Marks):**
When the patient is a **minor, unconscious, or mentally incapacitated**, the consent is given by a **legal guardian, next of kin, or a court-appointed representative**.
- **Example 1:** A parent consenting for a 10-year-old's teleconsultation for fever.
- **Example 2:** A husband consenting for his unconscious wife admitted to an ICU, with tele-monitoring by a cardiologist.
- The surrogate's authority must be **provable** (birth certificate, court order, or a written authorization).
- Under the **Mental Healthcare Act, 2017**, a person can nominate a **Nominated Representative** in advance to give consent during future mental incapacity.

**2. Withdrawal of Consent (2 Marks):**
The patient has the **absolute right to withdraw consent at any time**, even mid-treatment.
- **Example:** A patient on Day 3 of a tele-prescribed antibiotic course decides to stop because of side effects.
- The RMP must **document the withdrawal, advise safe discontinuation (especially abrupt stoppage of steroids, beta-blockers, etc.), and offer a handoff** to another RMP if the patient so desires.

**3. Emergency Exception (2 Marks):**
In a **life-threatening emergency** where the patient cannot give consent and no surrogate is available, **implied consent is presumed** under the **Doctrine of Necessity**.
- **Example:** During a routine video call, the patient collapses and stops responding. The RMP must immediately guide a bystander on CPR and emergency services, documenting the event.
- This exception **does not apply** to elective procedures, cosmetic treatments, or non-urgent refills.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Informed Consent" with "Implied Consent":** These are *not* synonyms. Implied consent is a *narrow emergency exception*, not the default. Many students mistakenly use them interchangeably, losing 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Emergency Exception:** When asked to "explain the types of consent," students often miss the **implied consent in emergencies** — a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Not Mentioning Surrogate / Proxy Consent:** The MCI 2002 framework explicitly recognizes the role of legal guardians. A 14-mark answer without this loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Withdrawal of Consent:** Students treat consent as a *one-time* event. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists "withdrawal" as a sub-topic. Missing it costs 2 marks.
>
> 5. **No Reference to IT Act 2000:** In any question about telemedicine, the **legal validity of e-signatures and digital records** under the IT Act 2000 is non-negotiable. Forgetting it is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Generic "Definition" without Telemedicine Context:** Writing a generic medical-consent definition (e.g., from forensic medicine textbooks) instead of a **telemedicine-specific** one signals a lack of subject understanding, leading to 1–2 mark deduction.
>
> 7. **No Examples:** A 14-mark question with no real-world example is considered incomplete. Always include at least **one example per sub-part**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Consent in Telemedicine:**

- [x] **Definition:** Informed consent = explicit + informed + voluntary + competent + documented agreement.
- [x] **Five Elements:** Disclosure (D), Understanding (U), Voluntariness (V), Competence (K), Record (R).
- [x] **Valid Consent Equation:** $C_{valid} = D \cap U \cap V \cap K \cap R$.
- [x] **Three Modes of Capture:** E-Sign (Aadhaar/DSC), AV Recording, Tick-box with audit trail.
- [x] **Retention Period:** Minimum **3 years**, encrypted, retrievable within 24 hours.
- [x] **Implied Consent:** Valid **only in life-threatening emergencies** (Doctrine of Necessity).
- [x] **Surrogate Consent:** For minors (<18 yr), unconscious, or mentally incapacitated — by legal guardian / next of kin / Nominated Representative.
- [x] **Withdrawal Right:** Patient can revoke consent **at any time**; RMP must document and ensure safe transition.
- [x] **Legal Statutes to Memorize:**
  - IT Act 2000 (Sec. 3, 4, 43A)
  - MCI Regulations 2002
  - BoG-MCI Telemedicine Practice Guidelines 2020
  - Indian Contract Act 1872 (Sec. 13–18)
  - Mental Healthcare Act 2017 (Sec. 5 — Nominated Representative)
  - Indian Majority Act 1875 (Age = 18)
  - DPDP Act 2023
- [x] **Re-consent Required:** Whenever the **treatment plan changes** during the care continuum.
- [x] **Comprehension Threshold:** Patient paraphrasing score ≥ 80% (5/6 elements).
- [x] **Witness Required:** For **illiterate patients** (thumb impression + impartial witness counter-sign).
- [x] **Liability Rule:** Absence of valid consent = strict liability for RMP under Consumer Protection Act 2019.
- [x] **Cross-border Note:** Telemedicine consent must also comply with destination country's data laws (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) for international consultations.
- [x] **Do NOT prescribe** Schedule X drugs via telemedicine without proper e-consent.
- [x] **Key Doc to Quote in Exam:** *BoG-MCI Telemedicine Practice Guidelines, 2020* — the syllabus anchor.
- [x] **One-liner Mantra:** *"No teleconsultation, no prescription, no advice — without valid, documented, informed consent."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intellectual Property Rights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT862 - TELEMEDICINE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intellectual Property Rights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intellectual Property (IP)** refers to creations of the mind — inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, and symbols, names, and images used in commerce — that are protected by law through patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, industrial designs, and geographical indications, enabling creators/owners to earn recognition or financial benefit from their inventions.

In the context of **Telemedicine (PEBMT862)**, Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) govern the legal ownership and protection of:
- Telemedicine software platforms and source code
- AI/ML diagnostic algorithms and decision-support systems
- Patient health records, medical data, and digital twins
- Medical device designs integrated with telehealth hardware
- Brand identity of telemedicine service providers
- Published research, case studies, and educational content

### 1.2 The Five Classical Branches of IP

| S.No. | Branch of IP | What it Protects |
|-------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Patent** | New inventions / processes / machines |
| 2 | **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical, software works |
| 3 | **Trademark** | Brand name, logo, slogan, mark |
| 4 | **Trade Secret** | Confidential business / technical know-how |
| 5 | **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic / ornamental design of an article |
| 6 | **Geographical Indication (GI)** | Goods originating from a specific region |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Ownership" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of IP like owning a house in a crowded city.**

Imagine you build a beautiful house (your invention/work) on a plot of land (the market). Without a legal deed, anyone could move in, claim it, or copy your design. An **IP Right is the legal deed** that says: *"This is yours for X years — no one else can occupy, copy, or sell it without your permission."*

Just as you can:
- **Live in it** (use the IP yourself)
- **Rent it out** (license the IP to others for royalties)
- **Sell it outright** (assign / transfer the IP)

…you can do the same with a patent, copyright, or trademark — except the "house" is intangible, and the "deed" is granted by the government (IP offices like the Indian Patent Office, USPTO, or EPO).

In **telemedicine**, when a hospital develops a proprietary AI model that detects diabetic retinopathy from retinal scans, the **algorithm is the "house"** and the **patent is the "deed"** preventing competitors from copying it for 20 years.

---

## 1.4 Why IPR is Critical in Telemedicine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Telemedicine Practice Guidelines (India, 2020) and the IT Act, 2000 (with SPDI Rules) explicitly recognize IP and data ownership as pillars of digital healthcare delivery.

Key reasons:
- **Innovation Incentive** — Without patent protection, companies would not invest millions in developing telehealth hardware, AI diagnostics, or wearable sensors.
- **Patient Data Ownership** — Telemedicine generates vast patient data; IP and privacy laws (DPDP Act 2023) govern who owns and may commercialize it.
- **Brand Trust** — Trademarks distinguish verified teleconsultation platforms (e.g., Apollo TeleHealth, Practo) from fraudulent ones.
- **Cross-Border Licensing** — IP treaties enable Indian telemedicine startups to license technology globally and vice versa.

---

## 1.5 Key National & International Statutory Bodies

| Body | Jurisdiction | Function |
|------|--------------|----------|
| **Indian Patent Office (IPO)** | India | Grants patents, trademarks, designs, GIs |
| **Copyright Office (Dept. of Industrial Policy & Promotion)** | India | Registers copyrights |
| **USPTO** | United States | US patents & trademarks |
| **EPO** | Europe (38 countries) | European patents |
| **WIPO** | Global (UN agency) | Coordinates international IP treaties |
| **WTO / TRIPS Council** | Global (164+ members) | Sets minimum IP standards |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

## 2.1 Patents — The "Invention Deed"

A **patent** is an exclusive statutory right granted for a **new, useful, and non-obvious** invention that is capable of industrial application. The patentee gets the **right to exclude others** from making, using, selling, or importing the patented invention for a limited period.

### 2.1.1 Patentability Criteria (Section 2(1)(j) of Indian Patents Act, 1970)

An invention must satisfy **all four** of the following:

1. **Novelty** — Must be new; never published/used anywhere in the world before the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — A person skilled in the art must not find it obvious.
3. **Industrial Application** — Must be capable of being made or used in an industry.
4. **Not Excluded** — Must NOT fall under Sections 3 and 4 (e.g., mere discovery, abstract theory, method of treatment of humans, atomic energy, plants/animals other than microbes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Telemedicine Note:** Software "as such" is NOT patentable in India (Section 3(k)). However, when software is combined with a novel hardware/technical effect (e.g., a novel AI-based ECG arrhythmia detector), it **becomes patentable** as a technical solution.

### 2.1.2 Types of Patents in Healthcare/Telemedicine

- **Product Patent** — Covers the device, drug molecule, or hardware (e.g., a specific portable ECG patch).
- **Process Patent** — Covers the method of manufacture or the diagnostic/treatment process.
- **Use Patent** — Covers a new use of a known substance (e.g., Repurposing of Drug X for treating COVID-19).

### 2.1.3 Term of Patent

- **20 years** from the date of filing of the application.
- For both product and process patents.
- Non-renewable after expiry — falls into the **public domain**.

---

## 2.2 Copyright — The "Expression Deed"

**Copyright** protects the **original expression of ideas** (not the ideas themselves) in the fields of literature, music, drama, art, cinematograph films, sound recordings, and **computer software**.

### 2.2.1 Works Protected in Telemedicine

- Source code of telemedicine apps
- Patient education videos, e-learning content
- Medical illustrations, 3D anatomical models
- Compilations of patient data (if original arrangement)
- Webpage layouts, UI/UX designs

### 2.2.2 Term of Copyright (Indian Copyright Act, 1957)

- **Literary/Dramatic/Musical/Artistic works** — Life of the author + **60 years**.
- **Photographs, Cinematograph films, Sound recordings** — **60 years** from the date of publication.
- **Government works** — **60 years** from the date of publication.
- **Anonymous / Pseudonymous works** — **60 years** from the date of publication.

### 2.2.3 Rights Conferred

- **Economic Rights** — Right to reproduce, distribute, communicate, translate, adapt.
- **Moral Rights** — Right to claim authorship, right to integrity (Section 57).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Copyright arises **automatically** upon creation — no registration is mandatory in India. However, registration provides **prima facie** evidence in court disputes.

---

## 2.3 Trademark — The "Brand Deed"

A **trademark** (™ / ®) is a distinctive sign capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one enterprise from those of others.

### 2.3.1 Components of a Trademark

- Word mark (e.g., **PRACTO**)
- Device mark / Logo (e.g., the green "P" of Practo)
- Combination mark
- Shape of goods (3D mark)
- Sound mark (jingles, e.g., the Intel chime)
- Service mark (for services like telemedicine)

### 2.3.2 Term & Renewal

- **Registration Validity:** 10 years.
- **Renewable indefinitely** in successive 10-year periods.
- **® symbol** is used only after registration; **™** can be used without registration to claim common-law rights.

### 2.3.3 Telemedicine Relevance

Brand dilution and cybersquatting are huge concerns. Domains like `apollo-telehealth.in`, `practo.com` are protected as both trademarks and domain names.

---

## 2.4 Trade Secret — The "Confidentiality Deed"

A **trade secret** is confidential business information that provides a competitive edge, is not generally known, and is subject to reasonable steps to maintain secrecy (Section 2(1)(j) explanation; common law in India).

### 2.4.1 Examples in Telemedicine

- Proprietary ML model architectures and training datasets
- Patient-clinician pricing algorithms
- Internal clinical protocols
- Vendor pricing lists

> [!NOTE]
> India has **no dedicated Trade Secrets Act**. Protection is under:
> - **Indian Contract Act, 1872** (Section 27 — confidentiality via NDAs)
> - **Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 66, 72 — breach of confidentiality and punishment)
> - **Common Law** action for breach of confidence

Unlike patents, trade secrets have **no time limit** — they last as long as the secret is maintained.

---

## 2.5 Industrial Design & Geographical Indication

### 2.5.1 Industrial Design
Protects the **visual/aesthetic features** of an article (shape, pattern, configuration). Filed under the **Designs Act, 2000**. Term: **10 years**, extendable by 5 years. Relevant for medical device casings, telemedicine kiosk enclosures, wearable form factors.

### 2.5.2 Geographical Indication (GI)
A sign used on products that have a specific geographical origin and possess qualities/reputation inherent to that origin. Filed under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**. Examples: **Alleppey Green Cardamom**, **Naga Mircha**. Term: **10 years**, renewable.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — All IP Rights at a Glance

| IP Type | Statutory Basis (India) | Term | Registration? | Symbol |
|---------|-------------------------|------|---------------|--------|
| **Patent** | Patents Act, 1970 | **20 years** from filing | Mandatory | — |
| **Copyright** | Copyright Act, 1957 | **Life + 60 yrs** (literary) / **60 yrs** (films/sound) | Optional | © |
| **Trademark** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | **10 years**, renewable | Mandatory for ® | ™ / ® |
| **Industrial Design** | Designs Act, 2000 | **10 + 5 years** | Mandatory | — |
| **Geographical Indication** | GI Act, 1999 | **10 years**, renewable | Mandatory | — |
| **Trade Secret** | Common Law + IT Act, 2000 | Unlimited (if kept secret) | Not registered | — |
| **Plant Variety** | PPVFR Act, 2001 | 15–18 years | Mandatory | — |
| **Semiconductor IC Layout** | SICLD Act, 2000 | **10 years** | Mandatory | — |

---

## 2.7 International IP Treaties Relevant to Telemedicine

| Treaty / Convention | Year | Key Provision |
|---------------------|------|---------------|
| **Paris Convention** | 1883 | Right of priority for 12 months (patents/designs), 6 months (trademarks) |
| **Berne Convention** | 1886 | Automatic copyright protection without formalities |
| **Madrid Protocol** | 1989 | Single trademark filing covering multiple countries |
| **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** | 1970 | Single international patent application covering 150+ countries |
| **TRIPS Agreement** | 1994 (WTO) | Sets minimum IP standards; mandates 20-year patent term; data exclusivity |
| **Budapest Treaty** | 1977 | Deposit of microorganisms for patent purposes |
| **Doha Declaration** | 2001 | TRIPS flexibilities for public health (compulsory licensing) |
| **Hague Agreement** | 1925 | International design registration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India is a signatory to all of the above EXCEPT the Hague Agreement (designs) and the Madrid Protocol (trademarks) — though India has its own bilateral arrangements.**

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility of IPR in Telemedicine

- **Medical Device Innovation** — Companies like **Tata 1mg, Sigtuple, Tricog, Niramai** hold patents on AI diagnostic devices for tele-cardiology, tele-pathology, and breast cancer screening.
- **Telemedicine Software** — **Apollo TeleHealth** and **Practo** protect their platform UI, code, and brand via copyright + trademark.
- **Cross-Border Telemedicine** — TRIPS enables licensing of Indian telehealth innovations to African/SE-Asian markets.
- **COVID-19 Response** — Patent pools (e.g., WHO C-TAP) and compulsory licensing provisions were activated to scale telehealth drug/vaccine access.
- **Health Data as IP** — Anonymized datasets (e.g., hospital EHR datasets) are increasingly treated as trade secrets / database rights.

---

## 2.9 Quick-Reference Computation Snippets

> [!NOTE]
> Although IPR is not formula-heavy, two simple calculations appear frequently in KTU exams.

### 2.9.1 Patent Term Remaining
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Remaining Patent Life} &= \text{Filing Date} + 20 \text{ years} - \text{Current Date}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.9.2 Copyright Term (Literary Work)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Copyright Expiry Year} &= \text{Year of Author's Death} + 60 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.9.3 Trademark Renewal Cycles
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Trademark Life (if renewed)} &= n \times 10 \text{ years}, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Patent Filing, Infringement & Licensing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The most commonly asked 14-mark question in Module 4 is *"Explain the patent filing procedure in India"* or *"Discuss the types of IPR with relevance to telemedicine."* The exhaustive steps below are model answers for both.

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Patent Filing Procedure in India (Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Invention Disclosure & Prior Art Search
The inventor documents the invention in an **Invention Disclosure Form** (technical field, background, summary, detailed description, claims, drawings). A **prior art search** is conducted across databases (Indian Patent Office's **INPASS**, WIPO **PATENTSCOPE**, Google Patents, USPTO, EPO Espacenet) to confirm novelty.

> Why: Saves time and money if the invention is already known.

### Step 2 — Drafting the Patent Application
Two routes are available:

**Route A — Provisional Specification** (Filed first to establish priority date with minimal details, valid for 12 months)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Priority Date (Provisional)} &= \text{Date of Filing of Provisional Spec.} \\
\text{Must File Complete Spec. Within} &= \text{Priority Date} + 12 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Route B — Complete Specification** (Filed directly with full details, claims, abstract, drawings).

Application must contain:
1. **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent
2. **Form 2** — Provisional or complete specification
3. **Form 3** — Statement and undertaking regarding foreign applications
4. **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship
5. **Form 26** — Authorization of patent agent (if applicable)

### Step 3 — Filing & Acknowledgment
Application filed at the **appropriate Patent Office** (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata). The filing date = **priority date**. A **filing receipt** with Application Number and date is issued.

### Step 4 — Publication
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date (or earlier on request via Form 9, with a 6-month pre-grant opposition window opening upon publication).

### Step 5 — Request for Examination (Form 18)
The applicant must request examination within **48 months** of the priority date, otherwise the application is treated as withdrawn. The Controller forwards it to an examiner.

### Step 6 — Examination & First Examination Report (FER)
The examiner checks for:
- Novelty, inventive step, industrial application
- Sufficiency of disclosure
- Clarity and scope of claims
- Compliance with Sections 3 & 4 exclusions

The **FER** is issued; the applicant has **6 months** (extendable by 3 months) to respond with arguments/amendments (Form 7 — Amendment).

### Step 7 — Hearing & Opposition
- **Pre-Grant Opposition** — Any person can oppose within 6 months of publication (Section 25(1)).
- **Post-Grant Opposition** — Within 1 year of grant (Section 25(2)).
- A **hearing** is conducted by the Controller; both sides argue.

### Step 8 — Grant & Sealing of Patent
If accepted, the patent is **granted, sealed, and entered in the Register of Patents**. The **date of grant** is the date of grant, while the **term of 20 years** runs from the **date of filing**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exclusivity Period} &= \text{Filing Date} + 20 \text{ years} \\
\text{Renewal Fees Due} &= \text{From 3rd year onwards, annually (Form 8)} \\
\text{Lapse if Unpaid} &= \text{6-month grace period (with surcharge) before lapse}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Renewal & Maintenance
Annual renewal fees from year 3 onwards. Failure to pay → patent lapses (no reinstatement beyond 6 months from due date, except via restoration petition within 18 months of lapse, Form 14).

---

## 3.2 Patent Infringement Analysis — Step-by-Step

### Step 1 — Identify the Patented Claim
Locate the granted patent's claim set and identify the **independent claim** (broadest scope).

### Step 2 — Map Accused Product/Process to Claim Elements
For each claim element, identify the corresponding element in the accused product.

### Step 3 — Doctrine of Equivalents
If the accused product does not literally infringe every element, check whether it performs **substantially the same function in substantially the same way to achieve substantially the same result** as the claimed invention.

### Step 4 — Defenses (Sections 107–114 of Patents Act)
- **Prior user** right (Section 51)
- **Bolar exception** (Section 47A) — use for regulatory submissions
- **Government use** (Section 100)
- **Compulsory license** (Section 84) — if patented invention is not worked in India or is priced unreasonably

### Step 5 — Remedies
- **Injunctions** (temporary, permanent)
- **Damages** or account of profits
- **Delivery up / destruction** of infringing goods
- **Costs**

---

## 3.3 Licensing & Technology Transfer — Analytical Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Identify IP Asset
Determine the patent, copyright, or trademark to be licensed. Confirm ownership chain (employment contracts, inventor agreements).

### Step 2 — Define License Type
- **Exclusive License** — Only the licensee can practice; even owner cannot.
- **Sole License** — Owner + licensee only.
- **Non-Exclusive License** — Owner can license to multiple parties.

### Step 3 — Draft Key Clauses
- **Territory** (e.g., India, ASEAN, Global)
- **Field of Use** (e.g., only for telemedicine cardiology)
- **Royalty Rate** (lump sum, running royalty, milestone-based)
- **Term** (usually co-terminus with patent term)
- **Sub-licensing rights**
- **Improvements** (who owns improvements by licensee?)
- **Quality Control** (especially for trademarks)

### Step 4 — Royalty Calculation Model
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Running Royalty} &= \text{Net Sales} \times \text{Royalty Rate} \\
\text{Where} \quad \text{Net Sales} &= \text{Gross Sales} - \text{Returns} - \text{Discounts} - \text{Taxes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telemedicine Example:** A US-based AI radiology startup grants an Indian hospital chain an **exclusive license** to use its CT-scan AI model in India for a **7% running royalty on teleconsultation revenue** generated using the model. Royalty payable quarterly, audited annually.

### Step 5 — Technology Transfer Compliance
Under **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999**, cross-border IP licensing requires reporting to the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** and may require approval under technology transfer regulations.

---

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — "Which IP Right to Use?"

| Scenario | Recommended IP | Rationale |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Brand name of telemedicine app | **Trademark** | Identifies source of services |
| New AI algorithm for ECG diagnosis | **Patent** (technical effect) + **Copyright** (code) + **Trade Secret** (training data) | Multi-layer protection |
| Patient education video | **Copyright** | Protects expression |
| Confidential clinical protocol | **Trade Secret** + NDA | No time limit, no disclosure |
| 3D-printed stethoscope housing | **Design Patent (Industrial Design)** | Aesthetic + functional design |
| Ayurvedic formulation from Kerala | **Patent** (process) + **GI** (if specific to region) | Combination strategy |
| Wearable sensor hardware | **Patent** (device) + **Design** (look) + **Trademark** (brand) | Multi-layered |

---

## 3.5 Compulsory Licensing — Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 84 of the Indian Patents Act** allows any person to apply for a compulsory license after **3 years** from the date of grant if:
> (a) Patented invention is not worked in India, or
> (b) Patented invention is not available at reasonably affordable price, or
> (c) Patented invention is not worked to the fullest extent reasonably practicable.

**Example:** A patented diabetes-monitoring telemedicine patch is sold at ₹50,000/unit in India but at ₹5,000 in the US. The Indian government can issue a compulsory license to a domestic manufacturer to produce it at ₹6,000/unit, paying a royalty (typically capped at 4% of net sales) to the original patent holder.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compulsory License Royalty} &= \text{Net Sales} \times 4\% \quad (\text{typical cap}) \\
\text{Example: Sales of 100,000 units} \times \text{₹6,000} &= \text{₹60,00,00,000} \\
\text{Royalty to Original Patentee} &= \text{₹2,40,00,000} \quad (\text{4\% of ₹60 Cr})
\end{aligned}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Module carries one 14-mark ESE question (with internal choice) and one 3-mark Part A question. Below is a calibrated model question bank for the topic *"Intellectual Property Rights"* under Module 4.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Intellectual Property Rights. List any four types of IPR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) are the legal rights granted by a sovereign government to creators/owners of inventions, artistic works, designs, and symbols to protect their creations from unauthorized use.

Four types of IPR:
1. **Patent** — protects inventions and processes (20 years).
2. **Copyright** — protects original literary, artistic, and software works (life + 60 years).
3. **Trademark** — protects brand names, logos, and service marks (10 years, renewable).
4. **Trade Secret** — protects confidential business information (no time limit).

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for 4 types = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the criteria for patentability under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For an invention to be patentable in India, it must satisfy the following criteria (Section 2(1)(j)):
1. **Novelty** — Must be new; not known or published anywhere in the world before the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — Must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art.
3. **Industrial Application** — Must be capable of being made or used in some kind of industry.

Additionally, the invention must **not fall under the non-patentable categories** listed in Sections 3 and 4 (e.g., mere discovery, abstract theory, method of treatment of humans, atomic energy).

*(1 mark each for three criteria = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse *trademark* and *patent* symbols or durations — this is the #1 cause of 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Always** mention specific **Indian Act + Section number** (e.g., *"Patents Act 1970, Section 2(1)(j)"*) — vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Copyright duration** must be **life + 60 years** (NOT 50 years) for literary works in India — the older 50-year rule ended with the 2012 Amendment.
> 4. For software/AI questions, **always mention Section 3(k)** of the Patents Act to show awareness of Indian exclusion clauses.
> 5. Draw **neat, labeled diagrams** (like the Mermaid blocks above converted to hand-drawn) — examiners allot 1–2 marks specifically for diagrams.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **various types of Intellectual Property Rights** in detail with relevant statutory provisions and durations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role and relevance of IPR in the field of telemedicine**, with at least three real-world examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) are statutory rights granted to creators/owners over their intangible intellectual creations. In India, the principal IP statutes are the **Patents Act 1970**, **Copyright Act 1957**, **Trade Marks Act 1999**, **Designs Act 2000**, and **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act 1999**.

**Body (6 Marks — 1.5 marks each for four types):**

1. **Patent (Patents Act, 1970)**
   - Protects inventions (product, process, or use).
   - Duration: **20 years** from the date of filing.
   - Criteria: Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(j)).
   - Symbols: None.

2. **Copyright (Copyright Act, 1957)**
   - Protects original expression — literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph films, sound recordings, computer software.
   - Duration: **Life of author + 60 years** (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic); **60 years** from publication (films, sound recordings, photographs).
   - Rights: Economic + Moral (Section 57).
   - Symbol: **©**.

3. **Trademark (Trade Marks Act, 1999)**
   - Protects distinctive marks identifying goods/services.
   - Duration: **10 years**, renewable indefinitely.
   - Symbol: **™** (before registration) / **®** (after registration).

4. **Other Forms (2 Marks shared)**
   - **Industrial Design** — 10 years + 5 years extension.
   - **Geographical Indication** — 10 years, renewable.
   - **Trade Secret** — No time limit; protected under IT Act 2000 + common law.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** IPR strikes a balance between rewarding creators and promoting public access to knowledge.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Telemedicine — the remote delivery of healthcare via ICT — generates vast quantities of intangible assets (software, AI models, content, brand identity, clinical data) that require robust IP protection to incentivize innovation, ensure quality, and enable commercialization.

**Body — Three Real-World Examples (2 Marks each = 6 Marks):**

1. **AI Diagnostic Algorithm — Example: Sigtuple's AI100 (Pathology):**
   - The AI algorithm for blood-smear analysis is protected as a **patent** (technical effect) + **copyright** (source code) + **trade secret** (training dataset). This multi-layered approach prevents competitors from copying the diagnostic logic.

2. **Telemedicine Brand — Example: Practo / Apollo TeleHealth:**
   - The platform name and logo are protected as **trademarks** (10 years, renewable). This builds brand trust and prevents fraud/counterfeit telehealth services.

3. **Wearable Telemedicine Hardware — Example: Tricog's ECG Cloud Platform:**
   - The portable ECG device is protected as a **product patent** + **industrial design** for the casing. The cloud-based interpretation software is **copyright** + **trade secret**.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Effective IP management is essential for sustainable growth of the telemedicine sector, ensures patient data confidentiality, and supports cross-border technology transfer under TRIPS and PCT frameworks.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*
**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for filing a patent in India**, mentioning all relevant Forms, fees, and timelines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Compulsory Licensing** under the Indian Patents Act. Under what circumstances can it be invoked, and what is its relevance for telemedicine and pharmaceutical patents? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Invention Disclosure & Prior Art Search (0.5 Mark):**
The inventor documents the invention and searches existing literature (PATENTSCOPE, USPTO, INPASS) for novelty.

**2. Filing of Application (2 Marks):**
- **Form 1** — Application for Grant of Patent.
- **Form 2** — Provisional or Complete Specification.
- **Form 3** — Foreign applications statement.
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.
- **Form 26** — Authorization of Patent Agent (if any).
- Filed at the appropriate Indian Patent Office (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata).
- **Filing Date = Priority Date**.

**3. Publication (0.5 Mark):**
After **18 months** (or earlier on Form 9 request) in the Patent Journal.

**4. Request for Examination (1 Mark):**
- **Form 18** within **48 months** of priority date.
- Failure = Application deemed withdrawn.

**5. Examination & FER (1 Mark):**
- Examiner issues **First Examination Report (FER)**.
- Applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months) via **Form 7** (amendment) or written arguments.

**6. Opposition & Hearing (1 Mark):**
- **Pre-grant** opposition (6 months post-publication) under Section 25(1).
- **Post-grant** opposition (within 1 year) under Section 25(2).
- Hearing conducted by Controller.

**7. Grant, Sealing & Renewal (1 Mark):**
- On acceptance, patent is **granted, sealed, and registered**.
- Term: **20 years from filing date**.
- **Annual renewal from Year 3** via **Form 8**. Grace period of 6 months with surcharge; beyond → restoration via Form 14 within 18 months.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A **compulsory license** is a government-authorized permission to a third party (other than the patent holder) to manufacture, use, or sell a patented invention **without the patentee's consent**, subject to payment of royalty. It is governed by **Section 84** (general), **Section 92** (national emergency), and **Section 100** (government use) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**Circumstances for Invocation (2 Marks):**
Any person may apply after **3 years from the date of grant** if:
1. The patented invention is **not worked in India** (not manufactured locally).
2. The patented invention is **not available at a reasonably affordable price**.
3. The patented invention is **not worked to the fullest extent reasonably practicable**.

Additional grounds (Section 92): **national emergency, public non-commercial use, or public health crisis** (refined by the 2005 Amendment post-Doha Declaration).

**Royalty & Procedure (1 Mark):**
- License granted by the **Controller General of Patents**.
- Royalty typically capped at **4% of net sales**, subject to negotiation.
- Licensee must demonstrate capability to work the invention.
- Patentee may be granted cross-license on reasonable terms.

**Relevance to Telemedicine & Pharma (3 Marks):**

1. **Pharmaceutical Patents** — Used to break monopolies on life-saving drugs (e.g., **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** — compulsory license for Nexavar cancer drug at ₹8,800/month vs Bayer's ₹2,80,428/month).
2. **Telemedicine Hardware & Software** — If a patented portable ICU monitor or AI-diagnostic is priced beyond reach, the government can issue a compulsory license to scale domestic manufacturing.
3. **Public Health Crises** — During pandemics (e.g., COVID-19), compulsory licensing enabled rapid manufacture of ventilators, oximeters, and vaccine IP sharing.
4. **TRIPS Compliance** — India's 2005 Patents Act 3rd Amendment incorporated **TRIPS Article 31** flexibilities, allowing compulsory licensing for export to countries with insufficient manufacturing capacity.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** Compulsory licensing balances patent monopolies with public welfare, ensuring that essential medical innovations — including those driving telemedicine — remain accessible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Module 4: Intellectual Property Rights**

- **IPR** = Legal rights over intangible intellectual creations.
- **Five core IP types in India:** Patent, Copyright, Trademark, Design, GI (Trade Secret is statutory by IT Act 2000 + common law).
- **Patent:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application = Patentability. **20 years** from filing. Governed by **Patents Act, 1970**.
- **Section 3(k)** — Software "as such" is not patentable in India; must show technical effect.
- **Copyright:** Arises **automatically**. Term = **Life of Author + 60 years** (for literary works). Governed by **Copyright Act, 1957** (amended 2012).
- **Trademark:** ® after registration; ™ before. **10 years**, renewable indefinitely. Governed by **Trade Marks Act, 1999**.
- **Industrial Design:** **10 + 5 years**. Governed by **Designs Act, 2000**.
- **GI:** **10 years**, renewable. Example: **Alleppey Green Cardamom**. Governed by **GI Act, 1999**.
- **Trade Secret:** No time limit; protected via **NDA + IT Act Sections 66/72**.
- **Compulsory License** — Section 84, available **after 3 years** of grant if not worked / not affordable.
- **Key Treaties:** **Paris Convention** (priority right), **PCT** (international patent filing), **Berne Convention** (auto-copyright), **TRIPS** (minimum IP standards), **Budapest Treaty** (microorganism deposit), **Doha Declaration** (public health flexibility).
- **Patent Filing Forms:** Form 1 (application), Form 2 (specification), Form 3 (foreign filing), Form 5 (inventorship), Form 7 (amendment), Form 8 (renewal), Form 9 (early publication), Form 14 (restoration), Form 18 (examination request).
- **Key Timelines:** Publication at 18 months; Examination request within 48 months; FER response within 6 months; Renewal from Year 3.
- **Telemedicine IP Examples to remember:** AI diagnostic algorithm (Patent + Copyright + Trade Secret), Telehealth brand (Trademark), Patient data (Copyright + DB rights + Trade Secret), Wearable hardware (Patent + Design).
- **Infringement Remedies:** Injunction + Damages + Account of Profits + Delivery-up + Costs.
- **Bolar Exception (Section 47A):** Generic manufacturers can use patented inventions for regulatory approval before patent expiry — relevant for telemedicine pharma.
- **DPDP Act 2023** (Digital Personal Data Protection) overlaps with IP in protecting anonymized patient datasets.

---
